The water rushed out of the faucet and onto my feet. The pressure of the water on the top of my foot came crashing down, enveloping my body. I was sinking. Not cold, but warmth encircled me. The water flowed endlessly out of that small hole. The pounding sound of the water resonated in my ears, drowning out the noises outside. I lost track of time staring at it. The gleaming metal reflected my weariness as water droplets bounced off my toes. I almost drifted off a couple times. The rhythmic sound began to lull me to sleep several times, but I resisted its sweet pull. If I didn't resist, then that would be the end of it. I wouldn't be able to do what I needed to do anymore. There was only one purpose left to me. So noisy. This was for the best. Why couldn't he understand that? Why did he want to stop me? He had driven me to this. Without me he would be happy. He had told me so once. The cruelty of those words hurt almost as much as the punishment. There was no smile when he struck me, but there was with those words. The child, our child, would be happier too. To grow up in a happy family with a happy parent. The tub was beginning to fill up. I threw the pills in my mouth and threw back some wine prepared for the long sleep. The door broke open, and my husband stormed in. Why did he stop me? It would have been better. They would have been happy. I would have been happy.

**Main Idea:**

In a dystopian future where memories can be extracted and sold, a faulty memory replication machine accidentally transfers traumatic memories to a young girl named Kate. These memories - involving violence, family betrayal, and murder - become confused with her own experiences. The story reveals that Kate's family (her mother Emma, grandmother, and Jacob) had previously tried to protect her by erasing dangerous memories of her violent tendencies, but the corrupted memory transfer has now restored and distorted these memories. Kate believes her family killed her father and seeks revenge with a knife, having already killed Jacob and planning to kill her mother and grandmother. The grandmother realizes their attempt to help Kate by manipulating her memories has backfired tragically, as Kate can no longer distinguish between implanted memories and reality, and her original violent delusions have returned stronger than before.

I'm not normally critical in my reviews but this book really is a waste of your money unless you truly have no idea how to cook. The recipes are entirely too simple. If you cook, you don't need this book. They are literally just giving you extremely simple recipes that don't include corn which you can do all on your own - things like basic grilled chicken with salt and pepper or plain steamed vegetables. On top of that they have mayo in a lot of the recipes (mayo has corn in it) yet they give you a recipe for ketchup (hunts ketchup is corn free). This inconsistency is baffling - why make your own ketchup when store-bought works, but use mayo that defeats the whole purpose? They also don't give practical advice at all. Like buy potato starch instead of corn starch as a substitute - advice so basic it's the first thing that comes up in any search. There isn't a single piece of info in this book that I didn't figure out on my first day from going on the internet. For example, learning that corn syrup is in most processed foods or that citric acid often comes from corn took me about 5 minutes of research. Skip this book and spend your time wisely. On google.

**The Hidden Underground City That Never Sleeps**

Beneath your feet lies an invisible metropolis—a thousand-mile network of tunnels that rivals the complexity of the city above. This subterranean world stretches so far it could connect the Pacific Northwest to Southern California, yet most of us never think about it.

Every morning, nearly a million people unknowingly contribute to one of the most remarkable logistical operations on Earth. Your coffee cup rinse joins a massive daily river of 80 million gallons—enough water to submerge a football stadium in less than a week. When storms arrive, this underground city transforms into a superhighway, managing six times that volume as rainwater joins the flow.

Like the city above, this buried infrastructure is aging. Some of these tunnels have been faithfully serving San Francisco for over a century, predating the Golden Gate Bridge by decades. Imagine—pipes installed when horses still clattered down Market Street continue working around the clock today.

Three treatment guardians stand sentinel at the system's end: Southeast, Oceanside, and NorthPoint. Here, an invisible battle wages daily against pollutants, transforming what was once waste into water clean enough to return to the Bay and Pacific Ocean.

This isn't just infrastructure—it's urban circulatory system, environmental shield, and public health protector rolled into one. As San Francisco prepares this network for the next century through seismic upgrades and expanded replacement programs, we're essentially performing surgery on a patient that can never stop working.

The city above depends entirely on the city below.

Daily Mail Reporter, 8 September 2012

Music legend Sir Paul McCartney has been awarded France's most prestigious public honour, the Legion of Honour, for his unparalleled services to music. The former Beatle was promoted to the rank of officer by President Francois Hollande himself during a private ceremony in Paris.

At 70, McCartney now stands alongside an elite group of international artists, including singers Barbra Streisand and Liza Minnelli, who have also received this esteemed recognition. The intimate ceremony, held at the historic Elysee Palace, saw President Hollande personally bestow the medal upon McCartney, surrounded by proud family and friends.

A clearly moved Sir Paul expressed his gratitude, stating, "It is such an honour to be awarded this." In a lighthearted moment, President Hollande praised McCartney's profound contribution to the arts, playfully admitting his preference for The Beatles over The Rolling Stones, and even quipping that he favored McCartney over fellow Beatle John Lennon in his younger days.

This rarely bestowed honour, established by Napoleon Bonaparte in 1802, is typically reserved for French nationals excelling in military or civil service, underscoring McCartney's extraordinary global impact. He joins a select group of British luminaries to receive it, including Queen Elizabeth II, actor Sir Laurence Olivier, author Graham Greene, and war veteran Henry Allingham. Already knighted by the Queen in 1997 and having recently captivated a global audience at the London 2012 Olympic Games opening ceremony, Sir Paul's latest accolade further cements his legendary status.

Everything had been planned from the start. Down to the smallest detail. Now that Lord Vader was on his way to purging the Jedi temple, Palpatine grinned with evil glee. As Master Yoda walked into the room, ready for the duel he knew would come, he watched as Palpatine raised a communicator and spoke into it. "Execute order sixty-five." The clone commander on the other end must have blinked behind his helmet, because he didn't say anything. "Do you hear me commander?" Palpatine repeated, with the Clone nodding "it will be done my lord." Turning to look at his tiny adversary, Palpatine grinned widely, showing off those pale yellow teeth of his before he paused. Something was wrong. Something had... At the same instant it dawned on him that he'd given the wrong order, a squad of clones broke through the door and began to fire their blasters at him. The last thing going through his mind, other than the blaster bolt, was dismay, and annoyance at the senator that had insisted that contingency order.

"The voice was growing fainter. Harry was sure it was moving away — moving upward. A mixture of fear and excitement gripped him as he stared at the dark ceiling; how could it be moving upward? Was it a phantom, to whom stone ceilings didn't matter?"
A phantom is a magical creature with the ability to move around unseen. In his second year at Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry, Harry Potter conjectured that the disembodied voice he heard moving throughout the school was perhaps a phantom. Later, that same school year, while witnessing a memory of fifty years prior, by means of T. M. Riddle's Diary, he thought of himself as "little more than a phantom, completely invisible to the people of fifty years ago".
Behind the scenes
- While never explicitly confirmed, Harry's initial conjecture strongly indicates the 'phantom' he first encounters is a genuine magical entity, capable of moving unseen and through physical barriers. His subsequent self-identification as a 'phantom' due to his invisibility fifty years prior underscores how the term's literal properties—unseen movement and lack of physical obstruction—form the basis for its powerful metaphorical application. This intentional narrative ambiguity compels readers to consider the profound implications of such an elusive and potent presence within Hogwarts.
- Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets (First appearance)

Notwithstanding the corporate politics surrounding TSR's editorial decisions, Jean Rabe faced the considerable challenge of continuing one of the most commercially successful fantasy novel franchises in publishing history. Given the established popularity and narrative craft of the preceding world creators, Margaret Weis and Tracy Hickman, any successor would inevitably encounter resistance from devoted readers. While one might argue that Rabe's work was disadvantaged by this reception context, an objective analysis of *Dawning of a New Age* reveals significant structural and stylistic deficiencies that transcend such considerations. Even accounting for the limitations imposed by TSR's revised gaming modules as source material, the novel exhibits fundamental weaknesses in characterization, descriptive prose, action sequences, and narrative coherence. These technical shortcomings suggest that the work's critical reception reflects literary merit rather than merely unfavorable comparative positioning.

“We are the ones we have been waiting for.” ― June Jordan

When driven by inspiration, a writer allows their heart to guide their words, sharing with the world their journey, motivations, and sense of self. 

Such a writer emerged in 1936: June Jordan, a groundbreaking African American author whose literary voice brought attention to social injustice and personal adversity. Through her poetry, essays, and plays, Jordan shaped a distinctive language that urged people to listen, contemplate, and reconsider their perspectives. Her youth was marked by hardship, including a distant and fraught relationship with her father. In Civil Wars: Selected Essays, 1963-80 (1981), Jordan candidly describes her challenging upbringing:

“For a long while during childhood I was relatively small, short, and, in some other ways, a target for bully abuse. In fact, my father was the first regular bully in my life.”

Yet not all her early recollections were painful. Supportive teachers encouraged her creative writing, and poetry became her method of self-expression. Over time, her verse evolved, delving deeply into her lived experience as a Black bisexual woman, earning her recognition as one of the most influential African American writers of her era. Jordan was unwavering in her advocacy for human rights, and her writing vibrated with a fierce, purposeful energy that spoke to the struggles of her time. Although some critics viewed her work as overly political, she refused to be silenced. As an activist, she championed civil rights, women’s rights, sexual freedom, and the right to self-identify.

The Poetry Foundation characterizes her work as presenting “a radical, globalized notion of solidarity amongst the world’s marginalized and oppressed.” In collections like Some Changes, Living Room, and Talking Back to God, Jordan utilizes conversational, sometimes colloquial language to discuss themes such as family, bisexuality, political repression, African American heritage, racial disparities, and remembrance. She became a passionate advocate for self-expression, inspiring those who longed for similar liberation.

Her openness about her bisexuality was significant, as she confronted stereotypes and prejudices from heterosexual as well as gay circles. Jordan staunchly defended sexual autonomy, viewing sexual repression as one of humanity’s most profound conflicts.

“Bisexuality means I am free and I am as likely to want to love a woman as I am likely to want to love a man, and what about that? Isn’t that what freedom implies?”

Her own childhood trials also led Jordan to advocate for children, as seen in works like His Own Where, Victories, New Life: New Room, and Kimako’s Story.

In 2002, Jordan succumbed to breast cancer.

June Jordan’s enduring spirit, resilience, and lifelong dedication to equality underscore why her body of work is essential reading.

While no one can deny that a serious problem exists in our schools, what is most alarming is that so few seem committed to taking immediate action about it
New York, NY (Vocus) February 12, 2009
A year to the day after 15-year-old Lawrence King was murdered at E.O. Green Junior High in Oxnard, Calif., because of his sexual orientation and gender expression, the Gay, Lesbian and Straight Education Network is calling on school officials and policy makers across the country to take action to make sure all of their students are safe.
"Our thoughts continue to go out to an Oxnard community that will never be the same after this senseless display of hate," said GLSEN Executive Director Eliza Byard. "Lawrence King was murdered simply for being himself. It is imperative that as a nation we do everything we can to make sure that the tragedy of Feb. 12, 2008 never happens again and that every student is safe, respected and valued in school."
While King's murder by a 14-year-old classmate is an extreme example of the hate directed at lesbian, gay, bisexual and transgender (LGBT) students in school, GLSEN's 2007 National School Climate Survey revealed that bullying and harassment of LGBT students is the norm rather than the exception in schools.
Nearly nine out of 10 LGBT students (86.2%) reported being verbally harassed, 44.1% being physically harassed and 22.1% being physically assaulted at school in the past year because of their sexual orientation, according to the survey of 6,209 middle and high school students.
Additionally, three-fifths of LGBT students (60.8%) felt unsafe at school because of their sexual orientation and about a third (32.7%) skipped a day of school in the past month because of feeling unsafe.
"While no one can deny that a serious problem exists in our schools, what is most alarming is that so few seem committed to taking immediate action about it," Byard said. "The good news is that schools can take simple, proven steps right now to make their students safer. Every child deserves that much."
GLSEN recommends four approaches that schools can take to address anti-LGBT bullying and harassment.
- Adopt a comprehensive anti-bullying policy that enumerates categories such as race, gender, ethnicity, religion, sexual orientation and gender expression/identity. Enumeration is crucial to ensure that anti-bullying policies are effective for LGBT students. Policies without enumeration are essentially as effective as having no policy at all when it comes to anti-LGBT bullying and harassment, according to GLSEN's 2007 National School Climate Survey.
- Require staff trainings to enable school staff to identify and address anti-LGBT name-calling, bullying and harassment effectively and in a timely manner.
- Support student efforts to address anti-LGBT bullying and harassment on campus, such as the formation of a Gay-Straight Alliance or participation in the National Day of Silence on April 17.
- Institute age-appropriate, inclusive curricula to help students understand and respect difference within the school community and society as a whole.
Vigils are taking place across the country to remember Lawrence King. Find out more at http://www.rememberinglawrence.org.
GLSEN, the Gay, Lesbian and Straight Education Network, is the leading national education organization focused on ensuring safe schools for all students. Established nationally in 1995, GLSEN envisions a world in which every child learns to respect and accept all people, regardless of sexual orientation or gender identity/expression. GLSEN seeks to develop school climates where difference is valued for the positive contribution it makes to creating a more vibrant and diverse community. For information on GLSEN's research, educational resources, public policy advocacy, student organizing programs and educator training initiatives, visit http://www.glsen.org.

For generations, humanity clung to the fragile peace negotiated after the Resource Wars, a peace built on the ashes of collapsing nations and the grim promise of nuclear deterrence. But beneath the surface, the old hatreds festered, fueled by dwindling habitable land and access to potable water. Einstein was wrong. Sticks and stones? Wouldn't that be a treat. We knew this day would come. It was only a matter of time before someone snapped and lit the flame that ignited the fuse bringing everything and everyone to their knees. His name was Rowell Farswy, a fanatic general who rose to power amidst the chaos of the fracturing Eastern United Front – a once-dominant techno-autocracy that had controlled the resource-rich Pacific Rim for decades. Farswy’s coup, executed with brutal efficiency, not only toppled the old regime but instantly escalated long-standing territorial disputes into an open, global conflict, culminating in his audacious, pre-emptive strike on the West Coast defensive grid. I am the lone survivor of my platoon, a drop of blue in a swarming sea of Rowell’s crimson legions and the encroaching grey of the irradiated fallout. My friends, allies, and comrades have all fallen, some to the insidious creep of radiation sickness from the initial tactical exchanges, others to the non-stop barrage of plasma rounds and conventional bullets that had cut through our lines as easily as a knife through warm butter. If it wasn't for the wayward tactical strike that slammed into their offensive rush I too would be among the lucky ones rotting away. Ironically I was unable to move as a large chunk of wood had been lodged in my left foot when the strike had hit. Heh sticks and stones eh? Maybe that old man was right in a way. When it all comes down to it a stick is just as deadly to me as any bullet, of course this maybe the blood loss talking. Another massive explosion rocked the house I had taken refuge in. Toppling a bookcase and shattering the remaining windows. The end was near now. A blissful peace awaited me to vanish from this Hell. A second explosion ripped a hole through the southern wall, showering me in a cloud of dust and debris. Through the hole was a wasteland of devastation, a once thriving city block was lit up like the red hot flames of the fiery pits of Hell. The end was near. Einstein was dead wrong about one thing, though... Far off in the distance, a mushroom cloud erupted, climbing higher and higher into the sky – a majestic beauty at odds with the ripples of devastation it created. This wasn't just another regional flare-up; this was the final, desperate gambit in what historians would undoubtedly call World War III, a conflict that had already decimated three continents. A wave of explosive heat knocked away everything in a 10-mile radius as easily as a child may knock over a tower of blocks. The wave grew closer, I could feel the searing heat ripping the flesh from my face. All I could think of was how short-sighted Einstein must have been. How he missed out on one important factor: his grim prophecy for World War IV – that it would be fought with sticks and stones – neglected the terrifying prerequisite. Someone would have to survive World War III first.

Gods don't bleed. The fact that the corpse—already decaying rapidly—looked so disturbingly ordinary unsettled him. Somehow, he had expected more: some trace of the divine, some sign that this being was not just flesh. Instead, every wound gaped open, but what poured out was only emptiness. He had actually done it. What no man believed possible, he had achieved. He felt a surge of wild exhilaration—his body quaking with adrenaline, on the verge of laughter or tears or both. 

Yet, one step remained. He hurried home, and found the familiar walls transformed, their dull grays alive with hidden meaning. Every crack, every imperfection morphed into a pattern, whispering secrets he’d never noticed before—truths he could now decipher. Heart pounding, he seized his pen and wrote the words that had crystallized in his mind:

*God is dead. God remains dead. Man has killed him.*

Some New York City cops may have turned their backs on Mayor Bill de Blasio but he hasn't cut ties with them yet. Mayor Bill de Blasio along with City Council Speaker Melissa Mark-Viverito announced today that they wish to name a street in honor of Detectives Wenjian Liu and Rafael Ramos who were shot to death execution style last month. Officers Wenjian Liu and Rafael Ramos were working overtime as part of an anti-terrorism drill in Bedford-Stuyvesant just before 3 p.m. when they were shot point-blank in the head by lone gunman Ismaaiyl Brinsley, 28, who later turned the gun on himself. Honored: Mayor Bill de Blasio announced today that he wishes to name a street in honor of Detectives Rafael Ramos (left) and Wenjian Liu (right) who were shot to death execution style last month . Re-named: Ridgewood Avenue (left), between Shepherd Avenue and Highland Place in Brooklyn would be referred to as 'Detective Rafael Ramos Way' and West 6th Street (right), between Avenue S and Avenue T in Brooklyn, would be called named 'Detective Wenjian Liu Way', reports the website of NYC.gov. Ridgewood Avenue, between Shepherd Avenue and Highland Place in Brooklyn would be referred to as 'Detective Rafael Ramos Way.' West 6th Street, between Avenue S and Avenue T in Brooklyn, would be called named 'Detective Wenjian Liu Way', reports the website of NYC.gov. A bill moving to name the streets after the slain detectives will be up for voting at a City Council meeting in January. 'Our fallen heroes will never be forgotten. Their memory lives on in their families, and in the NYPD family. And now it will live on in the streets of the communities these brave men lived in and protected. This is an expression of our pride in each of these men, and our sadness at their loss,' said Mayor Bill de Blasio today. 'We will never forget the tremendous sacrifice by Detectives Rafael Ramos and Wenjian Liu in service to our city,' said City Council Speaker Melissa Mark-Viverito. 'These courageous men were fathers, brothers and sons, and they were committed to the protection of every New Yorker who calls this city home. The street co-namings in honor of Detective Ramos and Detective Liu will stand as an ever-present testament to their legacy and memory, and will remind us all of the daily sacrifice of our brave men, women and families of the New York City Police Department to keep our city safe.' A few more City Council members shared their support for naming streets after some of New York's finest. 'This is a tribute to the tremendous sacrifice that our police officers make to keep New York City safe for all residents. This street co-naming will ensure that the memory of Detectives Liu and Ramos lives on in our city,' said Council Member Mark Treyg. 'Detective Rafael Ramos and Detective Wenjian Liu served New York City with honor and bravery. Their selfless sacrifice must never be forgotten. Co-naming these streets in their memory is one important way we can ensure that the legacies of our fallen heroes will live on in the hearts and minds of New Yorkers,' piped in Council Member Mark Levine. Tribute: 'This is an expression of our pride in each of these men, and our sadness at their loss,' said Mayor Bill de Blasio today of wishing to honor the slain cops .

Social Media erupted on Thursday night over a video showing Mr Wells linking child abuse and gay relationships.
It was taken at a hustings for the South Down constituency.
The DUP MLA suggested that homosexual couples were more likely to be in unstable relationships and children were vulnerable as a result.
Alliance North Down MLA, Stephen Farry, has said that Mr Wells must apologise and retract the remarks he made.
"Alliance will be looking to raise this matter with assembly officials and ask them to investigate whether he has broken any assembly rules," he added.
SDLP MLA Colum Eastwood said Mr Wells must immediately withdraw "these comments and issue a full apology".
He said Mr Wells had gone "far beyond the limits of acceptable behaviour".
Mr Wells said that during a debate in South Down on Thursday evening, he refused to agree with an audience member that "marriage should be redefined".
"I oppose the redefinition of marriage.  Indeed the assembly has voted three times on this matter and every time a cross party selection of MLAs have supported the current definition of marriage," he said.
"At the hustings event, I said that marriage was a stable environment to raise children. I am saddened that some people were trying to misrepresent my comments.
"Where there are non-stable relationships involving children, whether heterosexual or homosexual, the children suffer.  I make no distinction between anyone who neglects a child on the basis of their sexual orientation."

In today’s rapidly changing world, the success of development programs hinges on informed decision-making and effective communication. In Eritrea, the integration of Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and Public Relations (PR) has become a driving force behind transformative progress—not only bridging information gaps, but also empowering stakeholders to act with clarity and purpose.

Traditionally, GIS and PR have operated as distinct fields. GIS excels at mapping and analyzing spatial data, supporting planners and policymakers with comprehensive, actionable information. PR, meanwhile, takes on the crucial role of disseminating these insights, mobilizing communities, and shaping public understanding. Yet in the context of Eritrea’s development journey, the collaboration between these disciplines has proven indispensable. Their synergy now stands at the core of Eritrea’s most visible successes.

This collaborative approach becomes clear when reflecting on Eritrea’s ambitious achievements in infrastructure, healthcare, and environmental management. For instance, through the advanced capabilities of GIS, officials are able to swiftly identify regions vulnerable to communicable diseases like malaria. With this knowledge, healthcare teams and decision-makers can implement targeted interventions—sometimes even relocating communities from high-risk areas to healthier environments. But it is PR that ensures these initiatives are understood and embraced by the public, fostering a participatory spirit and collective responsibility.

The utility of GIS was particularly underscored during crises such as the 2011 volcanic eruption in the Southern Red Sea region. GIS enabled swift environmental assessments and strategic planning, mitigating disaster impacts. At the same time, PR channeled crucial information to affected communities, supporting rapid response and future preparedness. Together, these tools not only facilitated immediate relief but also informed long-term policies for risk prevention and environmental protection.

Agriculture, too, has benefited immensely. The construction of dams and water reservoirs for improved irrigation and food security relies on GIS for optimal site selection and impact analysis. Yet without the transparency and buy-in fostered by PR, such monumental projects might yield only short-lived results. Through clear communication and education, PR ensures that communities fully understand and support these investments, helping to secure their lasting benefits.

Eritrea’s abundant natural beauty—its diverse wildlife, forests, and escarpments—presents yet another opportunity for this integrated approach. Establishing and preserving national parks, or responsibly managing tourist attractions, are tasks greatly enhanced by GIS’s precision mapping and data analysis. PR campaigns then play an essential role in raising awareness about these national treasures, galvanizing stewardship among citizens and encouraging responsible tourism.

At its best, PR is more than just information sharing; it subtly shapes perceptions, builds consensus, and inspires action. When paired with the data-driven insights of GIS, it lays a foundation for wise use and preservation of national assets—from agricultural lands to industrial sites, urban centers to unspoiled coastlines. Eritrea’s proactive embrace of this partnership is turning information into tangible progress, where every community member has a stake in the nation’s sustainable development.

Indeed, the road to sustainable growth requires careful planning, data-driven strategy, and cohesive communication. As Eritrea continues to invest in the integration of GIS and PR, it strengthens its capacity to anticipate challenges, seize opportunities, and realize a vision of resilient, inclusive progress for generations to come.

The Seven Point Mind Training is supposed to be one of the most exhilarating teaching in Buddhism, but after reading this book I was greatly disappointed and wish that the author had not chosen the name of the training as the title of this book. This is just like one of the many confusing work borrowing the title of Buddhist teaching and as mentioned by HH Dalai Lama, indeed Alan Wallace is a Buddhist thinker (mind you, not teacher). The book is like an essay of his thought rather than a guide from a teacher to a student. It is shallow compared to the seven point mind training taught by the lamas at the Kagyu Institute. If you are one of the readers who seek such a training, don't be put off by this book and continue pursuing this training. I will not recommend this book for serious Buddhists seeking mind training.

By Nelson Groom for Daily Mail Australia and Australian Associated Press.

Congregants were reportedly left distressed after individuals believed to be supporters of the Islamic State allegedly issued death threats and displayed the group’s emblematic flag in Sydney on Wednesday. Eyewitnesses assert that a vehicle drove past Our Lady of Lebanon Church in Harris Park, western Sydney, on Wednesday evening, during which the occupants are said to have brandished a flag similar to those associated with Islamic State militants. A cleric from the church informed the Australian Associated Press that the individuals in the vehicle threatened to “kill the Christians” and to “slaughter their children.” “The rhetoric was stark and members of the congregation were understandably fearful in response,” he stated.

The incident occurred at Our Lady of Lebanon Church in Harris Park, where it was reported that suspected Islamic State supporters intimidated parishioners preceding a religious service. Monsignor Shora Maree, the Maronite Catholic parish priest, subsequently contacted law enforcement authorities following the threats directed at his congregation. Witnesses indicated to police that a small triangular flag inscribed with Arabic words—allegedly expressing the phrase “there is only one god and Muhammad is the prophet”—was displayed from the vehicle’s window.

Inspector Brian Jackson of the Rosehill Police confirmed that threats had indeed been made in connection with individuals near the church. Monsignor Maree reached out to law enforcement prior to the scheduled 7 p.m. mass on Wednesday evening, resulting in a police presence at the church while hundreds of congregants attended the service. It is understood that detectives are currently investigating the identities of those responsible for the threats.

This incident coincides with preparations by the Abbott Government to introduce stringent new counterterrorism legislation in the forthcoming Parliamentary sessions, measures specifically aimed at addressing and reducing support for the Islamic State among Australian citizens. The matter arises in the context of heightened national security concerns, exemplified by individuals such as Khaled Sharrouf, recognized as Australia’s most wanted terrorist and depicted in media sources brandishing the Islamic State flag.

"Follow the child" is the mantra Montessori teachers know by heart, and in Donna's book, it is made clear how to follow every child. Some children are easy to work with, giving immediate feedback and responding to the most gentle guidance. For those children, it is easy to "follow." Others are not so easy to "follow," and some are downright difficult. These are the children we find it difficult to follow or to approach each day with fresh and unbiased eyes. Donna gives many examples of the benefits to children of doing this. The benefits extend to all children in the class and school, not just the children who are not yet peaceful. Donna's book gives us ideas, strategies, and hope that if we continue to follow the child and hold his or her best interests at heart, we will help them become peaceful as a community, and the community will benefit hugely. An amazing read, one to keep close at hand and refer to often—especially when we have "children who are not yet peaceful" in our own classes.

To an unpracticed eye, the delicate reliefs and whimsical arabesques dancing across the walls of the Alhambra appear to have been lovingly sculptured by hand—each curve and flourish the product of some impossibly patient artisan who must have devoted lifetimes to crafting such inexhaustible variety within such harmonious design. This illusion proves most bewitching in the vaults and cupolas, which shimmer like crystalline honeycombs or winter's most intricate frostwork, their stalactites and pendants weaving patterns so complex they seem to mock the very notion of human comprehension.

Yet how quickly astonishment transforms into admiration when the truth reveals itself: this fairy palace is born not of superhuman patience, but of ingenious practicality! These are plates of humble plaster of Paris, cast in molds and joined with such skill that they masquerade as the work of angels. This clever deception—this method of dressing walls in arabesques and adorning vaults with grotto-work—first blossomed in Damascus, but the Moors of Morocco seized upon it with such enthusiasm and innovation that they transformed mere technique into art, giving Saracenic architecture its most graceful and fanciful soul.

The secret behind all this enchanted tracery proves delightfully simple: imagine the naked wall divided by the same practical grid lines that artists use when copying paintings, then watch as intersecting segments of circles dance across this framework. Armed with nothing more than these crossing lines—some straight as honesty, others curved as a lover's smile—the artists could work with both speed and certainty, conjuring from these humble intersections an infinite universe of patterns that somehow maintained their familial resemblance.

Gold leaf lavished itself generously across the stucco-work, particularly in those cupolas that caught and scattered light like captured starlight. Between these golden veins, artists painted with colors so vivid they seem to pulse with life—vermilion bold as fresh blood, lapis lazuli deep as midnight skies—all mixed with egg whites and applied with brushes fine as whispers. Ford notes that the Egyptians, Greeks, and Arabs of antiquity trusted only these primitive colors, and their preference echoes through every authentically Arabic corner of the Alhambra. What seems almost miraculous is how these ancient pigments still sing with color after centuries of patient waiting.

The lower walls of the grand saloons wear a different kind of beauty: glazed tiles that embrace them like jeweled armor, rising several feet from the floor in patterns that shift and play before the eye. Some bear the proud escutcheons of long-dead Moslem kings, their heraldry crossed with bands and mottos that whisper of vanished glory. These azulejos—as the Spanish lovingly call them, borrowing from the Arabic az-zulaj—carry the wisdom of the Orient in their very being. Their cool touch, their cleanliness, their stubborn refusal to harbor unwanted guests make them perfect companions for sultry climates, where they grace fountain edges, embrace bathing chambers, and line walls with their serene presence.

Ford, ever the romantic scholar, suspects these tiles of ancient nobility. Gazing at their sapphire depths and celestial blues, he wonders if they might be descendants of those very pavements that dazzled biblical eyes: "There was under his feet as it were a paved work of a sapphire stone," and again, "Behold I will lay thy stones with fair colors, and lay thy foundations with sapphires." Whether or not they trace their lineage to such exalted origins, these porcelain beauties certainly conquered Spain early in the Moslem occupation, and their descendants still gleam among eight-century-old ruins, testament to both their beauty and endurance.

These tiles proved so irresistible that Spanish craftsmen continue creating them to this day, adorning the finest houses throughout the peninsula—particularly in the sun-drunk southern provinces, where summer apartments welcome their cooling embrace. When Spanish rule extended to the Netherlands, these ceramic treasures traveled north, where the famously fastidious Dutch embraced them with the passion of the converted. How perfectly these Oriental gems suited the Dutch obsession with spotless homes! And so, through conquest and commerce, the azulejos of Spain and the az-zulaj of Arabia found themselves reborn as "Dutch tiles"—a charming case of mistaken identity that persists to this day.

Why did the Duck cross the road? To hand out moving violation tickets apparently. On Halloween, Fort Lee, NJ police used an undercover officer in a 6-foot-4 Donald Duck costume to bust 130 drivers for failing to yield to a pedestrian. As part of a pedestrian decoy program, the costumed officer would step into crosswalks. Drivers who failed to stop were pulled over by uniformed officers and issued a moving violation ticket carrying a $230 fine and two points on their license.

Fort Lee police chief Keith Bendul explained the department's goal: "Two years ago, we had 62 people struck... This year, we had 40, I'm glad to say. But zero is my ultimate goal." During the decoy operation, WABC reported instances of drivers honking for the duck to move or driving through the crosswalk.

The department clarified on Facebook that the officer was not crossing against a light. However, some drivers felt tricked. Ticketed resident Karen Haigh, who plans to fight her $230 fine, said the 6-foot-4 duck "scared me" and "I thought it was a crazy guy on Halloween." She added, "If it was a normal person I would have stopped, and I think all those people would have stopped." Bendul maintained the strategy was for public safety: "When you see a pedestrian, child, adult or duck, stop. Let them cross and proceed with your day."

**Strategic Analysis: England's World Cup Performance Against Uruguay**

**Key Performance Indicators:**
- England suffered a critical defeat to Uruguay in World Cup competition
- Analysis reveals systematic defensive vulnerabilities and tactical misalignment

**Defensive Strategy Assessment:**
Luis Suarez's opening goal exposed fundamental positioning weaknesses in England's defensive structure. The goal replicated patterns from previous matches, indicating insufficient tactical adaptation. Phil Jagielka's positioning allowed Suarez excessive space, demonstrating a failure to implement tight marking protocols in high-stakes situations.

**Tactical Execution:**
Uruguay's first goal resulted from superior ball movement that neutralized five England players simultaneously. This highlights the gap between England's defensive coordination and world-class attacking execution. The Liverpool striker's performance validated his reputation through clinical finishing.

**Personnel Deployment:**
Jordan Henderson's selection represented a strategic compromise. While Henderson delivered strong club-level performance, his international positioning differed significantly from his optimal role. The team required enhanced creativity and risk-taking capabilities. Ross Barkley's late substitution at the 64th minute showed immediate impact, suggesting earlier deployment could have altered match dynamics.

**Statistical Performance:**
Raheem Sterling achieved a 96% pass completion rate, demonstrating technical precision under pressure.

**Positional Optimization:**
Full-backs Leighton Baines and Glen Johnson, typically dynamic offensive contributors at club level, showed conservative tendencies that limited England's attacking potential. Performance anxiety and high-pressure environment appear to have constrained their natural attacking instincts.

**Leadership Performance:**
Wayne Rooney's central positioning yielded optimal results, matching Michael Owen's 40-goal England record. His performance validated the strategic decision to deploy key personnel in their strongest positions, resulting in England's most influential individual performance.

**Strategic Recommendation:**
Maximum performance requires aligning top talent with their optimal roles rather than accommodating tactical compromises.

There is a crimson splotch of blood on his handkerchief. It's minuscule, barely the size of the seed from an apple. It's much too insignificant for a man like him to notice, especially when it's on the inside of the fold, but it's there nonetheless. A tiny notice of what was to come. His children pester him and run around his ankles. His wife, abdomen swollen and round with child, kisses him on the cheek as he leaves their modest house. He hitches up his chestnut horses and goes off to fulfill his financial duties as master of the house. There is a scarlet splotch of blood on his handkerchief. It's smaller than a cherry stone. He is driving and unable to devote any attention to the handkerchief, focusing instead on the well-being of the horses. But it's there, signalling what's to come. He walks into his bustling business. He greets the weary employees and begins to work in his dusty ledgers and records, all the while waiting for deliveries of fresh books to bind and meetings with the anxious producers of manuscripts. His leaden pencil etches note after note. There is a ruby splotch of blood on his handkerchief. It's larger than the head of a doornail. He's busy, but despite that, he sees the vivid flash of red as he folds it up and puts it away. He tells himself that his eyes are just tired from hours looking at records. He ignores the damning proof of his transience. He meets with a nervous man, and they discuss a lucrative contract. The customer worries about distribution, cover designs and possible errors, scared of no one understanding his message. The man worries about an easy contract, so he won't have to stay late working, and a reasonable sum of money to bring home to his family. There is a burgundy splotch of blood on his handkerchief. It's larger than a cluster of hemlock flowers. He can't help but notice the bright blood that falls on his handkerchief after his violent bout of coughing. It serves as a stark message to him about what to do next. He excuses himself from the meeting, ignoring the searing pain in his lungs. He pulls a bottle of amber whiskey out of the jar of his drawer and swallows a burning mouthful. He hitches up his restless horses and heads for the house full of soon-to-be orphans, driving with reckless abandon and knowing that it makes no real difference if he dies at home or dies getting himself back. His handkerchief is drenched with blood. It's as sodden as the coats of his horses, foaming with sweat. He cannot see to drive as his entire body heaves with violent coughing. It stains the handkerchief like sand dropping into the bottom of an hourglass. He staggers through the door and makes for the bed. His wife rushes over, but it is just barely too late. His body collapses into the bed as his ravaged lungs draw in their final breath. There is a ghastly splotch of blood on his pillow, spilled from his pale lips. It's the size of a bullet. His wife ignores it as she stares in stunned disbelief. She did not need evidence of his mortality.

Can peas replace Prozac pills and apples replace Abilify tablets? Hippocrates, who believed in the healing power of nature, may have been on to something when he advised us nearly 25 centuries ago to make food our medicine and medicine our food.

In the intervening centuries, though, we seem to have ignored the first half of Hippocrates’s counsel—to make food our medicine—and misinterpreted his advice to make medicine our food. How else can we explain the record number of Americans who are taking prescription drugs to treat depression? Eleven percent of Americans—over 34 million people, from adolescents to seniors—take an antidepressant drug on a regular basis, an astounding 400 percent increase since 1988.

Drug manufacturers—responsive to the rising demand for depression treatments—have created an extensive cafeteria of drugs. The National Institute of Mental Health website lists over 50 approved antidepressant and antianxiety drugs—from Abilify to Zyprexa, from Anafranil to Wellbutrin.

The price for relief from depression through the consumption of prescription drugs is high. Wallets get thinner (the drugs are not cheap), and waistlines get thicker. According to experts, 25 percent of patients who take an antidepressant drug gain 10 or more pounds. Jack E. Fincham, PhD, RPh, professor of pharmacy practice at the University of Missouri, states, “This is clearly a problem for the majority of drugs used to treat depression, and while it doesn’t occur with every drug or for every person, when it does happen, it can be a significant problem.”

But what if a straightforward dietary change could chase away the blues? Imagine if relief from depression and a heightened sense of well-being were as simple as munching on a crisp, dew-kissed apple or popping tender, sun-warmed peas into your mouth—seven servings of fruits and vegetables bursting with color and fragrance, each day. What if food became our medicine?

Professor Sarah Stewart-Brown, a public health researcher at Warwick Medical School in England, and Professor Andrew Oswald, an economist at the University of Warwick in England, studied the eating habits of 80,000 people in Britain and were surprised to find that “happiness and mental health are highest among people who eat seven portions of fruit and vegetables a day.” They also noted that happiness did not increase when more than seven portions were consumed and declined when fewer than seven were consumed.

Understanding this cause-and-effect relationship between mood and food, however, requires further research. For example, does the consumption of seven daily servings of fruits and vegetables relieve depression and produce an enhanced state of well-being? Or is the reverse true? That is, do individuals who feel good about themselves pay more attention to their lifestyle choices, including eating a healthier fare, than others do?

While we wait for the next insight from this research, we might find it prudent to examine our own eating habits and experiment with the seven-a-day regimen. With obesity on the rise and consumption of fruits and vegetables on the decline in the United States, clearly we have room for improvement. Excluding french fries, Americans (adults and children) consume only two to four portions of fruits and vegetables each day.

Adding more fruits and vegetables—velvety apricots, tangy tangerines, sweet bell peppers, and lush, emerald-green spinach—certainly won’t harm our bodies. By making food our medicine, some of us might even be able to reduce our dependence on antidepressant drugs once we naturally view the world in a brighter, happier light—seeing not just the gray shadows, but a vibrant tapestry of flavors, colors, and scents that nourish body and mind.

From Green Right Now Reports

Give plastic a small opportunity and it seems to take over completely. Look how it has worked its way into back-to-school shopping. It covers binders and notebook covers, surrounds pens, glue and tape. It's wrapped around highlighters and crayons and notebook paper. Folders are wrapped in plastic. Plastic is wrapped in plastic.

But it's not the school supplies that are most worryingly plastic. The large collection of plastic disposables that are ready to bag, bundle and secure everything in your child's school lunch create a constant flow of waste heading toward the landfill.

Plastic utensils, plastic bags, plastic wrap and plastic bottles are used for a few hours and then thrown away, and maybe, sometimes recycled.

Solutions have increased, though. We now know how to pack a more environmentally-friendly lunch using reusable cloth snack bags or sandwich wraps. We can buy biodegradable paper sandwich bags and stainless steel food containers.

And then there's plastic. Reusable plastic.

This school year we found one plastic item that was hard to resist, and so we didn't, and here's why.

Rubbermaid's Lunch Blox Sandwich Kit is made of plastic, but it is recyclable, very useful and you could use it for years.

Blox offer the advantages of plastic; it's lightweight, snaps together securely and is cleverly shaped into just the right sizes. And being made of strong BPA-free plastic that's designed to last, it moves out of the throwaway category into that small area where usefulness convinces us that this product passes the environmental test.

The Blox line also quietly considers changing eating habits by providing compartments for healthy snacks, Bento Box style.

Kids...no, adults too, need to be eating healthier and Blox offers four boxes, to hold a sandwich and three packs of snacks. Technically, you could fill them with sugar and fat. But they're the right size for veggie sticks, a handful of nuts or a small portion of pasta salad.

Wait, there's more. Did we mention it all fits together in a block (Blox) with a Blue Ice pack to keep things cold placed in the middle.

Customers seem to have responded well to all this thoughtful design, judging by the very positive reviews on Rubbermaid's website (surely Rubbermaid wouldn't add fake positive comments and remove negative ones?).

One reviewer, fairly typical, basically said it all:

"This product is wonderful. You'll save money as you will not need to use baggies and they fit great in tall lunch boxes. The sandwich container fit the bread we use with more room for larger slices of bread. The snack size containers work great for pudding, crackers, fruit, cold salads and more. They also do not leak and lids are easy for children to take off. Also when kids are eating lunch they can snap the lids to the bottom of the containers so they do not get misplaced or accidentally thrown away. The blue ice fits in the middle to keep items cold plus you can purchase additional LunchBlox containers for more food. A wonderful durable product for school lunches."

Tax Law Overview
Tax law governs taxation on property and transactions, including property values, transfers and sales, and income-generating licenses. These encompass federal and state income taxes, local real estate taxes, sales and use taxes (including on out-of-state purchases), import duties, business license fees, and federal and state estate and gift taxes.

The 16th Amendment empowers Congress to tax "incomes, from whatever source derived, without apportionment among the several States, and without regard to any census or enumeration." The Internal Revenue Code is codified as Title 26 U.S.C. Both individuals and corporations must file income tax returns. While corporations follow many of the same rules as individuals, they are also subject to an intricate set of regulations addressing unique corporate issues.
