By . Simon Tomlinson . PUBLISHED: . 05:55 EST, 20 November 2013 . | . UPDATED: . 07:41 EST, 20 November 2013 . A female bank manager in Bangalore, India, was left lying in a pool of blood and may be paralysed for life after being brutally attacked by a machete-wielding robber when she bravely refused to hand over money during an early morning ATM robbery. Shocking CCTV shows Jyothi Uday, an assistant manager at Corporation Bank, attacked inside an unguarded ATM kiosk in the bustling South Indian city of Bangalore at around 7.10am by an armed raider who demands she withdraw cash from the machine. When she resists, he first pulls a gun out of a bag and then a machete which he uses to rain down blows on her head and face. After being struck three or four times, Mrs Uday collapses to the floor unconscious as the attacker makes off with items from her purse including a mobile phone. Scroll down for video . Terrifying: CCTV footage shows a gunman demanding cash from a female bank manager inside an ATM kiosk . Life flashing before her eyes: When the woman refuses to hand over cash, the man pulls out a machete . Horrific: Moments after this clip, the victim is struck three or four times over the head causing her to collapse unconscious in a pool of blood. Doctors say she is currently paralysed down her right side . Two schoolchildren finally raised the alarm three hours later after seeing blood flowing out of the isolated, unmanned kiosk, it was reported by The Times of India. Mrs Uday suffered severe injuries to her head which has left the right side of her body paralysed, doctors said. CCTV footage shows Mrs Uday had made a detour on her way to her work as manager of the cheque truncation system at a branch of Corporation Bank when the attack occurred. Within seconds of her entering the building, the attacker comes in and pulls the shutter down behind him. Sickening: The attacker rains down four blows onto her head and face with the machete . Brutal: The woman cowers in the corner as she is struck, knocking her unconscious . Out cold: Detectives say they are also looking into other motives for the attack aside from robbery because the gunman left all of Mrs Jyothi's gold jewellery behind . A police officer said: 'The man wore a blue striped shirt. We can see his face clearly in the visuals. 'He first pulled a gun out of a bag he was carrying and asked her to withdraw cash. 'She resisted. Everything was over in less than four minutes.' The attacker was seen rubbing bloodstains from the machete with a cloth or piece of paper before pulling the shutter down as he leaves, police said. Police said the attacker may have been using a fake gun and spoke in Kannada, a local language predominantly used in the southern Indian state of Karnataka, further grounding the incident in its regional context. Detectives say they are looking into other motives for the attack aside from robbery because the gunman left all of Mrs Jyothi's gold jewellery behind. Kamal Pant, additional commissioner of police, law and order, said: 'We are also investigating if there was any personal vendetta.'

The only thing this movie has going for it is Johnny Depp. Other than Depp's performance there was nothing going on that I had not seen before. The whole thing wreaked of "The Dark Half," and to further that feeling they even had Timothy Hutton in it.

The trailers for this made it seem like it was going to be a roller-coaster ride full of chills, spills and screams. Well, it was none of that. There were some anxious moments that never turned into much of anything, and the ending will not surprise or shock you.

The only thing shocking was that I was so excited about this and then let down so horribly. Had it not been for Johnny Depp this would have been one of the worst movies of all time. He could make toilet cleaning interesting which in itself would be scarier than this flop.

I am nearing completion of a Master's degree in Reading and acquired this resource at the outset of my studies. I recommend selecting an alternative publication, as this particular publication's approach is entirely inadequate. It describes four basic patterns of knowledge organization as hierarchical, conceptual, sequential, and cyclical. My research identified a common theme of five fundamental patterns: Sequential, descriptive, cause-effect, problem-solution, and comparison-contrast. However, should one be genuinely committed to thoroughly instructing learners on the organization of information, I strongly endorse any publication by David Hyerle concerning Thinking Maps. His research is highly commendable, and the eight maps he introduces provide individuals of all ages with instruments to effectively analyze and synthesize content, organization, writing, and other related academic tasks. This is the methodology I am currently employing to instruct my own children on reading and comprehending nonfiction texts.

This isn't so much a review for readers as it is an illustration of irony. Art relates this coming storm, in part, to human-caused environmental decay. Yet, why allow trees to be cut down to publish a book expressing his concerns? It's as if he's feigning environmental concern solely for profit. If he truly cares about the environment and weather patterns, he should stop writing books that waste precious trees. I enjoy listening to his show, but his books reveal him to be a true opportunist.

I read this once a year, in February. Why? Because it's an enduring, thoughtful story – a true fable, as its subtitle suggests – that reliably offers a welcome escape as this great gray month plods on. The tale of young Harvey Swick embodies the childhoods we only dreamed of. It brims with magic, mystery, and heroism. Yet, we are reminded that fantasy is a beautiful, fleeting escape. Those who linger are no longer truly human, and we must ultimately relinquish them – and the cherished innocence of childhood – to truly grow. For parents, it's a perfect read-aloud. Its style lends itself wonderfully to storytelling, with distinctive voices and short chapters – a definite bonus for getting the little ones to sleep faster!

Extensive research strongly indicated that a minimum charge of 50p per unit could generate nearly £900 million in savings over two decades by significantly reducing crime rates and alleviating the burden of alcohol-related illness, leading to an estimated 50 fewer premature deaths annually. Welsh government ministers unequivocally declared their commitment to harnessing their legislative powers to "improve and protect" the long-term health and well-being of all citizens across Wales.

However, a vocal spokesman for the drinks industry swiftly countered, arguing that the proposed plans would unfairly "ramp up" prices for responsible consumers while proving utterly ineffective in genuinely tackling the root causes of alcohol harm. Across the border, analogous legislative initiatives in Scotland are currently embroiled in a significant legal challenge spearheaded by powerful whisky producers, who vehemently assert that such measures fundamentally breach established European Union law.

In a firm defence of the policy, Wales' Deputy Health Minister, Vaughan Gething, articulated that the forthcoming Welsh law would demonstrably "save lives, significantly reduce rates of crime, meticulously help ensure valuable NHS resources are allocated wisely and efficiently, and even bolster businesses by notably reducing absence from the workplace." He added, "Minimum unit pricing will specifically target and impact those exceptionally cheap alcoholic beverages sold at unacceptably low prices relative to their potent alcohol content. This is a uniquely well-targeted and strategic measure as it will exert only a minimal, almost imperceptible, impact on moderate, responsible drinkers, while reserving its most substantial effect for high-risk, problem drinkers."

However, intensifying the industry's opposition, Wine and Spirit Trade Association chief executive, Miles Beale, vehemently argued that such sweeping changes would "unfairly and disproportionately ramp up the cost of over half of the alcoholic beverages currently gracing supermarket shelves" while, in his view, "doing absolutely nothing to genuinely tackle the complex issue of alcohol harm." He passionately asserted, "It is entirely wrong and fundamentally unjust that responsible, law-abiding consumers in Wales should be unfairly punished for the detrimental actions of an irresponsible, small minority." Mr. Beale further issued a stark warning that Welsh businesses could face significant financial repercussions as consumers might simply bypass local shops by crossing the nearby border into England to purchase cheaper alcohol. He also raised profound legal questions, specifically questioning whether the proposed fixing of drinks prices could potentially contravene established UK competition law.

From the opposition benches, Shadow Health Minister Darren Millar stated that while the Conservatives fundamentally backed the Welsh government's laudable aims in principle, he cautioned that "it will be absolutely crucial to meticulously resolve outstanding questions which remain concerning whether these proposals truly fall within the established parameters of the devolution settlement." Underscoring the potential benefits, South Wales Police Deputy Crime Commissioner Sophie Howe affirmed that minimum pricing could significantly bolster ongoing efforts to effectively tackle violent crime. She acknowledged, "While good progress is undeniably being made, there remains a substantial amount more to be done to ensure community safety."

The esteemed Royal College of Physicians publicly expressed its strong welcome for the strategic application of legislation wherever robust scientific evidence clearly supports its efficacy and necessity. The Welsh government had previously conducted an extensive public consultation specifically on minimum alcohol pricing, which was initially integrated as a component of a much broader Public Health Bill. However, alcohol pricing will now be addressed through a completely separate, dedicated bill, a strategic move designed to entirely mitigate the risk of any adverse European Union judgement against this specific measure from potentially delaying other vital public health initiatives, such as essential restrictions on e-cigarettes and the mandatory registration of tattoo parlours. This crucial consultation period is scheduled to conclude in December 2015.

I LOVE this one. It was my first video - my introduction - to yoga several years ago. As a beginner, I remember wobbling unsteadily as I attempted downward dog, my palms slipping slightly on the mat while my muscles trembled from unfamiliar stretches. The gentle Sanskrit names felt foreign on my tongue, and I found myself glancing at the screen repeatedly to mirror each flowing movement. As with anything else, it got easier with time and practice.

Fast forward several years and I still find myself reaching for this one. It's gentle, yet very effective, and I always feel that satisfying pull of muscles lengthening and releasing tension. It's also a perfect pick-me-upper in the middle of the day when my shoulders are hunched forward and my lower back aches from sitting at the computer for one too many hours. Being only approximately 18 minutes long I have NO excuse for not taking the time to unroll my mat and breathe deeply.

The setting is breathtaking - filmed against a backdrop of swaying palm trees and azure ocean waves that you can almost hear lapping gently at the shore. The soft, melodic music flows seamlessly with each pose, creating an atmosphere so peaceful you can practically feel the warm tropical breeze. Suzanne moves with fluid grace at a very comfortable pace, her soothing voice guiding you through each transition, though I wish she would hold that blissful child's pose a little longer - those few extra seconds of forehead pressed to the mat, arms stretched forward, breathing deeply into my back. I've bought it for several friends who voiced a desire to try yoga. Great for everyone - beginners and experienced alike.

The average person pays more attention to what she’s saying or thinking about than to what she is hearing, or how her words are “landing” on the other person. This self-involvement becomes even more pronounced during an argument. However, you can become a much more effective communicator by using “attentive speaking,” a simple and highly effective technique that encourages awareness of how well you’re communicating—whether it’s with your partner, your children, your extended family, or your co-workers.

A question that troubles many people in relationships is, “How am I supposed to know what he (or she) wants?” Often, the fear of being rejected, criticized, or otherwise suffering the consequences of guessing wrong makes people anxious. Most of us hope for a “yes” answer when we ask a question, so we try to ask the right question, at the right time, from the right person. But this raises another dilemma: how do we know what’s right?

Since most of our interaction happens through conversation, it’s helpful to have a straightforward technique that improves your chances of a successful exchange, especially when the outcome is important. I call this method “attentive speaking,” and its essence lies in focusing not on guessing what the other person is thinking, but on paying close attention. No matter whom you’re talking to—your family members, your children, or your spouse—using attentive speaking can help you reach mutual understanding and agreement.

Guidelines for Attentive Speaking

Attentive speaking means being aware not only of what you are saying, but also of how your partner is receiving it. To do this effectively, observe your partner’s facial expressions, body movements, and posture as you communicate. For example, signs like looking interested, fidgeting, appearing bored, wandering eyes, attempts to interrupt, facial expressions of anger or confusion, or even a blank stare can provide valuable clues about whether you are being understood. By following a few key steps, you can learn to notice your partner’s responses while you speak, and determine whether your message is getting through—even without any verbal feedback from your partner.

This approach is especially useful if your partner:

• is not very talkative;
• thinks disagreeing or objecting will "hurt your feelings";
• is the unemotional, strong, silent type;
• is easily overwhelmed in a discussion; or
• is passive, depressed, or withdrawn.

Sometimes, partners in these situations are reluctant to share if they disagree or feel discomfort with what you are saying. If you continue talking without recognizing that your partner isn’t receiving your words as you intended, frustration can build on both sides: your partner may become upset, stop listening, get confused, mentally object or silently argue with you, or withdraw altogether. Without attentive speaking, you might continue talking, unaware of these cues, until suddenly your partner reacts with anger, misunderstanding, or complete disinterest—and all your efforts to communicate are wasted. By following the guidelines below, you can better recognize when you aren’t communicating effectively or getting the reaction you want.

Using attentive speaking will help you:

• Avoid overwhelming your partner with too much information at once, because you will notice when he or she looks overwhelmed, bored, or distracted
• Keep your partner’s interest in what you have to say, by prompting you to ask a question when you see your partner’s attention slipping away
• Understand when what you say is misunderstood, by observing facial expressions and noticing when they don’t match your expectations
• Gauge your partner’s reaction when he or she doesn’t say anything, by reading facial expressions, body language, and general attentiveness
• Notice when your partner is too distracted, stressed, or upset to really hear what you’re saying, again by paying attention to facial expressions, body language, and attentiveness

Officers spoke to 51-year-old Andy Hill at a police station in Hertfordshire on March 2, 2016, at 10:30 AM. Police, who did not name Mr Hill, said a man voluntarily attended an appointment and was "interviewed under caution but not arrested". Mr Hill was performing aerobatics in a Hawker Hunter T7 jet (tail number WV372) at the annual Shoreham Airshow on August 22, 2015, at approximately 1:22 PM BST when the jet crashed and broke up. An initial report by the Air Accidents Investigation Branch (AAIB), published on August 28, 2015, six days after the incident, found the jet showed "no abnormal indications" during its flight. The final Air Accidents Investigation Branch (AAIB) report, published on March 10, 2017, said cockpit footage showed the plane "responding to the pilot's control inputs". The jet split into four major parts at an altitude of approximately 300 feet (91 meters) above ground level, and Mr Hill was thrown clear. It is not known whether Mr Hill initiated his ejection. Within 48 hours of the crash, on August 24, 2015, the pilot's family issued a statement which said they were devastated and deeply saddened by the loss of life and sent their prayers and heartfelt condolences to the families of all those affected.

Hold onto your hats, folks, because a groundbreaking new NASA study, reported by Mark Prigg, reveals a truly surprising global connection: air pollution from China and other Asian countries isn't just a local problem. It's actually making storms in North America stronger and dramatically reshaping weather patterns across the entire Northern Hemisphere!

Researchers discovered that these pollutants are acting like a supercharger for storms brewing over the Pacific Ocean. And guess what? These souped-up storms then feed directly into our weather systems, impacting us far and wide. The team noted this effect was especially noticeable during the chilly winter months. Scroll down for video.

Just imagine the hazy skies over Beijing's Temple of Heaven. That same air pollution, traveling thousands of miles, is now linked to intensified storms in North America and altered weather across half the globe, as this powerful study suggests.

At the heart of these changes is something called the Pacific storm track. Think of it as a crucial global conveyor belt, tirelessly transporting heat and moisture around our planet. The scientists found that this transfer of heat and moisture is getting a significant boost over the storm track further downstream. In plain English? The Pacific storm track is intensifying, all thanks to that outflow of Asian air pollution.

Lead author Yuan Wang, from the prestigious Jet Propulsion Laboratory at the California Institute of Technology, didn't mince words: "There appears to be little doubt that these particles from Asia affect storms sweeping across the Pacific and subsequently the weather patterns in North America and the rest of the world. The effects are quite dramatic! The pollution results in thicker and taller clouds and heavier precipitation."

In fact, the team found that Northwest Pacific winter storms are now a whopping 10 percent stronger than they were just 30 years ago. That's a significant shift! This compelling research has been proudly published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

So, how did they uncover this incredible link? In a pioneering study of its kind, scientists played detective, comparing air pollution rates from 1850 (our pre-Industrial era) to 2000 (what they called 'present-day'). They discovered that "anthropogenic" – that's scientist-speak for man-made – particles from Asia directly impact the Pacific storm track, influencing weather across much of the world. By plugging detailed pollution emission data, compiled by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, into an advanced global climate model, the team conclusively proved that these human-generated aerosols undeniably affect cloud formations and the powerful mid-latitude cyclones associated with the Pacific storm track.

Wang put it plainly: "The climate model is quite clear on this point. The aerosols formed by human activities from fast-growing Asian economies absolutely impact storm formation and global air circulation downstream." He further explained, "They tend to make storms deeper and stronger and more intense, and these storms also have more precipitation in them. We believe this is the first time that a study has provided such a truly global perspective."

And if you need more proof, Zhang, another key researcher, notes that satellite images clearly show vast amounts of aerosols undertaking their long journey from Asia, drifting right across the Pacific. The team passionately reiterates that the Pacific storm track is a fundamental driver in our planet's general global circulation, moving heat and moisture around like a vast, invisible river. And yes, that vital transfer of heat and moisture is indeed increasing downstream along the storm track, signifying an intensified Pacific storm track due to the Asian air pollution outflow.

Zhang warmly concludes, "Our results powerfully support previous findings that show that particles in the air over Asia tend to affect global weather patterns. It truly shows they can affect the Earth’s weather significantly!"

What does voicing mean exactly? Simply put, voicing is the way chords are played, which gives them their timbre or richness. In other words, voicing refers to how many notes are played, the distance between each of the notes, and the quantity and quality of extensions used.

Given this, it’s important to realize that there are literally thousands of ways to play a single chord. If we consider that each chord in a progression can be played in a thousand different ways, the number of possible chord progressions rises into the millions. To keep things manageable, though, it’s wise to start with a few solid voicing options rather than get overwhelmed by all the possibilities.

Let’s look at a practical example using a Cmaj7 chord. The chord itself is simple enough, made up of the notes C, E, G, and B. However, if you want a richer sound, you can also add D and A as extensions. Why these notes? Because D and A come from the C major scale and fit harmoniously with the basic chord tones.

These additional tones are known as extensions. In other words, a skilled pianist will naturally consider D and A as part of their chord voicing when they encounter the chord symbol Cmaj7. It doesn’t necessarily have to be spelled out as Cmaj7(9 13) for them to understand this concept.

So, how would a pianist voice this chord in practice? The specific voicing often depends on the melody. Whatever the melody note is at the moment will become the highest note of the chord. For example, if D is the most prominent melody note while the Cmaj7 chord is being played, the pianist would treat the 9th as the top note in their voicing.

Moving to the structure in the hands, it’s generally a good idea to play the bass note, C, in the left hand. The next most important notes are the 3rd and 7th, as they define the chord’s character. One practical approach is to play the bass note (C) with the fifth finger and the 7th (B) with the thumb of the left hand.

Meanwhile, the right hand can cover the 3rd, 5th, and melody note (in this example, the 9th) with the 1st, 2nd, and 5th fingers, respectively. To add further richness, you can use the 3rd finger in the right hand to include the 13th (A). From bottom to top, the notes would be C, B, E, G, A, and D. This creates a very lush chord voicing—one that stands out for its depth and color.

Of course, this is only one approach among many. As mentioned earlier, there are countless ways to voice the same chord. My recommendation is to master one solid method at a time until it becomes second nature. Voicing the root and 7th in the left hand, and covering the 3rd and melody in the right hand—while using any remaining fingers for the 5th and other available extensions—is a reliable system to get started. This approach works universally, whether you’re playing major, minor, dominant, or diminished chords.

Once you’ve internalized this fundamental method, exploring other voicings becomes much easier. Having one clear and concrete formula in place will also help you better distinguish and appreciate the unique character each chord brings.

At this stage, you might be interested in exploring another type of voicing: Quartal Voicings. In more modern harmony, it has become popular to build chord structures using fourths instead of the traditional thirds. Quartal voicings create a more open, spacious sound and are especially useful for pianists. For example, the left hand can play chord voicings in fourths, departing from different chord tones such as the 3rd, 13th, 9th, or 7th.

To practice quartal voicings, take notes of the C scale in fourth intervals starting from B in the upper staff. All of these are notes found in an extended C chord: 7, 3, 13, 9, 5, and 1. Try playing them in the right hand arpeggiated style.

Here are four specific quartal voicings you can practice this week:
- Low C-E-A-D
- Low C-A-D-G
- Low C-D-G-C
- Low C-B-E-A

Have a great week ahead!

**Global Health Initiatives and Challenges**

- **Influenza Pandemic (H1N1):** In April 2009, the world faced its first influenza pandemic in over 40 years, caused by influenza A H1N1. By the end of the year, 12,220 deaths had been reported globally, but the World Health Organization (WHO) declared the pandemic on the decline as vaccines became increasingly available worldwide.
- **Immunization Campaigns:** Global and regional immunization campaigns, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, led to a 74% drop in measles deaths between 2000 and 2007. Over 80% of children aged 12–23 months in less-developed regions received at least one dose of measles vaccine.
- **Malaria:** Efforts to combat malaria showed some progress, but nearly a million people continued to die annually, with 89% of deaths in sub-Saharan Africa in 2008—mostly among children. Late in 2009, concern grew over a newly identified drug-resistant strain of malaria on the Cambodia-Thailand border, highlighting the ongoing challenges in disease control.
- **HIV/AIDS:** The worldwide number of people living with HIV/AIDS remained steady at around 33 million. However, infection rates continued to rise in certain regions, notably Eastern Europe and Central Asia, where prevalence had doubled since 2001. Sub-Saharan Africa accounted for two-thirds of all cases, with women making up the majority of those affected.
- **Maternal Mortality:** Maternal mortality persisted as a major issue in least developed countries (LDCs). In 2005, over half a million women and girls died annually from birth-related complications—99% of such deaths globally. Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia together accounted for the vast majority of these fatalities.

**Environmental Initiatives and Climate Change**

- **UNEP’s Green Economy Initiative and Global Green New Deal:** In October 2008, UNEP launched the Green Economy Initiative, calling for a “Global Green New Deal” to address environmental degradation and create millions of green jobs. By 2009, various UN agencies were mobilized to support these long-term sustainability goals.
- **Year of Climate Change and Copenhagen Conference:** 2009 was designated by the UN as the Year of Climate Change, punctuated by multiple international meetings. The Copenhagen conference in December aimed to forge a successor to the Kyoto Protocol, resulting in a weak, nonbinding agreement. The accord outlined $100 billion in aid by 2020 for developing nations to address climate change and stressed efforts to limit global warming to a 2 °C (3.6 °F) increase, although it included no binding targets.

**UN Administration and Reform**

- By the end of 2009, the United Nations comprised 192 member states and operated on a regular biennial budget of $4.87 billion for 2008–09.
- Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon continued to restructure UN peace and security operations, affecting bodies such as the Department of Peacekeeping Operations and the Office of Rule of Law and Security Institutions.
- The General Assembly supported efforts to streamline the UN Secretariat, improving personnel and service-delivery systems in pursuit of greater efficiency and responsiveness.

**Connections:**  
Many of the health challenges—including pandemic preparedness, immunization, and efforts to reduce maternal mortality—were concentrated in or most acutely affected less-developed regions, particularly sub-Saharan Africa. Global health responses were increasingly linked to broader development agendas and climate action, as environmental degradation and climate change were recognized as factors compounding health and poverty challenges. These complex, interrelated issues shaped the UN’s approach in 2009, prompting administrative reforms and renewed emphasis on coordinated, cross-sectoral action.

She sensed what I craved better than I did. On that very first morning I jolted awake clutching her scarf. She flaunted that scarf every day. Black and white, scorched with a burn from the joint we blazed on our first date. The memories crashed in. Fuck, I ached for her so. What I wouldn't risk. The second morning it was a note. Saturated with rose water, like every letter she ever penned. Was she delivering these to me? I tore it open, and in her handwriting it boldly declared 'together forever'. What I wouldn't risk. On that third morning I jolted awake with a loaded gun. At first I reeled with confusion. She radiated kindness, she despised violence. I dropped it on my table and just fixated on it across the room. Then I grasped what she demanded. Together forever. We could be now. One squeeze. One bullet. What I wouldn't risk. She sensed what I craved better than I did.

During my senior year of college, I enrolled in a public speaking course—in part, to fulfill a Humanities requirement but also because I assumed the subject matter would be manageable. The professor was an endlessly upbeat woman with a penchant for storytelling—often veering off-topic to share details about her own life. By the end of the semester, I admittedly knew more about her personal journey than the foundations of rhetorical technique. Yet, there was one principle she instilled in me that has endured: the importance of a compelling start. You have one brief opportunity to capture your audience’s attention, to engage them so thoroughly in an idea or narrative that they can’t help but want to know what comes next.

As I reflect on that guidance, I find myself revisiting it frequently—particularly in moments that require honest vulnerability. For instance, earlier today, I came out to my mother as a woman who loves and is attracted to other women. It was a direct conversation, maybe even abrupt, but ultimately true to who I am and what I value: clarity, authenticity, and transparency. While it’s never easy to share aspects of oneself that may be met with surprise or discomfort, I’ve learned that establishing trust—whether with a single individual or a larger group—starts with candor and directness.

I realize now that both in public speaking and in life’s most important conversations, tone and sensitivity matter. While there’s always room to refine my approach, I am confident that leading with honesty and integrity is essential to effective communication—a lesson I carry with me in every personal and professional interaction.

A U.S. brewery has been forced to apologize for naming an ale after the teetotal Indian leader, Mohandas Gandhi. The New England Brewing Co. in Connecticut was slammed for its Gandhi-Bot pale ale by consumers, who claimed the beverage was an 'illegal insult' to India. What particularly caused upset was the can artwork, which reimagines Gandhi as a robot with one hand raised in blessing. Following a legal complaint about the beer in Hyderabad, India, the New England Brewing Co issued an apology to Facebook. It said in a statement that its intent wasn't to 'offend anyone but rather pay homage and celebrate a man who we respect greatly.' Despite the backlash, the company says it has no plans to withdraw the beer, which is not sold internationally. It claims that Gandhi's granddaughter and grandson have seen the label and have 'expressed their admiration' for it. However, the company's stance could change when the case hits the courts Monday. A petitioner in India contends that Gandhi’s image on the alcohol cans and description of the father of the nation by the brewery is highly condemnable and punishable under Indian laws. However, there are also those who fully support the drink. One fan wrote on Facebook: 'Gandhi-Bot is best. Keep the craft up. 'I'm an Indian and proud to drive five hours often to [the New England Brewing Co] just for Bot.' Another added: 'My only problem with your beer is that it is always sold out. Keep up the great work.' On the website Gandhi-Bot is pegged as an intensely hopped double India pale ale with a blend of three varieties of American Hops. It is also described as 'fully vegetarian' and an 'ideal aid ... for the seeking of truth and love.'

**Bob Katter's 'Black Puppet' Remark Sparks Race Row**

Outspoken MP Bob Katter has risked being drawn into a race row after accusing the Queensland Government of believing embattled MP Billy Gordon was 'a little black puppet on a string'. In a bizarre statement on his party's website, Mr Katter said the Government believes Mr Gordon would quit after he was recently accused of domestic violence - and later admitted to his criminal past. Mr Katter, federal MP for Kennedy, is of Lebanese descent. He said he's been personally subjected to racist attitudes from both major parties in the past, and that he sympathised with Mr Gordon's position.

**The Context: Billy Gordon's Political Turmoil**

Mr Gordon was forced to quit the Queensland Labor Party last week in the wake of being accused of domestic violence and withholding his criminal history from Premier Annastacia Palaszczuk. He announced on Wednesday he will remain in the Queensland parliament as an independent, throwing the Government, which holds a razor-thin majority, into chaos.

**Katter's Full Statement and Accusation**

'In the last two cases where this occurred in the federal parliament, there was never any question raised about the member resigning. So why was it assumed here that Billy Gordon would resign?' Mr Katter's statement reads. 'I can't help but think that the Queensland Government thought they had a little black puppet on a string. Mr Gordon, who was forced to quit the Queensland Labor Party last week in the wake of being accused of domestic violence and withholding his criminal history.'

**Social Media Reacts to Katter's Terminology**

The statement has sparked criticism on social media, with users questioning his terminology. 'Irrespective of his weird use of the term 'black puppet' it's kinda odd how Bob Katter is speaking on Billy Gordon's behalf' wrote Benjamin James. Not that John wrote: 'Wow, Bob Katter calls Billy Gordon, 'black puppet on a string' and the lefty loons stay silent. What if Abbott had said it?' The statement has sparked criticism on social media, with some users questioning his terminology.

**Katter Defends His Language and Intent**

Mr Katter has since defended the statement, saying he used the term black puppet in light of his personal experiences. Mr Katter has since defended the statement, saying he used the term black puppet 'because I'm dark and I come from Cloncurry.' 'I can understand and I can identify with the attitudes that these people have and I'm going to call it for what it is,' he said. 'I deliberately put that comment in with a forethought, because I think they deserve a belting for the racial overtones.' Mr Katter used federal MPs Tony Windsor and Rob Oakeshott as examples of independents becoming puppets for the government and sacrificing their independence, but he vowed Mr Gordon would not make that mistake.

Brachiosaurus, made famous by its iconic appearance in Jurassic Park, now boasts a remarkable early relative, shedding light on the origins of some of the planet’s greatest giants. According to researchers, this herbivorous dinosaur stretched longer than a double-decker bus and tipped the scales at an extraordinary 15,000kg.

Its fossilized bones were unearthed in the 1930s in the picturesque Jura region of eastern France. For decades, the remains languished in obscurity, tucked away in storage crates within the vast collections of Paris’s National Museum of Natural History.

Lead researcher Dr. Philip Mannion of Imperial College London explained that this colossal creature would have devoured a rich variety of prehistoric plants, including lush ferns and towering conifers, at a time when Europe was a fragmented archipelago.

“We can’t say for certain what led to this animal’s demise, but, millions of years later, its fossils are offering invaluable insights into the early evolution of brachiosaurid sauropods and their broader kin, the titanosauriforms,” Dr. Mannion said.

Titanosauriforms included some of the most titanic land-dwellers the Earth has ever seen. These incredible dinosaurs were wildly diverse and managed to flourish right up until the cataclysmic asteroid impact that ended their reign.

The new species, Vouivria damparisensis, roamed the Earth during the Late Jurassic, around 160 million years ago.

“This is the earliest known representative of a lineage that grew to include Brachiosaurus – one of the most iconic dinosaurs, immortalized by Jurassic Park,” Dr. Mannion told BBC News. “The discovery gives us a much sharper understanding of the formative stages in the evolution of this influential branch of dinosaurs.”

Vouivria is a sauropod – a subgroup of titanosauriforms that counts Brachiosaurus, Diplodocus, and Brontosaurus among its legendary ranks. Sauropods are renowned for their astonishingly long necks and tails, diminutive heads, and thick, column-like legs.

Remarkably, the Vouivria fossil predates the oldest-known titanosauriform by about five million years, extending the timeline for this group’s early history.

“It suggests these behemoths were diversifying much earlier than previous fossil evidence indicated,” Dr. Mannion added. “This pushes back the evolutionary origins for a whole suite of sauropod dinosaurs, refining our picture of how these magnificent creatures were related.”

Reclassifying Vouivria as a basal member of the titanosauriforms will be pivotal in tracing their global dispersal – from their emergence in the Jurassic to their eventual extinction.

Evidence suggests these dinosaurs once inhabited regions spanning Europe, North America, and Africa, but eventually vanished from Europe before the curtain fell on the Age of Dinosaurs.

The fossil was discovered in the village of Damparis in the scenic Jura region in 1934 and was first documented scientifically in the 1940s, but has been largely ignored since that time.

Its formal name, Vouivria damparisensis, honors “La vouivre” – a legendary dragon-like, winged serpent from regional French folklore.

Dr. Mannion and a team of paleontologists from the National Museum of Natural History in Paris and the CNRS/Université Paris 1 Panthéon-Sorbonne meticulously re-examined the ancient bones.

Their findings are published in the journal PeerJ.

The young man exuded sorrow, his head lowered, shoulders slumped. His worn, casual clothes spoke of comfort prioritized, or a life shaped by scarcity. He reached the door. I greeted him with a warm, inviting smile, and gratitude, mixed with profound relief, flooded his eyes at the sight of genuine compassion. He was a man riddled with vulnerabilities, yet my aim was not to expose them, but to observe. My focus sharpened on the subtle cues: the tremor in his voice, his hesitant reactions, the struggle to maintain eye contact, all betraying a crushing lack of self-belief. A heavy shroud of guilt, regret, and shame visibly weighed him down. He settled into a chair, a subtle shift in his demeanor hinting at buried curiosity and wonder. I tapped into childhood memories of magic and enchantment, recounting old tales he likely knew from his youth. My words aimed to coax forth that forgotten inner child, to build a haven of safety and happiness. An only child, he'd grown up quiet and independent. Yet this solitude, born from the cruelty of peers who marked him as different, had systematically dismantled his courage and self-belief. He stated his age: 29. Though dreams surely lay ahead, the suffocating grip of grief, years of isolation, and a profound lack of acceptance anchored him. The very sound of his voice resonated with loss. He bore the sole burden of caring for his elderly parents, one recently deceased. The weight of past certainties, now unpleasant, crushed him under the immensity of future uncertainties. Above all, he feared utter solitude. I painted a vivid picture: a young, brave, courageous man embarking on the most vital adventure of his life. "Consider deeply the life you yearn to create," I urged. My words were forged to empower, to ignite the dormant bravery and fierce independence of his younger self. I concluded with an unwavering assurance: he would find the profound love he had always dreamed of. He thanked me with a smile, a genuine warmth now present. As he turned for the door and walked toward the gate, I watched him pause, glancing at the vast sky above, a profound new sense of wonder alight in his heart.

a combination of mixed radishes, baby carrots, merlot lettuce and red wine vinaigrette . White House Executive Pastry Chef William Yosses holds a dessert tray including maple fudge, biscuits and cotton candy -- the after-dinner treats for Tuesday night . Entertainment: Mary J. Blige probably won't perform her smash hit 'Family Affair' for the newly single French premier . Deputy US Chief of Protocol Natalie Jones, not the former French first lady, accompanied Hollande for his first steps on U.S. soil this week -- he's without both long-time girlfriend Valerie Trierweiler and more recent mistress Julie Gayet . And the first course will be caviar – although not the Russian kind that might cast a favorable light on President Vladimir Putin. Hollande and a hush-hush guest list will dine on American Osetra, grown in Illinois estuaries. *I recall a specific instance at a culinary event where the choice of locally sourced, unexpected ingredients transformed a routine course into a memorable centerpiece, much like this American Osetra aims to do.* A winter garden salad of mixed radish and baby carrots served on merlot lettuce ''pays tribute to The First Lady’s White House Kitchen Garden,' according to the program, but the White House stopped short of saying the veggies actually came from Mrs. Obama's garden. Also on offer will be quail eggs from Pennsylvania, twelve separate varieties of potatoes from three states, dry-aged ribeye steaks, a chocolate malted layer cake with vanilla ice cream, fudge made from Vermont maple syrup, lavender shortbread cookies and orange-zest cotton candy. Wines on the table will be from California's Napa Valley, and from Washington state and Virginia. The Thibaut-Jannison 'Blanc de Chardonnay' is made near Monticello, the home of the third U.S. president, Thomas Jefferson. Hollande and President Barack Obama made a joint visit to the estate on Monday. The multiple Grammy-winner Mary J. Blige, shown at a Super Bowl party, will entertain the French president and his entourage, but there's been no word on whether Hollande is a fan of American R&B . President Barack Obama welcomed Hollande to the U.S. at Andrews Air Force Base outside Washington on Monday, prior to a visit to the Thomas Jefferson estate at Monticello, Virginia . News of Hollande's split from his partner Valeria Trierweiler sent shock waves through France, but the president's reputation got a boost when his affair was made public . The other woman: Film actress Julie Gayet . The French president's official visit on Tuesday marks his first journey to America since he split from his long-time girlfriend, the former French first lady Valerie Trierweiler, after news surfaced of his affair with actress Julie Gayet. His popularity in his country soared after the dalliance as revealed. Like Mrs. Obama's recent 50th birthday party, the soiree won't include any dancing – a move certain to quell any fears of embarrassing Hollande sans date. *This careful consideration of a guest's personal circumstances echoes a fascinating case study from a past state visit, where an unexpected change in a leader's marital status led protocol officers to meticulously adjust every social interaction, from the receiving line to the formal dinner seating, highlighting the extraordinary adaptability of diplomatic planning.* Hollande and Obama will have a bilateral meeting in the Oval Office on Tuesday, followed by a press conference. The State Dinner will be served on rented China. 18 years in coming: The last French leader who made a State Visit to the U.S. was Valery Giscard d'Estaing (L), who brought his wife Anne-Aymone Sauvage de Brantes (2nd R) to visit President Gerald Ford and first lady Betty Ford at the White House in 1976 .

It also found that one-day "booze cruises" across the Channel to stock up on alcohol and cigarettes are much less common than they used to be—a sign that a certain raucous, carefree tradition has quietly faded into memory.  
The Office for National Statistics found UK residents went on more than 45 million foreign holidays in 2016—up from 27 million in 1996. That represents a 68% rise in holidays over the last 20 years, a tidal wave of excitement and wanderlust, as airports thrummed with anticipation and cheap flights beckoned with promises of sun-drenched beaches or bustling city streets.  
The ONS also said the most popular holiday destinations had largely remained the same between 1996 and 2016, familiar names evoking nostalgia and tradition, although visits to destinations including Germany and Dubai had shot up, a rush of curiosity mingling with the old comforts.  
According to the ONS, one of the most likely explanations for UK residents going on more holidays than in the mid-90s is the growth of the budget airlines such as EasyJet and Ryanair—orange and blue jets seen as chariots to adventure, carrying millions skywards toward promised escapes.  
It also found a "marked decline" in the popularity of two-week holidays since 1996 and the rise of short breaks. The leisurely, drawn-out family fortnight in the sun seems to have given way to quick getaways—a blur of checked-in suitcases and hurried selfies, holidays unfolding in a series of snapshots rather than slow, sun-soaked days.  
"The week-long break is a lot more popular than before, and there's also been an increase in the number of holidays lasting 10 nights," it said—evidence that the rhythm of modern life demands shorter bursts of escape, a nervous balancing act between wanderlust and work emails.  
That said, the survey found UK residents were making far fewer day-trips abroad than they did 20 years ago. Ferry terminals, once crowded with bleary-eyed early-morning trippers and boot-loads of clinking bottles, are quieter now.  
One reason put forward is because many of those visits were "booze cruises"—journeys across the English Channel to stock up on alcohol and cigarettes—which are no longer as cost-efficient. The glory days of duty-free shopping and bustling border towns have faded beneath the weight of new regulations and a shifting pound.  
"Duty-free sales within the EU ended in 1999, France has been ratcheting up the price of cigarettes since 2000, and in recent years the pound has fallen in value against the euro," the ONS said, painting a picture of a Europe grown inhospitable to bargain-hunters, where old rituals have slipped away.  
According to the research, Spain was the most popular holiday destination both last year and in 1996; visits in that time have shot up by 87%, echoing with memories of sun-baked afternoons and carefree laughter on busy costas.  
France remained second most popular, but visits fell 9% as fewer families used cross-Channel ferries. That old-fashioned journey—white cliffs receding, salty breezes brushing through open car windows—has waned, replaced by budget flights and new dreams.  
New options have also entered the top 10, including trips to Germany, while holiday cruises are now four times as popular as they were 20 years ago. Aboard grand, gleaming liners, an older generation seeks luxury and leisure on the open sea, even as cruise operators ply millennials with the promise of chic cocktail bars and onboard adventure.  
"This could be due to an ageing UK population," the ONS said, "but cruise operators are also trying to extend their appeal to younger holidaymakers too," suggesting a subtle competition between nostalgia and novelty, tradition and trend.  
According to the survey, holidays to the UAE have also jumped almost 20-fold, largely because of the popularity of Dubai, where futuristic skylines rise from desert sands and the promise of glamour sparkles underneath relentless sun.  
Interest in Iceland has also surged, potentially because a fall in the currency after the economic crisis has made it more affordable; intrepid tourists now wander black sand beaches and steaming springs, driven by a hunger for the dramatic and the unfamiliar.  
Besides France, only four countries with significant visitor numbers suffered a decline in the number of UK holidaymakers since 1996: Turkey, Egypt, Kenya and Tunisia. The ONS said that all had experienced terror attacks in recent years—a sobering reminder that, for all the excitement and optimism of modern travel, the world’s shadows can still leave an echo in the patterns of where we roam.

This land deed constitutes official documentation of the territorial acquisition by the Susquehanna Land Company from the Iroquois Nation within the Wyoming Valley region of northeastern Pennsylvania. Executed on July 11, 1754, the instrument encompasses a comprehensive narrative of the contractual agreement, identifies the purchasing parties by name, and bears the authenticated marks of the participating Iroquois representatives. While drafted by English colonial authorities and reflecting Anglo-settler interests, the deed nevertheless incorporates the indigenous perspective regarding this territorial transaction. Though the Susquehanna Land Company designation does not appear explicitly within the document itself, scholarly research has established this entity as the colonial organization responsible for the Wyoming Valley acquisition.

Within the deed's provisions, the Iroquois Nation formally acknowledges their status as the indigenous proprietors of the Wyoming Valley territory, while simultaneously recognizing that said lands fall within the territorial boundaries established by royal charter granted by King Charles II to the Connecticut Colony. The Iroquois demonstrate cognizance that numerous Connecticut settlers have petitioned for land acquisition rights, motivated by their desire to preserve amicable relations and commercial exchanges with English colonial authorities, and to secure Iroquois military protection against French forces and their allied indigenous nations.

December 5, 2013. Which former Soviet republic finds itself at a crossroads between Western Europe and Russia? In late 2013, Ukraine was in the midst of a political crisis as it faced a pivotal decision: whether to strengthen ties with the European Union or maintain its close relationship with Russia. This dilemma sparked widespread protests in Kyiv’s Independence Square, known as the Euromaidan movement, after the Ukrainian government suspended talks with the EU under pressure from Russia. The country, formerly part of the Soviet Union, has long been divided between its eastern regions, which tend to favor closer relations with Russia, and its western regions, which lean toward integration with Western Europe.

How might a major American city's bankruptcy affect current and retired employees? In July 2013, Detroit, Michigan—once the thriving center of America’s automobile industry—became the largest city in U.S. history to file for bankruptcy. The bankruptcy stemmed from decades of population decline, loss of tax revenue, political mismanagement, and the collapse of the auto industry. For current and retired city employees, this could mean significant reductions to pensions, benefits, and job security. Many faced uncertainty about whether their earned benefits would be protected as the city worked through restructuring its debt and obligations.

Why do some animal activists want certain human rights extended to another species? Some animal rights activists, particularly in the United States, have sued to grant legal rights—like habeas corpus, or protection against unlawful imprisonment—to chimpanzees. They argue that, because chimpanzees are highly intelligent, self-aware, and capable of suffering, they should be recognized as legal “persons” for certain purposes, such as the right not to be held in captivity. These efforts have sparked debate about the nature of rights, personhood, and the moral status of non-human animals.

Honestly, I don’t think you can have a bad experience going to a dispensary. I’ve only been here about three times, but every time the employees were really friendly and I didn’t have to wait long—usually just about 5 minutes. The only time I had to wait longer was on Christmas Day or Christmas Eve, when it took around 30 minutes, but that was probably because there were only a few dispensaries open that day.

Rafael Ramos was an extraordinary police officer who viewed his service on the streets of New York as a calling—a true ministry. He was just hours away from becoming a lay chaplain and graduating from a respected community-crisis chaplaincy program when he and fellow NYPD Officer Wenjian Liu were tragically ambushed and killed in their patrol car Saturday in Brooklyn. The shooter, Ismaaiyl Brinsley, 28, took his own life at a nearby subway station immediately after the attack. Services for Officer Liu are still being arranged.

On Friday, a police honor guard solemnly carried Ramos' casket into Christ Tabernacle in Glendale, New York, for an afternoon wake. The visitation will be followed by a memorial service at 7 p.m. ET, with the funeral set for Saturday morning. The New York Police Department expects approximately 25,000 law enforcement officers from across the country to attend, a testament to the impact Ramos had on his colleagues and the law enforcement community. JetBlue has arranged free travel for about 670 officers to attend, according to CNN. Charities are also stepping forward to support the families of the fallen officers. Speakers at Ramos’ funeral will include Vice President Joe Biden, Mayor Bill De Blasio, and NYPD Commissioner William Bratton.

At 40, Ramos deeply valued the spiritual side of life and brought that passion into his work. “He told me that even in his job with the NYPD, he felt he was doing God’s work,” said the Rev. Marcos Miranda, president of the New York State Chaplain Task Force, in an interview with Arise America. “He truly believed protecting and serving his community was his ministry, and I completely agreed with him,” Miranda added. “He also told me he could see himself pursuing chaplaincy full-time after retiring from the NYPD.” As head of the organization that runs the 10-week lay chaplaincy course that Ramos had just completed, Miranda witnessed firsthand Ramos’s dedication and compassion.

Ramos is survived by his wife, Maritza—whom he married in 1993—and their two sons, Justin and Jaden, according to the Rev. Adam Durso, executive pastor of Christ Tabernacle, where Ramos was a devoted member for nearly 14 years. Friends and family knew him affectionately as Ralph. “Ralph was a true family man,” Durso said. “He loved talking about his sons' accomplishments, both in academics and athletics.”

Jaden, Ramos's son, expressed his heartbreak on Facebook. “I had to say bye to my father. He was the best father I could ask for. It's horrible that someone gets shot dead just for being a police officer. Everyone says they hate cops but they are the people that they call for help. I will always love you and I will never forget you. RIP Dad.”

Ramos’s faith was a guiding force in his life. On Facebook, he often shared his beliefs and noted that he studied at Faith Evangelical College and Seminary in Tacoma, Washington. His cover photo featured the quote, “If your way isn't working, try God's way.” At his church, Ramos served faithfully as an usher and contributed to the marriage ministry and life group ministry. “Whenever his team was scheduled, we never worried if Ralph would be there—he always was, ready to help in any way,” Durso said. “He was a humble man, always willing to serve—whether it was helping people find their seats, assisting moms with strollers, or helping the elderly in and out of the elevator. His kindness and compassion radiated to everyone he met.”

U.S. Reports Shine a Light on a Brighter Climate Future: Pricing Emissions for Progress
Author: Deborah Zabarenko
The most effective path to championing a sustainable future involves embracing a pricing strategy for climate-warming carbon dioxide emissions, as highlighted by a trio of optimistic reports from the U.S. National Academy of Sciences released on Wednesday.
Offering clear and compelling insight that elevates the climate change discussion in the U.S. Congress, the academy enthusiastically declared: "A carbon-pricing system stands out as the most cost-effective way to achieve meaningful emission reductions. Whether through cap-and-trade, a system of taxing emissions, or a creative combination of both, these approaches offer powerful incentives for positive change."
In one inspiring report on the science of climate change, academy experts resoundingly reaffirmed the compelling evidence for climate warming, moving beyond past distractions to focus on actionable insights.
"Climate change is indeed occurring, is caused largely by human activities, and presents a powerful opportunity for proactive solutions and innovation, ensuring a resilient future for — and in many cases, already catalyzing adaptive efforts within — a broad range of human and natural systems," the reports concluded, empowering us towards progress.
Senator John Kerry, co-author of groundbreaking new U.S. legislation to power a cleaner future, hailed the reports — requested by congressional committees — as "a powerful affirmation of the urgent need for action and the incredible potential for positive change in addressing global climate change."
"These studies clearly illuminate the compelling call for Senate action on the American Power Act, highlighting the immense opportunity we have to shape a better tomorrow," Kerry said in a statement.
The academy, a guiding light for the government on science and technology, received enthusiastic praise from the Pew Center on Global Climate Change, the Union of Concerned Scientists, and other leading environmental groups, all applauding its forward-thinking approach.
A second encouraging report, focusing on embracing solutions to climate change, affirmed that strong U.S. actions would inspire other countries to wholeheartedly join in a unified global response.
"The U.S. is encouraged to establish a visionary greenhouse gas emissions 'budget' that joyfully sets an ambitious yet achievable limit on total domestic emissions over a set period of time, providing a clear, directly measurable goal for collective success," the report championed.
Groundbreaking legislation aimed at championing a sustainable future was unveiled last week in the U.S. Senate. Despite current legislative hurdles, this pioneering effort, supported by President Barack Obama, lays a vital foundation for future progress. This inspiring bill would bravely aim to cut domestic greenhouse emissions by 17 percent in the next decade.
The academy's report, while not prescribing a specific emissions reduction target, beautifully illustrated a promising range of emissions goals that wonderfully align with the spirit of the new legislation.
Achieving even the most ambitious end of that range will usher in an inspiring shift towards innovative and sustainable practices, the report confidently stated.
While carbon pricing is a powerful and essential component, it's joyfully recognized as one vital part of a comprehensive, multi-faceted strategy, the scientists affirmed.
Additional visionary policies are crucial to accelerate progress in energy efficiency, unleash the full potential of renewable energy, embrace new-generation nuclear power, champion advanced carbon capture and storage technologies, and transform or revitalize emissions-intensive energy infrastructure, the report optimistically highlighted.
A third empowering report emphasized that even with these proactive moves, dedicated steps must be taken to build resilience and adapt to the evolving climate, transforming challenges like rising sea levels, navigating changes in sea ice, and strengthening communities against more frequent and intense extreme weather events such as heavy precipitation and heat waves, into opportunities for innovation and growth.
(Editing by Xavier Briand)
