Greed. Greed never changes. This is my last dispatch. I fear that I've passed Go for the last time. I write this from a boxcar on the Reading, where I've spent my last $200 for a place to stay—didn't it cost just $25 to stay here a few weeks ago?

It all started with that stranger in the silver roadster. He came speeding into town, said he wanted to buy up some property and improve this city. We laughed at him; oh, how we laughed. "As if anyone's gonna build a house better than a night on the Boardwalk," we'd chortle to each other. But we needed the money after being hit with school taxes and poor taxes.

We became more concerned as ol' Silver started buying up the town. "Why," Mr. Parker would proclaim at our town hall meetings, "not since the days of the Susquehannocks has anyone owned all of the land from St. Charles to Virginia!" We came up with a plan: we'd sell some property to the man with the strange hat who had been knocking on doors lately—that competition would keep rents down and keep people happy. The sheriff in town is an old family friend; it wasn't hard to talk him into arresting ol' Silver on some bogus charges. He was a queer fellow anyway, what with his beauty contests. By gum, the Reading would remain America's Largest Anthracite Carrier!

Nothing worked, though. Ol' Silver would always have a trick—a "get out of jail free" card? Come on!—and none of us had accounted for Ironsides coming into town and immediately buying up the utilities. You wouldn't believe how much our bills would fluctuate! One month I'm paying $12 for power, and the next month it's $110!

It was our fault too, in part. People liked living in the new houses. It was expensive, but you could still stay for a night on the Connecticut for $30. It all changed with those damn hotels. We couldn't afford $1,100 to stay on Illinois, and when the cash stops rolling, something else starts rolling—and always downhill. It didn't take long before ol' Silver owned everything.

"We have to take it back," old man Parker would say. We did everything we could. We levied income taxes and luxury taxes. A few hundred dollars went missing from the bank here and there, never to be accounted for. We made some revenue from "Free Parking" even though no one ever checked the town statutes, which made no mention of that practice. Things were still okay for a little while—our Christmas fund even matured. We should have known, though, that we were just buying time. Nothing would change.

We have no hospitals. We have no grocery stores. We have no future here. Only more luxury developments. Only more greed. Our last chance is on this railroad. Parker and some of the other citizens have a plan to hijack it and get out of this town—no, this isn't a town—this monopoly, where there's no place for us anymore. One of the young ones said that in school, he heard of a faraway land with sheep and wheat for us all, and with plenty of brick, ore, and wood so that we can build anew. Maybe we can find peace there and settle again. We have to take that chance.

Let me tell you about an exciting project we started at 21 Acres back in late 2015. We were lucky enough to receive a Regional Food System Grant from King Conservation District, which gave us the chance to build a hedgerow made entirely of native plants. The main goal? To see which types of beneficial insects those plants would attract and what kind of habitat they’d prefer.

We didn’t do this alone—some of the best local plant and insect ecologists helped us out, along with a whole crew of dedicated volunteers. Dr. Julie Combs, who’s an expert in ecology and invasive plants, actually designed the whole hedgerow setup for us. She created 18 planting circles, each about 10 feet across, that stretch along 300 feet of our property border.

Dr. Combs took a close look at our sandy loam soil, thought about which bugs we’d want to attract (and which pests we hoped to keep away), and even considered how much exposure the hedgerow would get from the elements. She suggested we try out different mulch types to see which ones the insects like best. Over the summer, our amazing volunteers put in more than 200 hours prepping the site, pulling out invasive plants, and getting 270 native plants in the ground—30 species in all!

On recent site visits, Dr. Combs and a team from KCD (that’s Mary Embleton, Joshua Monaghan, and Bill Knutsen) discovered some really promising results. We already spotted ground-nesting native bees settling in! In just the first year, we saw Canadian goldenrod and Douglas aster attracting a variety of beneficial insects—bumble bees, sweat bees, hover flies, and honey bees. Other plants, like coyote brush, Henderson checker mallow, big leaf and riverbank lupine, and manzanita, established themselves quickly and look like they’ll be great options for early-season food and habitat.

Next steps? We’ll be taking soil samples and getting ready to try out those different mulch treatments—wood chips, pebbles, and native soil—all in hopes of attracting even more beneficial insects to our new hedgerow. 

(Top photo: Hover fly on Douglas aster, by Kurt Sahl)
(Bottom photo: Coyote brush, photo by Miguel Vieira [CC BY 2.0], via Wikimedia Commons)

Sharon May, 46, a former teacher at Irlam and Cadishead College, was found by a professional conduct panel to have spent school funds on gift vouchers for her own use. She admitted to three counts of fraud at Manchester Crown Court in March of last year and received a suspended sentence.

The National College for Teaching and Leadership’s professional conduct panel described her actions as “serious dishonesty,” noting that May had deprived vulnerable pupils of public funds that were meant to support their educational needs. As the school’s special educational needs co-ordinator (SENCo), May had failed to respect the rights of these students and abused her position of trust, the panel added.

Concerns first surfaced in October 2014 over the possible misuse of a grant intended to improve mental health support for students. Funds from this grant, as well as from 1-2-1 Tuition budgets, had been used to purchase Trafford Centre vouchers, restaurant vouchers, and Center Parcs vouchers. In November 2014, May provided the local authority with information about this spending that the panel described as “false or misleading.” She resigned on 27 February 2015.

May ultimately admitted to three counts of fraud committed between 1 May 2010 and 3 December 2014. She was given a two-year suspended sentence and required to complete 200 hours of unpaid work.

In December of last year, the panel determined that a prohibition order was “both proportionate and appropriate,” concluding that a review period was unwarranted. They cited the seriousness of her sustained dishonesty involving significant amounts of public money designated for vulnerable students. As a result, May, now 46, was banned from teaching indefinitely.

"I have hemorrhaged [sic] my vocal cord again," she wrote on Instagram.
"I will need to cancel the remainder of my tour and get surgery to finally fix this once and for all."
The All About That Bass star, who had been due to round off her MTrain tour with a string of dates in August and September, told her fans she was "devastated, scared and so sorry".
She added: "I am determined to do what it takes to get better and come back around stronger than ever."
Her vocal struggles reflect a broader trend among recording artists, as she is not the only singer to have suffered voice problems in recent months. Sam Smith and Jess Glynne are among other recording stars to have had vocal cord issues.

Police fear there may be additional victims. According to a statement from La Mesa Police, Sean Banks, 37, met the victim through the matchmaking website ChristianMingle.com, where he used the username "Raritiy." Banks also operated under three other online aliases: "Rylan," "Rylan Butterwood," and "Rylan Harbough." Authorities expressed concern that "there may have been more cases involving other women." ChristianMingle features a safety section on its website, advising users to avoid contact with anyone who cannot accept "no" for an answer. The site is cooperating with the investigation, CNN affiliate KSWB reported. Banks was arrested on February 11 and booked on two counts of rape and one count of residential burglary, according to police. He has pleaded not guilty and was released on his own recognizance after posting $500,000 bail. When it comes to online identities, it can be difficult to know who’s really behind the screen.

NATO representatives and lawmakers from alliance nations will meet in Iceland Thursday to discuss security challenges likely to arise as the Arctic Circle thaws. Global warming is opening up more parts of the Arctic to development and raising security concerns. The area—one of the planet's most fragile and pristine ecosystems—sits atop a bounty of untapped fossil fuels. As melting polar ice makes the Arctic more accessible to ships, several countries are scrambling to claim jurisdiction in the area. "The economic interests are reflected in ... competing claims by relevant stakeholders, and resumed military presence in the area," NATO said on its website. "As it is a region of enduring strategic importance for NATO and allied security, developments in the High North require careful and ongoing examination." The U.S. Geological Survey estimates that 90 billion barrels of oil, 44 billion barrels of natural gas liquids, and 1,670 trillion cubic feet of natural gas are recoverable in the frozen region north of the Arctic Circle. At the same time, Arctic water is warming so quickly that the entire region could be ice-free by 2013. Russia, Canada, the United States, Denmark, Norway, Sweden, Iceland, and Finland are already competing to lay claim to the Arctic's icy real estate. Unlike Antarctica, which has a treaty that prohibits territorial claims, there is no agreement for the vast expanse of the Arctic, making questions about drilling rights and shipping lanes somewhat murky. According to the U.N. Convention on the Law of the Sea, countries are entitled to exclusive economic zones up to 200 miles from their shores, but some countries are trying to extend that zone. Russian scientists want to prove the seabed below the North Pole is part of the Eurasian continental shelf, an area called the Lomonosov Ridge. If that is the case, the region would be under Russian control. Moscow argued before a U.N. commission in 2001 that the ridge is an extension of its continental territory, but the United Nations asked for more evidence. Danish scientists are trying to prove that the Lomonosov Ridge is connected to Greenland, and Canadian scientists are looking for links between the ridge and Ellesmere Island, a Canadian territory. NATO Secretary General Jaap de Hoop Scheffer will attend the two-day conference in Reykjavik.

It begins slowly at first. One after another, the worn out, tired fixtures go dark. No one wants to mar this moment with artificial light. The ever-present electric hum that had become a constant companion falls silent at last, giving way to a pure, deep quiet not heard for too many years. They don't disturb it, moving equally softly through the comfortable, familiar darkness. The doors creak open from their vaults and cellars and chambers, now left open and abandoned. The survivors gather, in ones and twos and families, forming a steady line which moves towards the high ground. They know. Today is the beginning. The initial morning after the attack 20 years ago. The start of a new day after the all clear was given, and now they emerge to see it. The dawn of a morning without a forced eclipse. No one speaks. They don't have to. Some spread blankets against the frigid cold of the night, but most just stand and wait, facing east. An excited murmur breaks out from the smallest among them, who have never seen the like of this. A glow along the horizon line, only a few shades brighter. But enough. No one shushes them. As they watch, it spreads across the skyline. It's more than the children speaking now. The blackness becomes an inky blue. A deep violet. Out of nowhere, in the blink of an eye and an exhaled breath, it explodes into vivid red. Below, the remnants of their world that was are washed in color, but no one looks to those ashes of yesterday. All eyes turn upwards. In a single moment, the world is frozen in a rainbow of colors and clouds and the glowing orb which is advancing steadily higher. In that instant, they see tomorrow.

This book was recommended to me by a fellow tea shoppe owner so I found it on Amazon and downloaded it to my kindle in anticipation of an enjoyable read. I am an avid reader so I thought this would be fun! Unfortunately for me, this was a grueling book to get through...it was only through my commitment to never let a book go unfinished that I got to the end. It tried to be a murder mystery with random forced references to tea or Charleston. I thought the whole book was elementary in writing style and the story was so fictional and lacking depth it was painful to read.....

i have to agree with what everyone else said about how amazing this album is. honestly, i walked into the store that day feeling pretty cynical about modern music - everything seemed so manufactured and soulless. i grabbed this cd on a whim, not knowing what to expect, but from the very first track, i was completely blown away. it wasn't just the music that hit me, but the raw, unfiltered passion that poured out of every note. as the album progressed, i realized no two songs sound alike - each one took me on a completely different emotional journey, which is exactly why i've found myself listening to it over and over again, discovering new layers each time. thanks matt for having the courage to give us such an incredible, vulnerable piece of who you are, and for single-handedly reassuring me that real, authentic music isn't dead after all.

Barbara Ehrenreich worked hard on this book. She wanted to get a job at a big company and show people what it’s really like to work in an office. But she didn’t find a job she liked or get the office job she wanted. Still, she wrote a whole book about her experience. We learn that she hired people to help her find a job. She put her resume on job websites. She changed her resume many times. She went to job fairs and events to meet people. She even had a makeover.

Even with all this, I thought the book didn’t have much information. Some of her complaints were very harsh. It seemed like she didn’t really understand or maybe didn’t want to find a job. She also said some unkind things about Christians, which I thought was unfair and mean.

He can't believe it. He had been living for two months believing that he was the last man on earth. But there was someone else. Someone who responded to his hopeless AMA. The words on his cracked screen make his heart race: "Where are you?" He scrambled to type. "I'm at the Grand Canyon." The cut screen made his fingers bleed again. But it was worth it.

Seconds feel like hours as he waits for a reply. One minute passes. Two. Ten. Thirty. An hour. He begins to worry. Did something happen to the other person? Did they run out of battery? Or something worse? Are they ignoring him on purpose? Are they even real? He waits. Two hours. Never has he felt so alone. Not even when he thought nobody else was out there. He can't shake the feeling that this person doesn't need him. Doesn't want him. The day is hot, but he decides to take a nap to help the wait.

He wakes up, and anxiously scrambles to check for a reply. Three hours. No response. He begins to give up hope, and he looks over the edge. A deep drop. It couldn't hurt much before the end, could it?

She sees him for the first time in years. The last man on earth. The man who killed her sister. The man she swore to kill the next time she saw him. She tries her hardest, she tells herself he's not the same person, but she can't get rid of the anger. He's standing on the edge, looking down. She can sneak up on him, push him over. Would that make her as bad as him? He doesn't seem to be harming anyone anymore. She makes up her mind, and takes her first step towards him.

He knows he hasn't lived a good life. Maybe he doesn't deserve anyone else. She gets closer. He hasn't moved in minutes. He makes up his mind. She is just a few feet away. She can smell his stink. He says his last prayers. Just in case there is a God. She is at arm's reach. It would be so easy.

He jumps.

Respiration keeps going because cells need energy, and this is why we need oxygen. The diffusion gradient pulls the oxygen in, like a tractor beam, to where it’s needed in our cells. Once inside the mitochondrion—after oxygen crosses yet another membrane—molecular machines break up food molecules such as glucose. This breaking of chemical bonds releases energy, which is used to make a molecule called adenosine triphosphate, or ATP. The main purpose of these reactions is to produce ATP, which in turn powers every energy-requiring reaction in the cell—muscle contraction, nerve impulses, DNA replication, and so on, in all forms of life. Although a human body contains only about 10 grams of ATP at any instant, we make and use up our body weight’s worth of ATP each day because every action burns it up.

Oxygen is vital here because it acts as the final electron acceptor. As the mitochondrial machines break down food, they strip away electrons (and accompanying hydrogen protons). If these electrons built up, the process would stall. Oxygen accepts these electrons and protons, preventing backup and allowing the fuel-burning machinery to keep running. In doing so, each oxygen molecule (made of two oxygen atoms) splits apart and each atom then joins with two hydrogen atoms to form water. Nothing is really destroyed—matter just changes form. Each new water molecule travels through my veins to my lungs, leaves my body as vapor, and floats into the air. There, it might become a raindrop and eventually find its way into a plant, where it could be used to create food through photosynthesis.

Aristotle thought breathing cooled the blood, but in reality, breathing delivers oxygen for energy production—and by measuring my oxygen consumption, Vasiliki Costarelli can measure how much energy I’m using, also called my metabolic rate. That’s why I am lying on the couch; my energy consumption is being measured.

To be alive is to use up and release energy. Every living body burns fuel—literally. The chemistry of cellular respiration is the same as that of burning, or combustion. The energy released from food when you eat it is the same amount you would get if you burned it. Life is a slow fire: every cell must constantly burn fuel to build and maintain itself.

I can't convey how pleased my wife and I were with the entire process. We lived in Los Angeles for many years where buying a car can be an inhuman and degrading experience. We had a really good experience.

The unique metabolism of pancreatic cancer cells makes them a potential target for metformin, a drug commonly used to treat diabetes. Until very recently, it was believed that cancer cells relied on glycolysis, a type of metabolism that does not use oxygen, to generate their energy. However, scientists from the Spanish National Cancer Research Centre (CNIO) in Madrid and the Queen Mary University of London’s Barts Cancer Institute have recently discovered a different metabolic pathway in certain cancer cells.

For example, pancreatic cancer stem cells (PancSCs) use a more efficient form of metabolism, called OXPHOS (oxidative phosphorylation). This oxygen-based metabolism utilizes a part of the cell called mitochondria for generating energy. The team believes that by targeting these mitochondria with the anti-diabetic drug, metformin, they can effectively suffocate the pancreatic cancer stem cells.

However, the metabolic flexibility of some PancSCs allows them to escape the suffocating tactics of the diabetic drug, representing a significant risk factor for pancreatic cancer relapse. Fortunately, upon discovering this loophole, the researchers identified a method to overcome such resistance and compel all PancSCs to continue using OXPHOS.

Consequently, the team believes these positive results could open up avenues for developing treatments that stop stem cells from using oxygen. They are extremely positive that by doing so, they can prevent cancer from returning after conventional treatment, and a clinical trial is planned for later next year.

As explained by the study's first author, Dr. Patricia Sancho, the team believes they can exploit the cancer stem cell’s reliance on oxygen by targeting them with available drugs. By cutting off this energy supply, they can effectively kill the cancer.

These findings suggest that (a) pancreatic cancer patients could have a reduced risk of recurrence following surgery and other treatments, and (b) more treatment options may become available to them in the long run.

Due to its tendency to cause symptoms and trigger diagnosis only at a late and advanced stage, pancreatic cancer remains one of the most difficult cancers to treat. Indeed, many patients succumb to the disease within a year of diagnosis. Therefore, finding new treatment strategies is of paramount importance.

Crucially, even though PancSCs make up only a small proportion of the tumor, they possess the potential to generate new tumors, even after all other cells are eradicated, and are prone to spreading throughout the body. Thus, the team is convinced that PancSCs' reliance on targetable mitochondria offers a significant clue for future treatments.

Xi Jinping, who is due to succeed President Hu Jintao in 2013, said he would fight against "separatist activities" linked to the Dalai Lama.
He was speaking in Lhasa as part of a trip to mark 60 years since the Chinese Communist Party took over Tibet.
Analysts say the speech suggests Mr Xi is unlikely to veer from the policies of his predecessors.
Beijing has for years been critical of the Dalai Lama, regularly denouncing him as a "splittist" in the Xinhua News Agency.
At the weekend, Beijing was highly critical of US President Barack Obama's decision to meet the Tibetan spiritual leader.
The People's Republic of China government says Tibet has always been an integral part of China, and that their policies have brought economic development to a chronically poor area.
But many Tibetans accuse Beijing of cracking down on free speech, suppressing their religion and culture, and encouraging the immigration of many Han Chinese to the area.
The Dalai Lama, who fled to India in 1959, has publicly said many times in recent years that he wants more freedom for Tibetans, not necessarily independence.
Mr Xi, who is currently vice-president, made his speech in front of the Potala Palace, which is the Dalai Lama's traditional seat.
"[We] should thoroughly fight against separatist activities by the Dalai clique by firmly relying on all ethnic groups... and completely smash any plot to destroy stability in Tibet and jeopardise national unity," he said.
"The extraordinary development of Tibet over the past 60 years points to an irrefutable truth: Without the Chinese Communist Party, there would have been no new China, no new Tibet."
Little is known about Mr Xi's political leanings, because China's leaders-in-waiting are rarely allowed to make major speeches in the run-up to changes in the Politburo Standing Committee.
Mr Hu has often pushed the idea of a harmonious society depending on the sharing of wealth between the country's manufacturing hubs in coastal provinces like Guangdong and Zhejiang, and its rural regions like Xinjiang and Sichuan.

Arsenal Football Club has suspended contract negotiations with key players Mesut Özil, 28, and Alexis Sánchez, 28, until the conclusion of the current season. 

Sánchez has publicly indicated his preference to remain in London with an organization demonstrating a "winning mentality," which has generated substantial speculation regarding a potential transfer to Chelsea Football Club.

"I maintain confidence that both players desire to remain with Arsenal, and I am optimistic that the club will reach mutually beneficial agreements with them," stated Manager Arsène Wenger.

While both players remain under contract through June 2018, persistent speculation regarding their future commitments has dominated discourse throughout the current campaign.

The 67-year-old Arsenal manager emphasized that the contractual situations present "no immediate concern" and confirmed that the club is not compelled to divest key personnel, contrasting with previous seasons when financial constraints necessitated such decisions.

Chilean international Sánchez was quoted in domestic media stating: "I am content in London and committed to fulfilling my contractual obligations. I prefer stability within an organization that competes for major honors."

Wenger characterized Sánchez's statements as "overwhelmingly positive," adding: "Alexis has expressed his ambition to secure the championship—an objective shared universally within our organization."

Despite receiving a contract extension offer earlier this season, multiple former Arsenal personnel have suggested Wenger's 21-year tenure may be concluding amid escalating supporter dissatisfaction with perceived underperformance.

While Arsenal remains active in FA Cup competition, the club has been eliminated from Champions League participation and currently occupies sixth position in the Premier League table—a performance that has prompted organized supporter demonstrations calling for Wenger's departure.

Having promised clarification regarding his future "imminently" less than two weeks prior, Wenger stated Thursday: "This matter remains unresolved at present. However, my position is unambiguous. Whether my tenure extends two months or two years, my commitment level will remain constant and will not influence my professional approach."

Striker Lucas Pérez confirmed to Spanish media that Wenger has not communicated any decision to the squad, though he anticipates the manager's continuation.

Following six defeats in their previous nine fixtures, when questioned whether the uncertainty created operational disruption, Wenger responded: "Organizational priorities must focus on essential matters rather than seeking justifications for substandard performance."

Arsenal will face Manchester City on Sunday, with visiting manager Pep Guardiola characterizing criticism directed toward Wenger as "unacceptable."

Wenger reciprocated the professional courtesy, stating: "I maintain tremendous respect for his managerial capabilities and exceptional career trajectory. Once he completes twenty years at Manchester City, he will undoubtedly receive comparable scrutiny."

Wenger confirmed that midfielder Santi Cazorla's season has concluded due to the ankle injury that has sidelined him since October, while goalkeeper Petr Čech may also be unavailable for the next two fixtures.

The former AS Monaco manager commended the implementation of video technology during Spain's 2-0 victory over France on Tuesday, asserting such advancement "should have been implemented decades ago."

The technological intervention resulted in the disallowance of an Antoine Griezmann goal and subsequent validation of a Gerard Deulofeu goal following a 40-second review period.

Wenger concluded: "The delayed adoption of such obvious improvements reflects football's institutional resistance to progress and innovation."

The man in the hoodie could barely stop snickering like some demented hyena who'd just discovered stand-up comedy. I couldn't see his face, but that was normal for most clients meeting in the flesh—anonymity was half the business model in my line of work. We were sitting at the greasy spoon diner at Fourth and Union, just like he requested, where the coffee tasted like burnt rubber and the ambiance screamed "perfect place for questionable transactions."

With gloved hands that twitched with barely contained glee, he slid a small envelope forward. "The mark?" I asked, already mentally calculating my fee. He nodded enthusiastically, still holding back those irritating laughs. He was doing that pulsating jigger people do when they're laughing hard but not breathing, or when they want to laugh really hard but refuse to allow themselves the air to do it—like a defective whoopee cushion. Whatever. The money was most important, and crazy clients paid just as well as sane ones.

I opened the envelope and saw a picture of myself staring back. Well, shit. I looked up at the man with the kind of expression you'd give someone who just told you they collect toenail clippings as a hobby. "Is this a fucking joke?"

The man took off his hoodie and what was underneath was too horrifying to describe—imagine if nightmares had nightmares, then gave those nightmare-nightmares a really bad skin condition and a personality disorder.

***"SURPRISE MOTHERFUCKER!!"*** he shrieked with the enthusiasm of a gameshow host announcing prizes in the seventh circle of Hell.

I died instantly, which was honestly a relief considering the circumstances.

Once we were on our way down the express elevator to eternal damnation, he kept asking me if I got the joke with the persistence of a toddler asking "are we there yet?" I said I did, mostly because arguing with supernatural entities seemed like poor customer service etiquette. He laughed all the way to Hell—a sound like nails on chalkboard mixed with the dying gasps of hope itself.

Delayed onset muscle soreness (DOMS) is a common condition among athletes, typically manifesting 24 to 48 hours post-strenuous exercise with symptoms such as muscle pain, tenderness, swelling, and reduced training capacity. Consequently, exercise scientists have actively sought methods to mitigate DOMS severity and shorten symptom duration.

Despite the common prescription of oral analgesics for DOMS, robust data on their efficacy has been limited. To address this gap, researchers conducted a study where DOMS was induced in the non-dominant elbow flexors (biceps muscles) of 60 healthy volunteers (30 men, 30 women). The 60 subjects were randomly assigned to one of five groups: (i) placebo, (ii) aspirin (900 mg), (iii) codeine (60 mg), (iv) paracetamol (1000 mg), or (v) control. Doses of drugs or placebo were administered daily for 11 days following the DOMS-inducing exercise ('Managing delayed onset muscle soreness: lack of effect of selected oral systemic analgesics,' Arch Phys Med Rehabil vol. 81 (7), pp. 966-972, 2000).

Despite the comprehensive range of analgesics evaluated, the study found no evidence of effectiveness for any of the treatments in alleviating DOMS symptoms. Outcome measures included pain levels, mechanical pain threshold, and elbow joint range of motion (extension and flexion). Consequently, this well-conducted, placebo-controlled, prospective, double-blind study demonstrated no significant benefit from using paracetamol, codeine, or aspirin to treat DOMS at the administered dosages. The research suggests that pharmacological interventions with these specific analgesics are ineffective in reducing DOMS discomfort or accelerating recovery of muscle flexibility and strength. Therefore, the most effective strategy for managing DOMS remains preventative: implementing sensible training progressions that avoid abrupt increases in volume and intensity.

WASHINGTON (AP) — Without increased immigration, whites in the United States would not lose their majority status until 2046—three years later than current official projections—and the total U.S. population would not reach 400 million until after 2060, more than a decade beyond previous forecasts, according to Census Bureau estimates released Wednesday.

The Census Bureau reports that, by the middle of the century, immigration will overtake natural increase (births minus deaths) as the primary driver of U.S. population growth. These new figures highlight how projections can shift based on economic trends and immigration policy, diverging from last year’s official estimates that predicted white children would become a minority group by 2018 and the overall white population would follow by 2043. Under existing assumptions of rising immigration, the total U.S. population was expected to climb to 400 million by 2051, a benchmark that will be delayed if immigration remains at its current pace.

The revised 2046 projection for a white minority is one of three alternative scenarios the bureau modeled, this one assuming a recent, slower annual rate of immigration—about 700,000 newcomers per year. Demographers note that such scenarios offer valuable insights into the country’s demographic future if current economic conditions and immigration policies remain unchanged.

However, actual demographic changes will depend on unpredictable factors such as the pace of economic recovery, cultural developments, natural or manmade disasters, and potential immigration reform currently debated in Congress.

The United States currently has a population of 315 million, with non-Hispanic whites comprising less than 64 percent. If immigration levels hold steady for the next 50 years, the population is projected to reach 392.7 million by 2060. At that point, whites would represent 44.7 percent of the population, while blacks would make up 12.7 percent, similar to the current proportion. The Hispanic population, presently 16 percent, would grow to 29 percent, and Asians would increase from 5 percent to 7.5 percent.

Under this scenario, the moment when minority children become the majority would be postponed to 2019, one year later than the current official forecast. Meanwhile, the population aged 65 and older would surpass the under-18 age group by 2038—almost two decades earlier than projected if higher immigration rates were assumed.

These revised Census Bureau projections underscore the critical role of immigration in sustaining U.S. population growth. Like many industrialized nations, the United States faces an aging population driven by a rising median age and declining birth rates.

I was finishing the aptitude assessment. As most people I'd been very anxious, and had tried several online versions, and no matter what I answered I always got the result "Customer Service Representative". I have studied rigorously to find the least customer-service answers to the questions possible. I had read about everything from Advanced Mathematics to Abstract Philosophy, both subjects customer service reps are infamously bad at. Suddenly, as I was pondering if leadership or independence was the least customer-service like, the guidance counselor stood in front of my desk. --Your test please? --Sorry, could I just have another couple of seconds to finish up? --Your test is not necessary. We all know you are customer service material. --Excuse me? --You heard me. I looked around in shock, hoping for support from my classmates. As I was looking around, very much not like a customer service rep, my classmates slowly got up from their chairs. They formed a circle around me and looked down at me. I felt very small. --Customer service types are required to exit through the designated service exit, they said in unison. --But... --CUSTOMER SERVICE TYPES ARE REQUIRED TO EXIT THROUGH THE SERVICE EXIT. The crowd parted and revealed a clear path to a door. They roughly lifted me from my chair, toppling my desk and making my test papers fly through the air. Their hands gripped me as they passed me along towards the exit. As I got there they threw me, and my face hit the door, which had abruptly shut. I looked up and saw the white on black silhouette of a man. --THAT'S NOT THE SERVICE EXIT. CUSTOMER SERVICE TYPES are required TO exit through the service exit. Again they lifted me, and roughly threw me out the window. I landed feet first into a cubicle. --About time, said the guidance counselor. I was wondering when you would arrive. Customer service reps shouldn't resist that much. --Well, that'd be because I'm not customer service material. --Perhaps. Oh well, it is too late now, the headset is prepared. --The heads--- Mmmph grblph! The guidance counselor had shoved a phone in my face. My hands were quickly getting fitted with typing gloves. --Please, I'm not customer service material! --Customer service reps don't complain, the guidance counselor said and handed me a script. --Hold these, the guidance counselor said and handed me some complaint forms. After instructing me to keep my hands ready for typing, and getting berated because good reps answer faster than that, the guidance counselor left me sitting there. There were several other customer service reps around. One of them looked straight at me, and my gaze met his eyes. --We can't be expected to just sit here answering calls, can we? He did not respond.

LITTLE ROCK, AR (News release) - While Ebola poses a very low risk to Arkansans, the Arkansas Department of Health (ADH) is maintaining rigorous surveillance on the Ebola outbreak in West Africa to ensure the absolute safety of Arkansas citizens.

ADH is actively coordinating with hospitals, emergency medical service providers, faith-based organizations, the State Chamber of Commerce and the Departments of Education and Higher Education to ensure they have comprehensive protocols in place to screen and monitor individuals who may have traveled to Arkansas from the affected West African countries (Guinea, Sierra Leone, Liberia and Nigeria).

Arkansas has no active or suspected cases of Ebola, and the risk remains very low for the general public. Ebola only spreads from those who are infected AND are experiencing symptoms, such as fever or bleeding. Ebola is spread from person-to-person through direct contact with bodily fluids such as blood, urine, vomit, diarrhea, sweat, semen and breast milk or by exposure to objects contaminated with bodily fluids, such as needles. Ebola does not spread through air, food or water.

The most critical step to preventing the spread of Ebola is immediately identifying those with fever or other symptoms who have travelled from Guinea, Sierra Leone, Liberia and Nigeria within the last 21 days. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) has provided strict guidelines for the management of any individual who has been in the four West African countries currently involved in the Ebola outbreak.

I purchased my Ionic Pro Turbo from Wal-Mart in February 2009. For approximately five months, it was easy to use, completely silent, and very efficient. After that period, I began experiencing problems: it would frequently shut off, and I had to clean it every weekend. Although cleaning required little effort, the Ionic Pro continued to malfunction. 

I returned it to Wal-Mart customer service, but they informed me that the item was outside their 90-day return policy. I then contacted Ionic Pro directly. Their customer service representative gave me a confirmation number and instructed me to fax my purchase receipt. She also said I needed to wait 48 hours before contacting them again. Additionally, she informed me that I would be charged $29.95 plus shipping for a replacement product.

Due to these issues and the lack of satisfactory customer service and warranty, my wife and I have decided to purchase another air purifier from a company with better support and a stronger warranty.

Novak Djokovic started his campaign to win the ATP World Tour Finals for the third time with an easy win over Marin Cilic in London, beating him 6-1, 6-1 in under an hour. Djokovic, the world’s top-ranked player, has now won 28 indoor matches in a row and is likely to finish the year as number one if he wins his next two matches against Stan Wawrinka and Tomas Berdych. Djokovic played confidently and used his experience to control the game, while Cilic, who recently won the US Open, struggled to compete. In another match, Stan Wawrinka also won easily against Tomas Berdych, with the same 6-1, 6-1 score. So far, the matches have been very one-sided, but fans hope to see more exciting contests in the next few days.

My experience at this particular post office was refreshingly positive. The customer support supervisor went above and beyond to assist me. For context, I had been struggling to stop unwanted mail from being delivered to my home. After discovering an official form online meant to remedy the situation, I visited my local Ryder Station in Brooklyn—but was met with a dismissive, "I don't know what this is. Just throw out the unwanted mail." Unsatisfied with that response, I tried several other Brooklyn locations. Although staff at these offices were more courteous, none recognized the form and I left without help.

Determined, I made my way to the main Church Street facility in the Financial District. There, the Customer Service Supervisor was honest in saying she hadn’t seen the form before, but rather than send me away, she carefully read through the instructions, completed her section, and gave me a copy for my records. The entire process, wait time included, took just 30 minutes. 

As someone who also works in customer service, I recognize that it’s unreasonable to expect staff to have every policy or document memorized. However, what truly distinguishes outstanding service is a willingness to learn, understand the issue, and offer real solutions. I’m grateful to Lina and Betty at the Church St. Postal Service for embodying those qualities.

I am currently in the process of returning this item. It was a gift, and it failed almost immediately. I was genuinely thrilled to give it to my girlfriend as a replacement for her old iPod, certain that with her frequent travel schedule, having access to movies and music would be a valuable upgrade. However, before she even had the chance to use it outside the house, it began freezing up and refused to process more than 30 or 40 files without requiring a reset. Each reset took approximately five minutes just to reload its library. Given this unacceptable performance, we are now switching to a Zune.

"So, where will it be this time? Oh, this looks fun! Playing ball in the park with your friends? That looks like a good spot to restart from!" The bureaucrat pointed at the monitor, his stubby finger indicating a memory of young children playing soccer in a park. As I watched him, I briefly wondered what it might be like to exist without a soul in your body at all. Did he ever wonder what it was like to be only a soul, without a body? If this sniveling lump even had the capacity for such thoughts.

"No, not there," I replied. "Let's go to the last 30 seconds."

"Excuse me?" he asked, clearly surprised.

But I had already made up my mind. I was done. I couldn't do it anymore. I had lived the same life countless times, and every attempt at change brought me back to the same ending. Each decision, no matter how small, led inexorably toward that final moment. Face down in a ditch, my motorcycle twenty feet ahead—no matter how I tried, that was always where I ended up.

So instead of trying to outrun my fate yet again, I wanted to meet it head-on this time, making sure my last thoughts were of the things, the places, and the people I loved.

"Yes," I said. "I want to go back to 30 seconds before I die."

The bureaucrat punched some keys on his desk, and in the next instant, I felt my body rushing up to meet me. I felt my toes again, the grip of my hands on the handlebars, my eyelids heavy with exhaustion. How long had I been riding?

A faint whisper drifted to my ears: "I don't understand why he chooses this part every time..."

I shook my head, trying to clear it. I really should stop for coffee or something. There was a gas station just one exit away. I glanced up at the moon. It really is beautiful when it's so full, isn't it?

"Something's coming." I hadn't been sleeping, not really. Just resting my eyes. I cleared my throat quietly. "Is it her?" 

Mon shook his head. "Can't tell yet." He was leaning against a cinderblock, scanning past ruptured asphalt and rusted cars for movement. His eyes were better, so he had the scope. The collapsed concrete roof of our hiding place left only a foot-tall gap to peer through. Enough to see things coming before they saw us. That was the idea anyway.

"It's got to be her, right?" I sat up against the wall and ran my fingers through the can of bullets next to me. I knew how many there were, but counting them was comforting. 

Mon ignored my question. "How many left?"

"Seven." He already knew.

"Think she’s bringing water?"

"Dunno if it’s her yet."

I nodded, sweating. "Hey, don’t tell Sun I was sleeping." Mon didn’t answer, just kept his eye pressed to the rotting rubber of the scope, his mouth a firm line.

"Please don’t tell him. I don’t want him angry." 

Mon made a noncommittal sound, still watching.

"It’s gotta be her. It’s past time. I hope she found water. She must have found something—after last time I’m sure she was careful—"

"Get ready." Mon’s whisper shut me up. I scrambled to my knees and grabbed my rifle. As I loaded a bullet and pushed the bolt home I muttered, "Six left." I braced against the wall, scanning. 

There—moving between the cars—a shape. In the gray dusk it looked human. But the Strange always did, until they got too close. That’s why Mon had the scope; we had to be sure. You couldn’t let a Strange get too close.

I aimed, squinting for detail. Whoever it was, they kept to cover—no wonder Mon couldn't tell. Please have red hair, I thought, finger on the trigger.

"Is it her?" I was pleading.

"Aim." 

I swallowed, tightening my finger as the figure stepped into the open. Gray moving against gray. I heard Mon letting out a slow breath. I did the same, preparing to fire.

What if it’s her? I shoved the thought away—too late now. You couldn’t let them get too close. 

Mon’s breath caught. "Wait," he hissed. "It’s her."

I released the trigger and slumped aside, relief flooding me. A moment later and we would have killed her. And then where would we be?

"Thank God." 

Mon nodded, clapped my shoulder. "Thank God, it’s Friday."

I like this Fred Meyer on NE 82nd Avenue—it's close to my house in the Roseway neighborhood and they usually have the groceries and household items I need in stock. The only downside to this Fred Meyer location I've noticed over the past few months is how many times I'm approached by panhandlers in the parking lot. On several occasions, people have come up behind me and startled me and my kids near our Subaru. Today, a guy approached my Honda CR-V as I was parking and called me a few choice names when I refused to give him change. Many times I've seen people sitting by the main entrance either passed out or holding signs asking for money. I'm to the point I'd rather shop at the New Seasons Market on 33rd Avenue instead.

**Britain's Recycling Boomerang: China Says "Keep Your Trash"**

*When your recycling comes home to roost*

Britain's dirty little secret is coming back to bite us – literally. Our recycling isn't good enough for China anymore, and they're shipping it right back to our doorstep.

**The Great Recycling Rejection**

Nearly 70% of Britain's recyclable plastics get shipped to China because we can't be bothered to process them properly at home. But China has had enough of our sloppy seconds.

"Don't dump your dirty laundry here," China is essentially telling Britain, as they crack down on contaminated, low-quality waste that doesn't meet their standards.

**420 Tons of Shame**

This year alone, 17 containers packed with 420 tons of plastic have been rejected and sent back like a returned Amazon package nobody wants. 

The problem? British households are recycling like they're playing trash roulette – throwing everything into one bin and hoping for the best.

**The Coming "Plastics Mountain"**

Recycling experts warn of a looming "plastics mountain" if China slams the door shut completely. New regulations could ban Britain's unwashed, mixed-up waste entirely.

Environment Secretary Owen Paterson promises Britain will "grasp this opportunity with both hands" and build its own recycling infrastructure. 

But here's the irony: while preaching recycling, millions of tons of household rubbish were simply burned last year – up 25% – turning family neighborhoods into pollution zones.

**The Bottom Line:** Britain's recycling game is so weak, even China – the world's biggest trash processor – is saying "thanks, but no thanks."

*Time to clean up our act, or drown in our own plastic mountain.*

The local authority manages about 2,200 bridges and culverts and over 1,000 walls on its 4,195-mile (6,752km) road network, which is Scotland's longest and has the most road structures.

It is spending £350,000 on improvements to four bridges: Struy, Achfary, Blackburn, and Coronation.

Chairman Allan Henderson noted the region's many vital bridges, especially in rural areas, and the constant need to prioritize limited resources for essential repairs. He announced plans to propose a significant investment program for roads and bridges to the council in December.

Why give a restaurant a five-star rating with "factory" in the name? Think about it like discovering an authentic taco truck that actually makes their tortillas from scratch – it's all about authenticity. Arepas might be new to you, but they're beloved comfort food in Venezuela and Colombia, kind of like how a perfect grilled cheese represents American comfort food. This restaurant uses traditional white cheese and authentic ingredients that make these corn cakes truly special.

Picture the ordering style of Chipotle or your favorite local sandwich shop – there are seats, but you'll order at the counter and grab your own napkins. Don't expect a waiter to take your order or refill your drinks. However, if you're tired of the same old lunch routine and want to expand your palate beyond typical fast-casual options, this is your chance to try something genuinely different that you probably can't find at the mall food court.

This appears to be a science fiction story about a protagonist who has entered into a transactional relationship with mysterious entities. The narrator is part of a program where they sell their hair, nails, and other keratin-based body parts to these beings who use advanced "biometallurgy" - a process that converts biological keratin into valuable metals, particularly gold.

The story reveals a dystopian gig economy scenario where 500 people like the narrator have become "donors" in this bizarre marketplace. The entities have sophisticated technology that can transform human keratin into gold, which they initially sold to establish their capital base. The narrator receives payments that vary based on market conditions - starting at over $30,000 but declining to about $10,000 as more donors join the program.

The silver dollar serves as a timing mechanism, indicating when the narrator's body will have regenerated enough material for the next "harvest." The process is clearly painful and dehumanizing - they cut nails down to bleeding cuticles, completely remove eyebrows, and take all hair growth from the past 84 days.

The narrator has adapted to this cycle, learning to function with dramatically shortened nails and planning their life around these periodic harvests. The casual "Thanks Obama" at the end suggests this takes place in a near-future America where such exploitation has become normalized.

The story explores themes of bodily autonomy, economic desperation, and the commodification of human biology in a world where advanced technology has created new forms of exploitation.

Upon discovering this establishment today with a colleague, its operational status was not apparent from a distance. It is highly recommended that "OPEN" signage be displayed prominently in the windows. Furthermore, the availability of a complimentary parking lot should be advertised to the public. The premises were notably clean and inviting. The decor, however, was somewhat stark and sterile, lacking in vibrant color, which inadvertently gives the impression of a fast-food establishment. Consequently, the quality of the food exceeded expectations. It was excellent, with the feta appetizer and panini being particularly noteworthy. The cocktails were not prepared precisely as requested; however, considering the modest price point of five dollars, this was a minor issue. Brandi, the bartender, was exceptionally pleasant and engaging. While not intending to be critical, I believe the staff's attire could be enhanced. This establishment is clearly not a casual 'shot and beer' venue, and professional uniforms would be a significant improvement. Overall, this establishment is highly recommended for its culinary offerings and welcoming atmosphere. It serves as an excellent happy hour destination, complemented by convenient, complimentary parking. We anticipate a return visit.

A groundbreaking study, just published in April in the Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, reveals a crucial discovery: simply recalling childhood memories dramatically compels people to behave more ethically.

Through a meticulous series of experiments, Harvard University's Francesca Gino and Sreedhari Desai have now definitively demonstrated that participants consistently exhibited significantly increased willingness to help experimenters with extra tasks, harshly condemned unethical behavior, and generously donated to charity—solely when actively remembering their childhood, a stark contrast to recalling their teenage years. Crucially, this profound effect persisted regardless of whether the memories were positive or negative, though it's important to note the subjects did not have traumatic histories.

The mechanism is clear: these powerful recollections unleash a heightened, almost visceral, sense of moral purity. While actual childhood behavior varies, the *concept* of childhood universally connotes innocence—a profound frame of mind that immediately and directly shapes adult behavior. As Gino emphatically states, this is "promising research in thinking about ways in which people are following their moral compass with very simple interventions," underscoring the immense potential. The immediate and transformative applications are staggering: simple interventions such as posting subway signs encouraging people to remember childhood or decorating workplaces with nostalgic items could fundamentally shift public behavior towards greater ethics.

We snapped up this espresso maker based on the glowing reviews it earned in several places. The first one died after 9 months - the pump failed. After some more searching we decided to gamble on buying the DeLonghi again because it brewed phenomenal espresso for the price. Now after 3 months this second unit has also failed. The heating unit burned out. Fortunately, I saved all the packing, etc. this time so I hope to secure a replacement or refund. Bottom line: phenomenal espresso, dismal longevity. If you purchase this machine, preserve all the packaging so you can return it if needed. It has a one year warranty. BTW I don't think we did anything to trigger the failure. We read the manual and followed the instructions exactly. If we did cause both problems then the machine is simply too fragile for routine use in a normal household.

Working the night shift can disrupt the body’s internal clock, leading to fatigue and sleep deprivation. However, research increasingly shows that shift work may cause additional health risks, including a higher likelihood of developing diabetes, obesity, and certain cancers.

A study published in the journal Occupational and Environmental Medicine by researchers in Denmark found that women who worked night shifts faced a significantly increased risk of breast cancer. The study followed 18,500 women who served in the Danish Army between 1964 and 1999. Of these, 141 women developed breast cancer by 2005-06. Their medical histories were compared to those of 551 women who did not develop the disease. All participants completed an extensive questionnaire about their work habits, circadian preferences (“morning” or “night” person), and other factors influencing breast cancer risk, such as contraceptive use, number of children, hormone replacement therapy, and sun exposure.

After controlling for these factors, the researchers found that night shift work was associated with a 40% increase in breast cancer risk. The risk grew with more frequent night shifts: women working at least three night shifts per week for six years had twice the breast cancer risk compared to those with one or two night shifts. Notably, women who identified as “morning” people and worked night shifts had a fourfold increased risk of breast cancer compared to women working only during the day.

Previous studies, particularly among nurses, suggested a similar link between night shift work and breast cancer, but could not fully account for confounding factors like increased exposure to radiation or chemicals. By studying military personnel, the Danish researchers aimed to minimize such confounders.

One hypothesis was that night workers might have less sunlight exposure, leading to lower vitamin D levels—a known risk factor for breast cancer. However, the study found night workers actually had more sun exposure, as they were free during the day. This suggests that the increased cancer risk is likely due to biological effects, such as disruption of the circadian rhythm and lowered melatonin production caused by nighttime exposure to artificial light. Low melatonin levels are often found in breast cancer patients, and melatonin is believed to influence hormones involved in breast cell growth.

Genetic factors may also be important, as whether someone is a “morning” or “night” person is partially genetic. The study’s finding that morning types are especially at risk aligns with the idea that their biological clocks are more disrupted by night work.

While night shift work may be unavoidable for some, the study suggests that working one or two night shifts per week is unlikely to significantly increase breast cancer risk for most women.

A day before the United States observes Martin Luther King Jr. Day, park rangers placed wreaths at the Washington memorial honoring the civil rights leader. Members of King's family stood beside the towering 30-foot statue of him as crowds sang "Happy Birthday" at a ceremony Sunday. The civil rights leader's son said his father would have turned 83 Sunday, if he had survived the 1968 shooting that killed him.

"We're celebrating the best of what we are, but also what we must become, knowing that we've not arrived there yet," Martin Luther King III said. On his last birthday, in January 1968, Martin Luther King Jr. was planning a "poor people's campaign" to bring together Americans from all walks of life to demand "decent jobs with decent pay," his son said. "He did not live to see that come to fruition, and so, 44 years later, we're still challenging our nation, particularly in light of the fact that there's so much poverty rampant in this nation," Martin Luther King III said.

Among the many visitors at the memorial was Bill Janocha, from Stamford, Connecticut, who had traveled to Washington with a Boy Scout troop. "He brought beautiful things to our country," he said about the elder King. "He ... was a monument ... so all the tributes that we can give him are certainly just."

The Martin Luther King Jr. memorial in Washington opened to the public last year. Sunday's ceremony came several days after officials confirmed that a controversial quote on one side of the monument would be corrected. The line currently reads: "I was a drum major for justice, peace and righteousness." The quote, which holds a prominent place among more than a dozen King's most notable lines at the site, sparked controversy last summer when poet Maya Angelou said it made the civil rights leader appear arrogant.

King's original words, from a 1968 sermon at Ebenezer Baptist Church in Atlanta, were: "If you want to say that I was a drum major, say that I was a drum major for justice. Say that I was a drum major for peace. I was a drum major for righteousness. And all of the other shallow things will not matter." Angelou said that leaving out the "if" changes the meaning.

Interior Secretary Ken Salazar has given the National Park Service 30 days to consult with the Martin Luther King Jr. National Memorial Project Foundation, members of the King family and others to decide on a more accurate version of the quote, an official at the Interior Department said last week. King's son said Sunday that he supported the move, because future generations may not know the context of the quote.

"The context is what I think people have questioned, and understanding and knowing who my father was, it could be confusing. For us today, no, but for generations yet unborn, they may not understand," he said. "But I'm sure that when this is done, that everyone who comes will understand who and what Martin Luther King Jr. meant in terms of what he said about himself."

Through my home security surveillance system, I documented a TruGreen technician accessing my property to perform fertilization services. Despite the backyard gate being secured, the technician only treated the front lawn area, leaving the backyard service incomplete. However, TruGreen proceeded to charge the full service fee of $75 for what amounted to approximately two minutes of partial work. Upon reviewing my account one week later, I discovered the charge had been increased to $150 for this inadequate service.

This experience represents fraudulent billing practices, as payment was collected for services not rendered. I have since advised my network of colleagues and neighbors who utilize TruGreen's services to terminate their contracts immediately. While TruGreen may have obtained $150 through deceptive practices in my case, they will ultimately suffer significantly greater financial losses as I continue to share this experience and recommend service cancellation to others in my community.

I strongly advise consumers to avoid TruGreen due to their unethical business practices and fraudulent billing procedures.

"Come the fuck on!" Effluvium silently screamed into the void, his vaporous exterior erupting in brilliant crimson waves that pulsed and writhed like liquid fire. Tendrils of his gaseous form lashed out in frustration, leaving shimmering trails of incandescent particles that slowly dissipated into the ship's recycled atmosphere. It had been three torturous days since he and his volume-less partner had plucked this peculiar meat monkey from the verdant, water-soaked rock orbiting Sol, and for seventy-two agonizing hours he had desperately attempted to coax coherent speech from the creature, yet it stubbornly persisted in frantically wiggling those grotesque, pink flesh flaps instead of communicating like any civilized gastro-entity such as himself.

Effluvium had exhausted every method in his considerable repertoire to encourage the creature's first articulate words—from honeyed praise that made his particles shimmer with golden warmth, to withering scorn that turned his essence a brooding purple-black, to flooding its sterile containment chamber with his most treasured delicacy, pure Argon gas that sparkled like microscopic diamonds as it swirled through the air. He had learned with painful swiftness that the meat monkey most decidedly did not appreciate that particular gesture, based on how it had clawed desperately at the transparent walls while its face shifted through an alarming spectrum of colors.

"What's troubling you now, Effluvium?" Vaporian's voice drifted lazily across the cramped quarters, his odorous consciousness seeping into Effluvium's mind like thick honey laced with the essence of rotting vegetation and stagnant swamp water, carrying undertones of profound, cosmic boredom that made Effluvium's particles vibrate with shared ennui.

"It has been an entire solar week since we captured this infuriating thing, and it hasn't uttered so much as a single, coherent peep!" Effluvium wailed, his gassy body contorting and writhing like a miniature storm system, wisps of his essence breaking away to form agitated spirals that danced frantically through the air before rejoining his main form.

"These primitive creatures require patience," Vaporian replied with maddening calm as he began to seep through the ventilation system, his formless mass gradually filling every corner and crevice of Effluvium's chamber until their bodies merged into a swirling, iridescent cloud that pulsed with dual consciousness. "Besides, didn't it take you three hundred complete solar rotations to master even the most basic principles of coherent speech?"

"Shut up, Vaporian. I'm just a slow learner," Effluvium muttered, his essence darkening to a sullen amber that flickered with embarrassed sparks.

Vaporian's consciousness rippled with amusement, sending waves of mirth through their combined form as he directed his attention toward the meat monkey's sterile prison. The creature hadn't stirred a single muscle fiber since Effluvium's disastrous attempt at nutritional generosity, and Vaporian found himself genuinely curious whether the small being was perhaps sulking in the manner of a petulant child, or if it simply possessed a dramatically extended sleep cycle compared to most sentient species they had encountered. The meat monkey had compressed itself into a tight, defensive ball in the furthest corner of the chamber's cold metal floor, its limbs tucked protectively against its torso, and had maintained this peculiar position for what felt like an eternity. Their previous attempts at vocal communication had already proven utterly futile, met only with those bizarre flesh-flap movements and incomprehensible sounds.

The vagrant possessed an unsettling magnetism that defied explanation. His promises of untold riches should have been nothing more than the desperate fantasies of a broken mind—I'd encountered enough of his kind to recognize the pattern. They always spoke with such fervent conviction, eyes blazing with certainty that their delusions were gospel truth.

Twenty years as a hospital administrator had taught me to diagnose from across a room. Alzheimer's had carved its cruel signature into this man's features, and I whispered a silent prayer that such a fate would never claim me. The admission process was routine—better the sterile safety of our ward than the merciless streets that had clearly been his home.

Yet something about him gnawed at the edges of my consciousness. His clothes were a patchwork testament to years of exposure, fabric faded and worn to threads. But it was how he spoke my name—with intimate familiarity, as if we'd shared coffee just yesterday—that sent ice through my veins. And those eyes... Christ, those eyes haunted me with their terrible familiarity.

My fingers brushed against something foreign in my pocket as I reached for my keys. The object was impossibly small, no thicker than a sliver of glass, yet its surface promised a full gigabyte of storage. The old man's parting gift, slipped to me with the deftness of a street magician.

"Clever bastard," I muttered, unable to suppress a grudging smile.

In the sanctuary of my car, I adjusted the rearview mirror—a ritual ingrained by my father's cautious wisdom. The reflection that stared back was a stranger wearing my face, aged beyond my years by the relentless grind of administrative hell. Deep lines etched themselves like battle scars around eyes blackened by chronic insomnia.

Those eyes. My breath caught in my throat.

Those impossibly familiar eyes.

Anna Murray Douglass is best known as the first wife of black abolitionist Frederick Douglass. Consequently, her life illustrates the challenges facing women who were married to famous men. Her parents, Mary and Bambarra Murray, were manumitted shortly before her birth, which resulted in her being born as a free black in rural Maryland. She grew up in Baltimore, which led to her meeting a ship caulker six years her junior, Frederick Washington Bailey. Although it is unclear how they met, Murray facilitated his second escape attempt by providing money for a train ticket and a sailor’s disguise. She followed him to New York City, consequently leading to their marriage by the prominent black minister, Rev. J.W.C. Pennington. They adopted the surname Douglass as a consequence of their move to a Quaker community in New Bedford, Massachusetts.
While Frederick began his climb as an abolitionist orator, Anna cared for their children, born between 1839 and 1849: Rosetta, Lewis, Frederick, Charles, and Annie. In 1847, they moved to Rochester, New York, which enabled Frederick to begin publishing his newspaper, the North Star. The gulf between Anna and Frederick widened over the years because she could barely read and write and was rarely a part of his activist life and growing circle of prominent white and black abolitionist colleagues. The death of their youngest child, Annie, in 1860 caused Anna’s health to steadily deteriorate. She died on August 4, 1882 at their home, Cedar Hill, across from Washington, D.C. Following her death, she was carried back to Rochester, New York, where she was buried in Mount Hope Cemetery.
Shirley J. Yee, Black Women Abolitionists: A Study in Activism,
1828-1860 (Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1992), and William
S. McFeeley, “Anna Murray Douglass,” in Darlene Clark Hine, ed., Black
Women in America: An Historical Encyclopedia, Vol. I (New York:
Carlson, 1993): 347-48.
University of Washington

As European consumers shift from bars to drinking at home, industry giants like Heineken are adapting. Beer sales in bars and restaurants—the Dutch brewer’s main outlets—have fallen sharply across the continent in recent years. As a result, Heineken saw a 4.6% drop in sales in 2010, down to €7.89 billion ($11.3 billion). Didier Debrosse, president of Heineken’s Western Europe division, is seeking ways to address changing consumer needs. He has implemented a strategy to better align Heineken with modern European demand, moving away from bars and restaurants as primary sales points. Heineken has formed partnerships and promotions with supermarkets such as France’s Carrefour, providing direct access to home consumers. Initial results are promising—sales at Carrefour stores were 3% higher than any other outlet last year. The focus is on products easier to consume at home, such as cans for the terrace or bottles with meals. The aim, Debrosse said, is “to cover all the consumer moments.” Still, he maintains a long-term view: “It is a journey. It takes time to build a brand.” CNN’s Eoghan Macguire contributed to this report.

Today, our guest, historian Rob Zaretsky, Coca-Colanizes France.
The University of Houston's College of Engineering
presents this series about the machines
that make our civilization run, and the people
whose ingenuity created them.
Ah, spring in Paris! Walking along the Seine, passers-by admire
France's greatest monument: Notre Dame Cathedral. They gaze at the sublime western fašade.
Framed by the gargoyles and Rose Window is the tableau of a monumental ... bottle.
Blazoned across it is our age's eleventh commandment: Drink Coca Cola.
Fiction? Of course. But in the early 1950s, France thought it would soon be fact. The
nation was then swept by the perfect storm. At odds were two nations: one recently
battered by war and occupation, yet clinging to its venerable civilizing mission. The
other recently triumphant in war and dedicated to its commercializing mission. And the
storm raged around that sweet, artificial drink that, more than any other product,
reflected American culture and society.
Created in the 1880s as a pseudo-medicinal drink, Coca-Cola met no actual human need.
As a result, it was promoted by massive ad campaigns: signs, special delivery trucks
and snappy slogans. As such, Coca-Cola can be rightly considered, as one of the company's
presidents declared, to be "the essence of capitalism." It was also the essence of
American advertising chutzpah.
Barricades threatened to appear in Paris when it became known that Coca-Cola requested
a license to bottle their drink there. Communists wondered if France would allow itself
to "Coca-Colanized." Conservatives were equally appalled. Is the company, they asked,
serving as a front for a spy ring? Winegrowers suggested that Coca-Cola was, if not a
poison, certainly addictive. Why else, they concluded, would the company encourage free
tasting at public events? With such paranoid logic a billboard on Notre Dame seemed reasonable.
When the French government refused to grant the license, America exploded. Coke's president,
James Farley, reminded the French that his drink
"was not injurious to the health of American soldiers who liberated France from the Nazis."
An American editorialist noted that Coke tasted of democracy. While vodka fuels revolution, he wrote
"it is utterly fantastic to imagine two men ordering ... Cokes ... to toast the downfall of their capitalist oppressors"
By 1952, the French government finally granted Coca-Cola its license. Yet, the French continued to
resist. In 1953, only a sixth of the French said that they liked Coca-Cola "a lot", while two thirds
did not like it at all. Even today, on a per capita ba-sis, fewer French drink Coca-Cola than any
other Western European country. This sustained resistance, as historian Richard Kuisel details in *Seducing the French: The Dilemma of Americanization*, underscored a profound cultural anxiety about Americanization.
The newspaper Le Monde explained the affair's symbolism: Coke heralded a new world threatening
to eclipse France. America was everything France was not: a mass, urban, consumer society indifferent
to the past and enthralled by the future. France has, of course, become more like us in many ways.
Yet the distance still to be crossed can be measured by -- the pause that refreshes.
I'm Rob Zartetsky, at the University of Houston,
where we're interested in the way inventive minds
Robert Zaretsky is professor of French history in the University of Houston Honors College,
and the Department of Modern and Classical Languages.
R. Kuisel, Seducing the French: The Dilemma of Americanization. (University of
California Press, Berkeley, 1993).
