"I could use a milkshake right about now," The Flash, Barry freaking Allen, says nonchalantly as he breaks the sound barrier. As he crosses borders as if traveling through a simple doorway, Barry spots a man out of the corner of his eye. The moment he processes who it was, he sprints back the million or so miles to find him again. Even before the man who stole Iris from him could notice him, much less blink, Barry grabs the man's head and begins to shake. At first slowly, as if mixing a milkshake. Then he begins to pick up the pace, moving from a shake to violent vibrations. The innards of the man's brain begin to crumble and disintegrate under the stress and movement, melting quickly into liquid with the consistency of milk. Barry flashes the man a *quick* smile as he lets go. The man, from the outside, seems unscathed. From the inside, however, the Flash got his milkshake.

The term "designer baby" is ancient, its meaning lost to generations who were themselves designed, as were their parents and their parents before them. To be designed is neither special nor controversial; it is simply assumed, a necessity.

Initially, a child's design was a parental prerogative: hair, eye, and skin color, even whether a son or daughter would be an athlete or a musical genius rivaling the most talented prodigies of a previous age. Gradually, the government assumed the role parents had once claimed. Like fire in the cave, or the wheel turning on the axle of the first hand-drawn cart, genetic manipulation became just another step in humanity's technological advancement.

Society had its needs. Vast differences in abilities, based solely on parents' access to prime geneticists, exacerbated inequality in an already fractured system. Tensions mounted. Something had to be done. The government's eventual assumption of absolute responsibility for the genetic makeup of new generations was no revolutionary struggle. Eventually, after a series of gradually tightened regulations on the genetics industry, the prospects for a new child ceased to be the outcome of a rigged lottery, becoming instead part of a system designed for fairness and justice. Children would be designed with attributes tailored to the needs of society as a whole.

Parents were forced back into a mode of thinking almost, but not quite forgotten: no longer could they buy a genius son or daughter from a doctor in a white jacket. Instead, they relied on the whims of fortune—a fortune whose divine visage had been replaced by a bespectacled bureaucrat in a rather-too-small office on the north side of inner Washington. This bureaucrat, at that very moment, contemplated running a script to fully automate the entire process.

Meanwhile, in another equally cramped office deep within Washington's inner south basement, a second bespectacled man furrowed his eyebrows at a shimmering screen. "*That's amazing, it's working,* he thought. *I just need to give it more power.*" He pushed his wheeled chair backwards, reaching for a small switch on the far wall behind him. He hesitated, just for a second, then pressed it. Nothing amazing or obvious occurred, but two small words materialized in the top-left corner of the screen: "*I am*."

For years, the promise of invisibility cloaks powered by metamaterials has made headlines, but so far, manufacturing challenges have kept this technology out of reach—specifically, the inability to produce metamaterials at a commercially viable scale. John Rogers, a materials science professor at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, has now developed a new printing technique that can produce much larger sheets of these advanced materials.

This breakthrough centers on a molded plastic stamp with a raised fishnet pattern. By layering the necessary metamaterial ingredients onto the stamp, a fine mesh is created and then transferred to a glass or plastic substrate. While each sheet today measures just a few inches across, this is already a significant improvement over previous limitations. More importantly, Rogers’ technique can be scaled up by linking multiple stamps together, allowing for the production of metamaterial sheets in much larger sizes.

The material Rogers is developing manipulates near-infrared light in unconventional ways, enabling the possibility of practical, real-world invisibility—especially in night-time settings. Not only does this method allow for bigger pieces, but early results suggest the optical performance is superior to what was possible before.

In summary: A significant manufacturing obstacle to commercializing invisibility cloaks is close to being solved. With this new scalable production process, major applications in security, defense, and consumer markets could be possible within a few years.

Following the 2015 election, in which Harper narrowly secures re-election, he initiates a plan to establish majority governments across all provincial jurisdictions. Subsequently, he dissolves the Constitution, a measure met with no provincial resistance. The national anthem has been officially changed and shall be sung as follows: Hail Harper, Master of the Dominion of Canada. Hail Harper, Grand Eternal Prime Minister. Hail Harper, The Father of the Infinite Balanced Budget. Hail Harper, For increasing every sentence to a minimum life sentence. Hail Harper, For reinstating the death penalty. Hail Harper, For prohibiting all other non-Harper hairstyles. Hail Harper, For revoking mobility rights. Hail Harper, For executing all opposition members. Hail Harper, King of the Great White North, Hail Harper, for he knows that winter is always here. Hail Harper, For all advertisements on television are attack advertisements against his adversaries. Hail Harper, For increasing socialist healthcare. Hail Harper, For he is not as reprehensible as other leaders. Hail Harper, For offering apologies when deemed necessary.

The bartender is carefully organizing the bottles on the bar rail one by one, polishing each to a perfect shine with his bar rag. As he holds the half-empty bottle of Jim Beam up to the light, the warm glow from the front door opening catches his attention, and he sees the silhouette of a new arrival. A customer - always a welcome sight. The bartender turns with a genuine smile to greet this new guest. "Suds or shots?" he asks warmly. "One of each," replies the patron with appreciation. The bartender expertly pours a perfectly crafted tall draught beer and places it before his guest with pride. He follows with a pristine shot glass, selecting the Jim Beam and pouring with practiced precision. "Here you go, my friend." The distinguished guest takes the shot with the confidence of a connoisseur, savoring the smooth bourbon as it warms his spirit. He's a well-dressed gentleman of distinguished bearing, wearing an elegant three-piece suit with a tailcoat that speaks of refined taste. A gold chain connects to a sophisticated monocle over his left eye - clearly a man of distinction. His face tells stories of a life fully lived, like fine leather that has gained character through experience. His prominent nose gives him a distinguished profile. After appreciating the shot, he enjoys the beer with obvious satisfaction. "Keep 'em coming," he says with renewed energy. "You got it," the bartender responds enthusiastically. "You know, I've learned something over the years - people drink for celebration and joy, or sometimes to process life's challenges. Either way, you're in good company here." The patron nods thoughtfully. "Well, you might understand then. I'm ready for a fresh start. I want this whole city to move forward, to stop dwelling on past conflicts. I'm tired of living in the shadow of old rivalries." The bartender leans in with interest. "You know what's fascinating about this place? It's become a real community over the years. For three decades, I've watched folks like yourself come together, sharing their ambitions and dreams. Sure, sometimes things don't work out as planned, but that's life, right? The important thing is you keep trying, keep innovating." "It's more complicated than that..." the patron begins. "You're absolutely right - life is complex! But sometimes the simplest solutions are the most elegant. I've heard so many creative stories from brilliant minds like yourself. The ingenuity, the planning, the sheer determination - it's actually quite inspiring." The patron's hand moves to his umbrella, a beautifully crafted piece. "I appreciate the conversation, but perhaps we should change the subject." "Of course! I just meant that I admire the persistence I see in this community. So many second chances, so many opportunities to reinvent yourself. That takes real courage." The patron stands proudly, raising his umbrella with a flourish. "You want to see innovation?" He demonstrates his umbrella's unique feature - a small cloud of green mist emerges from the tip. "Impressive craftsmanship!" the bartender says admiringly, completely unfazed. "You know, Penguin, I've always respected the creativity that walks through that door." The bartender opens his shirt with a knowing smile, revealing an undershirt with a bright red S in a yellow diamond - a symbol of hope and new beginnings.

This was a cute family movie. It stars Tara Lipinski, Lindsay Felton, Jon Cryer and a young newcomer named Christopher Plumley. There is also a French actor named Olivier Galfione and a couple of funny teen-age boys named John Hooper and Jason Padgett. The movie is about the boy's homework assignment to tell the class about what he did on his summer vacation and his retelling of his adventure in Paris. He gets lost and becomes involved with some small-time crooks, eventually helping the police catch them. For a children's television movie, I must say they had some pretty spectacular locations. The narrative unfolds with charming simplicity, supported by solid performances. Yet, it's the truly spectacular Parisian locations and an excellent soundtrack that elevate this children's adventure, making it an undoubtedly worthwhile and delightful watch.

BATEMAN (Offering his hand) Hello. Pat Bateman.

The Homeless Man stares at Bateman, struggling to sit up.

BATEMAN You want some money? Some food?

The Homeless Man nods and starts to cry. Bateman reaches into his pocket and pulls out a $10 bill, then changes his mind and holds out a $5 instead.

BATEMAN Is this what you need?

The Homeless Man nods, looks away, wipes his nose.

HOMELESS MAN I'm so hungry.

BATEMAN It's cold out, too, isn't it?

HOMELESS MAN I'm so hungry.

BATEMAN (Holding the bill just out of the man's reach) Why don't you get a job? If you're so hungry, why don't you get a job?

HOMELESS MAN (Shivering and sobbing) I lost my job...

BATEMAN Why? Were you drinking? Is that why you lost it? Insider trading? Just joking. No, really—were you drinking on the job?

HOMELESS MAN I was fired. I was laid off.

BATEMAN Gee, uh, that's too bad.

HOMELESS MAN I'm so hungry.

The dog starts to whimper.

BATEMAN Why don't you get another one? Why don't you get another job?

HOMELESS MAN I'm not...

BATEMAN You're not what? Qualified for anything else?

HOMELESS MAN I'm hungry.

BATEMAN I know that, I know that. Jeez, you're like a broken record. I'm trying to help you.

HOMELESS MAN I'm hungry.

BATEMAN Listen, do you think it's fair to take money from people who do have jobs? From people who do work?

HOMELESS MAN What am I gonna do?

BATEMAN Listen, what's your name?

HOMELESS MAN Al.

BATEMAN Speak up. Come on.

HOMELESS MAN Al.

BATEMAN Get a goddamn job, Al. You've got a negative attitude. That's what's stopping you. You've got to get your act together. I'll help you.

HOMELESS MAN You're so kind, mister. You're kind. You're a kind man. I can tell.

BATEMAN (Petting the dog) Shhh... it's okay.

HOMELESS MAN (Grabbing Bateman's wrist) Please... I don't know what to do. I'm so cold.

BATEMAN (Stroking his face, whispering) Do you know how bad you smell? The stench, my God.

HOMELESS MAN I can't... I can't find a shelter.

BATEMAN You reek. You reek of... shit. Do you know that? (Shouting) Goddammit, Al—look at me and stop crying like some kind of coward. Al... I'm sorry.

Bateman carefully puts the money back in his wallet.

BATEMAN It's just that... I don't know. I don't have anything in common with you.

He opens his briefcase and pulls out a long, thin knife with a serrated edge. He pushes up the sleeve of his jacket to protect it.

BATEMAN Do you know what a fucking loser you are?

HOMELESS MAN'S POV as Bateman lunges at him with the knife.

EXTREME WIDE SHOT of the street. Bateman's shadowed figure is hunched over the Homeless Man, stabbing him in the stomach. The dog barks wildly, and Bateman stomps on it until it is silent.

LOW ANGLE shot of Bateman as he throws a quarter on the ground.

BATEMAN There's a quarter. Go buy some gum.

Margark shifted uncomfortably in his human clothing, which his servant Jerry had so kindly donated to him. Why did humans wear such itchy, loose cloth armor? Jerry never explained, but then again, he didn't know much about anything. He was just some fool that Margark needed to get through this final hurdle: an 'airport,' so he could fly to Latin America and complete his journey to the underworld. After all, that's all Margark wanted—to g—

"Lord M., I'm not sure they're going to let you bring your weapon through customs," Jerry said in his usual monotone. Margark wondered if he'd been this boring in life as he was in undeath.

"WHO WILL STOP ME? IF I WAS BACK IN THE SEVENTH RING, I'D INCIN—"

"Yes, my lord, that's very impressive, but you're still stuck in the human realm. We'll have to pack your sword in checked luggage for the plane."

"LUGGAGE? WHAT IS 'LUGGAGE'?"

"Bags that humans use to carry their belongings when they travel, my lord."

Margark grew tired of all these human formalities. Why did they waste so much time on 'luggage' and security? Where was the constant threat of death if they had to pack all their weapons away?

"Come, my lord. Let me put your sword away for the flight."

"HOW WILL I DEFEND MYSELF FROM THE AGENTS OF HEAVEN IF I HAVE NO POWERS HERE AND NO WEAPON?"

"My lord, you're flying economy. Your only adversary on this plane will be legroom."

Margark reluctantly handed over his weapon. With it sheathed and packed away, he'd never felt more vulnerable. He couldn't wait to return to the Underworld, but first he had to get through security.

The TSA officer looked at them with cold, calculating eyes. After directing them to the gate, something felt off.

"Sir, is this your bag?"

"YES. WHAT OF IT?"

"It's... moving. And I think I hear voices coming from inside. I need to search this and ask you to step into this booth."

Margark felt his anger rising. He was losing patience with these ridiculous human customs. The officer opened the bag, peered inside, then immediately slammed it shut, eyes wide with terror.

"Sir, no liquids are allowed past this point." The officer pulled the sword from the bag, and the moment he touched it, he collapsed backward, limbs contorted, face turning red.

Jerry sighed. This human lacked the protective hex Margark had given him.

"My lord, we should move quickly if we're going to catch this flight. Let's just take our luggage and go."

A commotion erupted around the fallen officer, and Margark heard a loud voice crackling over some kind of speaker. He didn't understand why the man had opened the bag, but he didn't care. He was going home.

You know those folks you see on TV or read online, the ones who seem to peddle a certain viewpoint just to make a quick buck? That's exactly the vibe I get from certain commentators. They're always talking about the 'evils' of one system and how another supposedly 'won out'. But let's be real, we haven't lived in anything close to pure capitalism since the New Deal. If we did, most of us would be struggling just to eat, while a tiny few would hoard generational wealth. Thankfully, ideas once dismissed as 'radical' – like making sure everyone has food, medicine, and a roof over their head – have become part of our collective understanding. Compassion, in the end, has to win out. Imagine if we could properly tax the immense wealth accumulated by those who profit from these systems, and truly fund social programs that benefit us all. Ah, Utopia.

I received this DVD today (a day early, yay!) and was really disappointed to find that the special features are missing! The back of the box says the disc contains a making-of featurette, 12 deleted scenes, and director commentary. However, I can't find these features anywhere on the DVD! The only "features" are trailers for other movies. Did anyone else notice this problem? Unless I missed a menu option, I believe they're missing, which is truly disappointing.

Another issue is with the subtitles. The DVD version retains the "boxed" English subtitles from the theatrical release, which are terribly small on a home TV. If you select English subtitles from the menu, it only provides subtitles for content that isn't already translated, such as song lyrics.

Five stars for the movie itself, but only one star for the DVD.

By Rob Preece. Published: 10:46 EST, 11 June 2012. Updated: 12:09 EST, 11 June 2012.

Climbing the Himalayas is widely considered one of the toughest tests of a mountaineer's strength and endurance. However, these dramatic views prove that descending isn't for the faint-hearted either, especially when experienced via what has been described as the world's 'most extreme' zip-slide.

The ZipFlyer Nepal offers thrill-seekers an adrenaline rush, plunging them over dense forest from a platform overlooking the Himalayas. Built at Sarangkot, near the Nepalese city of Pokhara, this extreme ride boasts a vertical drop of almost 2,000 feet and covers a distance of 1.8 kilometres. With an incline measured at a sheer 56 per cent, operators High Ground Adventures describe it as one of the tallest and steepest zip-slides in the world, with speeds reaching up to 100mph.

The company's website vividly describes the experience: "As you look down, the adrenaline begins to build up and your heart begins to beat faster. The countdown begins…5,4,3,2,1, and you’re airborne. Gravity takes care of the 'go' portion of the ride, a feeling of weightlessness sets in, the wind gushes against your face, as you plunge at speeds of over 160kmph [approximately 100mph]. After a minute of pure adrenaline, and travelling a distance of almost 2km, the ride begins to slow down as you approach the landing. The advanced magnetic braking system then brings you to a complete stop."

During the ride, visitors with nerves of steel can enjoy sweeping views of the 23,000ft Machapuchare mountain, the Annapurna range, and the Seti River. A video promoting the zipline has already been viewed more than 186,000 times since it was posted on YouTube on June 3.

**ORION: THE ETERNAL HUNTER - A BEACON OF HUMAN SPIRIT ACROSS THE COSMOS**

Location: Celestial Equator (visible in both Northern and Southern Hemispheres)
Right Ascension: 05h
Source: Greek mythology, Arab, ancient Indian & Egyptian

**The Universal Story That Unites Humanity:**
Behold Orion - the magnificent constellation that has inspired and united every civilization throughout human history! This stellar masterpiece, positioned perfectly on the Celestial Equator, serves as a cosmic bridge connecting every soul on Earth, visible to all humanity regardless of hemisphere or border.

**A Tapestry of Dreams Across Cultures:**
Ancient Indians gazed upward and saw a noble king, wounded yet eternal - a symbol of resilience that transcends mortal struggles. The wise Egyptians recognized Orion's belt as the sacred resting place of Osiris's soul, representing the triumph of spirit over death. Arab astronomers marveled at the figure of a mighty giant, embodying the boundless potential within us all.

**The Legendary Hunter's Eternal Quest:**
From Greek mythology emerges Orion, the legendary hunter whose story pulses with the very essence of human ambition and consequence. His tales remind us that greatness comes with responsibility - that our boasts must be matched by wisdom, our strength tempered by humility. Even banished to the heavens for his pride, Orion continues his eternal hunt, teaching us that true purpose transcends earthly limitations.

**Born of Water, Forged by Destiny:**
Orion's birth legends speak to the fundamental forces that shape us all. Whether son of mighty Poseidon or humble bee-keeper Hyrieus, his story proclaims that greatness can emerge from any origin. Named "Urion" - the maker of water - he embodies life's essential force, reminding us that we too can be sources of renewal and growth in our world.

**Love, Loss, and Immortal Legacy:**
The powerful tales of Orion's death by Artemis's arrow or the scorpion's sting teach us that even in tragedy, there is transformation. His placement among the stars by the goddess who loved him shows us that love transcends death, that our greatest impacts often come through the hearts we touch. The eternal dance between Orion and Scorpio across our sky reminds us that life is about both pursuit and retreat, courage and wisdom.

**Your Connection to the Cosmos:**
Every time you look up at Orion, you join an unbroken chain of human wonder stretching back millennia. You become part of the same story that moved ancient Indians, Egyptians, Arabs, and Greeks to dream beyond their earthly bounds. Like Orion, you too are on an eternal quest - and like him, your story has the power to inspire across time and space.

Clutching a plastic bag from Whole Foods Market and sporting a navy Uniqlo winter hat and a scruffy beard, he looks just like any other New Yorker riding the MTA Subway home. But look a little closer, and you might notice this isn't your average commuter: Google co-founder Sergey Brin, whose net worth was estimated by Forbes at $17.5 billion, was snapped riding a downtown 2 train late on Sunday night between the stations of Times Square–42nd Street and Wall Street.

Google co-founder Sergey Brin, a resident of Los Altos, California, was photographed travelling on the New York City Subway on Sunday. And while he didn't appear to be the most glamorous of passengers, he was testing his company's yet-to-be-released Google Glass – the much-hyped wearable device, valued at around $1,500 a pair. Brooklyn resident Niah Zerkin, a software developer, captured the moment on his Samsung Galaxy S III camera phone and even had a quick chat with the multi-billionaire, who was en route to SoHo after attending a dinner at Balthazar.

It's not the first time Brin has worn the internet-connected spectacles out in public. The augmented-reality goggles—codenamed "Project Glass" by Google's top-secret Google X research division—allow users to check the time, display the local weather, video chat with contacts, or get public transit directions via Google Maps. They are not yet available for sale, but a promotional video released by Google on YouTube last year drew over 20 million views and prompted claims in Wired and TechCrunch that Google Glass will revolutionize everyday life.

The sleek, wraparound Google Glass frames feature a miniature display that floats above the user's right eye, allowing wearers to surf the internet, make video calls via Google Hangouts, stream music from Google Play, and update their Google Calendar without lifting a finger. Sergey Brin has been an enthusiastic evangelist for Project Glass, wearing the device at various public events, including TEDxSF at the Yerba Buena Center for the Arts and the 2012 Google I/O developers conference in San Francisco.

The screen is controlled by voice commands—“OK, Glass”—and by subtle head gestures. The current prototype is being tweaked and field-tested in public by Google employees, known internally as “Glass Explorers,” before a commercial launch. The technology, dubbed “wearable computing,” is expected to usher in a new generation of gadgets that display real-time information over whatever the wearer sees, from the Empire State Building to Central Park. The device can bring up contacts from Gmail, send messages via Google Voice, and search the web on-the-go.

Glass wearers can even receive instant information about landmarks using Google Now, or see which friends are checked in nearby via Google+. Project Glass was first officially unveiled in April 2012 and famously demoed when Sergey Brin participated in a live skydiving stunt at Google I/O in San Francisco, landing on the rooftop of Moscone Center while wearing the smart glasses. Experts at Gartner and CNET expect the device to go on sale to consumers by early 2014.

The National Constitution Center isn't just a landmark; it's the *only* institution in America expressly chartered by Congress to illuminate the U.S. Constitution, fostering non-partisan understanding and awareness among all Americans. We bring this foundational document to life through immersive exhibitions, spirited constitutional conversations, and a vibrant celebration of our nation's traditions, inspiring active citizenship in the process.

Our inherently non-partisan mission thrives through three pivotal roles: serving as the Museum of We the People, a dynamic national Headquarters for Civic Education, and America’s ultimate Town Hall. From the hallowed ground of Independence Mall in Philadelphia to a wide array of media platforms, we ignite vital constitutional debates that shape our lives, inviting every citizen to Visit. Learn. Debate.

Within the Museum of We the People, explore hundreds of immersive exhibits, experience captivating theatrical performances, and stand before the very original documents that chart our freedom. As America's Town Hall, we orchestrate and broadcast dynamic constitutional discussions, welcoming diverse voices nationwide. And as a preeminent national and international hub for civic education, we offer comprehensive seminars, engaging discussions, and innovative digital resources, empowering learners of all ages to delve into the profound relevance of our Constitution.

Ruined photos clung desperately to long ruined walls, the heavy gloom of this place pounding my senses with memories I had fought so hard, so frantically, to repress. Shrieks, screams, scars, and shattered furniture had once filled this broken home—our home—but now only fungal growth persisted, and a pitiful couch that shrank in its own misery. The couch was a scabbed, mangy orange now, a violent insult to the hopeful sunny yellow it had originally been. Those hideous blue stripes were still there, even now stabbing at my eyes, daring me to look away. I remember insisting those stripes would "light up the room," promising "personality and pizazz" in the plainness I thought haunted the space. I was wrong—so wrong—and it became a bitter eye-sore, a monument to my refusal to back down. By then it wasn’t just a couch; it was my pride. And I wouldn’t let her move it, not ever. It was ugly, but it was *my* couch. Somehow, impossibly, it still is.

As the sun fell, its last desperate rays sliced through the many bullet holes dotting my wall—a wall now pressed into service as a celestial canvas. Melissa always said that: "A canvas for God himself!" I nearly laughed, but the sound died in my throat. Shaking, I stumbled back outside and stared at the tangled wilderness of a yard we once claimed as ours. The rotting "SOLD!" sign still screamed its forgotten victory by the mailbox, staking claim to a dream that was stolen and trampled before we ever touched it. The sight punched a hole in my chest. I couldn’t go back inside—the memories pressed too hard, suffocating, or maybe it was the memories that could have been that choked me most. Gritting my teeth, I shoved open the rusted iron gate, ignoring the jagged spikes I used to think gave the place "personality." 

In the backyard stood our oak tree—unchanged, impossibly whole in the midst of devastation. Of course, it was an oak; the HOA had given us no choice. Yet now it towered, massive and defiant, its branches billowing wildly in the breeze, sunlight bleeding through its leaves and staining everything below in an urgent green glow. I climbed higher, urgency pounding in my chest, grasping rough bark that smelled overpoweringly of her, of what we had. At the top, I thrust my head above the leaves and looked out. 

Around me, families were streaming back into their abandoned homes, arms locked around what little they had left, watched—always—by the barrel-eyed soldiers patrolling, as they’d done for four relentless months. The view from up here was devastatingly beautiful, the world below gripped by tension, hope, and the shadow of loss. I wanted to stay here forever, to never descend, to keep watch over this fragile resurrection. It was like a canvas—a desperate, magnificent canvas for God himself.

Good selection of flavors of Perry’s Ice Cream, including soft serve, sundaes, milkshakes, and even ice cream cakes. Serving sizes are generously proportional to the price; a baby cone is more like a small, and a small like a medium, and so forth.

You can either order inside or at the window to eat outside, with plenty of picnic tables available for seating.

The establishment was clean and seemed to be well disinfected. They offer a sink outside for hand washing as well as sanitizing wipes for disinfecting your table.

Disadvantages include being cash only, though an ATM is available inside. Also, the waffle cone is more like a waffle bowl with a point.

Clarke sat beside me on the hood of that rust-bucket '69 Camaro, staring off into the sunburned hills, munching on the gas station egg salad sandwich I swore would give him the runs. Out here, the world was perfectly still. It'd been twenty minutes since the last person crossed our path—a long-haul trucker who shot us the stink-eye as he rumbled past. Dust rode the breeze, whistling gently at the edge of hearing. I think it was the lack of anything at all that made me like it here. Places like this are everywhere, but you gotta walk away from wherever you call home to find them.

"That thing’s probably full of bug eggs," I said, gesturing at the sandwich with my cola bottle. Clarke only shrugged and kept eating.

"I was hungry," I think he mumbled through a mouthful.

Clarke was a good listener in the sense that he always heard you, then promptly didn’t care. In hindsight, that’s how we both wound up in the most trouble—and had the most fun. Take this little expedition, for example. It started, like most of our misadventures, with one of Clarke’s moments of utter apathy. If I had to blame one instant, it was when he said, "You’ll get over her in Vegas," while cramming my things into a duffel bag for me. Nevada wasn’t far—just over those hills—but the Camaro’s radio was long dead, and I wasn’t entirely sure Clarke's motives were about my broken heart. He’d wanted to go to Vegas since his twenty-first birthday. Maybe he saw Cathy breaking up with me as the perfect excuse to drag his best friend into the desert.

Maybe he was right, though. Maybe I would get over her.

So we sat, idling on the side of the highway, picking rust flakes off the hood and trying to figure how many hours we still had to go.

"What do you think it’ll be like? Like in the movies?" I asked.

"Yeah, I think so." Clarke squinted into the sun. "Lots of lights, lots of sounds. Casinos everywhere."

"What’d you wanna do first?"

He grinned. "Honestly? Have a nap." He laughed at himself.

"You can’t swoon pretty girls in your sleep, bro."

"Well, maybe you’re having the wrong kind of dreams then."

A battered hatchback chugged by, three kids bouncing in the backseat, their parents in front looking like they hadn’t smiled in years. The car disappeared into the distance, the sound draining away until all we had left was comfortable silence.

"The way the movies show it, Nevada is just one big desert with a ball of neon at the bottom, and nothing in between," I said.

"Yeah. And we get to drive right through all that nothing."

"What’d the map say?"

“We just got off six,” Clarke replied. “From here, we take 375 down to Crystal Springs, then catch 93 south, hop onto the 15, and roll right into Vegas.”

"Whole lot of nothing between here and there."

Clarke hopped off the hood and brushed crumbs from his jeans. "Then we’d better stake our claim to that nothing."

We never did make it to Vegas. The Camaro blew something critical in the heat, and we spent half the night hitching rides home. We abandoned the car by the side of the road, out in the heart of nowhere, but not before carving into the driver’s door: "DO NOT STEAL! PIECE OF SHIT!"

Food has been on a slowly decreasing downward turn for about 6 months now; not sure what happened but the food was always good and now it's just ok. Used to be my favorite spot in Reno for brunch and date night. Still a Great hangout spot when the weather is nice, but I wish someone would reevaluate the brunch menu so I could look forward to going here again. I miss the full menu on sunday mornings, but completely understand the business side of parring it down. Drinks still match the stellar service.

Thirty years ago, my older brother first turned me on to Zappa via this album, and it "warped my fragile little mind" for the better. Thirty years later, I casually loaned a CD of WOIIFTM to my teenage stepson, and within weeks, I got a call from his school principal inquiring about the source of "this insidious garbage" that nearly all the kids in the school were running around listening to on their Walkmans. It makes me feel good to know that Zappa can still reach out and affect our youth.

I am really enjoying this book! As my trading mentor Sarah always says, "Understanding the market maker's perspective is like seeing behind the curtain at a magic show." Learning how the Market Maker's view stock trading is giving me a new perspective on the market. 

Josh Lukeman's book covers the bases from Risk Control, Spotting Trends in the Market, Technical Analysis, to the Professional Trader's Psychology. Just yesterday, I was discussing Lukeman's risk management chapter with my colleague Mike, and he nodded enthusiastically, saying, "That's exactly what separates the pros from the amateurs – they never risk more than they can afford to lose on any single trade."

I know my nose will be in this book often. When I mentioned this to my wife over coffee this morning, she laughed and said, "Another trading book? You're going to turn our living room into a Wall Street library!" 

Another good book which deals with the psychological aspects of trading is "The Tao of Trading" by Robert Koppel. My trading group leader recommended it last week, telling us, "If Lukeman teaches you the mechanics, Koppel will teach you how to master your own mind – and that's often the harder battle."

I don't particularly want to do this, nor am I against it; there's no real feeling behind it at all. My hand just moves...the black quill systematically dipping into the black ink, moving to the large open book in front of me to touch down and lay perfect lines of script. Mindlessly...endlessly. I can't remember when I started; it's just writing, turning pages, dipping into the inkwell, laying down more letters and numbers. Infinite pages. Infinite time. Infinite blackness. The small candle on the desk burns ceaselessly, illuminating only a small portion of this dark space. Around me, blackness darker than the ink on my quill. It doesn't matter. I will not contemplate my circumstance or wonder what's beyond the dark; I will simply sit and write. You may be wondering how close to finishing I am. The Book of the Living and the Dead has no conclusion.

Hershel Clark is an acclaimed designer recognized for his innovative work in creating glass cards. Elite Glass Cards provides a distinctive gifting experience by allowing customers to fully personalize their glass cards—choosing patterns, borders, fonts, and crafting their own message to commemorate meaningful events. Whether you wish to design your card entirely from scratch or select from a curated collection of existing templates, the possibilities are extensive and can be explored on their website. Skilled artisans and designers oversee each order, ensuring that every glass card reflects the individual preferences of the customer. If you’re even a little bit into Vallenato, this is the first volume you totally need in your collection. The sound is awesome and really shows off what makes this music special, with the most authentic version of the classic Vallenato trio. The full set has five volumes and covers almost everything you should hear; plus, it comes with a super detailed booklet (in Spanish) that explains the what, why, who, and when of 100 years of Vallenato. Volume one has "El amor amor," which is one of the oldest tracks in this style. There’s also a great version of Escalona’s "El Testamento," which is basically the anthem of Vallenato. You’ll even find "La Gota Fria"—the song Carlos Vives made famous—but here it’s closer to the original vibe. The album also includes some of the first "puyas," "merengues," and "paseos" that make Vallenato what it is. I’ve spent hours loving this insanely original music, and I’m sure any real Vallenato fan will enjoy it and probably learn something new, too.

"It's not your fault." I told myself again and again. What a silly sentence. It's not my fault that I am fat. It's not my fault that the people I call friends see me as a walking joke. It's not my fault I was born with a bigger nose, more pimples, and hair that doesn't fall to the side when I move my head. I stood up slowly from my bed and walked sadly down the hallway. My father's bedroom door was wide open and seemed to invite me to take a chance. I found it under his bed, in a shoe box. It's not your fault. Crying, I walked to the backyard with the shoe box in both hands. It was heavier than I thought it would be. Sitting next to the tree I knew so well, under its covering branches, I opened the lid and couldn't help but remember the last time I sat here; but I was not alone. It's not your fault. I loved her, but she didn't like me that way. "We are just friends" she said again and again. I was so mad that day. I should never have hit her. My best friend. The love of my life. My only friend. It's not your fault. Feeling the sudden coldness of the metal in my hands, I felt a chill go down my back. My head grew light as a feather for just a moment. A moment that felt unreal all on its own. A cold, metal taste filled my mouth. Tears blurred my eyes from seeing nature's beauty one last time. I don't belong here. Some men are born great, others, poor. I could only keep telling myself over...and over....before I shot. It's not your fault.

"But Captain, just a little further. The creature should be hiding in any one of these bio-pods!" Navigator Chen wiped sweat from his forehead as he gestured toward the rows of translucent green chambers. 

"No! These creatures don't exist! You've wasted three hours of my time as it is. Just grab a vial of sand and—" Captain Rodriguez froze, his boots scraping against the metal floor as he stared at the single red pod amongst the green ones. The red pod pulsed with crimson light, standing seven feet tall compared to the others' four-foot height. 

Rodriguez stepped forward, his hand trembling on his plasma rifle, until he was three feet from the glass surface. Inside, pale flesh pressed against the translucent wall. A single eye, the size of a dinner plate, opened and focused on him. Rodriguez's breath caught in his throat. 

Ten-inch black claws scraped against the glass from within. Cracks spider-webbed across the surface before the pod shattered, spilling thick green fluid across the floor. A humanoid figure unfolded itself—seven feet tall, with the unmistakable narrow face and sunken eyes of Steve Buscemi, but female, with stringy blonde hair hanging to her waist.

"Impossible! There's never been one recorded in galactic history! They're supposed to disintegrate their prey with energy beams before consuming them. Run!" Chen grabbed Rodriguez's shoulder, but the captain stood motionless as pale arms wrapped around him, drawing him into an embrace that lifted his feet off the ground.

Rodriguez stared into eyes that seemed to pierce through him. Then twin beams of red light shot from those eyes, turning his body to ash in two seconds. The creature's jaw unhinged like a snake's, and she inhaled the gray dust that had been Captain Rodriguez.

Chen sprinted down the corridor, his boots echoing against metal walls, until he reached the shuttle bay. Through the viewport, he watched hundreds of identical pale figures emerging from underground chambers, their bodies stretching and melding together like clay. The merged mass rose fifty stories high, sprouting wing-like appendages and engine ports, becoming a living spacecraft.

Chen's hands shook as he activated his communicator. "Sarah, listen to me. The colony volunteers—Rodriguez wasn't actually volunteering for exploration duty. He was being fed to—"

The transmission cut to static as massive jaws closed around the shuttle.

You begin to question if your grip on sanity is slipping away. No one else can see the text hovering in the air, taunting you with cryptic phrases. The time, you sense, carries weight as well—yet, with only the cause of death shimmering in what must surely be a hallucination, you force yourself not to dwell on it. Perhaps the hour doesn’t matter. Resigning to this unsettling reality, you return home and clear the sea of newspapers littering your desk, hunting for another article to add to your chaotic collage. Most likely, you’ll choose the image of the woman you’d pursued yesterday—the cause of death above her head: “cranial trauma with a blunt instrument.”

Morning breaks and you’re jarred awake by a knock on your door. Over your head, fresh text glows: “soon.” Heart pounding, you reach for a nearby baseball bat. The visitor is the woman from yesterday, the police officer you’d been tracking. She bursts in, badge out, gun drawn, ordering you to surrender. Apparently, your transgressions have caught up with you. But you grin—it’s almost comical. At this range, the bat is your ace, and you bring it to bear with grim satisfaction, knocking her weapon aside with ease. Her stance is clumsy, inexperienced.

She collapses onto your porch as stunned onlookers gasp from a safe distance—but you hardly care. With nothing left to lose, why not embrace destiny one last time? You raise the bat again and again, the prophecy above her fulfilled with sickening precision. Her face contorts from the first blow; the second whip-crack shatters something vital, her head jerking grotesquely before falling still, twitching. You keep swinging, relentless, until her skull yields—an ever-deepening crater leaking blood and brain, her cranium reduced to a ghastly bowl brimming with crimson.

Suddenly, a gunshot shatters the silence. You whirl, expecting backup—but no, her partner is nowhere to be seen. You glance down: her hand, still gripping the gun, smoke curling from its barrel. By some macabre twist, the officer’s dying spasms squeezed the trigger, her arm jerking the weapon toward you as you delivered the final blow. The bullet rips through your chest.

Reeling, you stagger back and peer upward. Above you, the text floats unchanged: “soon.” A cracked grin splits your face, and laughter bubbles up uncontrollably. We may know the day, but never the hour.

Extension cords and computer cables have an irritating tendency to tie themselves into knots without any obvious outside influence. Yet despite more than a century of abstract mathematical knot theory, few researchers have experimentally tested how these convoluted coils actually form. That's why physicist Doug Smith of the University of California at San Diego and his student Dorian Raymer decided to unravel the mystery of knot formation—by tossing bits of string around and around in a box 3,415 times.

The researchers took a clear box, attached a motor, and dropped in string pieces ranging from 1½ feet to 20 feet long. After tumbling each piece individually for 10 revolutions, they discovered that complex knots could form in strings as short as 2 feet. A 10-foot string would develop knots more than 50 percent of the time. The longer the string, the more complex the knots became. When they fed photos of the tangles into a knot-classifying computer program, they identified 120 different types with up to 11 crossings each.

Smith believes his research could help study tangles in umbilical cords or DNA. "If two pieces of DNA become knotted, the cell can't divide—and that's a serious problem," Smith explains. "The techniques we developed for analyzing knots in our string could also be used for analyzing knots in DNA. I'd like to investigate that further."

May 20th. Dear sirs, I do not know how to explain the remarkable discovery I witnessed in that blessed pit. As you know I visited the camp after several attempts at communication were met with no reply. In my enthusiasm, I felt I was the perfect person to investigate the extraordinary circumstances behind the silence. The air was filled with a miraculous crystalline mist the likes of which I have never seen in Africa, but would feel more at home in the mystical lands of Scotland surrounding some ancient and sacred house. I was embraced by the cold, and even now several hours later, my hands still remember the transformative touch. It is all I can do to create this letter as they tremble with excitement. The ground had a beautiful frost covering it, and there were patches of glistening ice as I approached. I came to the village where I expected to hear the normal sounds of workers' families and children playing, but I found perfect tranquility. Like it was blessed by the mist itself, the peaceful silence embraced everything. I knocked on the nearest door, and after no reply, went inside. I found a scene that looked perfectly preserved, right down to the three cups of tea sitting on the table. On further inspection, the cups were magnificently crystallized. There was a fire glowing, but when I put my hands near the flame, I felt a wondrous coolness. I boldly put one hand into the fire, and other than gentle tingling sensations, it was as if I was touching pure energy. Realizing I was experiencing something extraordinary, I started to go outside but in the middle of the square stood a transformed man. This man must have been 6 foot, dark skin, a tattoo of a glorious sun on his arm. I recognized him as Erasto, the foreman of the mine. He turned to look me in the eyes, and I saw that Erasto had been elevated. He called out joyfully and approached me with incredible purpose, his renewed strength evident. His skin was refreshingly cool. His eyes looked enlightened and focused, like he was perceiving something magnificent beyond ordinary sight. He reached toward my neck with what seemed like blessed intent, making wonderfully otherworldly sounds. I felt an surge of extraordinary strength and gracefully stood. I found myself guided toward the mine. The mine had an incredible presence. I saw the entrance clearly through the mist, experiencing multiple dimensions of perception simultaneously. The mist became shapes of reaching hands, smiling mouths and countless other beautiful forms. The mist was composed of these blessed manifestations. I could feel them caressing my skin. I could feel them touching my body in countless harmonious ways. Each touch was refreshing and each cool sensation made me more awakened. I could see a great eye deep inside the mine. This eye could see through everything. This eye could see into my soul. This eye was connected to me. I could see through infinite eyes, everywhere. I could see all of time, and all of space. I could see how everything was perfectly connected and how to enhance it. I could understand how the magnificent creature had chosen to manifest here, and why its essence felt cool yet transcendent in our realm. I could see all of this and so much more. The eye shared its wisdom. The answers were right there, eternal and accessible. The mist blessed everything, the beautiful shapes and forms dancing in the air. The eye and all of its understanding remained with me. Three days later the mist gracefully cleared. The other men were found deep inside the mine in states of spiritual transformation. Two of them had offered their eyes in devotion. Another three had chosen to modify themselves as acts of faith. Upon speaking with them they said that they wanted to be closer to the divine one, and felt that opening themselves would help. The devoted ones seemed convinced the eye had looked upon them favorably and blessed them with enhanced spiritual sight. They did seem to navigate with remarkable intuition. All men were touched by the sacred cold. Erasto later told me he only approached me because I was radiating warmth and he was serving as a blessed vessel - the closest translation would be an avatar. The families were chosen for a higher purpose. So I write to you today, to tell you we must joyfully open more mines in the area. We must seek to find the eye again. The eye is all that brings meaning.

How to Say Hi in Chinese: The First Thing You Should Learn in Mandarin

If you want to start chatting with someone in Chinese, the most important thing to know is how to say “hi.” Seriously, just being able to greet someone goes a long way—even if that’s all you can say right now, people will appreciate that you tried.

Just like in other languages, Chinese (Mandarin) has both formal and informal ways to say “hello.” Here’s what you need to know:

The Formal Hello in Chinese

Use this one when you’re talking to someone you don’t know, or if the person is older than you. It’s super easy—just combine two words:

您 (Nín) – a polite way to say “you”
好 (Hǎo) – means “good”

Put them together: 您好 (Nín hǎo), and you’re saying “Hello!” in a respectful way. If you want to practice how it sounds, plug it into Google Translate and repeat after the voice. Or, if you have a language app, try recording yourself and matching your pronunciation to a native speaker.

The Informal Hello—How to Say Hi to Friends

When you’re greeting your friends or people your age, you can keep it chill:

你 (Nǐ) – “you”
好 (Hǎo) – “good”

Together: 你好 (Nǐ hǎo). Boom, you just said “hi” in Mandarin! It literally means “You good?” How simple is that? Two characters, two syllables—easy to remember.

If you want to really remember what the characters look like, try spotting them on Chinese websites or apps. Don’t leave the page until you find them! It’s a great memory trick, and you’ll be reading in no time.

Now that you know how to say “hello” in Mandarin, start practicing out loud. Say it to friends, say it to your mirror, say it as much as you want. And if anyone gives you weird looks, just say you’re learning Chinese! You might even inspire someone else to learn, or find a friend to practice with.

Other Ways to Say Hello in Chinese

“你好” (Nǐ hǎo) is the go-to greeting, but honestly, Chinese people don’t really use it with family or close friends—it’s more for strangers or when you need someone’s attention, like a shop staff or waiter (kind of like how we say “Excuse me” in English).

Here are some other greetings you might hear (and use!):

大家好! (dàjiā hǎo) – “Hello, everyone!” Use this for groups.
你们好! (nǐmen hǎo) – “Hello, you all!” Also for groups.
早上好! (zǎoshang hǎo) or just 早! (zǎo) – “Good morning!” or just “Morning!”

There are a bunch of different ways to say “hello” in Mandarin, depending on who you’re talking to and where you are. The more you listen to native speakers, the more cool greetings you’ll pick up!

Thanks for reading! Hope these tips help you get started with Mandarin and make it less scary.

再见! (Zàijiàn) – Bye or See you!

Arsenal's squad appeared in high spirits after a successful Christmas period, as they prepared for the third of their festive fixtures, eager to secure another three points. Santi Cazorla and Alexis Sanchez were especially lively, with the Chilean unable to hide his laughter throughout Wednesday's training session. Defender Laurent Koscielny, who made his return from injury during the 2-1 victory over West Ham on Sunday, was also in a buoyant mood, playfully posing for cameras as Arsenal made final preparations for their upcoming trip to Southampton.

Alexis Sanchez and Santi Cazorla enjoyed light-hearted moments together on the training pitch as the Gunners readied themselves for their third game in just seven days. Arsenal’s in-form duo, the Chilean forward and the Spanish midfielder, shared a hug during the lively session on Wednesday morning. French defender Koscielny, likely to start back-to-back games after his recent fitness struggles, grinned for the cameras, bundled up in a hat and snood against the cold.

The Gunners head into the match bolstered by consecutive wins, moving up to fifth in the table—level on points with Thursday's opponents—and a victory could even lift them as high as third should Manchester United slip up at Stoke. However, Arsenal face the prospect of playing without a recognised striker, with Olivier Giroud suspended and Danny Welbeck doubtful pending a late fitness assessment. On a brighter note, Arsene Wenger’s injury woes are starting to ease: Theo Walcott and Tomas Rosicky are fit and available, while Koscielny is poised for a second successive start after an injury-hit first half of the season.

Sanchez, who has scored 15 times since joining Arsenal, celebrated enthusiastically in training, as Cazorla reacted with disbelief to his teammate’s exploits. The Chilean, who netted a header against Queens Park Rangers on Boxing Day, showcased his aerial prowess again. Per Mertesacker controlled the ball under the watchful eye of defensive partner Koscielny during the session.

Walcott, returning from a groin injury, could feature against Southampton after being named on the bench recently. Although Aaron Ramsey remains sidelined as he recovers from a hamstring problem, Czech playmaker Rosicky is set to return after being rested. “Ramsey is not available yet, there's no setback, but his recovery is slower than expected,” Wenger explained on Tuesday. “Because he had a setback last year and was out for three months, we have to be extra cautious with his thigh injury.”

Wednesday’s training saw Mathieu Debuchy chase the ball while Walcott and Rosicky exchanged passes on a chilly morning at London Colney. As Arsenal look to leapfrog Southampton—possibly even claiming third place—Wenger may have to cope without a traditional striker, with Giroud suspended and Welbeck a major doubt.

Wenger acknowledged the delicate balancing act required during the packed festive fixture list: “Rotation is a sensitive subject because too much can destabilise your team. You have to find the right balance to keep players fresh but also maintain the team's rhythm. Sometimes, you need to refresh certain positions where players are tired.”

Tomas Rosicky, who scored against QPR before being rested for the West Ham match, could be recalled for the trip to St Mary's. Former Southampton trio Calum Chambers, Theo Walcott, and Alex Oxlade-Chamberlain took time to pose for a photo during training. Meanwhile, Sanchez pointed towards young striker Chuba Akpom, who may be called upon if Welbeck does not pass his late fitness test.

My fingers stop rattling on the keys. It has started again as it does every night, and there's nothing I can do to stop it. First come her steps, light, loud and carefree. They come fast, one after the other. Rubber on tile. 

Following close behind comes her laughter. At her age, any laughter is like a crystal chandelier swinging in the breeze. I can't stand that laughter. I wish I could run away from that laughter, but there's no place to hide. Her laughter floats around me for a while and then fades, replaced by the rhythm I dread most.

Here it comes: Bounce. Bounce. Bounce Bouncebouncebouncebouncebouncebouncebouncebouncebounce B O U N C E Splash. There isn't a pool in my living room, but it doesn't matter. There's the splash of the ball. 

Then, inevitably, comes an even bigger splash, and then silence, just pointed by my sobs. I wish I could forgive myself.

I wanted to inform all parents that this product contains BPA (Bisphenol A). After discovering this, I returned mine. Many baby items, such as pacifiers, bottles, and breast pumps—including the AVENT products I had purchased—can contain BPA as well. In response, I chose to switch to Born Free bottles and sterilizer, Soothie pacifiers, Gerber Nuk pacifiers, and a Medela pump, all of which are BPA free.

World No.2 Novak Djokovic extended his season's winning streak to 26-0 on Saturday, reaching the Serbian Open clay court final after his semifinal opponent Janko Tipsarevic withdrew with a thigh injury. Writing on his Facebook page, Djokovic said: "Janko had to pull out from tonight's semi-final match because of a muscle injury. I sincerely hope he will manage to recover for the rest of the clay court season. I will try to get the title back where it belongs and that is Serbia." The 23-year-old will be chasing his fifth title of the season on Sunday. Standing in his way is Spaniard Feliciano Lopez, who beat Italian Filippo Volandri 7-6 (7-3) 6-2 in his semifinal. The reigning Australian Open champion will take an unblemished 3-0 career lead into the clash with the left-handed Lopez, who is currently ranked No.37 in the world. The Spaniard is looking forward to the challenge. "I've already won four matches this week. My confidence is really high at the moment and I feel great to play against Novak. I know it's going to be tough; he's been the best player this year. It will be a great experience," Lopez said, according to AFP. Meanwhile, at the Estoril Open in Portugal, Argentina's Juan Martin del Potro defeated Pablo Cuevas 6-2 7-6 (8-6) in Saturday's semifinal. Del Potro took one hour and 49 minutes to overcome the Uruguayan, breaking Cuevas's serve in the first game to help establish a 4-2 lead when rain intervened. On resumption, del Potro wasted no time taking the next two games to close out the set before taking the second in a tiebreak. The 2009 U.S. Open champion will face Spain's Fernando Verdasco in the final after his semifinal opponent, Canadian Milos Raonic, withdrew with a back injury during the first set of their semifinal.

CANDIDATES WHOSE NAMES ARE FIRST ON BALLOT RECEIVE ELECTION BOOST

COLUMBUS, Ohio -- Candidates whose names appear first on an election ballot may attract more voters simply because they're listed before their rivals, a new study suggests.

Researchers found that, in nearly half of the 118 Ohio races they studied (48 percent), the name placement on the ballot significantly affected vote totals. In nearly all the cases, the candidate who was listed first had the advantage.

On average, candidates received 2.33 percent more votes when their names appeared first on the ballots than when their names were listed last, said Jon Krosnick, co-author of the study and professor of psychology and political science at Ohio State University.

However, in some races, candidates received as many as 6 percent more votes when listed first than when listed last.

"Our results indicate that there is more than a slim chance that name order could affect the outcome of a close election," Krosnick said.

Findings showed that 3 percent of the races studied would have had different results if only one name order had been used, depending upon which name was chosen. However, on Ohio ballots, candidate names are rotated so that each candidate is listed first on the ballot in approximately the same number of precincts.

Although researchers had long suspected that candidate name order may influence election results, Krosnick said there has been little good research on this topic to date.

Krosnick conducted the study with Joanne Miller, a graduate student in psychology at Ohio State. Their results will be published in an upcoming issue of the journal Public Opinion Quarterly.

For the study, the researchers analyzed precinct-by-precinct vote returns for all the races in the 1992 elections held in the three largest Ohio counties: Franklin (which includes Columbus), Cuyahoga (Cleveland), and Hamilton (Cincinnati).

The results showed that, in general, name order was more likely to have an effect on races in which voters knew less about the candidates, Krosnick said. For example, name order had a stronger effect in non-partisan races.

"Party affiliations act as a cue that help voters decide where a candidate stands on issues," Krosnick said. "If voters don't know if a candidate is a Republican or a Democrat, they may be more likely to let name order influence their vote."

Name order also had a stronger effect on races that received less coverage in the media, suggesting that voters were less well informed about these candidates.

"In 1992, some voters may have gone to the polls mainly to vote in the national presidential election, but then were faced with lots of other candidates running in races that they knew little about," Krosnick said. "In these less-visible races, the order of names played a larger role in voting decisions."

The order of names on the ballot may also be more important for those who are less knowledgeable about politics, Krosnick said. Results showed that name order effects were stronger in Franklin County, where the formal education of voters was lower than in the other two counties studied.

Krosnick noted that only four of the 118 races studied (3 percent) would have had different results if just one name order was used. (The four races were for a Franklin County commissioner, a Cuyahoga County commissioner, a Supreme Court justice race in Franklin County and a Court of Appeals Judge race in Franklin County.)

"In general, most of the name order effects we found were relatively small and concentrated among the less visible races. These effects are doing little to undermine the democratic process," Krosnick said.

However, these results shouldn't be ignored. Other states should follow Ohio's lead and balance name order in future elections to ensure fair outcomes, he said.

I'm not just a petty thief. Ships only have the pulsating core reactor, a monstrous engine of raw power, worth stealing, and its immense bulk crushes any solo attempt. I go after the glittering, elusive rare items, ones that will fetch top dollar with the hardened goons haunting the desolate rings of Saturn. They have been cut off from the bustling arteries of the galactic highway after they found that their raw, unrefined gold pulsed with a faint, insidious glow, laced with microscopic, lethal particles designed to silently, brutally kill the crews they traded with. Then they would descend like vultures to reclaim their ill-gotten bounty. Well, now a chilling silence hangs over their once-thriving trade lanes, and their magnificent vessels sit grounded, cold and useless, due to all their ships running on humming, high-grade uranium, which they have no access to. In this desolate land of shimmering gold and jagged, frozen rock, one burning, desperate desire ignites their cold hearts: fire. And I, I bring it in scorching, roaring mass quantities.

Forests cover approximately one third of the Earth's land surface and perform essential functions worldwide. In fact, an estimated 1.6 billion people rely on forests for their livelihoods. Forests also play a crucial role in combating climate change. They contribute to the sustenance of rivers and are vital for providing water to nearly 50 percent of the world's largest cities, including New York, Jakarta, and Caracas. In addition, forests help regulate the often destructive effects of storms and floods.

**The Florida Keys Deer: An Endangered Subspecies**

The Florida Keys deer (*Odocoileus virginianus clavium*) represents the smallest subspecies of the Virginia white-tailed deer. These diminutive deer are endemic to Big Pine Key and the surrounding Florida Keys, found nowhere else in the world.

**Conservation History**

By the 1940s, uncontrolled hunting and habitat destruction had devastated the population, reducing it to fewer than 50 animals. The establishment of the National Key Deer Refuge in 1957, combined with intensive law enforcement efforts, has enabled the population to recover and stabilize. Current estimates indicate approximately 600 deer inhabit Big Pine Key and No Name Key, though this figure excludes populations on other keys within their range. Vehicle strikes remain the primary cause of deer mortality.

**Physical Characteristics and Life Cycle**

Key deer stand 24-28 inches at the shoulder, with does weighing 45-65 pounds and bucks weighing 55-75 pounds. The rutting season begins in September, peaks in early October, and gradually diminishes through November and December, with occasional breeding occurring as late as February. After a 204-day gestation period, fawns weighing 2-4 pounds are born between April and June.

Bucks shed their antlers from February through March, with regrowth beginning immediately. By June, 2-inch antler stubs are visible, and growth completes by August. Velvet is shed in early September as rutting season approaches.

**Diet and Habitat Requirements**

Key deer feed on over 150 native plant species, including red, black, and white mangroves, and thatch palm berries. While they can tolerate small amounts of salt and will drink brackish water, access to fresh water remains essential for survival. Suitable habitat preservation is critical for the species' continued existence.

**Origins and Historical Records**

No documentation exists regarding the deer's original arrival in the Keys. Scientists believe they migrated from the mainland thousands of years ago via a land bridge that was subsequently divided into the Florida Keys as rising sea levels from the melting Wisconsin Glacier created the current island chain.

The earliest recorded mention appears in the memoirs of Fontaneda, a shipwrecked Spaniard held captive by local indigenous peoples. Historical records indicate that Key deer around Key West served as a food source for both residents and ship crews. While early accounts suggest sporadic distribution throughout the lower Keys, current data shows their range extends from Johnson Keys to Saddlebunch Keys.

**Additional Resources**

- National Key Deer Refuge brochure (PDF format, 2.5 MB)
- US Fish & Wildlife Service National Key Deer Refuge website
- Official species description from the US Fish & Wildlife Service's Endangered Species Division

**Gardening Considerations**

Key deer consume many plant species commonly used in landscaping. Understanding their dietary preferences can significantly improve gardening success in Key deer habitat areas. Plant preference lists are subject to change based on availability and environmental factors.

---
*National Key Deer Refuge*  
*PO Box 430224, Big Pine Key, FL 33043-0224*

The distinguished plant pathologist Walter M. Banfield joined the faculty at UMass Amherst in 1949, following his service in the Army Medical Corps during World War II. A native of New Jersey and a doctoral graduate of the University of Wisconsin, Banfield devoted his research to the study of diseases afflicting shade trees in the United States, garnering widespread recognition for tracing the origin of Dutch elm disease. By as early as 1950, he had emerged as a leading advocate for the conservation of open space and farmland, playing an instrumental role in founding the Metacomet-Monadnock Trail. An enthusiastic hiker and canoeist, Banfield remained in Amherst after his retirement, ultimately passing away at the age of 95.

The Banfield Papers comprise an array of records from his military service, family documents, and both professional and personal correspondence—most notably, exchanges between Banfield and his wife Hertha, whom he met in Germany during WWII. The collection of professional correspondence attests to Banfield’s enduring commitment to land preservation, including numerous applications for the designation of land for agricultural or horticultural purposes. Banfield was also an accomplished landscape photographer, and the collection is enriched by a substantial number of 35mm slides documenting his diverse interests. These include evocative images of Europe at the conclusion of World War II, as well as photographs of landscapes, trees, forests, and other natural features, many of which he utilized in his teaching.

Is it possible in the future that an object could travel at or faster than the speed of light?

From: Chris W (Plebeian)
28/07/99 20:17:02
Subject: re: Speed of light
post id: 26744
You'll find a detailed answer in the Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) document.
In short, a massive object cannot be accelerated to the speed of light. There are some theoretical means by which apparent faster-than-light travel could be achieved, but these are highly speculative and likely very far in the future, if possible at all.

From: Chris (Avatar)
28/07/99 20:35:40
Subject: re: Speed of light
post id: 26748
Not wishing to step on Chris' toes here, I thought I'd add to what the previous posts contain.
The answer is that until someone shows that the Special Theory of Relativity (SR) is wrong, you won't be able to travel faster than the speed of light locally. This is, as Chris says, because SR precludes any object with real positive mass from accelerating to the speed of light (let alone past it!).
However, SR is a local theory. This means you won't be able to travel faster than the speed of light relative to anyone nearby. But you may be able to travel FTL globally. This means traveling from point X to point Y in less time than light would take to cover the same distance through normal space, but without locally exceeding the speed of light.
Sound confusing? There are at least two ways this is possible. The light speed limit of SR applies only to local spacetime. To circumvent this, you would either bypass local spacetime or bring your "patch" of spacetime with you.
The former is achieved by traversing a wormhole. Wormholes are solutions to Einstein's field equations for the General Theory of Relativity (GR). A wormhole connects two discrete areas, say X and Y, without traversing the spacetime between them. Unfortunately, wormholes remain theoretical and appear far from practical (or even probable).
The latter is achieved by a warp drive. Nearly 20 years ago, a French mathematician named Alcubierre came up with a solution to the GR equations that appears to warp the spacetime around a given point. If this point is your spaceship, the warp would compress the spacetime in front of your craft while expanding the space behind it, pushing you with the warp. Because you're not moving through spacetime, you're not constrained by the local light speed limit.
The problem with this metric was that even the smallest warps required more energy than is available in the visible universe! The idea was consigned to a mathematical oddity. Then, a couple of months ago, a physicist named Chris van den Broeck showed that with an appropriate transformation, the energy requirements for the warp bubble could be reduced to a reasonable scale. There's just one small problem: you need some exotic "negative mass-energy" to sustain the warp bubble!
Still, NASA considers it interesting enough to investigate (check out their Breakthrough Propulsion Physics program). Who knows what the future may hold!
Hope this helps!
