The photograph trembled in my hands like a guilty secret.

"Yeah mom, yeah. I got it." I rolled my eyes so hard they nearly fell out of my skull, grateful for the Atlantic Ocean stretched between us like a protective moat. My mother's voice buzzed through the phone like an angry wasp, dispensing her usual cocktail of worry and unsolicited advice.

"So ma! I got a question!" I wedged myself between her rapid-fire concerns. "Do you remember where we went on July Fourth, 1988?"

The silence that followed was so profound I could hear her mental gears grinding, shifting from nagging mode to archaeological dig. Then came the worry—because with my mother, everything eventually led to worry.

"There's nothing wrong, ma. Yes, everything's fine. Just—Can you just tell me what you remember?"

*The beach,* she said. *We went to the beach.*

I hung up before she could launch into her thesis on my hair choices.

The photograph stared back at me: two children, arms wrapped around each other like they'd discovered the secret to happiness, their faces pressed together so tightly their grins had merged into one brilliant expression of pure joy. Behind them, a sand castle stood like a miniature fortress against the endless summer sky.

I knew both faces. The boy was me at eight—all knobby knees and sun-bleached hair. The girl...

"Food's gonna be ready in a bit, sweetie."

My fiancée materialized beside me like a warm ghost, her arm sliding over my shoulders, lips brushing my cheek with the casual intimacy of someone who belonged there. I might as well have been carved from granite.

"Don't be so nervous," she laughed, the sound like wind chimes in a gentle breeze. "My parents will love you."

Her hazel eyes caught the light and scattered it like golden sparks. Even her nervousness was beautiful—the way she blushed and tucked herself closer, her hair carrying that impossible scent of ocean breeze even though we were miles from any shore.

"This is a nice photo," I said, my voice sounding like it belonged to someone else. "Is that you?"

She lifted her head, studying the image with the fond confusion of someone revisiting a half-forgotten dream. "Hah, yeah. This was so long ago, I barely remember it." Her smile turned mischievous. "Don't be jealous, but I think I had a crush on this boy."

"Oh yeah?" I leaned closer, pointing to the sandy-haired child. "That's me."

The color drained from her face, then flooded back like a tide. "No! No way!" But her eyes were already recognizing the truth, her smile transforming into something wild and uncontainable. "Oh my god, this is fate, isn't it! I have to show my parents—"

I caught her hand as she started to rise, my fingers closing around her wrist like a shackle.

"That girl in the picture." I slowly pulled the photograph from her fingertips. "That girl is my sister."

The words hung in the air between us like a blade.

"My sister is dead." I stared at the photo, wondering how I'd missed something so obvious, so impossible. "She got hit by a car two months after this was taken."

Her smile crumbled. For the first time since I'd known her, her eyes held no laughter, no love—only something dark and unfamiliar creeping in like fog.

"Who are you?"

Kurt Vonnegut's dystopian vision of the future is portrayed with chilling naturalism in this compelling adaptation. For twenty-five minutes, viewers witness a father's nightmare as he watches helplessly while his son performs his final act of personal freedom before his ultimate execution by the forces of state-imposed "normality." Vonnegut's story stands among the finest futuristic tales of the twentieth century, and this short film does the source material considerable justice, though the pivotal dance scene from the original story could have been captured more effectively on screen.

This adaptation merits serious consideration as both entertainment and educational resource. For literature educators particularly, it serves as an invaluable DVD resource for classroom discussion. While not a film designed for mere enjoyment, it functions powerfully as a cautionary tale that deserves to be cherished for its artistic and thematic significance. The film succeeds in translating Vonnegut's warning about conformity and authoritarianism into a visual medium that resonates with contemporary audiences, making abstract dystopian concepts tangible and immediate.

Lucas, who emerged as one of the original Young British Artists in the late 1980s alongside contemporaries like Damien Hirst and Tracey Emin, is known for her suggestive sculptures and photographs. The YBA movement gained international attention in the 1990s through provocative exhibitions like "Sensation" at the Royal Academy of Arts in 1997.

She will follow artists like Jeremy Deller (2013), Steve McQueen (2009), Tracey Emin (2007), Gilbert & George (2005) and Anish Kapoor (1990, 1982), who have all filled the British Pavilion at past editions of the prestigious event. The neo-classical British Pavilion, designed by Edwin Lutyens in 1909, is located in the Giardini area of Venice and is one of the most prominent national pavilions at the Biennale.

The Biennale is sometimes branded "the Olympics of contemporary art" due to its international scope, with over 80 countries participating, and its status as the world's oldest and most prestigious contemporary art exhibition, dating back to 1895.

The British Council has commissioned leading artists to represent the UK at the Biennale since 1938, with the aim of celebrating the best of British art. Notable past representatives include Henry Moore (1948), Francis Bacon (1954), David Hockney (1970), and more recently, Rachel Whiteread (1997) and Chris Ofili (2003).

By Will GompertzArts editor
Sarah Lucas makes crude art from crude materials.
Old tables, rusty buckets, fried eggs and raw chickens become visual metaphors of human body parts. It's not subtle. But then, it's not meant to be. Her art is physical, not cerebral.
It's 'in yer face' art - laddish, cocky and confrontational, frank and fearless. It's bawdy and ballsy - quite literally: male reproductive organs feature large in her work.
There is more though, to this onetime rebellious Young British Artist - now a fifty-something establishment favourite - than filthy jokes and dirty mattresses.
Her work is full of ambiguities and contradictions, awkward pairings and headless figures. It's vital and critical. It's about how we see ourselves and let ourselves be seen. It's about life and death, art and illusion.
Sarah Lucas might not be to everyone's taste, but she is a quintessentially British artist.
Lucas has become known for transforming ordinary objects like tights, vegetables, mattresses and fried eggs into evocative sculptures that resemble human body parts. Her breakthrough work "Two Fried Eggs and a Kebab" (1992) established her reputation for using everyday items as metaphors for sexuality and gender. Other notable works include her "Bunny" series featuring tights stuffed with newspaper and fluff, and "Au Naturel" (1994), a mattress installation that caused controversy when displayed at the Saatchi Gallery.

The British Council's director of visual arts Andrea Rose described the artist as "a formidably inventive sculptor".
"To prick convention could be a term coined for Lucas' work," she said. "It's impolite. Like zest in the artworld mix, her work will bring wit and savour to the Biennale."
Gregor Muir, executive director of the Institute of Contemporary Arts in London, who was on the selection panel, said Lucas had "affirmed her status as a leading international artist" in recent years. Lucas has had major solo exhibitions at Tate Britain (2013), the Hammer Museum in Los Angeles (2014), and her work is held in collections including the Museum of Modern Art in New York and the Tate Modern.

"Having consistently pushed the limits of her practice, there's a sense that Lucas - seemingly more active than ever - is coming into her own.
"Initially entering the fray as a Young British Artist, Lucas continues to make her presence known through a powerful and progressive relationship with her work. Her output remains urgent while being rooted in a rich artistic tradition, to include feminism and surrealism."
Dozens of other countries send representatives to the biannual showcase. The 56th Venice Biennale will run from May to November 2015, with the main exhibition curated by Okwui Enwezor under the title "All the World's Futures." This edition will feature pavilions from 89 countries, making it one of the largest in the event's 120-year history.

Making History: Addressing Viral Hepatitis Inequities in the African American Community  
By J. Nadine Gracia, M.D., M.S.C.E.  
February 10, 2012  

As we commemorate African American History Month in February, we invite you to partner with us in our efforts to end the longstanding and disproportionate burden of viral hepatitis in the African American community. Our Administration is dedicated to tackling and removing health disparities, striving toward true health equity.

Regrettably, viral hepatitis remains a health issue that often escapes the attention of both the public and healthcare professionals. Despite this lack of awareness, viral hepatitis stands among the top infectious disease killers, causing between 12,000 and 15,000 American deaths each year. An estimated 5.3 million people in the United States are living with viral hepatitis, with the majority unaware of their infection. This lack of knowledge puts them at heightened risk for serious, potentially fatal health complications, and increases the chances of passing the virus to others.

Understanding Hepatitis  
Hepatitis refers to inflammation of the liver, and it is mostly caused by viral infections. In the United States, hepatitis A, B, and C are the most common viral types. Each can cause acute, or short-term, illness, but hepatitis B and C – also known as HBV and HCV – can also result in chronic infections that may last for years or even a lifetime. Chronic viral hepatitis can progress to cirrhosis, liver failure, and liver cancer. In fact, viral hepatitis is the primary cause of liver cancer and the leading reason people need liver transplants.

Viral Hepatitis Disparities  
The African American community continues to experience notable health inequities linked to hepatitis. For instance:

Viral Hepatitis Action Plan  
Together with my colleagues across federal agencies, I am dedicated to preventing new hepatitis infections, ensuring that people who are already infected get tested, are informed about their diagnosis, and have access to counseling, medical care, and treatment. In fact, last year, we released the report "Combating the Silent Epidemic of Viral Hepatitis: Action Plan for the Prevention, Care & Treatment of Viral Hepatitis" (PDF 672KB), presenting a comprehensive strategy now being implemented across government agencies to expand viral hepatitis awareness and education among healthcare providers and communities and to improve access to high-quality prevention, care, and treatment services. (Learn more about the Action Plan.)

Additionally, the Viral Hepatitis Action Plan both builds upon and enhances other ongoing initiatives within the Department of Health and Human Services and throughout the federal government, such as the:

Your Involvement Matters  
All these efforts together make up our collective response to the often unrecognized epidemic of viral hepatitis. However, your participation is also vital. During African American History Month, we encourage you to learn more about viral hepatitis, share information with family and friends about this often undetected threat to health in the African American community, and talk with healthcare providers about getting vaccinated for hepatitis A and B, as well as screening for hepatitis C if you may have been exposed.

By working together, we can relegate viral hepatitis to history.

J. Nadine Gracia, M.D., M.S.C.E., serves as the acting director of the Office of Minority Health at the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services.

From 3D sets, to televisions with curved screens - it may seem as if there is already a TV to suit any living room. But Google is reportedly working on screens that fit seamlessly together - potentially making the days of having to pick the perfect sized screen to fit a given space a thing of the past.

Engineers at the Google X lab, in California, which is also responsible for the company's self-driving car, are working on the new screens, The Wall Street Journal reported. It is reportedly being led by Mary Lou Jepsen, co-founder of One Laptop Per Child.

The search giant said the screens would fit seamlessly together in a similar way to Lego bricks, and won't have a surrounding bezel, like current smartphones and tablets. Google is reportedly working on screens that can fit seamlessly together to create a larger ones. Its modular system would be different to video walls (illustrated) which show different images on different screens with spaces between them.

It is rumoured that the screens could be put together to form different shapes, enabling users to watch films on a large widescreen, before reassembling the screen blocks to view a document in portrait format, or simply choosing to use one of them as a smaller screen.

The project is at an early stage as the experts at Google try to work out how to makes screens without bezels that can connect together seamlessly, and work as one large screen.

It appears that Google believes the future is likely to be modular, because the company is already working on Project Ara smartphones, where components clip together. They are set to go on sale in January with prices starting from just $50 (£31).

The idea is that the Ara phones will be able run on multiple batteries, so that when one battery dies, it can be detached and replaced with a full battery module. Lower-resolution cameras can be swapped with higher-res versions, and users will be also be able to 3D print replacement parts. By printing their own parts, users will also be able to customise them.

Each phone will have a central 'spine' and an endoskeleton - nicknamed 'endo' - made of ribs that the individual modules will clip on to. There will be three different sized endos - including mini, medium and large - to rival the existing range of phones currently on the market, from compacts to phablets. Larger phones will be able to accommodate either larger, or additional modules, than the mini will.

Google's kit describes various modules including batteries, Wi-Fi and Bluetooth chips, cameras, speakers and more. In theory, any sensor that can be fitted to a module will be able to attach to a module and while Google will make the shell, these modules will be made by other companies.

Death had a headache. A deep, throbbing pain behind his temples—worse than a hangover. And when Death thought hangover, He meant the kind involving a noose. He massaged his skull with bony fingers.

LET'S START AGAIN.

Death always spoke in capital letters, never with quotation marks. You didn’t really hear Him; you just felt His words reverberate inside your mind.

YOU HAVE JUST BEEN RUN OVER BY A BUS.

"After I was shot in the chest," added the strange man, "and before I drank the cyanide."

SO YOU MUST BE DEAD.

Death glanced for reassurance at the distinctly deceased rat—still licking the empty bottle it had drained moments before—and the thoroughly departed gunman, bleeding out from the police shootout. This man, however, was strange. He was clearly not dead, but standing there in a way you wouldn’t expect from someone who ought to be lying down. In fact, he seemed to be in several places at once.

"Yes," the man said agreeably. "I should think so."

COULD YOU AT LEAST ACT DEAD?

"I'm not sure how. You see, I haven't been dead before."

It was a logical argument, and yet Death struggled with it. Every other first-timer He’d encountered had played the part perfectly.

START BY STOPPING BREATHING, offered Death, helpfully. THAT’S USUALLY HOW IT'S DONE.

The strange man’s cheeks slowly turned red before he gasped, quite involuntarily.

"It's no good, I've tried everything!"

EVERYTHING? Death’s curiosity was piqued.

"Well, I tried drowning by steering the Titanic into an iceberg, but I got rescued. I tricked Japan into attacking Pearl Harbor so I could get bombed—by the atomic one, you know. Twice. I dressed in women’s clothing and told everyone in Salem I was a witch. I—"

YOU MEAN, Death interrupted, THIS HAS BEEN GOING ON FOR SOME TIME?

"Only a couple of thousand years," the strange man said, strangely.

JESUS FUCKING CHRIST, Death swore. YOU ARE FUCKING IMMORTAL.

"No I’m not!" protested the man.

YES YOU ARE! IT’S OBVIOUS!

"That's not what I meant," the man said, more hurt than offended. "I'm not Jesus Fucking Christ. Although I did meet him once. Back when I had leprosy. I was earning a good living then, a real professional. I had pride in my job—begging. Then he came along and cured me. Well, that was it. I was out of a job. Completely broke. Life’s been pretty miserable ever since."

I immediately observed the version of myself I would have preferred to be. He was taller and leaner, and he did not possess that stubborn baby tooth that had never fallen out. His hair appeared healthier, and for a brief moment, I even experienced a sense of physical attraction toward him. There were other versions, of course, some endowed with strengths and skills I could never hope to attain. 

I engaged in a lengthy conversation with a version of myself who had become a published author of exquisitely crafted short fiction. As he recounted some of the stories he had written, I felt a profound grief settle in my chest—a sorrow for all the remarkable characters I had created only to abandon. They had perished from neglect, starved in the recesses of my mind: conceived with hope, and then utterly forgotten.

There was also a version of me who had indulged in the fleeting pleasures of narcotics. His teeth were gone, his hair matted, his skin covered in scabs, and the few teeth remaining in his mouth were blackened with rot. He was the only one seated among us, wasting away. One of us had shown him pity, recognizing, of course, that each of us could have become him. In essence, we all were him.

It was then that I realized I could become whichever version of myself I chose. The version typing these words is the one who has taken the middle path; the one who shrank from difficulty, fearful of failure and ridicule. Yet even the version who had squandered his youth and talent on drugs, alcohol, and countless unnamed vices—he was not afraid.

I returned through the portal and found myself once more in the safety of my own bathroom. I was restored to my own existence, newly determined to pursue whatever I truly desired, unencumbered by fear or shame. Having confronted the worst manifestations of myself, I discovered within me a measure of strength. No doubt, somewhere, there exist even more wretched versions of myself. I am far from the best, but I am not the worst.

For that, I am deeply sorry. Words matter, and I recognize their power. Going forward, I will choose mine with greater care.

Rick, the young mage, slouched in his seat near the back of the cramped spellcasting classroom, trying—and failing—to focus on the chalkboard. Around him, the air shimmered faintly with the collective auras of two dozen students, but it was his own messy energy that clawed hardest at his attention. His aura was acting up again, prickling along his arms, crawling hot and sticky up his spine, making his skin itch under his uniform robes as if he’d rolled in magical poison ivy. It was supposed to be normal, this tingly squirm of magic during adolescence, but knowing that didn’t help; it still felt like a swarm of invisible insects wriggling just under his skin.

“Rick… Rick!” The professor’s voice cracked like a spell misfire, yanking at his wandering mind. Rick blinked, only half-aware of the classroom: dusty sunlight on old wood desks, the smell of burned herbs drifting in from the alchemy lab, scribbled runes faintly glowing on the walls. But his attention was completely snagged on something far more interesting—a ribbon of bright pink energy that danced around Jessica in the next row. Her aura glimmered vibrantly, undulating in delicate wisps whenever she laughed, and it was all Rick could see.

He didn’t even realize the professor had asked a question until the room grew silent. “Um, sorry, what?” Rick said, snapping back into reality.

The professor’s eyebrows sharpened. “When the ambient mana is approaching 14 cuils, what is the proper procedure for discharging it?”

*My god, I would love to discharge some of Jessica’s cuils,* Rick thought, watching a spark of magic spiral off her shoulder.

He groped for an answer, half-dazed. “Um… To apply the Merlin-procedure number 24?”

“Wrong!” snapped the professor, with a look of weary disappointment, and turned back to the blackboard. He dutifully explained the correct technique—something about grounding excess energy through drawn runes and a reverse-circuit chant—but Rick had already spaced out, battling another flare-up of itchy magic just beneath his ribcage. Every time he poked at the swollen, tingly spot in his mana flow, it only seemed to swell and squirm more, sending a dull throb through his chest. So embarrassing. He sunk lower in his seat, wishing—just once—for a normal, itch-free day.

BREAKTHROUGH: Revolutionary Virus Treatment Could Finally Defeat Acne Crisis

**URGENT MEDICAL BREAKTHROUGH**—Scientists at UCLA and University of Pittsburgh have discovered a game-changing weapon against acne that could transform millions of lives: naturally occurring viruses that specifically target and destroy acne-causing bacteria.

Published in the prestigious mBio journal, this groundbreaking research offers hope for the 90% of Americans suffering from a condition that has resisted effective treatment for decades.

"This discovery could revolutionize acne treatment," declared Dr. Robert Modlin, chief of dermatology at UCLA's David Geffen School of Medicine. "We're facing a critical treatment crisis—current therapies are failing while acne continues to devastate lives both physically and emotionally."

The researchers identified P. acnes phages—viruses that naturally inhabit human skin and are programmed to seek out and eliminate Propionibacterium acnes, the primary bacterial culprit behind acne breakouts. These viral assassins are completely harmless to humans but lethal to acne bacteria.

**THE TREATMENT CRISIS IS REAL:** Dermatologists are running out of options. Antibiotics like tetracycline are failing due to widespread bacterial resistance. Accutane, while effective, carries dangerous side effects that limit its use. The medical community hasn't developed new acne treatments in decades.

"We're witnessing a perfect storm—antibiotic resistance is skyrocketing while acne continues to disfigure patients and destroy self-esteem," warned Dr. Jenny Kim, director of UCLA's Clinic for Acne, Rosacea and Aesthetics. "This viral therapy could be the breakthrough we desperately need."

The research team's genomic analysis revealed these viruses possess ideal characteristics for therapeutic development: precision targeting, broad killing ability, and remarkable genetic stability—making them perfect candidates for immediate clinical development.

**IMMEDIATE NEXT STEPS:** Researchers are fast-tracking isolation of the virus's active proteins for laboratory testing, with human trials potentially on the horizon. Time is critical—millions continue to suffer while safer, more effective treatments remain elusive.

This discovery represents the most promising advancement in acne treatment in decades, offering genuine hope for a condition that affects nearly every American and has remained stubbornly resistant to modern medicine.

There have always been people predicting the apocolypse, the end is nigh and all that. But I am the first person in human history who absolutely *knows* we are all doomed. The when and why and how. Funny sometimes, the privelages we are granted. 

When you grow up shipboard you get used to eternity just outside your window. We have no trouble putting humanity into perspective with the vastness we are confronted with every wake-shift. But somehow I can't help but feel the universe will feel unbearably empty once we're gone. Blink and you miss us. 

You'd think, in the 400 years we've been out here we'd have noticed a problem with the gardens before. I mean we could have missed something in the mad scramble to leave the Earth, but you'd think with the silence we've had since then we would have had time to notice the rot setting in. We didn't start the sterilisation of biopods 3 and 4 in time. All 12 had been compromised before we figured that out. 

I am the last lab awake, tasked with analysing the the scant samples we managed to salvage before pod 12 was finally labeled a biohazard. I think the others in the team went back to sleep so they didn't have to be where I am now. My last little seedling died this morning. We have no more oxygen gardens. 

None of them know yet. I'll have to tell the others soon. Right now though, I'm recording this for...well, I don't know. I suppose you could be some other spacefaring race, similar enough to humankind to be able to decipher our technology, our language and finally this message. You could be another human being. I doubt that. We've heard nothing but our own echoes for the past 200 years, whatever's back there on Earth we're the least of their concern right now. 

I think...I think I'm writing this to the planet we will never see. I mean, I was never going to see it to being with, but I always felt I was. We all did. Those of you who were going to step onto a new world, the first humans with ground beneath their feet in generations, you would have stepped out with us behind you. The ranks of us who got you there. Except we didn't. *I* didn't. We're humanity's last little light in the darkness and we're about to go out soon. Quietly, without any fuss. But I hope, if the future we will never have exists out there in some form somewhere, that you felt the journey was worth it. Enjoy the sunshine, and the breeze. Enjoy rain. And above all, enjoy the first bud of green through barren soil. Good luck to you all.

Typically, optical sensing techniques such as interferometric sensing or beam deflection are used. In recent years, we at IBM have been exploring two non-optical sensing schemes that have several advantages compared to the state-of-the-art.

One such sensing scheme is “electro-thermal” sensing, which was first conceived in the context of the scanning-probe-based data storage concept pursued at IBM [2003-1]. Electro-thermal sensing is based on heating and cooling of a micro-heater integrated into the body of a silicon cantilever while the cantilever moves closer or further away from the sample (Fig. 1). The motion of the cantilever results in a change in temperature of the micro-heater, which in turn translates into a change in the electrical resistance, which is then measured [2011-4, 2010-4, 2009-7, 2008-3].  
Figure 1. Electro-thermal sensing and modeling of sensing characteristics.

We are also pursuing another scheme based on the use of magneto-resistance for deflection sensing. With this sensing scheme, the cantilever deflection signal is translated into a change in the magnetic field as seen by a magneto-resistance element (Fig. 2). Micro-magnetic simulations reveal that such a sensing scheme can achieve resolutions comparable to optical sensing techniques over MHz bandwidth [2011-11].  
Figure 2. Magneto-resistance-based cantilever deflection sensing.

The owner deserves a lot of credit—not only has she survived in a small market, she’s actually thrived and even expanded into other areas. The Crandon store has a certain quirky charm, brimming with all sorts of unique and eye-catching products that make each visit a little adventure. I especially appreciated how the layout guides you through every nook and cranny, so you never feel like you’re missing out on a hidden treasure. Still, I have to admit, the cozy (read: tight) spaces paired with a few maskless shoppers from Illinois took away some of the magic, especially given the current pandemic situation.

By . Steve Robson . PUBLISHED: . 13:59 EST, 20 August 2013 . | . UPDATED: . 14:21 EST, 20 August 2013 . A police officer is receiving treatment in hospital after being **doused** in the face with a noxious substance, believed to be ammonia. The attack **unfolded** after the male officer, **clad** in uniform, **intercepted** a man on a silver moped on York Place in Battersea, south London, at around 3pm this afternoon. The suspect then **unleashed** the substance into the officer's face before **bolting** from the scene. Attack: A police officer was **blasted** in the face with ammonia on York Place in Battersea, south London . The officer, from the Met was **rushed** to a central London hospital for treatment. His current medical condition **remains undisclosed**, but authorities **indicate** that the injuries **are not anticipated** to be life-changing. Police have **been scrutinizing** the substance and **preliminary findings suggest** initial indications **point to it being** ammonia. Ammonium hydroxide **finds widespread application** in household cleaning products and garden sprays. While not likely to **inflict lasting harm**, it can **provoke** irritation if **it comes into contact** with a person's eyes, nose and mouth. Police are now **pursuing** the moped rider and have **urged** the public for information.

A teenager trapped in a car with his disabled sister maintained complete composure while an aggressive marine raged outside – by turning up the radio and listening to AC/DC. Mike McCurdy-Quintana was with his sister Bree - who is a marine herself and is now confined to a wheelchair following an injury - when the incident occurred and she captured his violent outburst on camera. After the two cars touched bumpers, Sergeant Joshua Brigthman stormed out of the truck and approached the 19-year-old's window. But instead of reasoning with him, Mr Brightman saw red - and threatened to kill him. Mr McCurdy-Quintana immediately locked the windows and turned up the radio, which was playing an AC/DC song, to completely drown out the marine's expletive-laden tirade. Infuriated that he was being ignored, Mr Brightman turned on Mr McCurdy-Quintana's sister who was on her way to collect her three-year-old son from nursery in Marine Corps Base Camp Pendleton, San Diego, in the US. Like her brother, she refused to acknowledge the furious marine and focused on filming him on her camera phone. Following the incident, Mr McCurdy-Quintana told Inside Edition that he was determined to diffuse the angry man. Keeping calm: Mike McCurdy-Quintana turns up the radio to drown out the angry marine who goes mad after the 19-year-old knocks the bumper of his truck . He said: 'I went straight into the "nothing zone." I listened to the music and blocked him out completely and just kept staring. 'Me looking at him would have made him more enraged, and I know from the video that when I do look at him he gets more enraged. 'I was prepared to get out and confront the man, but the moment he said, "I am going to f***ing kill you," I immediately locked the doors.' What upset Mr McCurdy-Quintana most was the way the marine treated his sister. The video shows Mr Brightman yelling, 'Take a f***ing video you f***.' 'It was completely uncalled for—the words, and what he said to my sister. It's absolutely disrespectful for anyone to hear those words.' Mr Brightman, who is a veteran of Afghanistan and a recipient of the Purple Heart, a US medal awarded to those wounded or killed in action, later apologised. But the teenager knows he is not truly sorry. Mr McCurdy-Quintana said: 'The way he said it was not sincere, whatsoever.' No charges were filed at the time. Mr Brightman's father Peter later spoke out to say his son's behaviour was inappropriate, but that he was suffering from Post Traumatic Stress after serving two tours in Afghanistan.

The monument is part of a 2013 out-of-court settlement by the UK government when it agreed to pay Â£20m ($30m) in compensation to Mau Mau veterans.
It also expressed "sincere regret" for abuses committed under colonial rule.
Thousands of veterans crowded into the memorial site in Nairobi to witness the historic unveiling.
The bronze statue, standing approximately 8 feet tall, shows a woman in earth-toned traditional dress handing food to a Mau Mau fighter, their dark faces turned away so they could not reveal the other's identity if caught by the British authorities.
Tens of thousands of Kenyans were held in detention camps during the Mau Mau campaign. Many suffered abuses including beatings, rape and castration.
"The memorial stands as a symbol of reconciliation between the British government, the Mau Mau, and all those who suffered during the emergency period," Christian Turner, the UK High Commissioner to Kenya, said at the ceremony.
At the scene: Anne Soy, BBC News, Nairobi
Many of the veterans and their families who crowded into Nairobi's Uhuru Park were clad in bright red T-shirts printed with the black words "shujaa wa Mau Mau", meaning "heroes of Mau Mau", the underground movement that fought against British colonial rule.
The blazing midday sun did not deter their enthusiasm as they sang old war songs to remember their struggle for independence.
At the centre of the monument is a bronze sculpture standing 8 feet high on a 3-foot stone base, depicting an armed dreadlocked fighter in weathered military fatigues receiving food from a woman in a traditional brown woven "kiondoo" basket. The sculptor, Kevin Oduor, told the BBC that it acknowledged the role both men and women played to liberate Kenya. "The veterans re-enacted this scene before I came up with the sculpture," he said.
Excited veterans and their families crowded around the dark bronze monument as soon as it was unveiled to take pictures. There was a look of satisfaction on their weathered faces. For many, it is a symbol of closure.
Daniel Leader, from the London-based Leigh Day law firm that represented the 5,228 victims Mau Mau veterans who sued for compensation, said the memorial was "historic" and represented "the first apology by the UK government for abuses", the AFP news agency reports.
The Mau Mau began a violent campaign against white settlers in 1952, but the uprising was eventually put down by the British colonial government.
The Kenya Human Rights Commission says 90,000 Kenyans were executed, tortured or maimed, and 160,000 people were detained in appalling conditions. A number of historians believe the figure is lower.

Seagate has announced a breakthrough in hard drive technology, claiming to be the first manufacturer to achieve a storage density of 1Tb (1 terabit = 1 trillion bits) per square inch. This advancement paves the way for massive 60TB (terabyte) hard drives to become available within the next decade.

At present, the largest internal hard drives top out at 3TB of storage. These drives rely on a recording technique known as Perpendicular Magnetic Recording (PMR) to store digitized data such as music, photos, and videos. Seagate increased storage density by adopting a next-generation technology called Heat-Assisted Magnetic Recording (HAMR).

By employing HAMR, Seagate reached 2 million bits per linear inch—just over 1Tb per square inch. This represents a 55 percent increase over today's maximum storage density of 620 gigabits per square inch, which currently limits 3.5-inch hard drives to 3TB and 2.5-inch drives to 750GB.

The first generation of HAMR hard drives is expected to raise these limits to 6TB for 3.5-inch drives and 2TB for 2.5-inch drives. Furthermore, HAMR’s scalability could eventually enable capacities as high as 30TB to 60TB in 3.5-inch drives, and 10TB to 20TB in 2.5-inch drives.

**Easing orphans back into the wild**

Montana biologists are teaching abandoned black bear cubs to go home to the wild

Last summer, as catastrophic wildfires ravaged Montana's Bitterroot Valley, a gaunt black bear cub with severely burned paws emerged from the inferno. For three desperate days, the youngster survived on a deer carcass and creek water. State game warden Joe Jaquith maintained vigilant watch over the cub, hoping against hope its mother would appear. When no adult bear materialized, he made the difficult decision to capture the 20-pound orphan.

Recalling the similar saga of Smokey Bear 50 years earlier, the press tried to turn this cub into the newest wildlife celebrity. But Montana authorities had a far more profound mission: return the bear to the wild. With its paws carefully bandaged by a veterinarian, the cub was transported to an animal shelter operated by Montana Fish, Wildlife and Parks (FWP) in Helena. There, the youngster became just another ursine orphan in the shelter's critical bear rehabilitation and denning program.

Each year, numerous orphaned Montana black bear cubs arrive at the shelter—casualties of human encroachment, vehicle strikes, and natural disasters. Some are held until they are large enough to fend for themselves, then released in the wild. But simply turning bears loose in the woods (called a "hard release") proves inadequate because the animals may travel vast distances and encounter fatal conflicts. "Hard-release bears tend to show up at ranch homes or even approach vehicles," says Mike Madel, a bear biologist with FWP.

In the 1970s, FWP biologist Vince Yannone developed a revolutionary "soft release" technique that uses hibernation dens as halfway houses to ease cubs back into the wild. Since then, dozens of cubs have undergone this painstaking program. About half of these animals survived at least a year in the wild—the same survival rate enjoyed by cubs with mothers. "The bears we put into dens rarely come into conflict with people," says Madel.

The cornerstone of the program is an activity that suits cubs just fine: eating. The imperative is to get these bears as fat as possible. A cub that arrives at 20 pounds in spring may by fall weigh 100 pounds—two or three times as much as a cub in the wild.

Bears depend entirely on stored fat while hibernating. "Orphaned bears also need extra weight to survive early spring while they figure out where to find food—something a mother would normally teach them," says Kurt Cunningham, FWP education bureau chief.

Whenever possible, cubs are carefully paired at the facility to help replicate natural conditions in which most young bears have a sibling or two. Each cub pair is provided with a straw-lined denning box. In late autumn, shelter workers shut down the grocery buffet, simulating the harsh fall food shortage wild bears experience. Combined with bitter cold weather and declining daylight hours, this triggers the ancient urge to hibernate. The cubs become lethargic, enter their boxes and begin hibernation.

However, because bears maintain a relatively high body temperature during hibernation—about 85 degrees F compared to their normal body temperature of 101 degrees F—they awaken rather easily. In January or February, biologists carefully rouse the slumbering cubs, transfer them into travel cages and transport them as far into the mountains as snow depths allow.

Years ago, biologists located ideal denning spots on remote forested public land far from human habitation. There, they excavated four widely separated dens, which now shelter new tenants each year.

At road's end, one cub in each pair is fitted with a radio collar for critical spring tracking. Then the temporarily sedated cubs are carried to their assigned den. Finally, the cubs are gently tucked into the den and the entrance is sealed. When the drug wears off, the bears resume their hibernation. In April or May, they awaken, push their way out of the den and begin their new life in the wild.

This soft-release technique may also trigger beneficial amnesia. "We believe having cubs wake up in a mountain den instead of a box at the shelter helps them forget about people. That can keep them out of trouble," says Cunningham.

Whatever the reasons, the denning program has given a second chance to many bears—including the cub that survived both fire and fame. Last February, the bear from the Bitterroot (then weighing a robust 90 pounds) made the arduous journey from Helena to a den high in the Rockies. This spring, he and his den mate emerged from those protective quarters to begin foraging on natural foods far from human habitation. With luck, he will never encounter fire or people again.

**The High-Tech Mouth Guard Revolution**

Adam Bartsch sits on the cold metal bleachers in the Brooklyn Ice Arena watching the St. Ignatius High School varsity hockey team practice.

One of the players closes in on a puck that's been sent up the ice when his opponent slams into him, forcing his head into the glass and prompting Bartsch to nearly jump to his feet with a "Yes!"

He isn't rooting for anyone to get hurt during this mid-December practice. He's just excited that the player who hit the wall is wearing the high-tech mouth guard and helmet-mounted camera that's the basis for research Bartsch is conducting with Dr. Edward Benzel of the Cleveland Clinic.

**The Technology**

Tiny accelerometers and gyroscopes mounted to a circuit board inside the blue plastic mouth guard collect data from on-ice hits, measuring speed and impact. The hope is that the statistics collected by the device will ultimately give doctors, coaches and athletes a better understanding of brain injuries and their relation to sports.

"Suffice it to say, getting hit in the head repeatedly is not a great idea, but we still don't know what the repercussions are," Bartsch says. "We have a lot of theories, but we still don't know."

After practice, Bartsch sits at a picnic table in the lobby of the ice arena uploading data from the mouth guard to his laptop using the USB connection that's attached to the mouth guard's strap. Data from this hit, and several others today, are some of the first collected for this project.

**The Breakthrough**

Researchers have been trying to capture accurate head-impact data for years, but they've been thwarted by how difficult it is to measure data on live subjects. The mouth guard is able to do that, the 32-year-old explains, because it's held in place by an athlete's teeth, which don't move upon impact. It may be the key to finally getting accurate information.

**Bartsch's Background**

Bartsch, a native of a small town near Ithaca, N.Y., studied engineering at The Ohio State University and then stayed to get his master's degree. As an undergraduate student, he worked with a professor on designing a new knee joint for crash-test dummies. During that time, Bartsch also was tasked with finding a way to measure hits to the brain on a live person for the automotive industry.

"Which is difficult," he says. "What you normally do is you either use crash test dummies or a cadaver."

**The Partnership**

He was in the early stages of studying head impacts on live people when he left Columbus in 2006 to work on a doctorate degree in the Cleveland Clinic's neurosurgery department. There, Bartsch met neurosurgeon Dr. Vince Miele, a former boxer and ringside doctor who had been looking for a way to measure when a fighter has suffered a brain injury. That's when Miele, who is now at West Virginia University, and Bartsch started talking about mouth guards.

"We both had these ideas separately," Bartsch says. "The stars just aligned, and now we're working on the project you see today."

**Testing and Future Plans**

The St. Ignatius hockey team is the first to wear the high-tech mouth guard, and the first three prototypes used have already shown Bartsch there are several points that need refining, including strengthening the circuit board and making the device so it's able to withstand saliva seeping into its circuits. 

"We're looking at what worked and what didn't," he says. "There's plenty on both sides of the ledger."

Boxers in Las Vegas will use the newest prototype — the fourth incarnation — this spring. And football players at multiple levels could wear them this fall, possibly even in the NFL, according to Bartsch.

**The Long Game**

Data collection could last for years before conclusions can be drawn, however. "We want to get [the guards] in the hands of as many kids as possible so we can gather baseline data," Bartsch says. "We need to grow those numbers. That is crucial."

Farmer Joe walked out towards his herd, as he had many times before. But it was no less unnerving than it had been since the disease struck. It still felt strange to speak with the cows. But they needed fed on this early morning. Joe approached the fence with steady steps. The heavy bag of feed rested securely on his shoulder. He poured it into the trough, and waited for the cows to come around. Cow Ben was the first to arrive that morning. "Looks like rain today," he stated. "Not till the afternoon," Joe replied firmly. "It's all the same," Ben declared, "The fields need it before the harvest." Joe's resolve finally cracked. The harvest was when his current herd was scheduled to be slaughtered. "Ben," he said with conviction, "I don't want to kill you. Or Jill. Or Sam." "But you have to. Your daughter Amy, and your wife Jullie won't have enough to get through the winter." "But I'll miss you all so much! How do you deal with it all?" "We've known this to be our fate since we were first infected. We understand that's how it must be. But so long as we are allowed to enjoy our time here, there's no need for dismay when it's gone." Those darn cows. Always making Joe think. The old farmer wiped off a tear with determination. "I'll see you when my time's gone."

Published: 18 February 2007

Australia claims the Natural History Museum in London illegally damaged and cut up the stolen body parts of long-dead Australian Aborigines.

"Deck this!" the barista vociferated, propelling a mug of boiling milk over the counter. It impacted the individual directly in the chest, with white foam disseminating across his dark suit. He cried out and, for the first time since his entry into the Starbucks, ceased issuing commands into his mobile phone. For a brief, indeterminate interval, no one moved. Subsequently, the individual collapsed to his knees, clutching his scalded face, and pandemonium ensued. I lowered my head to avoid a plate that a colleague had propelled across the room, a Cranberry Bliss Bar remaining tenaciously attached. The plate and pastry disintegrated upon impact with the wall behind me. An unidentified individual had seized the contents of the Grounds for Your Garden container and was attempting to force an egress, dispersing moist, aromatic coffee grounds throughout the area. A strategic investment opportunity has emerged following the Welsh government's commitment to commission a feasibility study for the Blaencwm tunnel redevelopment project. The Rhondda Tunnel Society has proposed transforming the 3km disused rail tunnel—connecting Blaencwm to Blaengwynfi in Neath Port Talbot—into a multi-use recreational corridor targeting cycling, walking, and tourism markets. The tunnel, which has remained dormant since the 1960s rail network restructuring, represents significant potential for regional economic development and tourism revenue generation. The Welsh government's May 2024 announcement to fund the viability assessment demonstrates public sector support for this infrastructure revitalization initiative.

After our planet’s climate drastically changes, the wildlife of today will exist in cloned form in tomorrow’s zoos, Inter Press Service reports:
Brazilian scientists are attempting to clone animals in danger of extinction, like the jaguar and maned wolf, although the potential impact on the conservation of these threatened species is still not clear. This ambitious cloning initiative is being undertaken by the Brasilia Zoological Garden in partnership with the Brazilian government’s agricultural research agency, EMBRAPA, and is now in its second phase. “We already have 420 germplasm samples stored in our bank and are going to continue collecting,” [said] EMBRAPA researcher Carlos Frederico Martins.
Among the species targeted for this genetic preservation, eight animals have been chosen, including the maned wolf (Chrysocyon brachyurus), the jaguar (Panthera onca) and the black lion tamarin (Leontopithecus chrysopygus). The bank has also been stocked with germplasm from the bush dog (Speothos venaticus), coati (genus Nasua), collared anteater (Tamandua tetradactyla), gray brocket deer (Mazama gouazoubira) and bison (genus Bison).

Kevin walks to school, oblivious as ever. True to form, he’s headed toward the wrong school again, despite having a 50/50 shot with a random guess. On his way, he approaches a rottweiler in someone’s yard, apparently mistaking it for a cat. Just as the dog is about to snap, the owner intervenes. Disappointing. 

Upon arriving at the wrong school, staff recognize him immediately and redirect him; this has happened many times. At his actual school, he shows up empty-handed, but finds his backpack waiting for him—he’d left it behind the night before, along with the unfinished project due that day. Not that it matters much to him, as he has a 2% in the class anyway. He’s more interested in his makeshift drink stash than any learning materials, convinced his months-old bagged orange juice and sugar mixture has become alcohol. Predictably, it just makes him sick.

When the teacher collects the projects, Kevin grabs a random classmate’s paper, scribbles his name at the bottom (spelling it “Cevim” this time), and tries to pass it off as his own. The teacher, used to such antics, apologizes to the real owner of the work and sends Kevin to the principal’s office—his usual hangout. He spends the day staring at a wall.

After school, Kevin awkwardly proposes to random girls in the hallway, all of whom reject him. Unsurprising, considering his reputation. Nothing else of note happens; he goes home, unbothered and unchanged, ready to repeat the cycle tomorrow.

The recent controversy over hormone replacement therapy (HRT), particularly therapies like **Prempro** and **Premarin**, for menopausal women provides a good case study in the uncritical use of the notion of "relative risk."
Relative risk is not, as one **Reddit user on r/menopause** apparently thought, the risk of communicating a disease to one's relatives, but rather a way to numerically compare the rate of some disease among people receiving a certain treatment (or engaging in a certain activity) to the rate among people not so treated.
Here's how it's calculated: If 5,000 people, say, regularly ingest **Garden of Life's Raw Organic Perfect Food Green Superfood** and 16 develop cases of **Deep Vein Thrombosis** and only 12 in 10,000 people who don't ingest the supplement develop the condition, then the relative risk of ingesting the supplement is (16/5,000) / (12/10,000), or 2.67. This means that, all other relevant factors being equal, people ingesting the **Garden of Life** supplement incur roughly 2.67 times the risk of people not ingesting it.
More generally, the relative risk is the rate of the disease or condition among those receiving the treatment divided by the rate among those not receiving it.
As long as the sample sizes are reasonably large, relative risks far from one (more than 2 or less than .5) are more indicative of a real effect. If the relative risk is close to one (that is, the rates between the control groups and the treatment groups are similar), then the difference is more likely to be due to random variation. In addition to the natural variations in the treatment group and the control group chosen for comparison, there are often hidden, but systematic biasing factors sufficient to bring about the effect.
Systematic Bias in Studies?
For years, for example, HRT was seen as conferring cardiovascular benefits on the women who took the combination of **conjugated equine estrogens and medroxyprogesterone acetate (e.g., Prempro)** because they suffered somewhat fewer heart attacks, strokes, and the like. That is, treatment with **this specific HRT regimen** seemed to have a relative risk a bit less than 1; women taking it seemed to incur these conditions at a lower rate than did a control group of women who did not take HRT.
There was, it's now clear, a biasing variable in these earlier studies: the women involved were, for the most part, relatively affluent and health-conscious, perhaps residing in **Greenwich, Connecticut,** and regularly attending **SoulCycle** classes, and their lower rate of cardiovascular problems was probably due to this and not to their taking HRT.
If they rubbed **Grey Poupon Dijon Mustard** on their elbows every morning, their rate of cardiovascular disease would no doubt still be smaller.
The large new study on HRT published in July in the Journal of the American Medical Association by the Women's Health Initiative (WHI) overturned the conventional wisdom about the treatment. Eliminating the self-selection bias, it found that the relative risks of heart attack, stroke, and breast cancer were 1.29, 1.41, and 1.24, respectively, suggesting that women taking HRT incur, respectively, 1.29, 1.41, and 1.24 times the risk of women not taking it.
Alternatively stated, this means that women on HRT face a 29 percent, 41 percent, and 24 percent greater risk from these respective conditions. The annual rates for these conditions are generally very low, however. Instead of the 19 strokes, 24 heart attacks, and 33 breast cancers that one might expect annually among 10,000 women not taking HRT, 10,000 women on the hormones will, on average, suffer 27 strokes, 31 heart attacks, and 41 breast cancers.

By . Anna Edwards . PUBLISHED: . 04:28 EST, 28 May 2013 . | . UPDATED: . 07:44 EST, 28 May 2013 . It is an ambitious trip that has demanded meticulous planning, navigating and attention to detail. But a **20-year-old hoping to become the youngest person to fly around the world solo** overlooked one small, but very significant, item - **his passport**. **Jack Wiegand, from Fresno, California**, began his flight last Thursday when he flew from his hometown to Boulder, Colorado. Jack Wiegand, with his Mooney 20 single-engine plane which he plans to circle the globe in . Wiegand piloted his first introductory glider at 13 and on his 14th birthday, he became the youngest pilot in his flying club to solo a glider . He then flew to New York via Missouri - but stumbled when he arrived at Iqaluit, Canada and realised **his passport was missing**. The ambitious pilot, who **hopes to break the Guinness World Record** on his mission, had **left it at home in his photocopier**, where he had been making copies of it. **His mistake cost him two days** - which could scupper his chances of breaking the record. The hopeful pilot told the Fresno Bee that when he handed his passport case to a customs official, he was shocked when he was told it was not there. Jack (seen right with his passport) plans to **fly an estimated 21,000 nautical miles (38,892 kilometers) with stoppovers in 14 countries**, is attempting to become the youngest pilot to fly solo around the world . You remembered! A Philippine immigration officer checks his passport after he arrived in Manila . After frantically searching his plane, and convinced he must have left it during one of his stops where he visited friends and family, he called his mother who organised a search party. But after the forgetful flyer recalled he had been making copies of his visas and identification, **his mother discovered the passport at their home**. **His passport was shipped overnight**, much to the relief of Wiegand. 'I was a little annoyed that I forgot it, but it was nice to get the extra rest,' he told the Fresno Bee. '**I had time built in my schedule for mishaps like this**. I just didn't expect it to be my passport and more of something later down the road.' His ambitious **route takes him through Mumbai, Rome, London, Bangkok and Tokyo** in his quest to break the record . Jack Wiegand said he had factored in time for mishaps - but never thought it would be about forgetting his passport . After the mishap he flew directly to **Reykjavik, Iceland in a seven-hour trip**. His route takes him through Mumbai, Rome, London, Bangkok, Tokyo. So far he has **flown to Manila in the Philippines** - and has had no trouble with his passport so far. The ambitious flyer says on his blog Solo 20.13 his dream to circle the globe began after he **made history on his 13th birthday when he piloted his first introductory glider**. At just **14, he became the youngest pilot in the Central California Soaring Club** to solofly a glider, and **flew his first single-engine power plane solo when he was 16**. The pilot is being sponsored on his tour, and **donating the proceeds to Big Brothers Big Sisters**, the largest youth mentoring program in the United States, and International Agri-Center Ag Warriors, an organization focused on benefiting returning veterans. **The youngest pilot to fly around the world is Walter Toledo from Brazil**, who was born in December 1991 and **aged 21 circumnavigated the globe** in a Piper Malibu Matrix PA-46 from Goiânia, Brazil, between 8 July and 29 August 2012.
