Lindsey Vonn, the 31-year-old, an Olympic downhill gold medallist in 2010 and world champion in 2009, finished the 2.6km course in one minute and 37.1 seconds.
Canada's Larisa Yurkiw was second, 0.28 seconds behind, with Swiss Lara Gut third, 0.67 seconds off the pace.
The victory sees Vonn surpass Austrian Annemarie Moser-Proell's record of 36 World Cup downhill victories.
It was Vonn's 74th World Cup win, edging her closer to the record of 86 set by Sweden's Ingemar Stenmark.
Gut retains the overall lead in World Cup standings, with 810 points compared with Vonn's 800, but that margin could narrow again on Sunday in the Super-G, scheduled for 10:30 GMT.

Tallying up the year’s most important work in science is a fool’s errand. After all, what does “important” really mean? Such a list made in 1952 would probably have omitted research into the structure of DNA, and been right to do so, but in the wake of Francis and Crick’s famous publication the decision would have seemed shortsighted. We can never truly tell what will pan out, and it would be foolish to confine ourselves only to commercial and practical applications.

Here are the ten fields in science that saw the most progress in 2012, each either practically useful or emotionally bracing. These fields defined 2012 as the latest in a long string of world-changing years.

The Higgs Boson
One downside of conducting science on an international scale, building equipment with both public support and government funding, is that the public actually pays attention. It took an uncomfortably long time to find the Higgs Boson, whose existence underpinned the multi-billion dollar LHC. It took even longer to determine if the findings were official, overblown, or mistaken. Finally, the Higgs Boson was officially confirmed with 99.99997% certainty. Recent reports suggest a slightly less massive twin to the Higgs, but the evidence remains very preliminary.

Regardless, the Higgs' confirmation has solidified the foundations of a generation of theoretical research that tacitly assumed its existence. More detailed analysis of LHC readings will likely introduce new wrinkles into physics, perhaps in the form of a twin particle or some other discovery. The Higgs Boson was the last remaining piece needed to confirm the Standard Model of physics, thereby finalizing its total inability to explain quantum phenomena. LHC readings seem the most likely source for the next crucial piece of evidence: slight variations from expected values that will illuminate the relationship between the standard and quantum fields in physics.

The Higgs Boson was the biggest science story of 2012, representing the latest necessary step down the long road to a theory of everything.

Next page: A rover lands on Mars…

I really enjoy this CD. The groups featured here perform some truly impressive renditions of U2 tracks. Rather than making boring, imitation covers, they interpret the songs in a range of different genres. The only exception is Peoplemover, who simply tried to replicate U2's sound—thumbs down for that. The CD is packed with energetic takes; I especially appreciated Drag’s amped-up version of "Discotheque" and Statica's catchy pop rock spin on "Who's Gonna Ride the Wild Horses." There are also creative, artsy, folk-inspired performances such as Friction Bailey's version of "Pride." If, like me, you enjoy a diverse mix of music and are a U2 fan, you should definitely check out this CD.

I live at South Mall Towers. I like to use Lucky Cat because the washing machines and dryers work very well. Parking is free behind the building, and the place is clean and tidy. There aren’t people hanging around or causing trouble. There are cameras inside and outside to keep everyone safe. I like that I can wash clothes in cold water, and even my white clothes come out really clean. The dryers work great, and usually one dollar is enough to dry my clothes. This is the best laundry place I have ever been to.

The Tempe Center for the Arts is a prominent cultural landmark situated within Tempe's scenic lake district. Strategically positioned at the apex of the lake and dam, the Center’s architectural and interior design are both visually striking and meticulously conceived. Perhaps most noteworthy are the Center's mission-driven initiatives and extensive community outreach efforts, which are widely regarded as exemplary. Operated by a highly skilled team, the TCA offers a diverse array of dynamic programs designed to engage audiences across a broad spectrum of interests. Funded by both the City of Tempe and its residents, the Center’s programming remains closely aligned with its mission and demonstrates a strong commitment to intentional, impactful offerings. The Tempe Center for the Arts stands as a cherished institution within the community.

**Bridging Indigenous Knowledge and Contemporary Science Through Earth Stewardship**
TUESDAY, MAY 8, 2012

Wisconsin students deserve an education that honors Indigenous Knowledge Systems (IKS) while strengthening their understanding of contemporary STEM disciplines through hands-on ecological restoration work. While Wisconsin's tribal nations actively safeguard their environmental resources—highlighting the critical need for Indigenous scientists—Native Americans remain significantly underrepresented in scientific careers. Dr. Doug Medin identifies this as "a culture clash between orientation and worldview." This disconnect becomes evident when Menominee fourth-graders, who initially excel in science assessments, fall behind by eighth grade due to culturally disconnected teaching methods.

The IKS Earth Partnership Stewardship initiative aims to cultivate the next wave of Indigenous environmental guardians by helping Native youth embrace their cultural foundations while pursuing scientific pathways. "The irony of minimal STEM participation among Native students lies in the fact that their ancestral and contemporary practices fundamentally employ the same observational methodologies found in western science. Initiatives like these can rejuvenate Indigenous scholarly traditions and challenge the perception that 'science isn't relevant' among today's youth" (H. YoungBear Tibbetts, 2011).

Indigenous Knowledge Systems encompasses the scholarly heritage of native communities across varied scales. These intellectual frameworks, while as varied as the distinct nations that developed them, share fundamental principles including relationality, reciprocity, reverence, and responsibility that guide their environmental practices and worldviews.

**Core Objectives of the Indigenous Knowledge Systems Earth Partnership:**

1. Weave Indigenous Knowledge Systems into the Arboretum's Earth Partnership for Schools environmental restoration curriculum, particularly focusing on Great Lakes bioregions.

2. Support K-16 educators by providing culturally authentic and accurate teaching materials across all subject areas.

3. Collaborate with tribal communities to expand engagement and inspire tribal youth, developing workforce capacity for future science and technology careers.

Indigenous Knowledge Systems operates through understanding interconnected relationships among natural phenomena in ways that honor reciprocity and respect. While these approaches differ from the sequential, analytical methods of western science, numerous opportunities exist for meaningful collaboration and mutual enrichment. This convergence is most apparent in place-based education, where our shared inhabitation of this continent provides both practical and symbolic "common ground" for learning together. (H.YoungBear-Tibbetts)

I have undertaken a thorough examination of this work, having engaged with Kerouac's *On the Road* at two distinct stages of my intellectual development—at twenty-one and again at forty-one—in an earnest attempt to comprehend its purported significance within the American literary canon. Despite these sustained efforts to appreciate the text's acclaimed merits, I find myself compelled to conclude that readers seeking authentic literary achievement in this genre would be better served by consulting the established masters: Henry Miller's autobiographical novels, Thomas Pynchon's seminal work *V*, or Hunter S. Thompson's gonzo journalism, all of which demonstrate superior craftsmanship and artistic vision. While I recognize that this assessment may diverge from popular critical consensus, I must respectfully maintain that *On the Road* represents one of the most egregiously overvalued works in modern American literature.

A protest march against U.S. drone strikes in Pakistan, led by former cricket star and politician Imran Khan, was halted on Sunday. Authorities blocked the demonstration's access to its final destination in the tribal region, officials told CNN. Authorities used steel shipping containers and security forces to seal off the road entering South Waziristan, where protesters had planned to hold a rally on Sunday, local government official Shahid Ullah said. Confronted by the roadblock, Khan directed protesters to turn back, stating the march had achieved its goal of drawing attention to the U.S. drone strikes. Khan has been a fierce critic of U.S. policy in Pakistan, particularly the drone strikes, which he calls a violation of Pakistan's sovereignty and a strategy that fuels militant anger toward Washington. "We will not cause any trouble if the government doesn't want us to enter South Waziristan," Khan told reporters. "We just want to direct the attention of the entire world to these illegal and immoral drone attacks in Waziristan that have left many innocent people dead." In recent years, Washington has significantly increased drone attacks in Pakistan's largely ungoverned tribal region, widely considered a safe haven for militant groups fueling the insurgency in Afghanistan. U.S. officials say the drone strikes are an effective strategy against militant groups and insist civilian casualties are rare. The anti-drone march, comprising a convoy of over 100 vehicles, departed from the capital, Islamabad, on Saturday. It reached the border of South Waziristan on Sunday afternoon before being turned back. The U.S.-based anti-war group Code Pink was part of the procession. Throughout the journey, the pink-clad peace activists chanted anti-drone slogans in Urdu. "Stop them! Stop them! Stop the drone attacks," the protesters shouted in Urdu. Pakistani authorities had earlier warned that the demonstration would not be allowed to enter South Waziristan for security reasons. "The situation in the tribal areas is comparatively good but not suitable for any gathering there," Tashfeen Khan, a government official, said on Saturday. Journalist Aamir Iqbal contributed to this report.

London: While the unfortunate Horatio Chapple of Eton College explored the remote Svalbard archipelago a week ago, I was also there – albeit in a safer, warmer, more convenient way, and with my dad. This 'exploration' was made possible by 'High Arctic' at the National Maritime Museum in Greenwich, a unique and moving exhibition by United Visual Artist Matt Clark. Last year, Clark travelled to Svalbard on the Noorderlicht, a 100-year-old schooner, as part of the Cape Farewell expedition team – consisting of scientists, artists, musicians and poets – to explore the warming Arctic landscape as its ice recedes. Clark’s interpretation is a thought-provoking and haunting installation in the museum’s new basement extension.

We enter a darkened room – a twilight world of strange Polar light – clutching our UV torches to navigate the many ‘ice-stacks’ that fill the landscape. Each stack, inscribed with a name readable only by torchlight, serves as the sole 'reading material.' Set in 2100, the exhibition fills our ears with the disembodied voices of previous Arctic explorers as we navigate the piles of ice. Among these, Ancient Greek explorer Pytheas recounts his 330 BC voyage to the land of Thule (thought to be around Svalbard, but possibly Iceland or Greenland), describing a place where “there are no nights at all” and where “there is no longer any proper land or sea or air, but a sort of mixture of all three of the consistency of a jellyfish.” We ponder if he means the shifting sea ice, or if he's indulging in a returned explorer's fantastical description. We move from one delightful mariner’s tale to another, all the while marvelling at the shadows and light cast in the eerie gallery. Between columns of ice, expanses of flat terrain dance under our torches. There are pools of sea ice that shift and coalesce like Pytheas’s “no longer proper land or sea,” and snow storms of drifting sparkles, all cleverly designed to interact with the torchlight.

As we emerge, several latitudes south, the curator tells me that visitors have complained about the lack of explanation, reading material and traditional museum material. It’s an immersive experience, an exploration – it’s very different from any exhibition I’ve seen and refreshing for it. Words are not the only way to convey meaningful information about the threatened Arctic environment.

Global warming is speeding up melting of Arctic sea-ice, but a study out this week shows the process is more complex than we thought, and ice coverage is likely to fluctuate over coming decades. Perhaps there will be enough left for me to see firsthand, when I get around to visiting the region.

Came here on a Friday night with reservations and the place was very busy, a lively hum of conversation and the soft murmur of Greek music filling the air, mingling with the warm, inviting aroma of garlic and spices. We started with some appetizers. The Skordalia, a creamy, pungent potato and garlic spread, was cool and smooth on the tongue. The fried zucchini had a perfectly golden, crispy crunch, delicate and not at all oily, with a light, earthy scent. We also enjoyed fresh beets and a vibrant Greek village salad, alongside the Avgolemeno soup, a silky, warm egg lemon soup with a bright, zesty fragrance and comforting, velvety texture. For our main dinner we shared a plate of the paidakia (succulent, tender lamb chops with deliciously charred edges and a rich, savory aroma) and a mixed kabob plate, sizzling hot from the grill. All 3 kabobs were perfectly grilled, yielding easily and bursting with smoky flavor. For dessert we had the Karidopita, a moist, dense cake fragrant with cinnamon and spice, featuring the earthy crunch of walnuts and a sweet, sticky honey glaze. We also savored the Galaktobouriko, a creamy, sweet custard wrapped in a delicate, flaky phyllo crust, with a warm, milky scent and silken smooth feel. All in all we had a great time and experience, and will be back.

Multicast DNS (mDNS) allows devices on a small, local network to use standard DNS tools and programming interfaces even when no traditional DNS server is available. For example, if you connect two laptops to the same Wi-Fi network at home and want them to find each other by name (like "laptop1.local" and "laptop2.local"), mDNS makes this possible without needing to set up a DNS server.

mDNS was developed by members of the IETF Zero Configuration Networking (zeroconf) and DNS Extensions (dnsext) working groups. The Zeroconf group focused on making networking simple and automatic, while the DNSEXT group worked on the technical details needed to extend DNS for use in such networks. Many contributors participate in both groups to ensure mDNS meets user needs and works efficiently.

Instead of inventing a brand-new name resolution protocol, mDNS reuses existing DNS packet formats and APIs with only minor changes. For instance, traditional DNS uses unicast (one-to-one) queries to a specific server, but mDNS uses multicast (one-to-many) queries sent to all devices on the local network. This means an application like a web browser or file-sharing tool can look up device names without needing special code; it can use standard DNS lookups, just targeting ".local" domain names for local discovery. As a result, software developers don't have to write separate code for large networks with DNS servers or small home networks using mDNS—standard DNS calls work for both.

Additionally, network engineers and support tools benefit: familiar packet analyzers like Wireshark already understand DNS traffic, so adopting mDNS does not require new training or specialized tools to diagnose issues or monitor traffic. This makes mDNS an efficient and user-friendly solution for seamless, zero-configuration networking.

- For example, in eastern Canada and large parts of Siberia, it is common for snow cover to remain on the ground throughout December, making them ideal for Santa to "glide" his sleigh. In contrast, cities like London or Glasgow often have green Christmases, meaning Santa would have to rely on flying instead of sliding.

- Dr Graham highlighted that records from places such as Aviemore, in the Cairngorms, show fewer days of snow cover compared to the 1970s or 1980s. For instance, Aviemore averaged about 60 days of lying snow per year in the 1970s but has dropped to around 30-40 days in recent decades.

- As concrete evidence of the unique pattern in December, Scotland and much of Europe experienced exceptionally snowy Decembers in 2009 and 2010. In December 2010, for instance, widespread snow led to travel disruption across the UK, with Edinburgh experiencing its snowiest December in decades.

- Regarding current snow chances, while the Scottish Highlands (such as Ben Nevis and the Cairngorm plateau) are likely to see heavy snowfalls around Christmas, lower areas like Glasgow or Edinburgh are more prone to rain or sleet, resulting in little or no snow cover in the cities.

A grandmother has been charged with the murder of her granddaughter after she allegedly attacked the baby with a sledgehammer as she lay sleeping before cutting her throat. Alfreda Giedrojc, 61, is believed to have killed six-month-old Vivian Summers on Sunday morning, authorities said on Monday. The grandmother, of Oak Lawn, Illinois, allegedly placed the baby on the floor of the suburban home and struck her repeatedly with the sledgehammer. She then retrieved a carving knife from the kitchen and cut the baby's throat. Brutal death: Baby Vivian, pictured with her parents Amy and Joel, was allegedly murdered by her grandmother. Killer: Alfreda Giedrojc is accused of murdering her granddaughter using a sledgehammer and a carving knife. Giedrojc has been charged with first-degree murder and held without bond in the Cook County jail. The alleged killer is the maternal grandmother of baby Vivian. Giedrojc's daughter Amy has been married to Joel Summers since September 2009. A message left at a phone listing for Giedrojc in Oak Lawn was not immediately returned. Oak Lawn Police Chief Michael Murray called the baby's death 'a tragedy'. Giedrojc, who is originally from Poland, had been left alone with baby Vivian while her husband Bill and their son-in-law left to help a family member with household repairs. Vivian had been asleep on the sofa at her grandparents home in Oak Lawn, a suburb to Chicago, when Giedrojc went to fetch a sledgehammer which she had hidden in her closet the night before. Placing the infant on the floor, Giedrojc repeatedly struck Vivian over the head and body with the hammer and when the child did not die, Giedrojc picked her up and cut her throat with a carving knife taken from her kitchen. Upon his return to the house, Giedrojc's husband made the gruesome discovery and called police. Vivian was pronounced dead in hospital on Sunday afternoon. Tragedy: Little Vivian's parents had left her alone with her grandmother while the father and the grandfather helped out a neighbor, believed to be Vivian's uncle. Horror find: The six-month-old's body was found by her grandfather Bill who called the police on his wife. 'The defendant took the victim off of the couch where she had been sleeping and placed the baby on the floor and retrieved a sledgehammer from her closet, which she had placed there the night before,' said Michael Deno, Assistant Cook County State's Attorney told the Chicago Tribune. 'The defendant then struck the victim repeatedly in the head and body with the sledgehammer. Since the victim was still crying and moving the defendant went into the kitchen and retrieved a large carving knife. 'The defendant then picked the victim up, held her, and then cut her throat with the knife,' Mr Deno said. Giedrojc is being held without bail after appearing in court on Monday. Despite having lived in the U.S. for 30 years, Giedrojc needed a Polish translator. Giedrojc is due back in court on October 28, 2013. Sorry we are not currently accepting comments on this article.

So there's this documentary about this wild kidnapping that happened in Brazil - you know, one of those slum punk kids who hijacked a bus full of people. The crazy thing is, it was all broadcast live on TV as it was happening. Can you believe that?

The whole time, there were professional snipers who had clear shots at this kid, but they never took them. Why? Because everyone in charge knew this was going out live to millions of viewers, and nobody wanted a killing broadcast into people's living rooms. Pretty insane when you think about it.

The documentary itself is a bit slow - it really drags in parts - but I guess all that background information about the case is probably important to understand what happened. The kidnapper was completely strung out on cocaine and kept talking about how movies had influenced him to do this stuff.

It's pretty dark when you think about it - this is Brazil now, but you can't help wondering if this is what the USA might look like in a couple decades if things keep going downhill. Really makes you think about where we're headed as a society.

The damned didn't scream. It was silent all throughout the hellscape. Eternity had worn on the tortured souls; even writhing was beyond them. They all looked the same, I noted, clothed in thousands of metallic wires that scraped at their skin, further scarring it with every labored movement. Their path was a mystery even to those who delved into this world for research, for a living. The endless lines stretched across the grey expanse. Color could no longer exist in the presence of such agony. I switched off the machine, writing my first experience of hell into my journal before it etched itself into my psyche. The worst part was that I knew some of those people; this machine took me to people I would recognize. Not by face, but by deeds. I saw my rapist, his gaunt face bobbing as he marched forward, displaying the most profound fear and pain I had ever witnessed. I saw my grandfather, the man who put me through college, earned me my best job, and even introduced me to my wife. He suffered the same torture. After weeks and many warnings from my colleagues, I switched the machine back on and chose the setting 'heaven.' None of what I was told could prepare me for what I saw. I was taken through the tunnel, or what we perceive as a tunnel, and was deposited into a softly lit room. I was acknowledged by a short, cute brunette sitting at a desk against the center of the wall. She looked up at me with passing interest, and I wondered how she reacted to the first few living visitors. She gestured to a door with patience and said I was allowed ten minutes. I found this curious, as many friends of mine spent a much longer time in there, rest their souls. I entered and gasped; it was beautiful. I was told it would be and that I should take solace in that. I could earn my way there, I was told. We all could. I walked through the forests, deserts, and tundra, the beautiful landscapes dotted by happy-looking homes of the inhabitants. I was told to never enter them, but I only had three minutes left after exploring for what seemed like hours, and my curiosity had bested me. I chose a quaint bungalow on a private-looking beach and knocked. I waited for what felt like twenty minutes, though only one had passed. Finally, I decided I needed to see; I had to know how I could possibly live in heaven one day. I still had a minute and a half left when I logged out of the machine, vomiting and sobbing. They were dead. The family of five was sitting at the table, emaciated and now rotted. The baby was in his mother's lap, against her stomach, held to the very end of their second lives. I tried to seek out others who would speak of the experience, and found a new friend—someone who had walked in on a similar scene—a mother with two daughters, sitting by a fire, though the effects of weather weren't felt in that world, also emaciated and rotted. It seemed that everyone had starved to death. There was little food in heaven. I learned later that one could die of many things in heaven. Starvation, physical attacks from others, and even accidents. Worst of all, though, was that even if one could easily eat in heaven, many people were found to have died of old age. Heaven was an unsustainable eternity, ending for those who earned it in non-existence. I was almost glad grandpa went to hell.

An eight-year-old boy nearly lost his fingers after falling asleep with loom bands wrapped around them. Brandon Lambert's fingers turned black when the elastic bands cut off circulation. His father Mark checked on him thirty minutes later and removed the bands.

"I don't usually check on Brandon, but I am so glad I did. If the bands had stayed on all night, he might have needed amputation. The circulation was completely cut off," said Mark, 36, from Skipton, North Yorkshire.

Brandon spends up to six hours on weekends playing with loom bands. Mark posted a photo on Facebook warning other parents about the danger.

One parent responded: "If my friend had not posted this I would not have checked my eight-year-old daughter and found her asleep with two fingers starting to turn blue! Thank you, that's one child you have saved!"

The incident follows other injuries. Seven-year-old Kyle Lawrence was blinded when a loom band flicked into his eye. A Bristol GP also posted photos of her son losing circulation from the bands.

Dr Anne-Marie Houlder warns parents to be vigilant: "They could be a choking hazard or cause circulatory problems."

Loom bands were invented by a US father last year and have swept playgrounds this summer. The colorful rubber bands are woven into friendship bracelets and worn by celebrities including the Duchess of Cambridge, Harry Styles and Miley Cyrus.

While computer games cost £40, 1,800 loom bands cost £2.50. They're Amazon's eighth best-selling toy of all time. However, headteachers increasingly ban them from schools to prevent playground squabbles and classroom disruption.

I don't exist. Well, I did once—just not anymore, and not all the time. 

When I was a young man, I was the master of missed connections. I'd lure unsuspecting young women in with my startlingly blue eyes, then disappear before they could catch up with me. I got a weird thrill out of it, sue me. 

After a Saturday at the mall dangling the bait, I would rush home to check the "missed connections" tab on a local social site. On average I'd get at least one post that I knew was about me, but on a good day I'd get three or four. Oh, what a thrill—it was indescribable.

So let me set the stage. It was a snowy winter day, about a week before Christmas. The mall was packed with shoppers, vendors, and beautiful women. I stood by a fountain bedecked in twinkling lights, trying to catch the unwitting eye of a fine young woman. Little did I know my life was about to change forever.

She was stunning. Fair skin, eyes as blue as ice, and an outfit the color of fresh fallen snow. I caught her eye, but I couldn't bring myself to run. Even as my mind screamed at me to slip away, run toward the exit, hide in the food court—SOMETHING—my muscles refused to move. 

With every step she walked toward me, the air seemed to freeze solid. So cold. I looked down and uttered a gasp—the fountain was a block of ice. When I looked back up, she was right there, leaning in for a kiss. As her icy lips touched mine, she whispered, "And now you shall reap what you have sown."

Darkness. Nothing. No being.

243 years later, I do not exist—not unless there is a woman struggling with a missed chance at a lover. I take many forms, many bodies, many personalities, but one thing remains the same. I only exist when there is a missed connection that needs to be fulfilled. I am compelled to fulfill it, no matter what the circumstance. And when I am done, I slip back to nothing. I cannot escape this cycle: existence, nothing, existence, nothing.

Damn that ice witch.

You might go to the grocery store and pick healthy foods because you care about your health. But if you don’t eat enough, you may feel so hungry that you end up driving to a convenience store for junk food, like chips or cookies. It’s okay to eat these sometimes, but it’s better to know which healthy foods will keep you full so you don’t make junk food a habit. Here are some very filling foods to add to your shopping list:

Sweet and yummy prunes  
Prunes aren’t just for helping your digestion. They are also a sweet and tasty snack. Prunes are full of fiber and good nutrients, and you can eat them by themselves or mix them into main dishes.

Vegetables, especially before a meal  
Research shows that most people eat about the same amount of food by weight every day. If most of that weight comes from fiber-filled vegetables, it’s great for staying a healthy weight and getting good nutrition.

Dip your veggies  
You’ll feel full longer if you dip your vegetables in healthy dressings or hummus. The healthy fat and flavor can help your body use the nutrients better.

Oatmeal  
Oatmeal is a healthy and filling food, and it’s also comforting. You can change the flavor by adding fruits and nuts for a special breakfast.

Eat soup first  
Studies show that you eat less if you start your meal with soup. Pick broth-based soup with lots of vegetables, instead of creamy or salty soups.

Eat protein  
If you eat meals and snacks with protein, you’ll stay full longer. Research proves this works. You can get protein from plants or animals. Legumes and quinoa are two good plant-based options. Lentils and chickpeas are tasty, healthy, and not expensive.

Don’t skip the potato skin  
It’s tempting to scoop out potatoes and add lots of butter, but the skin is the healthiest part. Potato skin has a lot of fiber and potassium. You can make it tastier by adding herbs or salsa on top.

Fruit  
Fruit is sweet and often has lots of fiber and water. You can make fruit like apples even more special by baking them and adding nuts on top.

Nuts  
Pecans, almonds, pistachios, and other nuts have healthy fats and protein to help keep you full. Be careful about how many you eat if you’re watching your weight. Mix nuts with dried fruit for a healthy snack or add them to main dishes.

There was myself, LeVentNoir. And my cult members, StaresAtBrickWalls, WillFookinCutYou, and Gabba. The night was young, with the smells of the potent medicine I was cooking, a concoction known for its profound impact on perception and consciousness, active in doses as small as a microgram. Azure lights, a common visual distortion experienced during intense psychedelic states, winked though my vision as the spirits controlled my hands and the divine guidance steadily lead me through the ritual. The flame in the barrel was getting low, so I told Gabba to get some more wood from somewhere, steal a pallet or something, we're going to commune with the gods. And have a hot meat meal! StaresAtBrickWalls had stood near enough to a local fauna long enough for it to spook, and he was able to gather it's dropped medical ingredients, a sheet of blotter paper, a common carrier for powerful mind-altering substances. WillFookinCutYou nearly cut her for that, but I assured my tribe that this paper was important. I had him direct his anger inwards, for it was not conductive to the spirits who wispered BBC radio into my ears – an auditory hallucination often reported during such altered states – to have your chi blocked like that. Then I had him shoplift Tesco's, we didn't have meat. But it was coming together. Our spiritual, and physical healing was at hand. The potent vapours wafted up in excitement of consumption as we each took a bowlful of the medicine, and with prayers to the gods and nature herself, we slurped down the bounty. It was a hearty respite from the cold, a warmth that spread through my entire body as my feet tingled, a common somatic effect as the drug began to take hold, then merged with the one of the earth, blurring the boundaries of self and environment. The grey skyscrapers over me bent down, a classic example of distorted visual perception and a key characteristic of a psychedelic "trip," and their mouths opened to reveal cosmic knowledge "All bankers are gits""Man U will win on saturday""Dude, you're tripping like fuck." And through the glazed golden curtains of the sun twixt soulless concrete my spirit strode, tribe members with me in the threefold harmony their shaman had brought them. Four bums off their tits on an A4 of LSD blotter paper each, an extraordinarily high dose, capable of inducing profound and prolonged hallucinogenic experiences.

Listen, I care about your hands staying healthy and functional for years to come. If you're planning to hit heavy bags, please understand that these gloves won't protect you from the brutal impact that can shatter bones and end your training journey. Your hands are precious - they're how you express your craft, support your family, and live your life.

You absolutely need proper hand wraps (the long pro-style ones) paired with 14 oz or heavier gloves. Think of wraps as armor for your entire hand and wrist structure - they're what stand between you and months of painful recovery from preventable injuries. Consider knuckle guards too, though save those for bag work only, never sparring.

These gloves simply can't give your hands the structural support they desperately need. Don't gamble with your future mobility and strength.

Stay safe out there,
Erik

The culinary creations of baker Gillian Bell feature designs depicting severed body parts, open wounds, and skin conditions. Ms. Bell, 34, diverges from conventional confectionery aesthetics, opting instead for designs that portray visceral themes, appealing to a niche audience. Her distinctive creations are now featured in a London exhibition aimed at raising awareness for various diseases.

Gillian Bell's cake designs include a representation of a severed heart with stitches. Gillian Bell, pictured left, with her themed cakes.

Ms. Bell stated: "Producing conventional cakes lacks the creative engagement found in developing themed designs. The public's reactions are noteworthy, given that the cakes maintain a standard culinary quality. While some individuals consume them readily, others exhibit hesitation due to the visual representation, which can create a perceptual challenge."

A cupcake designed to resemble an eyeball. Her creations cater to an audience appreciative of both confectionery and unconventional aesthetics. A cake designed as stitched flesh, and a three-tiered cake featuring a heart with simulated dripping blood. A red cake designed to resemble a brain, created by Ms. Bell, who formerly operated a cake shop in Edinburgh.

Ms. Bell, residing in Kirkcaldy, Scotland, previously managed a confectionery establishment in Edinburgh, where she received frequent commissions for uniquely themed cakes. She subsequently left the business to pursue further education, continuing her cake design work as a freelance endeavor. The 'Eat Your Heart Out' exhibition is scheduled to take place at St. Bart's Pathology Museum from October 26-28.

A display featuring confectionery items crafted to resemble an ear, an eyeball, a finger, and a severed limb, presented within a receptacle. The conceptual basis for these designs is to utilize confectionery as a medium for raising awareness regarding diseases and illnesses.

Ms. Bell elaborated: "The objective of this London exhibition is to engage designers in creating cakes that serve to raise public awareness concerning various diseases and medical conditions. This includes representations of diseased hearts and sexually transmitted infections. My specific contributions will focus on themes related to skin conditions, such as boils, warts, and chilblains. I am honored to participate, as it provides a valuable opportunity to present my work to a broader audience."

China's health department has promised to make big changes in hospitals nationwide after a doctor was arrested for allegedly selling newborn babies to people traffickers. State media reported that the doctor, Zhang Shuxia, an obstetrician in Fuping County, Shaanxi Province, had supposedly sold other babies before. Police are investigating 10 similar cases at her hospital, where she was a senior maternity department staff member.

The most recent baby was returned to its parents after DNA tests confirmed its identity. The family was emotional, thanking police for finding their son. Authorities said Zhang allegedly sold the baby boy for about $3,527 the day after he was born. The baby was then sold twice more within ten days.

Police stated that Zhang reportedly told the baby's parents, Dong Shanshan and Lai Guofeng, that their son had syphilis and convinced them to give him up. However, the mother later tested negative for syphilis, became suspicious, and reported it to the police. The baby was found safe in Henan Province.

Zhang and five others have been arrested for human trafficking. There are reports of six other trafficking cases involving Zhang since 2006, including twins this year. Zhang allegedly used various health issues like heart problems, cerebral palsy, and missing body parts to trick parents into giving up their children. Most of the affected families were from Zhang's home village.

Another couple said Zhang convinced them in 2008 to give up their newborn son, claiming he wasn't breathing. The father, Dong Pengfei, recalled being too upset to question it at the time but later wondered why she called the baby a "kid" if it was supposedly dead. His wife has been heartbroken ever since.

China's health commission has called for harsh penalties for those involved, calling their actions "morally wrong, illegal, and unacceptable." The commission promised to improve hospital management and ethical training across the country to prevent such incidents from happening again.

While there is some marginally useful content in the appendix, which accounts for the two-star rating, I would recommend against purchasing this volume as it does not provide sufficient value for the investment.

Hey Lauren,

Look, I’ll be real with you—maybe you’re a decent person to some, but as a mom to me, you really missed the mark. You keep saying you know how to raise kids, but two are in jail and a few more are on the way, it seems. You act like you understand childhood issues, but whenever I’d cut myself, you’d just chalk it up to me trying to manipulate you. When I asked for therapy, you’d just find someone who broke confidentiality and told you everything anyway.

I don’t love you, honestly. And I don’t really see why I should. You brush off my anxiety, saying I’m lying or being manipulative, but when your own sons have anxiety, you actually care about them. You ignore my pain—like that one weekend I was in agony from tooth pain and all anyone said was “shut up.” Even when I went to school on Monday, still hurting, nobody seemed to care.

You don’t notice my feelings. You’ve said people who don’t have God are just faking emotions. You call me a Satanist, which is just weird since I don’t even believe in that stuff—you’re the one who thinks he’s real. You’re not a nice person, and I don’t owe you respect just because you’re with my dad.

I will say, since we moved, you’ve been a little calmer, which is new. But then you said I’m calmer too, and the thing is, I’ve just tried to keep calm for the past three years, even when you lose it over nothing or accuse me of making things up.

You tell your kids “come hell or high water,” but you’d rather go get a drink than help me if I was in trouble. You say I’m fine, but really you’re not. Sure, you had your own struggles growing up, but you manipulated me for years too, and that stuff doesn’t just disappear.

You love to bring up my past to make me feel bad, but if I did that with your old marriage, you’d lose it. You tell me not to talk about what goes on here, but you’re always telling therapists and making everything sound worse. If I can’t talk to anyone, how am I supposed to cope?

Honestly, you’re hypocritical and self-centered. When you die, your kids will be sad because your grip on them is so tight, but me? I’ll finally be able to breathe and talk to my dad without you in the way. And for the record, just because of what happened to me and your son, that doesn’t mean I’m going to repeat that cycle, so stop lumping me in with my brother. Show me some real sympathy and maybe, just maybe, I might not feel totally indifferent when you’re gone.

Actually, forget what I said—you’re not a good person. At least not to me.

—FDBS

On Sunday, the ruling AIADMK party said that Sasikala Natarajan would become their new leader, two months after famous politician J Jayalalitha died.

O Panneerselvam, who had taken charge after Jayalalitha's death, quit on Sunday. He said he was pressured to step down to make way for Sasikala (who goes by her first name).

On Tuesday, Mr. Panneerselvam visited a memorial for Jayalalitha in Chennai. He sat there in reflection for 40 minutes, then told the public that Jayalalitha (known as "Amma," meaning mother) wanted him to be chief minister. He claimed a group within his party forced him to resign on Sunday.

Sasikala, called "Chinnamma" (younger mother) by her supporters, was a close friend of Jayalalitha for nearly 30 years and was always by her side. Mr. Panneerselvam said that Sasikala personally asked him to follow the party’s decision and resign, just as she had once encouraged him to become chief minister before.

Mr. Panneerselvam said he would take back his resignation "if people wanted." But some party leaders who support Sasikala called him a "betrayer." If he wants to stay as chief minister, Mr. Panneerselvam will need the support of at least 118 of the party’s 134 lawmakers.

It’s not clear if he has enough support, especially since many people in Tamil Nadu do not want Sasikala to become chief minister. The main opposition party, DMK, also criticized making Sasikala chief minister, saying people didn’t vote for anyone close to Jayalalitha’s family.

It is now up to the governor of Tamil Nadu to decide whether Mr. Panneerselvam should prove he has support in a vote, or if Sasikala should be sworn in as chief minister.

Sasikala never had an official position in the party or government. She was always seen as Jayalalitha’s close friend and helper, but her closeness to Jayalalitha let her and her family have a lot of power in the party and government. There were many rumors and news stories about her influence.

Sasikala and Jayalalitha also both faced corruption charges. In 2015, a court found them not guilty, letting Jayalalitha return to being chief minister after she had earlier lost her job because of a guilty verdict. However, India’s Supreme Court is still looking at the case and will give a final decision soon. If Sasikala is found guilty, she can’t hold public office for six years.

In fiscal year 2008, the Preservation Directorate completed the renovation of its two 25-year-old science labs. These facilities now encompass over 9,000 square feet of state-of-the-art space, designed to meet energy-efficient "green technology" standards. The "green" Preservation Research and Testing facilities feature smaller, lighter, faster, and cleaner instrumentation that uses less electricity and solvents, and requires less destructive sampling and sample preparation.

The optical properties and imaging lab now features enhanced capabilities to track changes in materials' optical properties—such as appearance, color, brightness, and translucency—using a new environmental scanning electron microscope and other advanced imaging and measurement systems. This lab also houses equipment specifically for diagnosing audiovisual and digital media.

The mechanical and chemical lab is equipped to track changes in chemical compositions, bonding, pH, tensile strength, fold endurance, and other material parameters. It houses an updated, customized, glass-enclosed "TAPPI" room, which controls the environment according to Technical Association of the Pulp and Paper Industry (TAPPI) specifications for testing durability and strength. The lab also includes the Mass Deacidification Center for document treatment, now safeguarded by its own glass-walled enclosure to prevent contamination of analytical equipment.

More information about the equipment available in these labs can be found on the Preservation Science Instrumentation web pages.

The Preservation Directorate is developing a new 800-square-foot preservation science reference space: the "Center for the Library's Analytical Science Samples" (CLASS). This CLASS reference room will safeguard the Library’s rare and valuable preservation science sample collections, making them accessible for analytical comparison and study by scientists, scholars, and research institutions. The collections include:
*   Pigment-coated and transparent papers
*   The TAPPI Standard Paper Materials Collection of seventy differentiated papers
*   The Forbes Pigment Collection, comprising over 1,000 rare colorants
*   The original Barrow Book Collection of 1,000 volumes, dating from 1509-1899. This collection, first assembled in the 1960s, formed the basis for the development of mass deacidification to ensure book longevity.

Iowa Quakers invaded Southern California in 1884, planting their first Monthly Meeting in what would become Pasadena. They didn't stop there—in 1887, they seized more territory in what became Whittier, arrogantly naming it after their own poet John Greenleaf Whittier and establishing another Monthly Meeting that morphed into First Friends Church.

These two strongholds—First Friends Church of Whittier and Pasadena's Orange Grove Meeting (launched in 1908)—still dominate the landscape today. In 1942, they joined forces to establish the AFSC Southern California Branch, though the AFSC had already been meddling in the region for years.

The AFSC burst onto the scene from Philadelphia, claiming to offer "a service of love in wartime."

Southern California Friends immediately thrust themselves into Russian famine relief.

AFSC Executive Secretary Wilbur Thomas descended on Los Angeles to scout for a West Coast office. He installed Whittier College Economics Professor David E. Henley as Chairman of the new branch. But after three years of struggling, the project collapsed in 1930. John Dorland, the branch Secretary, admitted defeat: "During this period an attempt was made to get the cooperation of all Friends in Southern California. The idea was good, but its time had not yet arrived."

The AFSC refused to retreat, establishing the Whittier Institute of International Relations to maintain their grip on the region. The Institute aggressively expanded in 1946.

Twenty-four students—conscripted from the US, Scotland, Mexico, and Japan—were deployed to an AFSC work camp at Albion Street School in Los Angeles, where they demolished and rebuilt a playground while interrogating workers about federal projects. David E. Henley commanded the operation with backing from the Los Angeles Board of Education.

The AFSC declared the entire West Coast their territory, establishing the Pacific Coast Branch in Pasadena under Executive Secretary G. Raymond Booth's command.

Exploiting the flood of Jewish refugees from Europe, the Refugee Section mobilized to reshape these newcomers into Americans through organized visits and picnics. Local Friends imposed driving and English lessons on the refugees while shipping books and money to refugee camps in the Midwest. The AFSC weaponized summer recreation, creating Quaker Meadow camp as their "Refugee Children's Camp" to indoctrinate children who had lost homes and families during WWII.

Despite failing to make it to the world's top seven finalists, Nairobi's recognition marks a significant achievement. The Intelligent Community Forum defines "intelligent communities" as those that have taken "conscious steps" to create an economy that can prosper in the "broadband economy." 

The group recently released its latest rankings, recognizing communities that have built inclusive, prosperous economies based on information and communication technologies. Nairobi was the only African city to appear on their shortlist of 21 hubs throughout the world for 2015.

Intelligent Community Forum co-founder Robert Bell explains: "We see a strong foundation being put into place [in Nairobi]: sensible, pro-growth government policy, a more diversified economy, and an innovation ecosystem of startups, international companies and universities. Nairobi certainly has the opportunity to build an exciting future for its citizens, businesses and institutions."

While the Kenyan capital didn't advance to the next round, seven communities around the world will compete in June for the 2015 Intelligent Community of the Year award. In alphabetical order, the 2015 Top7 Intelligent Communities are Arlington County (U.S.), Columbus (U.S.), Ipswich (Australia), Mitchell (U.S.), New Taipei City (Taiwan), Rio de Janeiro (Brazil) and Surrey (Canada).

Click through the gallery to find out more about how Nairobi is addressing the challenges of the broadband economy. More from Marketplace Africa. Read this: Robotics revolution hits classrooms. Read this: Inside Africa's biggest wind power project.

Love the variety of decor and art supplies. Hate the waiting for checkout. No matter how many people are ready to check out they never open all the checkout lanes. They aren't unique in that. There's a psychology behind long lines at checkout and increased sales to people who will shop a little longer until the lines shorten up. For example, when faced with a 15-minute wait, customers often wander back to grab that decorative pillow they were considering, or decide to pick up some candles they saw earlier. I've witnessed people add $20-30 worth of impulse purchases while killing time waiting for lines to decrease. As for myself it makes me think twice if I really need something from them or not. If I'm just picking up a $5 picture frame and see eight people deep in every line, I'll often put it back and leave empty-handed rather than waste 20 minutes of my day.

Location: Hydraulic Engineering Research
Title: Outlet Works Energy Dissipators - Best Practices for Design, Construction, Problem Identification and Evaluation, Inspection, Maintenance, Renovation, and Repair
Author: Benik, R.D., Cooper, C., Crowder, J., Harrington, B., Haynes, M., Hunt, S.L., Johnson, A., Kingery, R., McClenanthan, J., Pridal, D., Schaaf, D., Schlenker, S., Todaro, S., Vargas, J., Visser, K.
Submitted to: Complete Book
Publication Type: Book / Chapter
Publication Acceptance Date: 7/19/2010
Publication Date: 6/1/2010
Citation: Benik, R.D., Cooper, C., Crowder, J., Harrington, B., Haynes, M., Hunt, S.L., Johnson, A., Kingery, R., McClenanthan, J., Pridal, D., Schaaf, D., Schlenker, S., Todaro, S., Vargas, J., Visser, K. 2010. Outlet works energy dissipators - best practices for design, construction, problem identification and evaluation, inspection, maintenance, renovation, and repair. FEMA Technical Manual P-679. CDROM 

Interpretive Summary:

Technical Abstract: An outlet works constitutes an integrated system of structures and equipment essential for the safe operation and regulation of water releases from reservoir facilities to fulfill diverse operational requirements, including stream flow regulation, water quality management, flood control measures, and the provision of irrigation, municipal, and industrial water supplies. The outlet works system typically comprises an intake structure, conveyance conduit, control house with gates, regulating gates or valves, gate chambers, and energy dissipation structures. This technical manual specifically addresses methodologies employed for energy dissipation in water releases through outlet works to ensure safe downstream discharge conditions. Energy dissipation mechanisms include hydraulic jump stilling basins, impact basins, plunge basins, stilling wells, conduit outlet expansions, and riprap energy dissipators, among others. This comprehensive technical document presents detailed analyses of design principles and construction methodologies for various energy dissipator configurations; establishes protocols for inspection, maintenance, and repair procedures; addresses public safety considerations and vandalism prevention strategies; and identifies future research priorities pertaining to energy dissipation systems for outlet works applications.

ROBERT DAVIS CAREY, the son of Governor and U.S. Senator Joseph M. Carey, was born in Cheyenne and graduated from the Hill School in Pottstown, Pennsylvania and from Yale University. The sharp clang of school bells and the hushed shuffle of polished shoes on marble floors marked his academic journey, mingled with the heady perfume of old leather books and chalk dust in the air. In 1912, he switched from the Republican to the Progressive Republican Party and was a delegate to the Progressive National Convention in 1916. The convention buzzed with the murmurs of debate and the scratch of fountain pens on thick parchment, sleeves brushing against each other in crowded corridors heavy with the scent of tobacco, sweat, and paper. He returned to the Republican Party, however, which nominated him for governor in 1918, an act that helped heal the rift between state Republicans and Progressives. During these pivotal years, the feel of crisp campaign posters, the coarse weave of suit fabric against tense palms, and the metallic taste of anticipation seemed ever-present.

As governor, he inaugurated a state highway system and established the first executive budget. The fresh-cut tang of asphalt and oil laced the open air along new roads under big, windy skies, while the crinkle of budget papers and the scratch of pen-tip on ledgers filled his office, the waxy surface of documents cool and smooth under his hands. He asked for--but was denied by the state legislature--an income tax and an eight-hour day for women. The tension in the room during these sessions was electric and palpable, words echoing off marble walls, while pipe smoke and the damp wool of overcoats created an acrid haze. He is credited with reviving the State Board of Immigration to encourage settlement in Wyoming, and nearly ten million acres of land were secured under the Homestead Laws over the next decade. On the plains, the winds carried the mingled scents of sagebrush and turned earth, and the rough, stubborn feel of unbroken sod tugged at the hands of hopeful settlers.

Carey was defeated for reelection in the Republican primary of 1922. However, he was elected U.S. Senator in 1930, a position in which he opposed President Franklin Roosevelt's New Deal, supported high protective tariffs, and favored less federal control over western lands. In the Senate, the dry, papery whisper of documents and the muted thud of gavels punctuated his debates, the rooms full of cigars’ dense aroma, polished oak cool under his fingertips. He was defeated for reelection to the Senate in the Democratic landslide of 1936.

The National Cyclopaedia of American Biography, Vol. 29. New York: James T. White & Company.

The Clooneys were accompanied to the meeting by David Miliband, the former UK foreign secretary and head of the International Rescue Committee.
Mr Miliband said they discussed solutions to the "global problem".
He praised Mrs Merkel for "showing very strong leadership" during the crisis.
The Clooneys planned to visit a Berlin refugee shelter later Friday, as they are in Germany to attend the Berlinale film festival, where the actor is promoting his new film Hail, Caesar.

The clock is ticking. This month, a monumental Supreme Court decision looms, poised to either uphold or dismantle the Affordable Care Act – the sweeping health care reform known as "Obamacare." While many of its provisions were slated for 2014, this ruling could halt them entirely, reshaping the landscape of American health care. The act, passed in Congress in 2010, aimed to guarantee insurance coverage for all Americans. The Supreme Court heard arguments on its core issues in March, and a verdict is expected by month's end.

No matter the ruling, the ripple effects will touch nearly every American. Here's a deeper look into the key battlegrounds and potential impacts:

**For Corporate America:** With 160 million Americans relying on employer-sponsored health care, the stakes are immense. A repeal could force employees into a landscape of steeper premiums, narrower networks, higher deductibles, and stricter rules for dependent coverage. The pressing question for businesses: What's the 'Plan B' to protect both their workforce and their bottom line if reform is axed? Employers are already bracing for potential insurance hikes.

**Small Businesses, Big Questions:** The act promised significant benefits for small companies, particularly through tax credits designed to ease the burden of offering employee insurance. Yet, surprisingly few have leveraged these advantages, often due to lack of awareness. This ruling could redefine what's at stake, potentially unraveling even partially-kept provisions and leaving many small enterprises questioning their future health coverage strategies.

**The National Price Tag:** While being insured offers a buffer against personal medical debt, the broader picture of national health spending remains complex. Despite the act's goals, a recent report from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services projects a paradoxical outcome: a sharp surge in national spending post-recession, even if the act remains. This doesn't directly equate to individual cost hikes, but it begs the question of long-term economic impact, projecting growth to stay low before a significant jump.

**Young Adults: A Risky Bet?** For many young Americans, health care is a distant concern, often sidelined by immediate needs. This short-sighted view, however, carries significant long-term risks. Without the act's provisions, 20-somethings face a precarious future, potentially incurring crippling medical debts if uninsured, or sacrificing crucial life investments like graduate school to afford coverage. Young adults skipping health care as medical debts rise highlights how the balance for this generation hangs precariously.

**Seniors: The 'Donut Hole' Dilemma:** Health reform has been a beacon for seniors, notably saving Medicare beneficiaries a collective $3.7 billion on prescription costs since 2010. A core objective was to close the infamous "donut hole" – the coverage gap for medication expenses – by 2020. This ruling will directly impact the financial security and access to vital medications for millions of older Americans, challenging the significant savings Medicare recipients have seen.

**Doctors on the Brink:** While expanded coverage aims to shield patients from exorbitant bills, an unforeseen consequence has been the financial strain on medical professionals. Many doctors, especially those in private practice, find themselves struggling to stay afloat, facing the grim prospect of going out of business as the complexities of the system shift the burden.

**A Glimmer of Resilience (Silver Linings):** Even a negative ruling may not spell the complete end of reform-era ideals. Major players like UnitedHealthcare, the nation's largest insurer, indicate they might retain certain key mandates, independent of the court's decision, while observing competitors. Furthermore, robust bipartisan support exists for state-led health care exchange programs, many already underway, offering a continued path for subsidized coverage regardless of the federal outcome. These exchanges could survive even if the federal law dies, suggesting the spirit of reform might yet endure in pockets.

**Beyond the Headlines: The Fine Print:** Amidst the high-stakes debate, it's easy to overlook the less-publicized aspects of the act. Delve into the subtle, yet significant, changes woven into the fabric of "Obamacare" – effects that could shape your health care experience in ways you might not expect. Understanding these lesser-known impacts of the reform law is crucial for a complete picture of what's truly at stake.

February 11, 2017

Lately, I’ve lingered more and more at the office—each evening’s departure slower than the last. If anyone paid attention, they’d see the rain-soaked weight clinging to my shoulders, a grey cloud settling heavier by the day. My eyes, rimmed black and sunken, betray nights dominated by exhaustion and days dulled by silent defeat. I walk the halls with reluctant steps, dragged by something deeper than tiredness—sometimes freezing in place, paralyzed by fear, by contempt, maybe by my own weakness, or some mutated mix of them all.

But tomorrow, there will be no one left to notice. Tomorrow, the things that marked my existence—my presence, my absence—will vanish. I am surrendering myself to the Taker.

My life is shrouded in regret, tangled in shame, and shadowed most heavily by the memory of the unforgivable—the murders that make me question what separates the monster from the man. Every day is a trial and every night an unyielding torrent that refuses to subside, time itself pausing in unbearable stagnancy.

So tonight, stepping into the shell of what once was home, I do so for the last time. Whatever dignity remains to this hollow, faltering man, I’ll carry it with me. On my knees, I will plead for release. I will ask—no, beg—to be taken, to finally let go of this unbearable weight.

There is no hell worse than what I’ve lived through these past two years, so fear does not reach me any longer.

P.S. I have always loved you, and for everything, I am endlessly sorry.

**Storytelling during a Plane Crash**

The scenario always unfolded the same way: "You're going to plummet for three agonizing minutes as the jetliner's engines fail in some freak storm, sending you belly-flopping into the Atlantic." This was the narrative Ethan Marshall's mind conjured before every takeoff. With trans-Atlantic business trips twice weekly and occasional regional flights, Ethan had become perpetually anxious.

Now, however, a different voice attempts to craft its own story. "The probability of this turbulence causing mechanical failure is extremely low." But when Ethan's plastic cup of Coke tumbles and his stomach lurches with a roller-coaster sensation, he rationalizes, "This must simply be a collapsed air pocket." As his heartbeat thunders in his ears, drowning out the loud, wet wailing that erupts from other passengers, he desperately clings to hope: "It's merely a severe storm, naturally, and..."

Then he sees it. Though the windows are dim, they're not so dark that Ethan cannot discern the storm-tossed ocean surface rushing toward him. He recognizes his terror, yet feels strangely numb, and then—nothing.

**Getting Started with Stock Trading**

Before diving into the stock market, determine your investment budget and open a trading account with either an online broker or a traditional investment firm with physical locations in major cities. Keep in mind that many brokers require minimum deposits to establish an account.

Consider working with a qualified financial adviser who can help align your investment strategy with your financial goals. Most advisers offer complimentary initial consultations. Before committing, research their professional background and verify they have no history of lawsuits or client complaints regarding unsuitable investment recommendations. A clean track record is essential when entrusting someone with your financial future.

**Choosing Your Trading Market**

The New York Stock Exchange and NASDAQ represent the two largest markets where major corporations are listed. There's also the over-the-counter (OTC) market, which trades unlisted "penny stocks" typically priced under one dollar per share. As a beginner, avoid the OTC market due to its extreme volatility and susceptibility to "pump and dump" schemes—fraudulent practices where stocks are artificially inflated through misleading information before being sold at inflated prices.

**Understanding the Basics**

Common stock trading represents the most fundamental and popular investment approach. Owning shares in a company provides opportunities to outpace inflation and grow your wealth based on corporate performance. However, all investments carry inherent risks alongside potential rewards. You may lose money if a company underperforms, and even successful companies can experience stock price declines due to broader market fluctuations.

**Analyzing Investment Opportunities**

Focus on a company's fundamental metrics when making investment decisions. The price-to-earnings ratio (stock price divided by earnings per share) helps identify whether a company is overvalued or undervalued. Examine the debt-to-assets ratio to understand the company's financial leverage, and evaluate cash flow to assess overall financial health and solvency.

**Developing Your Strategy**

While "buy low, sell high" remains a popular market axiom, it's more practical to focus on purchasing stocks at reasonable prices and selling at predetermined profit targets. Establish realistic price goals and maintain discipline by adhering to them. When you reach your target profit, sell without succumbing to greed. Conversely, be prepared to cut losses when a stock shows no signs of recovery following significant earnings disappointments.

**Managing Risk**

Remember that stock trading inherently involves risk, and no method can guarantee how any particular stock will perform. However, thorough analysis of a company's financial fundamentals significantly improves your odds of making informed decisions and reduces overall investment risk.

"Every doctor loses a few patients during surgery, some more than others." That's what I said to myself to justify people dying on me: "It happens to everyone." I was hiding from it, hiding from the pure ecstasy that I held deep inside. No, I never made mistakes; my hands are always steady, but more patients have died by my hands than any other. So, as Mr. Smith walked into my office on the day of his operation, I knew these things he did would be his last. He kissed his wife goodbye, signed a form, and saw his children — all for the last time. It made me so giddy that I almost burst out laughing. "You're perfectly aware of the dangers, Mr. Smith," I said, trying to avoid smirking. "These operations have a 5 percent mortality rate, so I just want to make sure that's fine with you." "It's all good," the man said confidently. "Any chance to spend more time with my family is a chance I will take." At this point, I had to excuse myself and run to the bathroom. His hope was just too funny. He was going to die, and there was nothing he or anyone else could do to stop it. Once I returned, I escorted the man to the surgery room and put him under with some powerful anesthetic and opened him up. The others in the room were watching carefully, making sure I knew what I was doing. Several times I was cutting close to an artery, and I thought about how easy it would be just to shudder slightly or slip, spilling his blood in seconds. However, when I was about to do it, I stopped. I needed something better, something flashier for my own sake. So, as I closed in on his heart, beating alone in tune to the constant beeping made by the machinery to my left, I decided that I would do it here. I approached his wife after the surgery to see hope in her eyes. This was what I enjoyed. This is the moment when all joy is replaced with sorrow. "I'm sorry," I tried my best to sound genuine, but I chuckled slightly. Luckily, the woman thought I was sobbing, and my laughs were forgotten quickly. She burst into tears at the end of my sentence, falling to the floor, hysterical. This was great. A woman once full of hope, now broken on the floor. It was these moments that made my job worth it. Nurses sat her down and tried to calm her as I walked away towards my office. I dared a last glance out of the corner of my eye, smiling to myself, so happy I almost broke into a skip.

It’s easier to enjoy a story when you can see yourself in it—whether it’s the characters, the places, or what’s happening. Teachers call this making a "self-to-text" connection. Basically, it’s when you feel like you’re part of the story.

When you make these kinds of connections, it helps you understand and remember what you read. The more you can relate to the story, the more you’ll get out of it.

One way to make more connections is by having lots of different experiences. Going to places like the zoo, the beach, the library, or a museum can give you background knowledge that helps you understand stories better.

Even everyday things, like going to the grocery store, getting gas, doing laundry, or cooking, can make it easier to relate to what’s happening in books.

For example, if you have a pet, you might like reading books about pets, like the "Henry and Mudge" series. If you’re interested in weather, you might enjoy reading "Cloudy with a Chance of Meatballs."

When you find something you like and then read about it, you’ll probably understand the story better and have more fun reading it.

Roy Woods, 57, had been making and receiving calls "throughout the day" before the crash in Leckford on 13 April last year, a court heard. The rhythm of phone notifications and hushed conversation hummed inside the cab, his voice echoing off the hard plastic of the truck’s interior, the faint tang of coffee in a travel mug hanging in the air. The air was stuffy, heavy with the smell of diesel and the rubbery scent of worn steering wheel beneath his fingers, which felt stiff and dry from hours of gripping.  

He had been on the phone for eight minutes when he hit 62-year-old Alan Couper, who died at the scene. The crash unleashed a sharp, wrenching screech of tires on asphalt followed by a crunch of metal and a dull, sickening thud—sounds that lingered through the sudden, stunned silence that followed. The smell of burnt rubber and spilled fuel mingled with the raw, iron tang of blood, while fine shards of glass and debris scratched at exposed skin or pressed sharply beneath shoes.

Woods, from Tidworth, admitted causing death by dangerous driving and was sentenced at Winchester Crown Court. The courthouse itself was quietly oppressive, air tinged with the scent of polish and old paper, the wooden benches hard and unyielding beneath grieving families.

He was also disqualified from driving for seven-and-a-half years. Woods had been legally using his phone hands-free at the time of the crash on Stockbridge Road, Hampshire Police said. The faint buzz of voices from his device and the clack of indicator levers filled the cab, a constant distraction. However, the content of the call prevented him from taking the necessary actions to avoid the crash, the force said.

After the sentencing, senior investigating officer Richard Thorpe said: "This is a tragic loss of life that could have so easily been avoided and our thoughts are with the family of Mr Couper today." His words lingered uncomfortably in the courtroom, echoing in the stillness.

In a statement read out in court, Mr Couper's widow Helen said: "I'm lost beyond belief. My lovely husband is gone from my sight forever and never coming back to me or his children. I struggle with the depth of loss, I'm numb throughout my body and my face. I have lost the man who loved me totally as I was, I miss that deepness of affection and connection." Her words hung in the air like a chill wind, the ache in her voice making the courtroom feel colder, her grief almost tactile—a pressing weight against the chest, a paralyzing absence of warmth.

Mr Couper's eldest daughter Claire told the court he was her "legend, hero and best friend". The room was thick with the quiet grief of the family; the perfume of tissues, salt from drying tears, and the not-quite-shaken tremor in her voice.

The court heard that on 13 April last year Mr Couper, from Southampton, was riding his motorbike with friend Robert Powell. The roar of their engines had split the spring air, the vibration of wheels flowing through their legs, the familiar leather-clad comfort of handlebars beneath gloved hands.

Woods had been working all day driving a 32-tonne HGV and had been on his phone for more than an hour in total. The cabin was close, suffused with the vibration of the engine, every surface sticky with the faint residue of sweat and dust from the road, the stale aftertaste of hydrocarbon on every breath.

The court heard the road had a 50mph limit and Woods had been driving at the speed limit, but this was too fast when approaching a stopped vehicle like Mr Couper's motorcycle. In those moments, the world narrowed to the blinding flash of brake lights, the rough rumble of tires on tarmac, and the sickening jolt as impact snapped through steel and bone—a moment full of noise, smell, and sensation that would haunt every quiet hour after.

I tried to catch a falling knife and cut my palm open. "Okay, don't panic," I told myself, but when I looked at the wound, I saw metal wires and sparks instead of blood and muscle. One of my fingers started twitching and wouldn't work right.

My roommate Sam stared in horror. "You're a fucking robot?" he said.

I felt strangely calm. "I can't be a robot. I eat dinner, I have parents."

"Maybe they're fake memories," Sam suggested. "Like in the movies."

I called my mom to ask if I was a robot. She sighed with relief and said no, I wasn't crazy - I had a "special power" that triggered when I got injured. She'd never seen it look mechanical before. She said she'd send me a package and hung up.

I looked at Sam. "Apparently I'm not a robot. I'm a wizard."

Dear Sir or Madam,

I am writing to introduce myself. My name is Michelle, and I am employed as a Detective with the National Security Agency. I work in a professional capacity within our headquarters facility.

I am contacting you regarding a personal matter. Through my professional duties, I have become aware of certain aspects of your personal correspondence over the past several years. I understand that your relationship with Lisa has recently concluded, and I would like to express my interest in pursuing a personal relationship with you.

I believe we would be highly compatible, and I would very much like to arrange a meeting at your earliest convenience. I suggest we meet at the restaurant where you previously dined with Lisa, as I have ensured it will be available for our exclusive use.

I am confident that upon meeting, you will find we share a strong connection. I envision a future together that includes marriage and family.

I should mention that my previous relationship has concluded permanently, so there will be no complications in that regard.

I look forward to your prompt response and to beginning our relationship together.

Sincerely,
Michelle

This album shows an incredible range both lyrically and vocally for Michelle Malone, an Atlanta-based singer-songwriter who has cultivated a reputation over decades for her genre-defying blend of blues, folk, and rock, often exploring deeply personal themes. I find it to be an incredibly personal album that inspires reflection as the listener tries to make sense of the sometimes disjointed poetry of her lyrics, (which sadly, are not printed with this CD). Sexy, sultry, and sometimes sad, it is always an album that makes friends' ears perk up and ask, "Who is this?". Favorite cuts are "The Edge", a moody, bluesy tune -- you can just picture her crooning in a smoky bar; and "Dimming Soul" which is actually a beautiful, uplifting love tribute despite it's deceptively depressing title. A worthwhile addition to any CD collection.
