Gluten Free 101

Until recent years, "gluten" was a word few diners cared much about, let alone restaurant chefs and servers. At its core, gluten is a protein composite generally found in foods containing wheat, rye, barley, malt, and other grains. It's gluten we have to thank for making dough rise and for providing airy structure to baked goods.

However, it's not all chewy bread and fluffy cakes—for a growing number of people, gluten consumption causes intense intestinal discomfort. While the degree of gluten intolerance varies drastically, the most severe form is celiac disease, a digestive disorder in which ingested gluten destroys the intestine over time, preventing absorption of nutrients.

Gluten is found in most processed foods, even those that don't specifically list wheat or grains as ingredients. Consequently, a gluten-free diet typically eliminates all processed foods as well as foods containing wheat and grain products.

The gluten-free diet has grown in popularity in recent years. Many people, even those without a doctor's formal diagnosis, adopt the diet hoping to reduce discomfort and improve quality of life. Others, equating the diet with healthy eating, adopt it hoping to lose weight, leading many to question whether gluten-free eating is just another diet fad.

Despite the debate, increased public awareness has led to more gluten-free options at restaurants. While this makes dining out easier and more enjoyable for those with gluten intolerances, cross-contamination remains a lingering concern. Cross-contamination can occur when gluten-free foods are prepared in the same kitchen space or with the same utensils as foods containing gluten. Precautionary measures must be taken to avoid cross-contamination, including separate pizza pans for gluten-free pies, different pots for boiling gluten-free pasta, dedicated gluten-free fryers, and distinct gluten-free prep areas. Most importantly, chefs, cooks, and servers must be knowledgeable about the impact cross-contamination can have on their patrons.

Tips for eating gluten-free at a restaurant:
• Call the restaurant to verify it can accommodate your dietary restrictions.
• Inform the server of your allergy and/or dietary restrictions.
• Ask if the restaurant has a separate gluten-free menu or a list of gluten-free items.
• Ask how the food is prepared to address cross-contamination concerns.
• Don't hesitate to send your order back if it has been incorrectly prepared.

Restaurant review platforms can help you find restaurants that accommodate gluten-free dining by searching for establishments marked as "Gluten Free Friendly."

"Welcome! It's such a pleasure to have you join us for dinner," John said warmly, greeting his guest. "Please, allow me to take your coat." Andrew hesitated briefly, then offered John a polite smile. "Thank you, but I wouldn’t dream of troubling you. I did want to bring along a 1963 red, though. I hope it complements your discerning palate, my friend!" 

John accepted the wine with a gracious nod, masking his discomfort. "You truly have exceptional taste—this is a remarkable choice. Thank you, that's most generous of you! Please, let me get your chair. Make yourself comfortable. May I offer you some water? I thought we might save the wine for dinner, to ensure it’s paired perfectly with the meal." 

Andrew dabbed discreetly at his own face and returned the host’s hospitality with a smile. "Everything smells wonderful in here—may I ask what delicious dishes you’ve prepared tonight?"

John cleared his throat into a napkin before replying. "I’ve made seared salmon, braised steak, and a full rump roast. Please, feel free to choose whichever appeals to you most; there are also a selection of sides and three different salads as well." 

Andrew suppressed a cough and nodded in appreciation. "You’re far too kind. With so many tempting options, I hardly know how to decide!"

John steadied himself, maintaining composure. "It would be my pleasure to serve you. I imagine you must be quite hungry after your journey."

Andrew struggled in his seat, clearly unwell, but managed a few words. "I wouldn’t want to impose—"

John interrupted gently, "It's no trouble at all. Let me bring the dishes so you can choose what you’d most enjoy. Then we can open that exceptional wine together."

Andrew gasped, clutching his chest as he collapsed in his chair. As John watched, his guest’s last moments were marked by a final, piercing glare before all was still. 

John composed himself, quietly murmuring, "At last, that’s over."

Atheism has become the world's intellectual authority. The church held this position when religion was at its peak. In recent decades science and technology have given people so much power and free time that philosophers, scientists, and technologists are sought to answer the most difficult questions.

Philosophers, scientists, and technologists share with atheists a willingness to ask questions. Atheism is a state of mind—a way of being. Atheists, free thinkers, agnostics, and humanists fundamentally ask questions and doubt everything. They habitually investigate what underlies things. They do not believe anything absolutely. They know that absolute belief closes the door on further learning. The useful knowledge gained from this approach shows that Atheism represents the learning edge.

The term Atheism generally includes atheists, humanists, agnostics, free thinkers, and all who cannot make the leap-of-faith required to believe in a god or accept religion. Some atheists see religion as a nuisance, while others recognize the harm it causes.

When philosophers, scientists, technologists, or atheists face a difficult problem they know answers come from formulating pertinent questions. They do not take a leap-of-faith to the first answer offered.

Brave atheists must face the severity of the world’s turmoil. Monumental atrocities are rampant:
- Population has increased beyond the planet's capacity.
- The planet is becoming chemical soup.
- Countless species are becoming extinct.
- Deserts are increasing.
- Global warming and ozone depletion concerns are growing.
- Fish stocks are declining.
- Clean water supply is decreasing.
- Forests are being destroyed and not replaced.
- Topsoil is limited and eroding.
- Unstoppable disease is possible.

These are the main environmental atrocities. Equally serious are the social ones:
- Fifteen million die from hunger each year.
- An equal number are blinded or maimed for life by malnutrition.
- One in five lives in abject poverty.
- Wars fueled by religious differences kill and injure many.
- Political upheavals and economic instability are constant threats.
- Greed and crime are increasing and worsening.

All these atrocities are related, directly or indirectly. Overpopulation is the root cause of many environmental problems, but poverty-stricken people have more children than those with healthy incomes.

Propagating false information about messiahs makes matters worse. Every effort is needed to get individuals to face the global condition. This includes taking away their imaginary gods and revealing the insignificance of their folklore about messiahs.

Submitted to: Insect Pathology
Publication Type: Book / Chapter
Publication Acceptance Date: 7/4/2011
Publication Date: 2/16/2012
Citation: Harrison, R.L., Hoover, K. 2012. Chapter 4: Baculoviruses and other occluded insect viruses. In: Vega, F. E., Kaya, H. K, editors. Insect Pathology. 2nd edition. London, England: Academic Press. p. 73-131. Interpretive Summary:
Technical Abstract: Baculoviruses rank among the most thoroughly studied insect pathogens. Members of the Baculoviridae family possess a large, circular double-stranded DNA genome housed within the enveloped nucleoprotein core of a rod-shaped virion. Baculovirus replication is unique, producing two distinct virion phenotypes: occlusion-derived virions (ODV), found within proteinaceous viral occlusions, which initiate host infection; and budded virions (BV), which subsequently spread the infection to other cells and tissues within the host. Isolated exclusively from insects, baculoviruses initially garnered significant interest due to their potential in insect pest management. The development of cell culture systems for propagating baculoviruses, coupled with the application of molecular biology methodologies, transformed them into popular expression vectors and invaluable research tools. This progress profoundly expanded our knowledge and understanding of these viruses. This chapter provides a comprehensive summary of the current state of baculovirology. It delves into baculovirus classification, taxonomy, and phylogeny, alongside features of related virus groups. Key topics include the basic characteristics of baculovirus virions and viral occlusions; mechanisms of infection, gene expression, and replication; their pathology, ecology, and use as pesticides; and potential new frontiers for research into this fascinating group of viruses.

It was evening when I met him—and I suppose that makes sense, given what happened. I was extremely drunk and heading to Jennifer’s house. I was walking, not out of responsibility, but because I had crashed my car a few weeks earlier while driving drunk. Jennifer lived on the other side of the Mansfield cemetery, which is known as a creepy place, especially at night. Not that I cared much, considering how drunk I was, but people in town often tell scary stories about places like that.

The night was warm, but the shadows were long. Many of the streetlights near the cemetery were old and broken; local kids had messed them up and nobody ever fixed them. Inside the cemetery, it was pitch dark—there weren’t any lights at all. So I found myself stumbling through the darkness, determined to convince Jennifer to take me back. I don’t remember much of my walk until I saw something falling from the sky. It was dark, silent, and crashed down to earth like a meteor made of pure shadow. The shape was huge, like a vulture—a massive, black bird you’d expect to see in legends or dreams. Some people call this kind of creature a “thunderbird” in folklore.

The thing landed in a drainage ditch at the edge of the cemetery. I expected it to hit the ground with a loud thud, but didn’t hear anything. Curious, I walked over to see it up close—but when I got there, it was gone. Instead, I found what looked at first like a homeless man sleeping in the ditch. Then I realized he was hurt—badly. He reached out to me, and I helped him get to his feet. His face was bloody, and his beard was soaked with blood. His jacket looked like it had bullet holes in it, and he looked really sick. His face was swollen and oddly colored—almost like the pale grubs you find under rocks or, in the darkest stories, eating away at corpses in graveyards like this one.

Immediately, I sobered up. I forgot about Jennifer and focused on helping this man. I supported him as we made our way to a nearby gas station that also worked as a small diner, hoping to call an ambulance. It turned out the place was closed for the night, but they left their lights on to deter burglars. The man didn’t speak until we got there, but then he asked me for some change to use the pay phone—or maybe he didn’t directly, but I could tell what he needed, and I gave him what I had left from buying booze earlier.

He made a phone call, speaking a language I didn’t recognize. After he hung up, he turned to me. “Thank you, my friend,” he said in a deep voice that was clearly not used to speaking English, but he went on. “You do not know how helpful you have been.” Now that we were standing under the orange glow of the streetlight, I could see he wasn’t just some random vagrant. His hooded jacket, which had seemed filthy before, was actually very high quality. His features were striking and looked oddly ancient—not just old, but as if he had lived for centuries. Even wounded, he seemed strong in a way that’s hard to explain.

“I have no money to give you,” he said, “but I think immortality is a fair price for what you have done for me tonight.”

A statement from Prince William was read at a French trial. Six people were accused of violating his and his wife's private life and helping in the act.

The images were taken in 2012 when Prince William and his wife, Catherine, the Duchess of Cambridge, were on holiday in Provence, France. The photos appeared in the French magazine Closer. Separately, the French regional newspaper La Provence printed pictures of Catherine in her swimwear.

Paris-based agency photographers Cyril Moreau and Dominique Jacovides are accused of taking intrusive, zoomed-in photos of the royals from a public road. These included topless pictures of Catherine, the Duchess of Cambridge.

The trial is being held in Nanterre, near Paris. Others accused in the case are Laurence Pieau, the editor of Closer; Ernesto Mauri, the chief executive of the Mondadori group (which owns the magazine); Valerie Suau, a photographer for La Provence; and Marc Auburtin, who was the publishing director of La Provence at the time.

A prosecutor asked the court to impose "very significant fines". Meanwhile, a lawyer for Prince William and Catherine asked for "very large financial compensation".

Paul-Albert Iweins, representing Closer magazine, stated that Prince William and Catherine were seeking 1.5 million euros (about £1.3 million) in compensation. He argued that the couple was already often in the public eye, including having their wedding broadcast, and that the photos didn't actually violate their privacy and even showed them in a good way.

Prince William and Catherine had been staying at a large country house (chateau) in Provence. It was owned by Viscount David Linley, who is Queen Elizabeth II's nephew.

Prince William's written statement was read aloud by the couple's lawyer, Jean Veil. He said: "My wife and I believed we could go to France for a few days to a private villa owned by a family member and enjoy our privacy there. We know France and the French people, and we know they are generally respectful of private life, including that of their guests."

He added: "The secret and sneaky way these photographs were taken was particularly shocking to us because it violated our privacy." He explained the images were "all the more painful" because of what happened to his mother, Diana, Princess of Wales. She died in a car crash in Paris in 1997 while being chased by photographers.

The court was told that mobile phone records placed Mr. Moreau, 32, and Mr. Jacovides, 59, in the area between September 4 and 6, 2012, which is when the topless pictures are thought to have been taken. Both men deny taking the pictures used by Closer.

Photographs of Catherine in her swimwear, published in La Provence, are believed to have been taken by Ms. Suau, 53. However, she told the court she had no intention of violating the couple's privacy.

Prince William and Catherine are not expected to attend the court, which will announce its decision on July 4.

In 2012, they started a lawsuit. A Paris court then banned Closer (which is a different publication from the UK's Closer magazine) from printing any more of the images.

At the time, Prince William and Catherine's office at St James's Palace released a statement. It described the incident as "reminiscent of the worst intrusions by the press and paparazzi during the life of Prince William's mother, Diana, Princess of Wales".

**Introduction:**
This film adaptation fails to capture the essence of its source material and stands as a disappointing entry in the war movie genre.

**Body:**
The movie does not live up to either the original book or the previous film adaptation. The plot lacks coherence and direction, leaving viewers confused about the story's purpose. The production appears to be a transparent attempt to capitalize on the success of Saving Private Ryan, resulting in a formulaic and uninspired effort. Talented actors are unfortunately wasted in roles that fail to showcase their abilities. Even veterans who lived through the actual events, like my grandfather who served in the Pacific during World War II, found the film unconvincing and poorly executed. The dialogue is particularly weak, creating scenes that drag and fail to engage the audience.

**Conclusion:**
Ultimately, this movie is not worth the time investment. I would only recommend it to viewers seeking a sleep aid, as the tedious dialogue and lackluster storytelling will likely put audiences to sleep rather than entertain them.

Coffee? Not really my thing. Never has been. Honestly, I can’t even stand the stuff—it gives me a headache just thinking about it. But, you know what? I am absolutely the “coffee with the cute girl from work after finally (and awkwardly) asking her out” kind of guy. So here I am, walking up to the coffee shop, taking a deep breath before I push the door open.

There she is. Corner booth. Brown hair, pretty face, and—let’s just say—a very perky attitude. Yeah, attitude. Let’s stick with that. She spots me as I walk in, so I give her a nod, a grin, and what I hope is a cool little salute. She smiles back. Suddenly, Busted’s “Falling For You” is playing in my head. Scratch that—it’s actually playing in the shop. For a second, I think this might be my moment.

She waves me over with her left hand—and that’s when I notice the ring. On her finger. Well, damn.

Indian opener Murali Vijay **slaughtered** Australia in their Brisbane fortress with a brilliant 144 to help **propel** the tourists to 311 for four at the close of play on the opening day of the second Test on Wednesday. The 30-year-old **forged** an opening partnership of 56 with Shikhar Dhawan, stalled a little in the sweltering Gabba heat after lunch and then **exploded** after reaching his fifth Test century in the final session. His innings came to an end after five-and-a-half hours when he stepped down the pitch in search of a 23rd boundary and was caught behind off Nathan Lyon, Australia's off-spinning hero from their 48-run victory in the first Test. Indian opener Murali Vijay **slaughtered** Australia in their Brisbane fortress with a brilliant 144 . Vijay (pictured sweeping Nathan Lyon during the second Test) reached his fifth century for India . Australia's players, including Brad Haddin (second right), have ice lollies in an attempt to keep cool . 'It was mentally challenging but when you are playing for your country you've got to do your stuff to the best of your ability,' Vijay said. 'You can only go close to perfection, I think I did pretty well today.' Ajinkya Rahane, who **constructed** a 124-run partnership with Vijay, will resume on 75 not out on day two along with Rohit Sharma (26) as India look to inflict a first defeat on Australia at the Gabba since 1988. It was a **brutal** first day as stand-in Australia skipper for Steve Smith, who lost Mitchell Marsh to a hamstring problem after the all-rounder had **snared** his first Test victim in Dhawan with the only wicket to fall before lunch. Debutant paceman Josh Hazlewood was also forced to leave the field midway through his 16th over, having **captured** his first two Test wickets after lunch. A miserable day for the Marsh family was **crushed** when Mitchell's brother Shaun, recalled to replace injured captain Michael Clarke, dropped Vijay on 36 and again on 102. Test debutant Josh Hazlewood (right) limps off as captain Steve Smith (second left) loses another bowler . Australia lost so many bowling options they were forced to give opening batsman David Warner a whirl . Warner's fiancee Candice Falzon kept nice and cool watching Australia toil in the field at the Gabba . 'Eventful day,' said Australia coach Darren Lehmann. 'It would be nice to have a full contingent out there. 'We've got get back in the game, they **destroyed** us today.' Vijay had **demonstrated** his rich vein of form with knocks of 53 and 99 in the first test in Adelaide and fully vindicated returning skipper captain Mahendra Singh Dhoni's decision to bat first after winning the toss. **Smashing** his third half century of the series with a drive through the covers for four in the first over after lunch, he was then forced to weather a period of pressure as Hazlewood **demolished** Cheteshwar Pujara (18) and Virat Kohli (19). Vijay kept his cool through a second session in which the tourists were only able to add 62 runs to their lunch score of 89-1 but **unleashed** his stride again when the bowlers tired in the afternoon sun. Successive fours off Shane Watson **rocketed** him to his century but he initially looked unaware of having reached the milestone before whipping off his helmet and raising his bat to the visitors' dressing room. The temperature was so hot that an Australian reporter fried an egg on the outfield. Ajinkya Rahane, who **constructed** a 124-run partnership with Vijay, will resume on 75 not out on day two . Shane Watson (right) came in for some tap from the Indian batsman on a **devastating** day .

🚀 NASA just tested a flying saucer for Mars missions! 

The rocket-powered test vehicle launched from Hawaii and reached near-space speeds, testing breakthrough tech that could revolutionize how we land on Mars:

🍩 A giant inflatable air brake that slowed the craft from 3.8x to 2x the speed of sound
🪂 The largest supersonic parachute ever flown - twice the size of the one that delivered Curiosity rover!

"We are literally re-writing the books on high-speed parachute operations," said NASA's Ian Clark.

HD cameras captured incredible footage, giving scientists unprecedented insights into high-altitude flight dynamics. This tech could be game-changing for future Mars exploration! 

#NASA #Mars #SpaceExploration #Innovation #Science

An enjoyable read and what seems like genuinely practical advice—a rare and valuable combination. I wouldn’t call myself a wine novice, but more of a wine apprentice, still developing my palate and understanding. Living in Europe these past five years has helped sharpen my sense of what I know and, perhaps more importantly, what I still don't. Gluckstern's book goes a long way toward filling in those gaps, offering both clear explanations and delightfully candid opinions. I especially appreciated his acerbic (and occasionally cranky) commentary, which makes the book not just informative, but also entertaining. 

He covers an impressive range of topics, from distinguishing quality in wine to demystifying oak aging (which he calls the “MSG of wine”) and explaining the nuances of different grape varieties. The chapters on practical wine housekeeping tips are accessible and refreshingly unpretentious, making everyday wine enjoyment feel less intimidating. I also found his guidance on navigating restaurant wine lists and thoughtful recommendations for food and wine pairings particularly useful. Altogether, Gluckstern’s approach blends expertise with humor, making this book both a great personal resource and a thoughtful gift for anyone—even seasoned enthusiasts—looking to broaden their wine knowledge.

I've been going to Village Optical for 3 years and genuinely cannot imagine entrusting my eyewear needs to anywhere else. The service is consistently outstanding! The manager, Laurie, is exceptionally skilled at what she does. She has a remarkable ability to help me select the perfect frames, is incredibly informative, and wonderfully patient. She always thoroughly answers any questions I may have. The doctor there is also exceedingly pleasant and consistently addresses any concerns I might have.
I would wholeheartedly recommend Village Optical to anyone seeking caring, personalized service.

I woke up after the most refreshing nap only to find myself in an all too different place. Gone was my cluttered apartment with its mismatched furniture and peeling wallpaper. Instead, I found myself in a sterile, cramped single room—gray concrete walls, a narrow cot, a small dresser, and nothing else save for the ever-present telescreen mounted high on the wall, its cold blue light casting shadows across the bare floor.

The air tasted metallic, heavy with the smell of industrial disinfectant and something else I couldn't identify. Outside the single window, the sky hung like a dirty sheet, neither day nor night but that perpetual twilight that seemed to blanket this place. Was I dead? Had I somehow crossed into some bureaucratic afterlife?

I looked over at the television screen—the telescreen, as I somehow knew to call it. "MANDATORY EXERCISE TIME FOR SECTOR 7," it announced in its mechanical voice. "CITIZEN 6079 SMITH-W, REPORT FOR PHYSICAL JERKS." As I walked over, the screen's eye focused on me, scanning, analyzing. The number it barked felt like a brand burned into my consciousness. I didn't know how I knew it was mine, but every fiber of my being recognized it as surely as my own name.

"Pick up the slack, 6079!" the voice commanded. I opened my dresser, finding only identical blue coveralls hanging in perfect rows. The fabric felt rough against my skin, smelled of industrial soap and sweat from previous wearers. It all fit too well, as if tailored specifically for my body—a body that felt different somehow, leaner, more angular than I remembered.

I wandered outside into corridors that stretched endlessly in both directions, lit by harsh fluorescent strips that hummed with electrical current. The floors were polished concrete, worn smooth by countless footsteps. Pipes ran along the ceiling, occasionally dripping condensation that had stained the walls with rust-colored streaks. People moved past me in identical coveralls, their faces blank, purposeful. No one made eye contact. No one spoke. The only sounds were the shuffle of feet, the distant hum of machinery, and the occasional bark of orders from telescreens mounted at every intersection.

I found my job in a massive gray building that squatted like a concrete toad among dozens of identical structures. The Ministry of Truth, read the letters carved deep into its facade, though the words seemed to writhe and shift when I wasn't looking directly at them. I could not for the life of me remember ever visiting this place, yet my feet knew exactly which entrance to use, which elevator to take, which corridor led to my destination.

The office was a vast hall filled with rows upon rows of desks, each with its own telescreen and typewriter. The ceiling disappeared into shadows far above, and the walls were lined with posters of a man with a heavy mustache and penetrating eyes that seemed to follow every movement. The air was thick with the clatter of keys, the rustle of papers, and the low murmur of the telescreens providing constant updates and instructions.

I sat down at "my" desk—number 101, Row 47, Section B—and began to work. My hands moved without conscious thought, blotting out anything that insulted the glorious state and promoted the heretical ideas of capitalism. Articles about chocolate rations, war reports, productivity statistics—all flowing through my fingers like water, being reshaped, restructured, perfected for public consumption.

A lady walks behind me, her footsteps soft on the worn linoleum. "Hey George," she says, her voice carrying a familiarity that chills me to the bone.

For the second consecutive year, CNN International won the coveted 'Cable & Satellite Channel of the Year' at the 2014 Asian Television Awards in Singapore on Thursday. It was also named 'Cable & Satellite Network of the Year'. The network took home five awards in total. The Best News Program title went to CNN flagship program News Stream. Hosted by CNN anchor Kristie Lu Stout in Hong Kong and produced by Ravi Hiranand, News Stream is a daily hard-news show that uses technology and social media to give viewers a deeper understanding of the day's top stories. This innovative approach demonstrates how traditional journalism is adapting to the digital age, incorporating real-time social media feeds, interactive graphics, and mobile technology to enhance storytelling and audience engagement. The program's success illustrates the growing importance of multi-platform journalism in reaching global audiences across different time zones and cultural contexts.

Kristie Lu Stout was also awarded Best Current Affairs Presenter. The anchor and correspondent is among the network's most popular Asia-based faces, and also fronts CNN's monthly On China show. Lu Stout has also led coverage of major breaking news stories for CNN, most recently covering the 'Umbrella Movement' pro-democracy protests from the heart of Hong Kong. Her reporting on the Umbrella Movement exemplifies how international correspondents serve as crucial bridges between local events and global audiences, helping viewers worldwide understand the complexities of regional political movements and their broader implications for democracy and human rights. This coverage required navigating sensitive political terrain while maintaining journalistic integrity and safety protocols during civil unrest. This is the second Asian Television Award for Lu Stout during her 14 years with CNN International.

The CNN Freedom Project: Every Day in Cambodia, which exposed the horrors of child sex trafficking, was awarded Best Documentary Series. CNN's investigation, spearheaded by human rights activist and Academy Award-winning actress Mira Sorvino, uncovered an underground system specializing in the sale of pre-pubescent girls. In the gripping documentary, CNN followed the story of three girls sold into sex slavery as 'virgins' by their own mothers. This investigative journalism demonstrates the power of media partnerships in tackling global humanitarian crises, showing how news organizations can collaborate with celebrities, activists, and law enforcement agencies to expose criminal networks and drive policy change. The documentary's impact extends beyond awareness-raising, as it has been used by NGOs and government agencies to secure funding for anti-trafficking programs and has influenced legislative efforts in multiple countries. Every Day in Cambodia is part of the CNN Freedom Project initiative, which has been honored extensively for its ongoing efforts to bring modern-day slavery to an end. The project exemplifies how sustained journalistic commitment can create lasting social impact, providing a model for other news organizations seeking to balance commercial imperatives with public service journalism.

Executive Vice President and Managing Director of CNN International, Tony Maddox, said: "After an extraordinary 12-month news cycle, it's an absolute honor to receive these awards and be recognized by our peers for the work we do. "To be named both Cable & Satellite Channel of the Year plus Cable & Satellite Network of the Year is truly remarkable, and is a testament to the dedication and talent of our teams in Asia and around the globe. Kristie Lu Stout and the News Stream team live and breathe news, and are always willing to go the extra mile to tell the stories that matter. It's great to see that passion recognized in this way. "I am also proud the CNN Freedom Project has been recognized again. Human trafficking is a shameful trade that must be stopped. At CNN we will continue to use the full range of our international resources to track and champion the story." This commitment reflects the evolving role of international news networks in addressing global challenges, demonstrating how media organizations can leverage their distribution networks, technological capabilities, and editorial resources to drive social change while maintaining journalistic standards and commercial viability.

CNN International earlier this year won an award for its Typhoon Haiyan coverage as well as being crowned News Channel of the Year at the Royal Television Society Awards. The network's disaster reporting showcases the critical infrastructure and expertise required for effective crisis journalism, including pre-positioned equipment, trained correspondents familiar with local conditions, and established relationships with emergency services and aid organizations. This type of coverage serves multiple real-world functions: informing affected populations about safety measures and aid distribution, helping international audiences understand the scale of humanitarian needs, and providing accountability reporting on relief efforts and government responses.

Last evening, the Indian Naval vessel INS Tabar executed a decisive maritime operation against piracy by successfully engaging and neutralizing a pirate mother ship in the strategically vital waters of the Gulf of Aden. This action represents a significant assertion of maritime security principles and the international commitment to freedom of navigation in these critical shipping lanes.

Maritime piracy constitutes one of the most enduring forms of asymmetric naval conflict. During the American Revolutionary War, the Continental Congress employed the more diplomatically acceptable term "privateer" to authorize designated vessels to conduct operations against British maritime assets. The British Crown sanctioned similar activities, while French forces engaged both belligerents. Following American independence, as the United States emerged as a major maritime commercial power, it confronted the Barbary pirates operating from North African ports, particularly Tripoli. These maritime raiders had systematically terrorized Mediterranean shipping routes for centuries, establishing tribute and ransom collection as a profitable enterprise—a practice bearing troubling similarities to contemporary piracy operations.

The nascent American republic found itself compelled to address this threat due to the absence of viable alternatives. As frustration mounted within the Adams and Jefferson administrations and Congress, authorization was granted for naval construction programs, providing the necessary assets when Algeria declared war on the United States for refusing tribute payments. Following two conflicts spanning 1801-1805 and 1815, the United States secured unimpeded maritime access to the region.

This historical examination proves relevant as contemporary circumstances demonstrate concerning parallels, albeit with substantially elevated security implications. The ultimate destination of ransom payments remains largely unaccounted for in current analyses. Given Somalia's documented history of supporting extremist Islamic activities, it appears prudent to assess that significant portions of these funds may be channeled toward financing terrorist operations in other theaters.

God gives each person a unique nature and the tools to shape their own character and fulfill their purpose. What matters most for a nation is not its laws, but the character of its people, since a country is just the sum of its individuals. Institutions often reflect the strong influence of one person's life—just as great movements or changes have come from the actions of individuals like Luther or Wesley. History is really the story of a few determined people. So, each person should recognize their own value and stay grounded.

Everyone has their own special beauty. In any group, you notice differences in voices, styles, and expressions that show the richness of human nature. Nature has given everyone the foundation for greatness, if they are willing to build on it.

While everyone shares human abilities, the way each person uses them creates their unique identity. No one else can think your thoughts, speak your words, or do your deeds. Your actions and qualities are your own, and cannot belong to another.

Many experts and research studies suggest that entry-level or very inexpensive guitars often suffer from poor build quality, including issues like fret buzz, warped necks, and unreliable hardware. According to guitar technicians and luthiers, problems such as twisted necks and bad intonation can make the instrument difficult or even impossible to play in tune, leading to frustration for beginners. Research in music education also notes that low-quality instruments can negatively impact motivation and learning outcomes for new players. Experts typically recommend investing a bit more to get a basic instrument from a reputable brand, as it can make a significant difference in playability and enjoyment.

Do not buy anything from this store. My experience has been a complete disaster and a waste of money. I purchased a couch for myself and a mattress for my mother, using separate transactions to keep things clear. Despite this, the store mixed up our orders and contact information. My mother’s mattress arrived a week and a half late, and all delivery calls and emails went to me—even though I don’t live with her. I was only helping her by making the purchase.

Because of their mistake, I missed the delivery call. I left a voicemail explaining the situation, yet I was refunded only $66 out of the $300 I paid for the mattress and delivery, after a $149 “no show” fee. When I called customer service, they just gave me scripted apologies and took no real responsibility for the mix-up.

To make matters worse, my mother, who recently went through a difficult divorce and is finally getting back on her feet, paid $234—and is still sleeping on the floor with no mattress. This level of incompetence is unacceptable. Thanks to The Dump, a simple purchase turned into a nightmare for both of us.

The aircraft was now in a sharp descent. All around, people cried out in prayer, offering their final farewells to those they cherished. Yet in Row B, always in first class, Gloria grasped Stephen’s steady hand and returned the warmth in his devoted gaze. A whole lifetime of affection was conveyed in this final meeting of their eyes—eyes even deeper blue than the sea beneath them. Stephen noticed Gloria trembling; worry was beginning to take hold, so he leaned in and kissed her gently, pulling her close as he whispered for the last time, “I love you.” They were both smiling at the moment of impact.

What is it about Thomas Cromwell that makes him such a significant figure in our political history? Hilary Mantel summarises his story here.
For almost a decade, Thomas Cromwell was chief minister to Henry VIII, at the centre of his glittering and savage court and standing by his king, as David Starkey has said, 'like Alistair Campbell with an axe.'
Indefatigable propagandist for his ferocious master, he helped engineer England's break with Rome and made Henry head of the new church in England. He disposed of Henry's unwanted wives and organised new ones.
His power in church and state was unprecedented.
He was arrested in the council chamber, held in the Tower and beheaded in July 1540. Henry married his fifth wife on the day of Cromwell's execution. Within weeks he was lamenting the death of 'the most faithful servant he had ever had.'
But his story had a most unlikely beginning.
He was born in Putney around 1485, the year the Tudor dynasty came to power. His father was a brewer and blacksmith, a neighbourhood bully well-known in the local law courts. His mother's name is unknown and there is no record of his education.
By his own account a 'ruffian,' he quit England while still in his teens. When he returned after some 10 years he spoke several languages and had acquired expertise in finance and law. He had also, it was said, learned the New Testament by heart.
For some years he was 'man of business' to Cardinal Wolsey, England's all-powerful Lord Chancellor. When the Cardinal fell, Cromwell moved into royal service. He was soon established as a master of parliamentary tactics and as a man who challenged and remade ancient institutions.
He was said to have promised the king he would make him rich, and he proceeded to do it, diverting church lands and treasures into royal hands. A religious reformer, he persuaded Henry to allow an English bible, but he could not turn the king's old-fashioned heart towards protestantism.
Cromwell accrued offices, titles and personal wealth. He was, an ambassador said, 'magnificent.' A patron of scholars and artists, his household was a magnet for the ambitious. His work schedule was unrelenting, his mind ingenious, and he managed his volatile monarch not only by producing quick results but also by what his opponents saw as self-serving ruthlessness and manipulative charm.
On the way to the top he made dangerous enemies, including Stephen Gardiner, the bishop known as 'wily Winchester', and the irascible magnate Thomas Howard, Duke of Norfolk.
In 1540, to the fury of Cromwell's conservative opponents, the king made him Earl of Essex. But Henry was unhappy in his fourth marriage, which Cromwell had arranged, and while his position was destabilised his enemies moved in, accusing him of heresy and treason.
The real Thomas Cromwell?

I predict that the way we discover and verify knowledge—the scientific method—will undergo greater transformations in the next half-century than it has since its inception 400 years ago. Already, over the past ten years, we’ve started to see clear signs of this evolution.

A fundamental principle of today’s scientific method is reproducibility: the notion that experiments should yield the same results when conducted by others. This is intended to safeguard objectivity—making sure we aren’t just fooling ourselves.

Yet, as Jonah Lehrer compellingly demonstrates in his 2010 New Yorker piece “The Truth Wears Off” (pdf), the majority of experiments are never independently replicated. Of those that are, very few replicate the original results, and often even the researchers themselves cannot reproduce their own findings. This problem is especially acute in the biological sciences.

Stranger still, repeated attempts to reproduce experiments are less successful as time passes, as though the effects themselves are slowly vanishing. The initial scientific response followed the classic arc of denial: First there was no decline; then, if there was, it was rare; next, it might be common, but not significant. Now, a growing number of researchers consider the decline real, prevalent, and deeply consequential.

Consider the illuminating work of John Crabbe, who meticulously designed experiments on mouse behavior to be identical across three labs: Albany, New York; Edmonton, Alberta; and Portland, Oregon. Every conceivable variable was standardized—the same mouse strains, shipped simultaneously from a single supplier; identical cages, bedding, light, diet, and even the same type of surgical glove for handling, and same testing equipment used at the same hour each morning.

The logic was airtight: if ever a set of experiments should produce matching results, these should. “If any set of experiments should have passed the test, it should have been ours,” Crabbe maintains. “But that’s not the way it turned out.” When a particular mouse strain was injected with cocaine, the increase in activity varied wildly: six hundred centimeters in Portland, seven hundred and one in Albany, and an astonishing five thousand in Edmonton. Tests for anxiety produced similarly erratic shifts, with no pattern—one mouse strain was most anxious in Portland, but a different strain topped the list elsewhere.

What Crabbe’s findings imply is unsettling: many striking scientific findings may be statistical mirages, mere noise mistaken for signal.

Lehrer’s article surveys emerging explanations for this reproducibility crisis. In essence, the problem stems from science’s obsession with positive results. This positivity bias permeates the scientific enterprise: prestigious journals prioritize positive findings, negative results are ignored or shelved, and scientists all too often design studies to ensure something positive turns up—or continue tweaking and rerunning things until it does. One proposed remedy, by irreproducibility researcher Jonathan Schooler, is radical transparency: pre-registering experiments in an open-source database and publishing all findings, positive and negative, as they occur. That way, the database would reflect reality, where most findings are negative, and any breakthrough that persists will truly be robust.

In an ideal world, science would be an endless lottery of random experiments on random subjects, eliminating all bias. The logistical and resource requirements are prohibitive—except perhaps in certain niche fields. There have been a few experimental moves toward publishing negative results in biomedicine (like the Journal of Pharmaceutical Negative Results), but a more systematic embrace of failure and null findings would go a long way toward revitalizing and future-proofing the scientific method.

His comments follow criticism of the policy from the body representing Welsh universities and opposition parties.

Mr Jones told BBC Wales he was "not attracted to the idea" of "putting huge amounts of debt on other students".

A policy review by Prof Sir Ian Diamond is due to be finished in September.

**Background: How Welsh student funding currently works**
Students from Wales currently only pay £3,810 towards their tuition fees, wherever they study in the UK. The rest, up to £5,190 a year, is paid for by the Welsh government. This means Welsh students pay much less than students from England, who typically pay up to £9,000 per year.

**The debate over the current system**
The chair of Universities Wales has said tuition fee grants should be replaced by a means-tested grant to help poorer pupils. (Means-tested grants are financial support given only to students from families with lower incomes.)

Prof Colin Riordan has warned the policy threatens the future of higher education in Wales because tens of millions of pounds ends up in the coffers of universities in England when Welsh students choose to study there.

But, in an interview with BBC Wales Today, Mr Jones said: "Means testing is not where we are going on this.

"We will have to look at what the Diamond review says but I am not attracted to the idea of simply doing what England does, and means testing and putting huge amounts of debt on other students.

"What we will never do is put Welsh students in the same financial position as their colleagues in England.

"That is the fundamental principle of what we will do in future."

**What other political parties propose**
The Welsh Conservatives have said they would scrap tuition fee subsidies and pay half of students' rent instead.

Plaid Cymru would also scrap the grants, and instead pay Welsh students working in Wales after graduation £6,000 a year, up to a maximum of £18,000.

The Welsh Liberal Democrats' policy is to replace tuition fee subsidies with maintenance grants (money to help with living costs rather than tuition fees), while UKIP says it would like to cut tuition fees.

I've been here for god knows how long. Quite literally God, though I refuse to utter that bastard's name. I've lost count of how long I've been in my prison—millions of years, billions, who knows? Being imprisoned isn't as bad as people make it sound—it's Hel. Every day, tortured with the most painful poisons, taunted with the bodies of my once living sons—sons I brought into this world so they could make change. The only thing they're changing, though, is my mental state. Though I cannot visit them physically, I can still visit living relatives on the outside in spirit—quite literally. I even have a horse! It saddens me that my descendants and family are given flak for my notorious reputation. It's gotten to the point where Sleipnir won't even acknowledge me, and he was one of my favorites. It's all fine though; I know I'll be out. Ragnarok is rounding the corner as we speak.

In the shadow-haunted streets of southern Italy, where ancient church bells toll over territories carved up by crime families, the Catholic Church faces a chilling dilemma that strikes at the very heart of sacred tradition. The air in these mafia strongholds grows thick with unspoken threats and whispered allegiances, where even the most holy ceremonies have been twisted into tools of darkness.

An archbishop in one of southern Italy's most fear-gripped mafia strongholds has proposed a desperate 10-year ban on naming godparents at religious ceremonies—a radical measure born from the church's anguished recognition that its sacred rituals are being coldly exploited to weave webs of criminal power. In the suffocating grip of organized crime, mob clans have perverted the innocent joy of baptisms and confirmations, transforming these blessed moments into sinister opportunities to forge alliances between criminal dynasties and extend their poisonous influence into the hearts of the next generation.

The role of godfather—'padrino' in the language that once spoke only of protection and guidance—now carries the weight of menace, binding children to a legacy of violence before they can even speak their first words.

Pope Francis, his voice heavy with righteous fury, visited a mafia powerbase in June where he delivered a thunderous condemnation that reverberated through the criminal underworld. In words that cut like a blade through the oppressive silence of intimidation, he declared mobsters 'excommunicated' for their evil deeds—cast out from the very church whose sacraments they had defiled. Previously, his pleas for their repentance had echoed unanswered through the marble halls of cathedrals that stood like islands of hope in seas of corruption.

Msgr. Giuseppe Fiorini Morosini, bearing the crushing weight of shepherding souls in this moral wasteland, says he first raised this desperate proposal two years ago, only to watch it crumble against the Vatican's bureaucratic resistance. The Congregation for Divine Worship and the Discipline of the Sacraments had turned away from his plea, leaving him to minister in the suffocating atmosphere of Reggio Calabria—a city trapped in the iron grip of Italy's most merciless criminal organization.

In the unforgiving toe of Italy, where Mediterranean sunshine seems unable to penetrate the darkness of fear, Msgr Morosini serves as archbishop of territory controlled by the Ndrangheta—a name whispered with dread across continents. These shadow emperors command an estimated 80 percent of Europe's cocaine trade, their tentacles of corruption reaching from the bomb-scarred streets of Belfast to the blood-soaked cartels of Colombia, weaving a global web of violence and despair.

The haunting image of Al Pacino's Michael Corleone, standing cold-eyed at a baptismal font while ordering executions, no longer seems like Hollywood fiction but a chilling reflection of reality—where sacred vows are spoken with blood on unseen hands.

Following Francis' soul-stirring condemnation, Msgr Morosini has summoned the courage to raise his proposal once more, carrying his burden to the Vatican's hallowed halls last weekend. In that meeting, heavy with the weight of moral crisis, Francis listened with the gravity of a shepherd watching wolves circle his flock. The Pope's openness to the idea flickered like a candle of hope in the oppressive darkness, as he asked the archbishop to gather support from fellow bishops before returning with their collective voice.

With the weariness of a man who has seen too much evil masquerade as holiness, Msgr Morosini speaks of this suspension as 'a powerful decision'—words that carry the desperate hope of a community gasping for moral air. In the suffocating reality of Ndrangheta territory, he has witnessed how the mafia's family bonds are not just preserved but deliberately strengthened through their calculated manipulation of the church's most sacred moments.

His voice heavy with the burden of truth, he told La Repubblica: 'This measure could help to stop this expansion, which from a mafia perspective, is fundamental'—each word a prayer that the church might finally sever the unholy bonds that have turned blessing into curse, hope into horror.

I don't know if I will overcome this writer's block. The fantasy novel I have been working on this past month has evolved into an intricate world in which the fate of the characters will be decided within the next few paragraphs. I just haven't decided which way to take. One leads to a good ending where lovers are reunited. It may be a little too cliche and perhaps even cheesy. The other path is a darker one which ends in hopelessness. I just can't decide. It's not the usual writer's block. Rather, it is me refusing to go forward at a crossroad, trying to peer into the distance of two roads, trying to figure out which one I should take. Perhaps I will let the author within me rest for tonight. Indeed, within this realm of my mind, he will sit alongside the road, build a campfire and go to sleep in his traveler clothes. I feel a bit sleepy too. For, the night often whispers advice into the ears of thinkers, leaving them with a clear path when they wake the next day. The night will wait for me. However, for now my attention turns to the lady on my left. She is my source of inspiration at this moment in my life. As she is sitting there on the bed, trying to finish some part of her thesis for tomorrow, I find myself making subtle contact by resting my shoulder against hers. Then, as I listen to her complain about her struggles with the format of her work, I smile. This is one of those moments when you realize you are completely and utterly happy. A song plays on her playlist, one that would fit right in a student cafe. Although I don't really listen to the lyrics, as I am writing this, I hear the words "Let's Tesselate". Perfect music for work in the night: Not too smooth, not too rhythmic. Just enough to keep you both relaxed and alert. I could describe at great lengths her incredible smile, her narrow hazel eyes hidden behind fashionable glasses, her awesome curves who put my teenage fantasies to shame, and that intellect fused with great charisma which make her the most interesting person on Earth to talk with about anything and everything. Indeed, I could describe all of her characteristics, her personality and her physique. Yet, I could, but I won't. I think I will give her a kiss instead and then go back to those realms within my mind. The traveler in my head will wake once again and look at the crossing roads, undecided. I may not get much done tonight, but the last thing she needs right now is a partner sleeping by her side while she has to labour on this thesis. Therefore, I will stay at her side and bask in the tranquil happiness of this fine night.

I hear him scream and leap out of bed, racing to his room. I flip the light switch but nothing happens—stupid switch hasn't worked properly in weeks now. I can't see him on the bed. I hear him whimper from the closet. I open the doors, and his tiny hand grabs mine and pulls me in, down below the hanging shirts and pants, down to the floor and right against the wall of the closet. The floor is wet. He's shaking—I've never seen him so scared of a nightmare like this.

"Hey buddy," I say. He shushes me. "We have to be quiet." I hold him and oblige, wondering what he'd seen in his dreams this time. A monster? A clown? What could make a little boy this scared?

Then I hear something above me. At first, I think it's his breathing, but it's too raspy. It's wrong. I look up and see it. Red eyes stare down at me, black limbs stretch out around the closet, and fanged teeth glisten with saliva that drips to the closet floor. I open my mouth to scream and move to cover him, but he stops me, pressing his tiny hand against my mouth and leaning close, right to my ear, to whisper the words that send shivers down my spine: "It's not the creature you need to be afraid of..."

By . Snejana Farberov . A car that trailed an official motorcade carrying Sasha and Malia Obama like a persistent shadow on to Pennsylvania Avenue prompted a security lockdown at the White House Tuesday, turning the nation's most secure residence into a fortress under siege.

A U.S. Secret Service spokesman said the blue Honda Civic entered the security checkpoint at 17th Street and Pennsylvania and was immediately stopped by Secret Service uniformed officers like a fish caught in a net. The male driver, later identified as Internal Revenue Service employee Mathew Evan Goldstein, 55, was arrested and Washington DC police checked the car for explosives with the thoroughness of archaeologists examining ancient artifacts.

Scroll down for video . Breach: Members of the Uniformed Division of the Secret Service investigate an unauthorized vehicle outside the White House gates in Washington May 6 . Precaution: The area around the White House was locked down after the car was able to pass a road block like water slipping through a crack in a dam . Person of interest: The driver of the blue Honda was taken into custody by Secret Service, captured as swiftly as a butterfly in a collector's net . The bearded, bespectacled Mathew Goldstein, 55, an IT specialist with the IRS, held a pass for the U.S. Treasury building located next door to the White House like a neighbor with a key to the wrong house.

People were unable to leave the White House grounds while the car was being checked out, trapped like birds in a cage until the all-clear was given. The lockdown at 1600 Pennsylvania Avenue came shortly after 4.40pm as the Obama daughters were returning home from the Quaker-run Sidwell Friends School, their routine homecoming disrupted like a peaceful afternoon shattered by thunder.

The Honda operated by Mr Goldstein, who had with a pass for the U.S. Treasury building, followed the Obamas' motorcade into a secure area like an uninvited guest crashing an exclusive party, where it was stopped by uniformed officers. Daily commute: Malia (left) and Sasha Obama (right), pictured here in January with the president, were on their way home from school when the incident took place . In trouble: Goldstein, pictured seated on a bench, has been charged with unlawful entry and was transported to city police for processing .

Pictures of the car showed it traveled about 50 yards down Pennsylvania Avenue past the security barrier like a runner who had crossed the starting line too early, but it remained outside the main gate of the White House. About an hour later, the lockdown was lifted at the White House like morning fog dissipating in sunlight.

Secret Service spokesman Edwin Donovan said Mr Goldstein has been charged with unlawful entry and was transported to city police for processing. Mr . Goldstein, a graduate of George Mason University, has worked as an IT . specialist with the Internal Revenue Service since 1990, building his career like a steady climber ascending a mountain over decades.

The . 55-year-old has been active in the atheist circles, navigating these communities like a compass pointing toward his firmly held beliefs. On his profile page . on the site Secular.org, Goldstein described himself as a secular . humanist and member of the Democratic Party, wearing his affiliations like badges of honor.

Secure location: The Honda entered the security checkpoint at 17th Street and Pennsylvania without permission, slipping through like an unwelcome visitor at a locked door . Thorough search: Washington DC police checked the Honda Civic for explosives with the precision of surgeons examining a patient .

He also has affiliation with the non-profit group Secular Coalition For America and Freedom From Religion Foundation, connecting to these organizations like branches extending from the same ideological tree. The two-block stretch of Pennsylvania Avenue in front of the White House was closed to automobile traffic by US President Bill Clinton in 1995 as a security measure, creating a protective moat around America's most important residence.

Chiroqui lounged lazily in the crook of a massive umbawa tree, its broad branches arching high over the endless sea of grasslands below. The plains, bronzed under the midday sun, undulated in every direction as gusts of wind swept across their island home—an isolated world ringed by distant cliffs, dense with myth and legend. From his lofty perch, Chiroqui sat the afternoon watch, his keen eyes tracking the slightest flutter in the undergrowth for prowling predators—jaguarine ghosts that slid between streaks of shade and light, or the great feathered serpents the elders whispered of in fearful reverence.

The tribe below bustled with quiet industry: women weaving grass mats under canopies, children darting among the roots in games of tag and stealth, hunters returning with the morning’s catch. Chiroqui’s mind wandered, lulled by the rhythmic sway of the tree, his senses half-dulled by heat and repetition. Still, he forced himself to scan the horizon; ever since the omens of the last full moon, everyone had spoken of danger on the wind.

It was then that a glint pierced his reverie, blazing sharp and unnatural in the noonday brilliance. It flashed from deeper within the plains, impossible to mistake for the muted shimmer of the temple’s gold tiles or the sunlit reflection off a hunting spear. Startled, Chiroqui narrowed his eyes, heart pounding as he searched for the disturbance’s source. The tall grasses rippled innocently, betraying nothing of what might lurk beneath.

He shook his head. Perhaps it was only a hallucination—his mind, fatigued from sleepless nights and shifting shadows, playing tricks. He forced a slow breath, letting his body relax against the bark. That was when the glimmer returned, brighter, unmistakably nearer, its origin obscured by the undulating grass but heading directly toward his clan-mates.

Sudden fear banished his lethargy. Chiroqui pressed forward, branches bowing under his lithe form as he peered through a tangled veil of leaves. Below, something moved: a flickering shadow, impossibly large, gliding silently at the forest’s edge. Alarm rising, Chiroqui swung down the mighty trunk, landing with practiced grace in the grass. Heart hammering, he cut a swift path through the brush, vaulting fallen logs and dodging startled birds as he sprinted toward the heart of the village.

By the time he reached the last cluster of trees, breath burning in his lungs, the war band was already forming ranks. The air was thick with tension, warriors arrayed in two disciplined lines, obsidian-tipped arrows notched and ready, the blades at their belts catching what little light made it through the canopy. Their faces, painted in ochre and ash, betrayed curiosity as much as fear—none could recall a more portentous sight.

Three hundred yards beyond their defensive line, in the far pasture where wild goats grazed, stood a tableau both wondrous and dreadful: two dozen giants, their skin pale as driftwood, their garments shifting in patterns never seen before—silvery, geometric, like the river under moonlight. They stood unnaturally tall, armored in odd textile and gleaming leather. Clutched in their furred hands were slender rods of polished metal, their purpose unknown yet menacing.

A tense whisper rippled among the warriors as they tried to fathom the visitors’ intentions. Were they spirits sent by Theinrien, their wrath drawn by some slight? Or wayfarers from the legend-haunted lands beyond the cliffs?

The air snapped with purpose as Chieftain Kamaro appeared, draped in the blue-green plumage and beaded vestments reserved for times of dire need. His arrival parted the ranks; his very presence a declaration of authority and resolve that steadied the men and women behind him. With a roar that echoed over the plains, Kamaro raised his bow—sinew taut with both hope and desperation—and loosed an arrow toward the strangers, bounding after it with his obsidian mace raised high.

The nearest of the foreigners, startled into motion, lifted one of their alien rods and pointed it squarely at Kamaro. There was a sharp crack—smoke belched from the iron tube and the sound of thunder rent the air as Kamaro was hurled backward, tumbling motionless to the dust. At that moment, everyone knew: the age of old certainties had ended. The world had changed, and its echoes would drown the island in sorrow and fire.

What do you get when you take a TV actor and pair them with some of the best musicians working today, like Patrice Rushen? You get a really smooth, polished jazz and soul album! It's too bad this album didn't get much attention in the United States - it was actually popular in the UK. Maybe they have better taste in music than we do here! That's actually where I first discovered this album, listening to a British internet radio station. TC has a really nice voice - smooth and easy to listen to. Queen Latifah (Dana Owens) isn't the only one from the TV show "Living Single" who can sing! If you can find a copy of this album, definitely give it a listen!

Pale grey, sodden and wet, the voices of hags began to stir. Within my ears they whispered, "My dear, oh please do not perturb, for these words are quick and fleeting, and though I do not wish to rush, soon enough your entrails will yourself find turned to mush. Your end is now, your sun is set, so take the dagger’s sharp plunge to the deep. Lie with the rest, inside this churned-up marsh." 

And so, I took a final look upon that witch of dread. With the knife, I took my life and made the reeds my bed—alone, at last, again.

It's November 11th and Thanksgiving is around the corner. I got a nice new table for the family to enjoy Thanksgiving dinner around, but I needed to order chairs. I found this product on Amazon and thought it would be a good fit for my table. I placed the order and selected the shipping option. Amazon offered free 3-5 day shipping. Unfortunately, the company reported on my tracking that it didn't get picked up by FedEx until the 21st with a delivery date of the 23rd. The 23rd came and went, but no chairs arrived. FedEx and Amazon then changed the tracking report to show pickup on the 24th with delivery on the 28th. I guess I just have to accept this delay. The problem is, I don't have chairs! I'll have to stand the whole time. This is poor business practice.

Even though this was a food chain restaurant, I would say that it served one of the best Japanese BBQ in town. Premium all-you-can-eat BBQ for $59 - $64 per person depends on the day. It maybe was pricey, but for the quality of food, the taste, the ambiance (the atmosphere and mood of the place), the service, it was worth it. The restaurant was so busy at night that there was almost no chance for walk-in customers. Hence (therefore), the reservation was a must. However, we tried to avoid such rush hours and always came during the happy hour. No reservation needed and better discount for food. The meat was all marinated (soaked in seasoning sauce to add flavor) quite well with soy sauce and sesame sauce. It was yummily savory (having a pleasant salty or spicy taste) and a bit of sweet. The mushroom was wrapped inside aluminum foil with butter and spices. They were all delectable (delicious) and mouthwatering (so appealing it makes you want to eat it). I would highly recommend the Bone-in Kalbi Short Rib (Korean-style marinated beef short ribs) and Yaki-Shabu beef (thinly sliced beef for grilling). The Kobe Kalbi Style Short Rib (expensive premium beef short ribs) may not be worth the price, though. Overall, this restaurant should be a favorite place for any Japanese or even Korean BBQ lovers.

President Barack Obama has **declared** the state a disaster area, **unleashing** federal aid to **devastated** areas.

Soldiers and emergency teams have **heroically rescued** more than 20,000 people from their homes or cars after the floods, which were **triggered** by torrential rain.

Emergency workers told the BBC some people were still **trapped** in their homes.

The neighbouring states of Alabama and Mississippi also **endured** severe weather, although rains are lessening as they **sweep** west over Louisiana and further.

One of the worst-**ravaged** areas is the Louisiana capital, Baton Rouge.

William Daniel, an official there, told the BBC from an emergency control centre that between 8,000 and 10,000 people were **crammed** in shelters.

"We're still **receiving urgent phone calls** about people who're **desperately trying** to get out of their homes because they can't get out because either there's water **surging** in to their houses or their streets are **completely blocked**.

"It is definitely an unprecedented flood here in Baton Rouge. Houses that have never ever even come close to flooding have water three and four foot high **gushing** in to the houses."

"This is a flood of epic proportions," JR Shelton, the mayor of Central City **declared** to local media. "When we talk about floods now, we'll talk about the great flood of 2016. "Everything else **pales** in comparison."

Shanita Angrum, 32, **frantically called** the police when she realized her family were trapped in their home. An officer arrived and **rushed** her six-year-old daughter to safety.

"Snakes were everywhere," she told Associated Press. "The whole time I was just **desperately praying** for God to make sure me and my family were OK."

John Mitchell, a 23-year-old Louisiana resident, was **forced to fight** his way to safety **swimming** with his girlfriend and her one-year-old daughter. They were **dramatically rescued** by police officers in a boat.

"This is the worst it's been, ever,'' Mr Mitchell said. "We tried to wait it out, but we had to **escape**."

Several rivers in Louisiana and Mississippi are **raging** and overflowing.

The state is prone to bursts of extreme weather; thousands of acres of Louisiana were **deliberately flooded** in 2011 to **divert surging water** from the flooded Mississippi River and to **protect** cities, including Baton Rouge and New Orleans, that lie downstream.

In 2005, New Orleans **suffered** one of the worst natural disasters in US history, when Hurricane Katrina **slammed** the city. The storm **killed** nearly 2,000 people and **displaced** one million. Thousands of homes were **flooded and destroyed** in Louisiana and along the Gulf coast.

"Well, everyone watching is getting excited for this game of Ball, Harry." "They sure are, Leroy. With two of the best players getting ready for the game, everyone's waiting to see what they'll do this time." "That's right, Harry. We have Locke against Nightingale, and no one knows what will happen!" "The people taking bets made a smart choice there, I think. Both sides had equal chances since the game was announced, but then both players tried to cheat the betting, so it got too crazy for them. Tell me the basic rules again, Leroy?" "Sure thing. The game was simple; each player has a ball, and they have to get their ball and their opponent's ball back into their own goal. They can do anything except kill." "Gee, that sounds kind of boring." "That's why cheating was allowed, Harry. Ooh, it's starting- both players have come onto the field." "Both completely covered up, Leroy. Do you think they're really there?" "Hard to say. Last time, Locke had someone else play for him. Nightingale likes to play herself. But for a big game like this, anything could happen!" The two people in the middle of the field bowed to each other without speaking as the third person- who had the balls- walked towards them. The third person gave a ball to each of the other people and ran away as fast as he could. The two people stood for a second before suddenly both jumping backwards towards their own goals. They both threw what was in their hands into their own goals. Nothing happened. "Ooh, this is interesting. It looks like both of them had plans to steal the balls right from the start." "Both of them thought their plan had worked, too. Let's see the slow replay." "Ah, so Locke stuck both balls with needles on silk strings, and had them on strings, but Nightingale had loosened the sensors and had them on suction cups. They both threw their handfuls into their goals, but..." "That is interesting. Neither of them counted as goals. Now what are they going to do?" "Who's that just came onto the field? Why are they- OH! OOOOH!" "Folks, it looks like an Outside Player win! It seems the redhead in the cloak has outsmarted both our players today! Well done, new person. We name you the Kingkiller!"

The sprawling fields of wheat, gooseberry, and teak – a family estate of over 10 acres – were an initial surprise for journalists visiting Mr. Singh's Rajasthan village. This was no subsistence farm; his family lived in a six-room, single-storey house, a picture of "affluent background" that, according to the Indian Express, starkly contrasted with the narrative of a desperate farmer. To put this in perspective, 65% of land holdings across India are tiny, often less than an acre. Yet, in states like Rajasthan, medium and large holdings — exceeding 15 acres per plot on average — dominate, making up 70% of all farmland.

His suicide note, now fiercely contested by his family, claimed he was "driven out of home" by crop devastation. Yet, villagers painted a different picture: local damage, while present, paled in comparison to the ravaged fields of truly worst-hit regions.

Farming, it seems, was not Mr. Singh's true calling. Though he toiled on his father's land, he also dabbled in tying Rajasthan's famous colourful turbans, a craft he hoped might offer a different path. Twice he tried to enter politics, only to be denied a party ticket. His digital footprint, a Facebook page with 66 friends and a snapshot of him casually leaning against a Toyota sedan, further suggested a spirit yearning for something beyond the fields. "The man's heart was not in farming," a journalist observed, a sentiment echoed by many in his village.

He was the eldest of three brothers; one joined the police, another found work in a private firm in Jaipur. Mr. Singh, by all accounts, was the reluctant one, tied to the soil but looking elsewhere. This wasn't an isolated sentiment. A 2014 survey by the Delhi-based Centre for Study of Developing Societies revealed a startling truth: 76% of young Indian farmers longed for different work. Over 60% dreamed of leaving for city jobs, viewing farming as a cycle of stress, risk, and meager rewards.

Perhaps it was a desperate gamble for recognition, a final bid for change. Mr. Singh journeyed to Delhi, to a farmers' rally orchestrated by the anti-corruption Aam Aadmi Party, reportedly hoping to meet its leader, Arvind Kejriwal. His neighbours whispered he "wanted to draw attention to the problems of farmers."

Then came the final, tragic act. He ascended to the crown of a tree, a scarf in hand. He tied it around his neck. His life ended there, a stark, public spectacle. While some reports speculate he might have slipped, making it an accidental hanging, the police investigation continues.

Whatever Mr. Singh's ultimate motive — to spotlight the crisis on his farm or to carve a path in politics — his heartbreaking death unequivocally cast a harsh glare on the deepening crisis gripping Indian agriculture.

The crisis is real and devastating. "This is the worst farm distress I have been seeing in the last 10 years. It is a very serious crisis," laments farmer Ajay Jakhar. His words are underscored by chilling statistics: hundreds of debt-stricken farmers have reportedly ended their lives; rural wages have plummeted to a decade-low; tractor sales, a barometer for agricultural health, are down by a third; and outstanding farm loans have surged dramatically in just three years.

A perfect storm of factors fuels this turmoil. Experts point to plummeting international commodity prices, hitting a 10-year low, leaving growers of cotton, potato, and rubber particularly devastated. Even wheat, usually shielded by government premium purchases, has suffered, its harvests wrecked by relentless unseasonal rains.

Compounding these woes is a glaring absence of effective risk mitigation. Agriculturists highlight urgent needs: sharper, more precise weather advisories, particularly medium-term forecasts, and crucially, a comprehensive, workable crop insurance scheme.

Curiously, the political storm surrounding the Bharatiya Janata Party government's land acquisition bill, vehemently denounced by opposition parties, seems largely detached from the farmers' immediate, raw anxieties. "There are clear and present dangers right now," one farmer states bluntly. "We are not even thinking about the land acquisition bill."

As the crisis deepens, many believe Prime Minister Narendra Modi must swiftly offer reassurance to prevent further escalation. "The government is losing the war of perception," warns Mr. Jakhar. "The BJP is being seen as an anti-farmer party." It would come as no surprise if Mr. Modi addresses farmers directly in the upcoming edition of his monthly radio address.
