|Female island fox sporting her new GPS collar|
In December, radio tracking collars and advanced GPS tracking collars delivered crucial insights on island fox locations and movements during the Catalina Grand Prix motorcycle event.  
Catalina Island Conservancy biologists pinpointed the whereabouts of island foxes near the race course, monitored fox activity throughout the event, and confirmed that no foxes were directly harmed by motorcyclists. At the end of January, the island foxes fitted with GPS collars will be recaptured so that detailed data can be extracted from their collars. These state-of-the-art devices log location data every 75 minutes. By outfitting a group of island foxes with these specialized collars before the motorcycle race, researchers chronicled movement patterns prior to the race, during the event, and in the weeks that followed.  
Once the complete data set is gathered and analyzed, biologists will be able to determine if the race altered the animals’ behaviors and how long it took for the foxes to resume their typical routines.  
This project exemplifies how cutting-edge technology empowers us to make smarter decisions in our interactions with wildlife, especially endangered species like the island fox.

"What does Bruce Wayne do with all of his money? That's the question that every Gothamite has pondered at some stage of their lives. As of today, they have their answer, and it's far more sinister than they'd ever assumed. Most assumed he spent it on expensive cars, expensive getaways or elite parties that would make even Stanley Kubrick blush. Bruce Wayne has managed to make over a billion of his dollars just disappear, a paper trail so complex that the Panama Papers are just one step in a trail longer than the yellow brick road(and likely filled with more heartless and brainless cowards than the aforementioned road). 

Where has that billion dollars gone? One might use it as an explanation for the blatant corruption that has plagued Gotham for decades. According to forensic accounting expert Dr. Sarah Chen from Gotham University's Business School, 'When we see this level of financial obfuscation from a major public figure, it typically indicates either massive tax evasion or funding of illegal activities. The complexity of Wayne's shell companies rivals what we've seen in major international corruption cases.'

Has Bruce Wayne fallen into the same corrupt filth that his belated parents fought to protect? Unfortunately not every handsome billionaire can be as such a fierce humanitarian as Lex Luthor. Economic policy researcher Professor James Mitchell of the Gotham Institute notes, 'The Wayne family's charitable foundation has dramatically reduced its public disclosure in recent years, while simultaneously increasing its offshore holdings. This pattern is consistent with what we observe in cases of elite financial misconduct.'

Where is The Batman in all of this? Instead of beating up petty bag snatchers and dealers, why not go after the REAL criminals, like Bruce Wayne. That's what's wrong with vigilantes like Batman, they're too caught up in the violence to care about real issues. Criminologist Dr. Lisa Rodriguez from Gotham City College observes, 'Vigilante justice consistently targets street-level crime while ignoring white-collar corruption that causes far more societal harm. It's a pattern we see repeatedly - the wealthy and powerful remain untouched while the desperate and disadvantaged face violent retribution.'

Gotham is sick, and it will be until vile scum like Bruce Wayne are stopped. My name is Gordon Godfrie: Good night America."

Mobile connectivity is improving environmental resource management. Despite some device-related hazards, machine-to-machine (M2M) communication enables remote monitoring and analysis of resource consumption—from homes to vehicles to irrigation systems—leading to conservation solutions. Smart power grids, for instance, monitor power consumption and automatically redirect power, significantly reducing greenhouse gas emissions (CTIA infographic). The transportation sector holds similar potential for emission cuts.

In agriculture, mobile technology can dramatically cut water consumption. Wireless soil monitors track moisture levels for efficient irrigation. Farmers often overwater, as one explained to Scientific American: “The biggest problem we’ve got with irrigation is we just don’t know—we use old wives’ tales to decide when to irrigate.” CTIA suggests this technology could reduce water use by up to 6 trillion gallons annually.

Watch for our 2013 mobile report, outlining broader trends, next week. In the meantime, review last year’s report, “15 Ways Mobile Will Change our Lives,” here.

I was curious about how difficult it was to grow ferns indoors from spores, so I took a frond or part of a frond from a holly fern (Cyrtomium falcatum) at work and brought it home. I took some damp toilet paper at work and wiped it on a Asplenium nidus to collect those (I didn't want to cut off the frond).
At home, I took a smallish (about 6 inches / 15 cm square) clear plastic container, washed it, filled it with vermiculite, and added water.
I let the vermiculite and water sit for a minute, and dumped out the excess water (holding the vermiculite back with my hand).
I held the frond above the wet vermiculite and brushed the spores off onto it.
I unfolded the toilet paper with the Asplenium spores, peeled it to a single layer, and set it on the vermiculite.
I replaced the top on the tray, stuck it in an out-of-the-way spot protected from temperature fluctuations and lots of light (artificial), and waited.
Now I have this:
It's not the most exciting image. But those green spots? Those are spores that have become gametophytes. This is encouraging.
Ferns have a roundabout life cycle, where spores, under the right conditions, develop into "gametophytes," which are photosynthetic and form eggs and sperm (each gametophyte produces both, though usually at different times, to avoid self-fertilization). The eggs stay put, but the sperm travels across the moist ground looking for an egg to fertilize. If it finds one, the two combine to form a sporophyte, which is the ferny-looking thing most know as a fern. (Though the gametophytes are just as much ferns as the sporophytes.)

The inferno clawed and gnawed at everything. Steel screamed against steel with thunderous crashes. Heavy footsteps pounded while voices roared, wild cries splitting the air apart. This was the substance of my visions, the clay from which I was molded. Then came the transformation - broad wings unfurled, razor talons emerged, rough scales covered my flesh, and my blood turned to liquid fire. The hunger overwhelmed me. Rodents, hounds, felines, oxen, livestock. I devoured them without mercy, savoring each morsel completely. Flight became instinct. The sky opened as a new realm of existence. The visions persisted. As a young wyrm, ash and flame filled my sleeping mind. The darkness expands with my growth, accompanied by mounting fear and shame. Distant voices echo through time. "Sieg Heil, Sieg Heil!"

The meal was not very satisfying, failing to leave a positive impression or a sense of fullness. Nothing truly stood out, making the entire experience rather forgettable and offering no compelling reason to return. Portions were consistently on the small side, leaving us questioning the value for money and feeling underfed.

We were a table for 2, and notably, we were the only ones there for the entire duration, which created an unusually quiet, almost sterile atmosphere and raised immediate questions about the restaurant's general appeal or popularity.

The beef tongue was served a bit cold, lacking the fresh, hot sensation of a dish just off the grill, which raised concerns about its preparation and holding temperature. The udon pasta, while creamy and flavorful, was regrettably too small to share, reinforcing the pervasive issue of meager portion sizes and limiting its utility as a communal dish. The salmon collar took a surprisingly long while to come out, especially considering the empty dining room, but it was exceptionally tasty and ultimately served as the redeeming highlight of an otherwise disappointing meal.

Service was average at best; an unacceptable standard given that we were the only table and should have received exemplary attention. This lack of urgency meant that despite the small dishes and empty restaurant, there were noticeable, extended waits between each course, dragging out the meal unnecessarily. Compounding this, the salmon collar, which was the standout dish, was also the very last item to arrive, further disrupting the flow and diminishing the overall dining experience.

West Ham's Soccer Team Wants to Score More Goals This Year

West Ham is a soccer team in London, England. Their coach, Sam Allardyce, wants his team to score 50 goals this season. He thinks if they can do that, they'll be one of the top 10 teams in their league.

Last year, West Ham scored 40 goals. The year before that, they scored 46 goals. So Sam thinks if they can get to 50 goals, they'll do really well.

The team is pretty good at stopping the other team from scoring. Last year, they kept 14 "clean sheets" - that means the other team didn't score any goals in 14 games!

West Ham bought a new player named Enner Valencia for 12 million dollars. But one of their best players, Andy Carroll, got hurt and can't play for four months. So now they're looking for another good player who can score goals.

Sam said it's hard to find good players in England because they cost too much money. So now he's looking for players from other countries in Europe who might be cheaper but still really good.

West Ham's first game of the season is against another London team called Tottenham. They're playing at West Ham's home stadium.

The team bought several new players this summer and let some other players go to different teams. The owners of West Ham want the team to play more exciting soccer that's fun to watch.

As a piece of entertainment, this movie may be OK. But why use the real names of real people (some of whom are still living) to tell a story which, for the most part, is completely inaccurate? Other reviewers have rightly pointed out the most blatant inaccuracies in this film. However, having seen and met most of the artists depicted in the film — and having had the fortune to know some of them personally, such as Muddy, who even dined at my home in Lyon, France — what worries me most is that their personalities are also very inaccurately portrayed. For instance, Muddy, who had incredible charisma, is portrayed here essentially as a very tepid character who is primarily concerned with chasing women. And Howlin' Wolf — a tough man, certainly, but a very bright and articulate one — is here portrayed as the terrorizing persona he adopted on stage, but never off it. The true story of the Chess saga (and the Chicago blues) is yet to be told properly.

Andrew Hasler, 28, was killed during a violent attack at his home in St Ives, Cambridgeshire, on 8 January.

Matthew Sharpe, 40, has pleaded guilty not only to Hasler’s murder but also to the attempted murder of his 27-year-old ex-girlfriend, Charna Knights. Her life was saved thanks to the brave intervention of a neighbour.

Cambridge Crown Court heard that the neighbour, alerted by “a commotion,” discovered Sharpe on top of Ms Knights, while Mr Hasler lay nearby, gravely wounded. Mr Hasler had suffered multiple stab wounds and died from a single fatal injury to the heart.

The neighbour managed to pull Sharpe off Ms Knights and kick the knife out of his hand before restraining him. Sharpe subsequently escaped but was apprehended after jumping into the River Great Ouse in St Ives. Police found he had inflicted injuries on himself, requiring hospital treatment.

Detective Inspector Lucy Thomson praised the neighbour’s courage, saying he “put his own life at risk in order to help Charna” and that his swift actions “ultimately saved her life.”

In a statement, Mr Hasler's family described him as a “much-loved son, brother, grandson, nephew, and friend,” adding, “Kind-hearted and living life to the full, he came into the world early and has left us far too soon.”

Appearing via video-link from Bedford Prison, Sharpe was told by Judge David Farrell, “There’s only one sentence I can impose, and that’s life imprisonment. I will hear submissions to determine the minimum period you must serve.”

Sharpe, of Eynesbury, St Neots, is due to be sentenced on 7 April.

Owning A Dog Linked With Having A Healthy Heart
Owning a pet, especially a dog, can improve one's heart health, according to a new statement by the American Heart Association.
The statement said that people who own a dog have lower risk of heart disease, although it is not a cause and effect relationship. It is more likely that healthier people tend to have pets and get more exercise than other people. For instance, an already active person might be more inclined to adopt a dog, which then further encourages outdoor activities like daily walks or playing fetch in the park, thereby cementing their healthy lifestyle.
"Pet ownership, particularly dog ownership, is probably associated with a decreased risk of heart disease," said Glenn N. Levine, M.D., professor at Baylor College of Medicine in Houston, Texas. Levine is chairperson of the committee that analyzed the studies conducted on pet and heart health, and wrote the latest statement.
Previous work conducted on pet ownership has shown that people with pets are more likely to live longer, happier lives than those who don't have any pets. For example, an elderly individual living alone might find companionship and a sense of purpose in caring for a dog, leading to reduced feelings of loneliness and a more structured daily routine. Also, for some children, exposure to dogs early in life makes them less sensitive to allergens, which in turn reduce their chances of developing allergies; a child growing up with a family dog, for instance, might be less likely to develop asthma or hay fever compared to peers without early pet exposure.
In one of the studies reviewed by the committee, pet owners reported higher levels of physical activity than other people. This study included 5,200 adults and found that about 52 percent of dog-owners got the recommended amount of physical exercise. This could be as simple as taking a dog for two 20-minute walks each day, adding significant moderate-intensity exercise to their routine that a non-owner might miss.
Pet owners were also more likely to have lower cholesterol levels and blood pressure. This is likely linked to the increased physical activity and reduced stress that pet interaction can provide. For instance, the calming presence of a dog and the routine of daily walks can contribute to a more relaxed state and better cardiovascular metrics over time.
"In essence, data suggest that there probably is an association between pet ownership and decreased cardiovascular risk. What's less clear is whether the act of adopting or acquiring a pet could lead to a reduction in cardiovascular risk in those with pre-existing disease. Further research, including better quality studies, is needed to more definitively answer this question," Levine said in a news release.
Levine added that people shouldn't go and adopt a dog just because of its benefits on heart health.

I did want revenge on the heroes. After their HQ malfunctioned and burned down the apartment building, I was angry. My anger was a wildfire in my chest, devouring every reasonable thought until only ashes and rage remained. I wanted to do something. Eighty people died—eighty human lives snuffed out like candles in a storm. There were thirty survivors, thirty fragile lights flickering in the soot-black darkness of loss. Supposed to be thirty one. Me. But when I figured out I got the fighting abilities of most of the heroes—the straight out punch out, flip-a-guy street fighting skills that hit as hard and fast as a freight train—I decided to keep things secret.

The heroes hadn't done a damned thing for the survivors and the dead of the apartment building. Left it all up to the Red Cross and the welfare agencies and the overburdened charities, who looked as exhausted as deflated parade balloons after a windy, cold Thanksgiving. Okay, I get it, nobody really trusted the heroes at the moment, but heck, three of the guys could have brought gold back from other planets, plucking it like apples from some cosmic tree. Two of the ladies could have built houses for the survivors in minutes, as if tossing together card houses from full decks. They did shit. Absolute shit.

I busted into my first place two weeks later. Transport hub and research centre for some of the less speedy heroes. It was straight midnight, black as a crow’s wing. Was going to bust the place to hell and back. I could have. Two cleaning men were on the late shift. Overheard one of them talking about how he was kicking a few extra bucks so his sister's kids could have hot lunches at school instead of cold sandwiches—meager meals pale as ghosts. I left out of the bathroom window. I know, I know, I'm a big wuss. I let hot pizza get the best of me—my stomach twisting with guilt like a snake in a rain barrel. But hell, I want to hurt the heroes, not get cleaning people fired. You think the heroes would care these guys don't have a paycheck anymore? Like asking thunder to care about the ant it crushes.

The next place I tried to fuck up ran shuttles up to what formerly was the heroes’ sub-orbital HQ. Now this one was on me. I could have checked it on Google. I got all the way to the launch pad before I figured out the heroes gave (not leased, gave) free launch time to NASA so they could send resupply missions to their own stations. For fuck's sake. I did steal one of their heromobiles. It glided like a shark in deep waters—quiet, dangerous, unmistakable. Sold it on the black market. Gave the money anonymously to seventeen-year-old kids who had lost their parents in the apartment fire, kids who wore grief like heavy coats in midsummer. I took a week off from my 'villain' career. I was planning to come back on Monday. The Terror Squad found me on Monday. They wanted to recruit me. I had a choice to make. FIN.

I sit down at the table and suspiciously watch my SO make eggs. *How are you still alive...? * "WHAT DID YOU SAY?! * She spins around, staring at me aghast. *Oops, must have said that out-loud..* "Yes, you did." *Damn. * "Honey,"I point."The eggs?" She turns around, turns off the stove, then turns back to me. "What do you mean, *how am I still alive? *" "Uh..." I profoundly respond. "Oh my stars,"she curses, rolling her eyes."Is it the black patches that spread over my body?" "YOU CAN SEE THEM, TOO?!" "Of course I can see them! I *told* you that the doctor said I had a skin condition. It's like the opposite of vitiligo. You know how vitiligo, the condition Michael Jackson had, causes skin to lose its pigment and turn white in patches? Well, mine is the complete reverse. My skin is actually *overproducing* pigment, so instead of white spots, I get these dark, black patches. See? This is what I talk about. You never listen to me!" "Huh, I'm sorry, I was thinking about something, babe. What'd you say?" *I don't know what was going on. But all I knew was that I* **was** *going to figure out why the black patches had covered her body and she wasn't dead yet. *

Spread Your Wings, It's Time to Fly

The next piece of the International Space Station might look like a cocoon when it's packed inside Space Shuttle Atlantis for the trip to space. But once it opens up in space, this part will provide one-fourth of the station's power and will look more like a huge butterfly.

The two main pieces are clearly visible when hanging from a crane in the building where they work on space station parts. Connected in the middle are the six-sided P3 part (left) with a rotating joint, and the P4 part (right) with its solar panels folded up.

This P3/P4 piece is the main cargo for the STS-115 mission, planned to launch from Kennedy Space Center in Florida in late August or early September.

"When folded up for launch, this piece is about 45 feet long. But once in space with the solar panels spread out, it will stretch almost 240 feet across," says Robbie Ashley, who manages space station missions for STS-115.

The main goal of this mission is to start building the space station again by putting this piece in place during three spacewalks. These pieces are part of an 11-piece frame that will eventually stretch more than 300 feet when the station is finished.

"The P3/P4 piece will be attached to the end of the P1 piece that's already in space," explains Ashley. "It will do two main things. First, it will make power. This power system will create, store, share, and control electricity for the space station. It will add to the power already there from the P6 piece. Also, the P3 half has a machine that will rotate all the outer pieces, including the solar panels, so they stay pointed at the sun to make the most power."

On Earth, the P3/P4 piece weighs almost 35,000 pounds - one of the heaviest space station parts. It will be weightless in space, so the astronauts can take it out of Atlantis' cargo area using robot arms. The astronauts will then move it into place and attach it to the P1 piece. The astronauts will need three spacewalks to do everything.

The containers holding the supports for the solar panels can be seen on the end of the P4 piece. Once opened, its two large solar panels will provide one-fourth of the total power for the finished station.

A big job during the spacewalks will be opening the two solar wings. When spread out, each wing will be 115 feet by 38 feet. They open in opposite directions and each has a center support holding a solar "blanket" on each side. Each wing uses nearly 33,000 solar cells and on Earth would weigh more than 2,400 pounds. The two new wings can make enough power for 30 average homes, based on each home using 2 kilowatts of power.

The P3/P4 piece will be moved from the Space Station building to the launch pad in a special container. It will then be put into Atlantis' cargo area for the STS-115 mission launch.

"But will you remember me after you go back?" she asked me. Her eyes glistened from the moonlight as we sat together in the middle of the empty basketball court. "Of course I will. You have no idea how these few weeks being with you have changed me. I can *not* forget you" I replied with an uncontrollable smile on my face. *she thinks I'm going to forget her after i go back to college. Thats so cute. If only I could make her understand how much my outlook on life changed after I asked her to be my girlfriend* 

We stared into each other's eyes for a few more seconds before she pulled her gaze away. "Cheesy moment" she muttered under her breath. *she's adorably cute i have no idea how I'm going to be okay when i go back* I thought to myself as I admired her features. Her glasses made her look smart and elegant but I knew behind those glasses was a witty girl always ready to fire back an answer. 

The leaves of nearby trees rustled in the wind as I embraced the quietness and solitude, but I missed hearing her voice. "Neeza" i said softly but loud enough for her to hear. As she turned towards me, her face was dimly lit but I could still see that smile on her face. The one I would've done anything to see. The genuine happiness on her face brought me joy. 

"You don't need to worry about me forgetting about you. You will *never* become a memory. And I hope I will *never* become a memory. But sure as hell, these times we spend together will **never** become a memory." We stared deeply into each other's eyes again. Then she replied ever so softly "Hopefully this relationship will *never* become a memory."

It's hard to tell how vital this visual diary, with its slightly faded colors and the soft rustle of its pages, truly is in the grand, often intimidating, scheme of photography. More than a mere collection, it's a very personal travelogue, a whispered confession laid out in vivid squares. Shore is relentlessly obsessed with himself and where he goes and what he sees – a raw, almost forensic gaze capturing the sun-drenched diners, the peeling paint of motel rooms, the fleeting expressions on strangers' faces, and the endless stretch of highway. As a summary of early seventies pop culture, it is fantastic, an unfiltered snapshot of a bygone era where you can almost hear the distant, tinny music and smell the stale cigarette smoke clinging to its scenes. You find out what people looked like – their wide-lapel shirts, their shaggy hair, the easy smiles and vacant stares – and more importantly, what everything else looked like: the garish patterns of wallpaper, the chunky dial phones, the chrome gleam of classic cars, the mundane clutter of everyday life frozen in time. I appreciate this intimate, almost archaeological aspect of the book; it feels like sifting through a forgotten time capsule, a detailed, sometimes startling, reference guide to 1972, complete with its muted color palettes and raw textures. I think some of the photography, with its crisp focus and honest composition, is top-notch, though I confess that the sheer volume sometimes overwhelms, suggesting the book would be stronger if edited down a bit, allowing each image to breathe more deeply. While more interesting in its granular detail than a more polished, less candid account, it sometimes packs less of an immediate punch, feeling more like a slow, immersive wander than a sharp, sudden revelation. Could you live without the quiet hum of its nostalgic observations? Of course. But once you've felt its unassuming pull, do you truly want to? No.

**The Dangerous Price of Beauty: Why Women Risk It All for the Perfect Look**

Picture this: You're scrolling through Instagram, mesmerized by Beyoncé's flawless waves or Jennifer Aniston's effortlessly chic bob. That nagging voice whispers, "I *need* that look." But what if achieving it could cost you more than just money?

A shocking new study reveals that **one in five American women are willing to gamble with their health** for beauty—even when they know the treatments could be dangerous. Yes, you read that right. Despite understanding the risks, nearly 20% of women would still pursue hazardous beauty treatments to get that coveted celebrity look.

The numbers are eye-opening: While over half of women worry about long-term damage from beauty treatments, 7% have already experienced allergic reactions. Yet the allure of transformation proves irresistible.

**The Celebrity Copycat Culture**

We're living in an era where 53% of women draw beauty inspiration from celebrity photos and videos. Jennifer Aniston, Sandra Bullock, and Jennifer Lopez top the list of most-copied hairstyles, while stars like Beyoncé, Paris Hilton, and Selena Gomez influence the weave and extension trends.

But here's the reality check: What works for A-listers with professional stylists and unlimited budgets can spell disaster for everyday women.

"Many of those glamorous looks can do more harm than good, leading to long-term problems," warns LaQue Gushon-Harris, president of beauty research organization LQS and Associates. "Unfortunately, most women are not even aware of the repercussions."

**The Hidden Dangers Lurking in Your Beauty Routine**

The survey of 1,000 American women uncovered some alarming truths. Those trendy weaves and extensions that give you Beyoncé-worthy hair? They could be causing **traction alopecia**—permanent hair loss from constantly pulling at your follicles.

Even seemingly innocent treatments carry risks. The top five beauty procedures—manicures, pedicures, hair coloring, teeth whitening, and perms—all have potential side effects ranging from skin swelling to overly painful procedures and hair loss.

**The Confidence Trap**

Despite the dangers, women aren't backing down. Nearly half say beauty treatments boost their confidence and make them feel better about themselves. In a culture where one-sixth of women go to extreme lengths to mimic their favorite celebrities, knowledge of risks might not be enough to stop them.

The most startling finding? When told their treatments would cause **permanent damage**, 4% of women said they'd continue anyway, and 6% would just reduce frequency. It's a sobering reminder of how deeply beauty standards have penetrated our psyche.

**The Education Gap**

Here's the silver lining: 67% of women say they would stop if they truly understood the health risks. The problem? A staggering 56% want more information about side effects, and nearly a quarter have no idea about the dangers of their treatments.

"It is very important to be educated about whatever it is that you are doing to your body," emphasizes Gushon-Harris, who travels nationwide teaching women about cosmetic treatment dangers. "These damages can be lasting and life changing."

**The Bottom Line**

Beauty shouldn't come at the cost of your health. Before you book that next appointment chasing a celebrity look, ask yourself: Is temporary perfection worth permanent damage? The answer might just save your skin—literally.

By Nina Golgowski . PUBLISHED: 10:29 EST, 11 November 2012 . | . UPDATED: 11:57 EST, 11 November 2012 . An Atlanta community has painted over a French artist's mural commissioned by a nonprofit organization to improve their neighborhood - because they say its depictions are 'satanic'. The artwork covers a wall in southwest Atlanta by French graffiti artist Pierre Roti and features a fantasy creation of a part-human, part-alligator, part-fish creature under the commission of non-profit group Living Walls. 'The fishes were satanic, the scales were satanic. You know, they just don't feel that it represents their community,' Angel Poventud, an upset resident from the wall's Pittsburgh neighborhood, told WSB-TV. Defaced: Part of French artist Pierre Roti's mural, commissioned by a non-profit group in Atlanta, is seen before upset residents covered it with paint on Friday calling it 'satanic' Upset: Seen as part alligator, part human, part fish, some residents were upset by it calling for its removal before taking matters into their own hands . Aftermath: The wall is seen on Friday morning after grey paint was rolled across it with the help of a former State lawmaker who lives nearby . However, residents who had enjoyed the mural were outraged to wake up on Friday morning and see sections of grey paint rolled across it - just days after a petition was signed by more than a thousand residents to save it. 'To see it being defaced that way, it was disappointing and saddening,' nearby resident Adrainne Gray told WSB-TV. There with her was her young son Everrett who said he had enjoyed the mural too, making a face over the work's aftermath to show his disgust at its covering. 'I didn't think it was nice,' he said. What's now at the heart of the issue is whether Living Walls had permission to create the mural in the first place, as fans of the mural spent the remainder of Friday scrubbing off the grey paint. Restoration: Fans of the mural spent the remainder of Friday scrubbing off the paint to restore it to its former display . Failed petition: A woman scrubs at the covered mural on Friday that was defaced despite a petition that collected more than 1,000 signatures to save it . Hard work: The 23-year-old graffiti artist, seen during its creation, spent a month working on it as one of many artists commissioned by the group world-wide . The property belongs to the Georgia Department of Transportation who have said that they never issued a permit for the mural. Arguing their case, Living Walls showed signed applications approving the mural by three Atlanta city departments. The group said they didn't realize they needed City Council to pass an ordinance approving the work as well. 'Nobody in the Pittsburgh community knows what this represents. Nobody. That is unbelievable,' former State Representative Doug Dean told Creative Loafing. Illegal: Brewing controversy is whether either side had right to paint on the wall with it belonging to the Georgia Department of Transportation, Councilwoman Joyce Sheperd seen upset at the scene . Disgust: A resident upset by the grey paint rolled across the mural shows his disgust . Mr Dean was one of the many who helped cover the mural that morning with a Facebook posting helping organize the event titled: Painting OVER, of the WALL on University Ave. Asked if it's true he didn't have a permit to paint over the wall on Friday himself, he told CL: 'That's right. Didn't try to get a permit ... I don't think I have to. I'm a tax payer.' Amid the outcry over the work which took the 23-year-old artist a month to create for the Atlanta non-profit - whose stated goal is to provide artists from all over the world an opportunity to 'create a more intimate relationship' between themselves and the community - more than 1,000 signatures were collected to save it. Future: What's now uncertain is the future of the mural despite its restoration efforts on Friday . Regardless, Mr Dean said those numbers shouldn't matter. 'It is so wrong for them to get petitions from people who live in Capitol View and Sylvan Hills and not come to Pittsburgh and talk to us,' Mr Dean said of its location. 'All we want is a process for how to deal with artists who come to our neighborhood and do what they want to do. We have some art we want to do in our community,' he said. The mural has since been restored to before its defacing, but GDOT says they will most likely have to cover it up again.

Anchorage has grown safer over the years, experts attest.

ANCHORAGE – On March 27, 1964, Alaska experienced North America’s greatest recorded earthquake. A 9.2 Richter quake killed 130 people and caused over $300 million in damage.
Could it happen again? Experts confirm strong quakes will rock our state, but improved building codes mean structures are better equipped to withstand them.

Anchorage sits on shaky ground, particularly the Turnagain Bluff. In 1964, 75 homes there tumbled into Cook Inlet as the ground gave way. Today, top municipal planner Sharen Walsh affirms, homes on the bluff meet far higher standards.
“Ground failure is the issue,” Walsh stated. “Designs must account for it, ensuring homes remain intact even if the ground shifts.”
Walsh noted that homes in earthquake hot spots, such as the bluff and Bootlegger’s Cove, mandate structural engineer design. Many feature special, movable foundations. Most Anchorage buildings constructed since the 1980s now meet rigorous, quake-resistant design standards.

Yet, cautions persist. Despite lessons learned, the exorbitant cost prevents mandatory retrofitting of older buildings to current safety standards, a concern for some. Another issue: survivors of the ’64 quake may grow complacent, underestimating present risks.
Experts warn every earthquake is unique. Its strike location and ground response will dictate damage. Anchorage must remember its past — what happened once could happen again, though hopefully with a better outcome.

"Looking at your shoes will lead to an immediate arrest, please stay calm." The words floated over the intercom and into my ears, but it took a second for their meaning to penetrate my brain. Once I realized what had just been announced I snapped my head upwards, breaking the eye contact I had just been making with my shoelaces. Looking around I could see many confused faces, and murmured conversations about the peculiarity of the announcement could be heard throughout the terminal.

I shuffled forwards in the queue to reach the front of the line, loaded my bags onto the conveyor belt and stepped forward. "What was that about?" I asked the member of airport security working the metal detector. 

"Don't you worry," she began to speak, but suddenly a look of shock crossed her face. "Ma'am, what are you doing?" 

I turned to see what had caught her attention—a woman in the adjacent line was gazing downwards at her feet. She did not respond to the guard's question. The security guard immediately drew a gun and pointed it at the woman. "I need you to get on the floor right now ma'am," the guard commanded. People from other queues looked over to see what was happening.

Ignoring the security guard's order, the woman slowly raised her head. As I made eye contact with her, my first instinct was to look away, but I could not manage it. Her eyes were an indescribable colour—somehow they held more emotion than anything I had ever felt. I could feel her gaze pierce my eyes, travel through my optic nerve and settle on my brain. A crushing feeling filled my skull before the world turned black.

An orientalist, and a monk of Rennes in Brittany; date of birth and death unknown. He entered the Capuchin Order and spent the formative years of his religious life in arduous missionary endeavors in the Levant, where he immersed himself with fervent zeal in the rigorous study of Oriental languages. His mastery of these tongues quickly captured the attention of his superiors, and, being summoned to Rome, he was commissioned by the Congregation of the Propaganda for the intricate translation of several seminal works into Arabic. The first monumental achievement of his extensive labours in this field was the meticulous translation of "L'Abrégé des annales ecclesiastiques de Baronius ", continued by Sponde to the year 1646. The work was published at Rome in three volumes quarto, the first of which appeared in 1653, the second in 1655, and the third in 1671. Britius had also been instrumental in a comprehensive translation of the Bible into Arabic presenting the Vulgate text in meticulously arranged parallel columns, which was published by Mazari, at Rome, in 1671 (3 vols. fol.).
The works of Britius are now exceptionally scarce, as virtually the entire edition of both translations was dispatched to the East for crucial deployment in the missions' endeavors.

Waited over 2 and a half hours and didn't even see a nurse. The ER staff now directs all patients to wait in a small back room, which seems intended to make the main ER lobby appear empty and give a false impression that wait times are minimal. Don’t be misled by the emptiness of the main lobby—every patient is actually packed into a room that's barely half the size of the original ER waiting area, where they sit waiting, often anxiously, to first see a nurse practitioner before ever being evaluated by an actual ER physician. This process feels intentionally disjointed and inefficient, possibly as a way to double bill patients—since after finally being seen by the nurse practitioner, people are usually sent back to wait again just to be seen in the ER itself. 

The whole experience is incredibly frustrating and lacks transparency. We are literally sitting in a makeshift emergency extension hallway, EX2, a location many patients probably don't even know exists. Since our arrival—over two hours ago—the only hospital employee to interact with us was registration staff, who came in just to collect our insurance information and ensure the hospital gets paid. No one else, not a single nurse or doctor, has checked in on us, explained the delay, or provided any updates about our care or the expected wait. The sense of being forgotten and shuffled out of sight is palpable and distressing. At this point, we’re giving up on TMC and leaving, hoping that urgent care can provide the help that this hospital has completely failed to deliver. TMC took us to a hidden, empty part of the hospital and promptly forgot about us, leaving us to fend for ourselves.

He reached the milestone on Sunday during the England match against Sri Lanka.  
At 31 years, five months, and five days old, he saluted the England balcony after reaching his landmark moment.  
Remarkably, he surpassed Indian legend Sachin Tendulkar's record of 31 years, 10 months, and 20 days, which had stood since 2005.  
"It's a very special moment for me," Cook said. "You forget about all the hard times you have as a cricketer."  
To cap off an unforgettable day, England then cruised to a comfortable win, which meant the team have now won the series against Sri Lanka.

I have a very substantial home theatre system, but have limited space available for the center channel speaker—only 6 inches high, 17 inches wide. I was able to audition a Boston Acoustics center, a Definitive Technology Pro1000, a Klipsch Icon XL-12, and the Klipsch RC-10 right in my own living room! The Boston couldn't handle the power; its sound would distort and lose clarity during louder scenes. The Definitive Technology was good but didn't have the high-frequency detail, making dialogue and subtle effects feel muted. The XL-12 was clean and accurate, but its sound was a bit thin, lacking warmth and bass—you could hear the crispness, but explosions felt hollow. The Klipsch RC-10 had it all: it filled the room with powerful, tight bass that you could feel in your chest, smooth warmth that made voices natural and full, and high-frequency detail that brought out whispers and delicate sounds with sparkling clarity. It sounded the best of the four, delivering strong dynamics even at low volume, and was the most efficient, needing less power to fill the room. It's rare to be able to compare that many speakers at once, let alone in your own house, so I thought it would be helpful to share my impressions. Bottom line—if you're limited on space for your center channel speaker, this is the one to buy. Oh yeah, it didn't hurt that I got it from J&R Music for $149.99—no tax, free shipping!

The Fifth Carbon Budget is a plan to reduce the amount of carbon dioxide (a gas that causes global warming) the UK puts into the air by 57% by 2032. This is compared to how much was released in 1990.

Announcing this goal helps make people who invest money in the UK’s energy system feel more confident, because they know there’s a plan to fix old energy systems.

But the Committee on Climate Change (CCC), which gives advice to the government, says that the UK won’t reach these goals unless the government makes better rules.

Barry Gardiner, from the Labour party, said the rules should be even stronger, but he’s glad the Fifth Carbon Budget is happening. He also said it was difficult for Energy Secretary Amber Rudd to get people to agree, especially because of disagreements after the UK voted to leave the European Union (EU).

People who care about the environment and people who work with energy are also happy the Fifth Carbon Budget will happen, but they think the goals should be tougher so the world can try to keep temperature rises as close as possible to only 1.5 degrees Celsius, like in the Paris climate agreement.

Some people were worried the government didn’t care as much about stopping climate change because they cut money for clean energy and decided to leave the EU, which helps control a lot of the UK’s energy rules.

But Amber Rudd said that climate change is still one of the biggest long-term risks for the UK. At a meeting in London, she said climate change is still a serious threat, and that the UK should not ignore Europe or the rest of the world. Even though leaving the EU makes it harder to fight climate change, the UK is still committed to doing it.

Business leaders at the meeting agreed with former Labour Climate Secretary Ed Miliband, who wants the UK Prime Minister to officially accept the Paris climate agreement right away.

The latest CCC report says the UK is on track so far with its promise to cut carbon emissions by 80% by the year 2050, according to the Climate Change Act.

But the report says the UK needs new ideas to keep cutting emissions. It mentions houses, farming, and transport (like cars and planes) as areas where pollution is actually going up, not down.

For example, more people are buying bigger cars now because rules changed so people pay the same tax for an environmentally-friendly Prius as they do for a gas-guzzling Porsche.

The report also says the government should support carbon capture and storage technology, which is a way to make burning fossil fuels less polluting. The Prime Minister once said this was very important for the UK, but then stopped giving money to support it.

Professor Dave Reay from the University of Edinburgh said people might not pay attention to this report because they are so focused on the UK leaving the EU, but it’s important because the UK needs to do better at fighting climate change in all areas, like farming, transport, saving energy, and using technology to capture carbon.

Marylyn Haines-Evans from the National Federation of Women’s Institutes said the Carbon Budget being agreed to is good news for future generations and for British nature, like animals and plants.

A group that doesn’t believe climate change is a big problem, the Global Warming Policy Forum (GWPF), said the government should have waited for the next government to make this decision, especially because things are changing a lot after the EU vote.

Simon Bullock from Friends of the Earth said that after all the confusion from the Brexit vote, this decision gives businesses more certainty. Now people wanting to invest money to help the UK use less carbon will feel safer. But he also said that the real challenge is to make the rules even stronger to make sure the UK meets its carbon goals.

Environmental groups were also happy that the decision about a new runway at Heathrow airport was delayed after the Brexit vote, because they think more time is needed to think about how it affects climate change. They also hope that Brexit makes French worker unions reject plans for a new nuclear power station at Hinkley.

The Labour party says the government waited so long to tell Parliament about the Carbon Budget that they actually broke the law, which says both parts of Parliament have to agree to the budget before the end of June. Now, this can’t happen in time, but the government says the budget will still become law.

Kids as young as ten will get to see pictures of Britney Spears before and after they were changed with a computer to make her look different. The goal is to help kids feel better about themselves and understand that pictures in ads and magazines are often edited to make people look "perfect," even though that's not real.

Britney shared pictures of herself before and after editing to show how much photos can change. In the edited photo, her legs look skinnier and her skin looks smoother. Kids will also see pictures of models and actresses, like Keira Knightley, whose bodies were changed with computers to look a certain way.

Kids will learn that people use computers to make hair look shinier or muscles look bigger in photos. They’ll also look at ads from companies like Dove and United Colours of Benetton that use pictures of regular people and people of different races.

Teachers will get notes to help explain to kids how magazines change pictures and how it can make people feel about themselves. A leader named Lynne Featherstone said that she wants kids to know they are important for more than just how they look.

By . Mark Prigg . Amazon has poached the man behind Google Glass and the firm’s smart contact lenses, and is believed to be developing its own interactive eyewear. Babak Parviz broke the news on his Google+ page, saying: 'Status: Super Excited', alongside an Amazon logo.

Scroll down for video.

Parviz designed the optics in Google Glass and the firm’s smart contact lens—and is now moving to Amazon to work on a secret project. Google's smart contact lens can measure glucose levels in tears using a tiny wireless chip and miniaturized glucose sensor embedded between two layers of soft contact lens material. These prototypes are capable of generating a reading once per second. To further enhance their usefulness, Google is also investigating how the lens could serve as an early warning system for wearers, integrating tiny LED lights that illuminate to indicate when glucose levels cross critical thresholds.

Parviz worked in Google's secretive 'X' lab, where he contributed to both Google Glass and the smart contact lens for monitoring glucose in diabetics. Before joining Google, he was an affiliate professor at the University of Washington and worked at Microsoft on a smart contact lens system. Late last night, he updated his Google+ profile to reflect his new position. He wrote, 'I founded and led a few efforts at Google (among them, Google Glass and Google Contact Lenses are public so far :) prior to moving to Amazon and work on a few other things now...'

His transition from the Glass team came after stepping down in December last year. At the time, he said, 'After founding, building, and running the Google Glass team for nearly 40 months, I stepped down this year. It was an amazing ride and it gave me the opportunity to work with some really wonderful people. Together, we made a radically new piece of technology real and possibly started a new platform in computing and communication!'

The timing of Parviz's move is particularly interesting, as Amazon CEO Jeff Bezos recently debuted the new Amazon Fire Phone. The handset boasts a 4.7-inch display with 720p HD resolution, a quad-core Qualcomm Snapdragon processor, 2GB of RAM, and six individual camera modules to create its unique 3D effect.

The move to hire Parviz comes on the heels of Amazon’s first smartphone announcement. The Fire Phone incorporates head-tracking technology for a 3D display effect, called ‘dynamic perspective.’ Introducing the phone, Bezos remarked, 'There is always more to see by changing your perspective.' Apps using this feature include a Maps app that lets users see around buildings and tilt the phone to 'look around' an on-screen map.

Amazon also revealed a new app called Firefly, which takes advantage of the phone’s camera and visual recognition software to identify objects like books, CDs, and even music, then look for them on Amazon. Firefly can also recognize phone numbers, web addresses, and TV shows. According to Amazon, it can recognize 100 million items in less than one second, and once an item is identified, it can take users directly to the corresponding Amazon page to make a purchase. Firefly is activated by holding down the dedicated camera button.

In addition to these features, the Fire Phone comes with a highly customized version of Google’s Android operating system, similar to what’s found on Amazon’s Kindle Fire tablets. It also includes special tangle-free headphones, a Gorilla Glass 3 screen with a rubber frame, and aluminum buttons. Users benefit from unlimited photo storage via Amazon Cloud and can sync with the company’s new Fire TV set-top box to send media to their television. Launch apps will include Netflix, HBO Go, Hulu Plus, and ESPN.

Dr. Babak Parviz’s high-profile move to Amazon has sparked rumors that the firm is developing a smart contact lens—offering another glimpse into Amazon's ambitions in innovative hardware.

"Bruce, have a seat! We need to chat." Bruce settled into the chair with a curious expression. "Kim, what's going on? How are things with the family empire?" "Oh, don't you worry about my ventures, Bruce! But we do need to discuss that little favor you owe me." Kim said with a playful grin. "Kim, I... I still haven't sorted out the money situation..." Her smile grew wider. "I know, Bruce! Listen, something exciting is brewing and we need to create the perfect distraction. You're going to do something so incredible that the whole world will be absolutely captivated!" "But I thought the wedding with Kanye was..." "BRUCE! I'm the mastermind here! We have an opportunity and you're going to help us seize it!" "Sorry Kim, I didn't mean to overstep." "All good! Now tell me Bruce, what do you think about femininity?" "Well, I've always been fascinated by it, honestly. There's something so beautiful and powerful about the feminine experience!" "I'm thrilled to hear that... Caitlyn."
