The surfaces, illumination, furniture, customers, even the atmospheric music flowing from massive, hidden speakers carried an overwhelming clinical coldness. Chatter filled the space, a monotonous backdrop broken by the frequent chime of crystal. Two forms occupied a booth, invisible to most yet instinctively shunned by everyone. One reclined upright against the soft crimson padding, studying his companion with focused attention. His appearance blended seamlessly with the establishment, remarkable only due to his chosen associate. The other appeared disheveled by this venue's immaculate expectations. The tiny loose fibers might as well have been a beggar's tattered banner, exposed for all to witness and judge. She curved inward, fingers interlocked before her, gazing into her beverage with cosmos-tired vision. Their cocktails, radiating with mysterious luminescent processes, remained ignored on the surface between them. "Are you certain?" he inquired, digit lazily tracing his glass's edge. The resulting vibration was swallowed by the murmur and vanished like whisper in a storm. "Yes," she replied softly, offering confirmation. "I no longer desire it." "You craved it four millennia past." "I craved you four millennia past." He offered her a silent grin, recollecting. "You were the finest I'd ever encountered, naturally I'd bestow upon you my blessing." "Please... It's excessive now." "You understand that time will pursue? And I'm uncertain how severely it might pain you, I've never attempted it." "It would be pleasant to experience terror once more." "And you comprehend what you'd need to accomplish—what we'd need to accomplish?" She confirmed and smiled briefly. "I simply... I long for them. Every single one." They remained there momentarily longer, then he extended his palm toward her. She lifted her gaze, vision brimming with moisture and settled her palm in his. His vision illuminated with azure hints as he lowered to press his mouth against it and where his mouth made contact, her flesh became just slightly more ashen. "If this shall be your final one, I'll ensure it becomes memorable," he declared as he drew her gently upward. They departed, his limb sheltering her frame.

The Calgari had never met him. But then came the Xylorans, the Ghothlak, and the Keflhar. Two of these peoples had stories, deeply woven into their religions, about a man who was born among them and preached about loving one’s brother and similar things. In the end, He departed peacefully. Then came the Fighal, the Bhurlaf, and the Werewe, all of whom had encountered Him—and now, they formed a harmonious alliance of nations. Of the four hundred civilizations in the galaxy, 386 had received His Word, learning about Love and Heaven. And in all of them, the man’s name was the same: Jesus. Three thousand years ago, he had come to Earth. Before that, his last appearance had been with the Gholhari, where He unified their world’s religions, bestowed technology beyond their current achievements, and left—again, peacefully. Among every civilization, His disappearance never went unnoticed. Every major theologian and philosopher formed their own theories about what had happened. But we knew things they didn’t. And we intended to keep the secret.

The singer, originally from Pontypridd, received a 35-year prison sentence in 2013 after confessing to multiple offences. The Independent Police Complaints Commission (IPCC) announced that a detective sergeant and two detective constables should face misconduct proceedings. South Wales Police stated it accepted the IPCC's conclusions. The oversight body reviewed the force’s handling of reports and intelligence about the singer going back to December 2008.

The IPCC determined that a detective sergeant failed to take adequate steps to advance investigations, resulting in a key witness’s allegations being dismissed as unsubstantiated. They recommended that this sergeant face gross misconduct charges. Additionally, the two detective constables were found not to have pursued all reasonable investigative avenues and also face misconduct proceedings.

The IPCC also advised that the same detective sergeant should face gross misconduct proceedings regarding a separate allegation that a 15-year-old girl had been raped by a different suspect.

"The process has been complex," said Jan Williams, IPCC commissioner for Wales. "We have thoroughly examined numerous reports and allegations about Ian Watkins spanning four years, to clarify what was known, when, and how officers responded. We intend to release a detailed report, including our findings on South Wales Police’s broader organisational response, after the conclusion of ongoing misconduct proceedings."

The inquiries began following an internal review, after South Wales Police referred itself to the IPCC. Assistant Chief Constable Jon Drake said, "We accept these recommendations and will act quickly on the investigation’s findings, including moving misconduct proceedings forward this autumn. Protecting vulnerable people and children remains our top priority, and we now have dedicated staff focused on child protection, working closely with partner agencies."

Joanne Mjadzelics, Watkins’s former girlfriend who repeatedly reported him to the police, said the IPCC investigation had vindicated her. Speaking to the BBC, she said she was still haunted by the experience and disappointed that dismissal was the most severe consequence the officers might face. She was acquitted last year of possessing child abuse images, which the court heard were intended to expose Watkins’s crimes. "Every new revelation feels like further vindication for me," she said.

The children’s charity NSPCC Cymru described it as "deeply troubling" that the IPCC found the abuse claims were not properly investigated. "Reporting child abuse is an incredibly brave step, so it’s essential people can trust that their concerns will be addressed immediately," a spokesperson said.

The IPCC is also due to release another report examining how South Yorkshire Police handled allegations related to Watkins in 2012.

I was lurking in a shadowy alleyway, wrapped in my garish, galactic Nyan Cat sweater. "Hey, I heard about you from my cousin," he muttered, glancing around nervously. "I was wondering if you could hook me up."  
"Follow me," I replied, voice low. I pushed open a battered metal door that creaked as it revealed a cramped, dimly lit room with a wobbly table in the center. I dumped the contents of my weathered messenger bag onto the scarred tabletop: a chaotic jumble of colorful USB drives, ancient floppy disks, and scratched CDs.  
“These CDs are loaded with lost Vines,” I explained, tapping the stacks carefully. “These floppies? Each one holds a legendary, standalone meme. These USBs are the real treasure, though. Five-gig behemoths, absolutely packed with deep-fried memes, forbidden formats, obscure reaction images—the works. But they don’t come cheap. Forty bucks a pop for these primo, vintage meme USBs.”  
He squinted at the faded, handwritten labels, lips moving soundlessly as he weighed his options. Finally, he selected eight battered floppies and held them up, a gleam in his eye.  
"I'll take eight flops," he said.  
“That’ll be…” I hesitated, mentally tallying, “eight dollars even.”  
He slid a fistful of wrinkled singles across the table, and I handed him the precious disks. He slipped out into the night, and I began gathering my meme contraband back into my bag—when a cop barged in, catching me off guard.  
In my panic, a cascade of dank memes spilled out of my pockets and fluttered to the concrete floor.  
“No, these aren’t memes, I swear!” I stammered, desperately scooping up meme cards, glitchy GIFs, and vaporwave edits as they tumbled everywhere. My hands fumbled, and before I knew it, a nightstick crashed down.  
“Git gud m8” was the last thing I heard before the world went black.

Strategies for Preparing Homeschooled Students for the New SAT Essay

The prospect of composing a timed essay can be a significant source of anxiety for students. For homeschooled adolescents intending to take the SAT within the upcoming year, it is advisable to begin preparation for the essay component well in advance. Familiarity with the SAT essay format and scoring criteria permits students to approach the task with greater confidence. Systematic writing instruction, supplemented by relevant extracurricular activities, can further enhance students’ rhetorical skills and self-assurance.

Incorporating a writing curriculum that emphasizes practice with timed essays is foundational. WriteShop II, for example, is appropriate for 9th and 10th grade students. This program emphasizes mastery of writing mechanics and fosters the development of organized, concise written expression.

The following evidence-based strategies are intended to prepare students for the SAT essay requirement:

Vocabulary Development and Expressive Precision

Cultivating a robust vocabulary and the capacity for precise expression requires sustained effort. Students should be encouraged to be deliberate in both written and oral communication, avoiding ambiguous phrasing and imprecise word choices. Actively seeking clarification and understanding of unfamiliar vocabulary, as well as conscientiously rephrasing statements for clarity, will help students convey complex ideas more effectively—an essential attribute for high-stakes timed writing tasks.

Adaptation to Handwritten Composition

Given the SAT essay is handwritten, students who are accustomed to composing digitally may encounter unanticipated challenges. Regularly practicing the handwritten drafting of assignments can ameliorate this transition. Students should evaluate their penmanship for legibility, ensure standard paragraph indentation, and maintain a reasonable level of spelling accuracy. Early intervention in areas of weakness is preferable to last-minute remediation.

Addressing Perfectionism in Timed Writing

The SAT essay context necessitates the production of a coherent first draft within a limited timeframe, precluding significant rewriting or revision. Effective time management should allow for a brief final review to correct minor errors in language and mechanics. Students, particularly those prone to perfectionism, should internalize the expectation that the SAT essay represents an initial draft rather than a polished, final product. Exercises that mirror this process—such as non-erasable sketching—can be useful in helping students become comfortable with incremental, visible improvement and imperfection.

Analytical Reading and Argument Evaluation

Analytical skills are central to the SAT essay, which requires students to explicate how an author constructs an argument. Collaborative reading of SAT prompts, with family members or peers tasked with identifying distinct elements such as factual support, coherence of ideas, and stylistic or rhetorical strategies, can foster critical engagement. Independent analysis of editorials, sermons, or academic blog posts—accompanied by annotation, underlining, and margin note-taking—enables the practice of identifying and categorizing evidence, logical reasoning, rhetorical devices, and appeals to emotion or authority. Soliciting feedback from an experienced writing mentor or educator further supports the development of effective paraphrasing and argument synthesis skills.

Conclusion

In accordance with the recommendations of the College Board, there are no shortcuts to excelling on the SAT essay. Success is predicated on well-developed instincts in grammar, vocabulary, and composition. Systematic, early preparation increases the likelihood of achieving a favorable outcome on test day.

For a discussion of foundational principles underlying the new SAT essay, see: New SAT Essay Tips: Understanding the Essay.

Author: Daniella Dautrich, WriteShop alumna, independent writer, and educator.

I stayed at the Dewitt for 2 weeks and I had a very pleasant experience. While I am aware of some recent posts stating that they had cold rooms, perhaps like arctic enclaves, I never had any issues with my room temperature. It's worth mentioning that my room, #201, was a street facing room with a view of the lake, an unobstructed path where the wind, like a persistent, unseen sculptor, could blow at, and even then, it wasn't an issue. I was provided with a small space heater, a miniature sun for colder days, however, it wasn't used very often since I felt the room got too hot, almost like a cozy oven.

I want to take a moment to bring some special attention to the ladies that work at the front desk. Thank you both very much for your kindness. Everyday, whether it was at 6am when I was leaving or when I returned to the hotel, both ladies always greeted me with a smile, bright as morning sunbeams, and either told me to have a great day or asked me how my day was. Their consistent warmth was like a comforting melody throughout my stay. My wife, who was with me, also had the same experiences and was very much appreciative of their hospitality, which felt like a refreshing oasis.

For me, parking was an issue, a persistent thorn in an otherwise smooth stay. Not to the fault of the hotel, but the parking garage manager could give a lot better deal to hotel stayers. $29 a day without "in and outs" felt like a hefty ransom, rather than a convenience fee. I understand that the hotel is downtown Chicago, a concrete jungle where space is precious, but I still feel the parking garage could give a better deal to the hotel.

Lastly, the hotel has good onsite laundry, an exercise room, steam shower and sauna. All of which were always clean and in working order. My only problem worth mentioning is the water pressure in the shower, which was a mere whisper compared to a proper cascade, and the Wi-Fi, which moved at a digital snail's pace, constantly requiring reconnection as if it were a shy, retreating tide. Despite these small irritations, overall it was tolerable, like a minor hum in an otherwise harmonious symphony.

Because of the ladies that work at the front desk, I will be returning to this place. Thank you both for your kindness.

NCWIT Fact Sheet

The National Center for Women & Information Technology (NCWIT) is a non-profit community of nearly 900 universities, companies, non-profits, and government organizations nationwide working to increase girls' and women's meaningful participation in computing. NCWIT equips change leaders with resources for recruiting, retaining, and advancing women from K–12 and higher education through industry and entrepreneurial careers.

Find out more about us.

Why Computing Matters

Computing permeates every aspect of our society, creating high demand for technological innovations that transform how we think, connect, conduct research, and build products. This demand drives the economy and directly impacts the job market; the U.S. Department of Labor estimates 1.1 million computing-related job openings by 2024, but more than two-thirds could go unfilled due to an insufficient pool of college graduates with computing degrees. As a highly versatile and sought-after skillset, computing underpins every other STEM discipline and remains essential in today's information economy.

What We Know: Diversity in Computing is Lacking

Despite computing jobs offering some of the highest available salaries, we're failing to make computing education accessible to all and attract diverse talent to the field. Additionally, women already employed in the technology industry are leaving at staggering rates, meaning we're not retaining diverse talent either.

We are Revolutionizing the Face of Technology

NCWIT works to increase the meaningful participation of girls and women in computing using a three-pronged strategy—and it's working. Learn more about NCWIT's:

A piece of Columbia’s protective outer layer, along with its heat-resistant tiles, was found where it landed in Nacogdoches, Texas.

Johnson Space Center (Texas), Feb. 3 (Reuters): Rescue teams searched gardens and forests in Texas and Louisiana for pieces of the space shuttle Columbia today, as experts started their investigation into why the shuttle broke apart.

For the third day, hundreds of police and soldiers spread out across East Texas and Louisiana to find shuttle debris and remains of the seven astronauts who died on Saturday, when NASA’s oldest shuttle disintegrated high over Texas.

Body parts and pieces of the shuttle were scattered over an area more than 160 kilometers (about 100 miles) long and 16 kilometers (about 10 miles) wide, much of it in dense forests.

NASA scientists are carefully going through large amounts of data, paying close attention to signs that the left side of the shuttle overheated and that the shuttle made a sudden, sharp movement just before it broke apart.

An independent investigation board, led by retired Navy Admiral Harold Gehman, also began its work today. Gehman had previously led the investigation into a Navy ship bombing in Yemen. This board’s investigation will happen alongside NASA’s own internal review.

Bill Readdy, NASA’s associate administrator for space flight and a former shuttle commander, said safety is always a major focus for NASA. “We always work to improve safety. We have made a lot of upgrades to the shuttle over the past 10 years. Even though it looks the same on the outside, many parts have been improved,” he told reporters in a video call.

Columbia broke apart just 16 minutes before it was supposed to land in Florida, ending a 16-day science mission. This happened almost 17 years after the shuttle Challenger exploded during takeoff.

Data sent from Columbia—which first flew 22 years ago—showed that the temperature on part of its left side rose quickly by 32 degrees Celsius in just five minutes as it was coming back into the atmosphere.

Four minutes later, “we saw an increase in air resistance on the left side,” said shuttle program manager Ron Dittemore. The shuttle’s controls tried to correct this by turning the shuttle to the right, but soon afterwards, communication was lost.

Dittemore said that the shuttle’s controls moved in ways the team had never seen before. There was no clear single cause, but Dittemore said, “We are becoming more confident that the problem was caused by heat, not by a structural issue.” When a shuttle re-enters Earth’s atmosphere, it faces temperatures as high as 1,650° Celsius. Special protective tiles are designed to prevent this heat from damaging the shuttle.

Dittemore also mentioned that a piece of insulation from the fuel tank hit Columbia’s left wing about 80 seconds after launch, but engineers did not think it caused serious damage at the time.

Debris from Columbia is being sent to Barksdale Air Force Base in Louisiana, where the independent board will meet.

NASA stated yesterday that human remains have been found, but it has not confirmed that remains of all seven astronauts have been recovered.

At the entrance to the Johnson Space Center in Houston, people have left flowers, signs, and balloons to honor the fallen crew. Across the United States, people have attended church services and visited space centers to pay their respects.

The tragedy touched people around the world, not just in the US. The Columbia crew—five men and two women—included Kalpana Chawla, the first Indian-born woman in space, and Israeli Air Force Colonel Ilan Ramon, the first Israeli astronaut.

NASA has grounded its remaining three shuttles until the cause of the accident is found and fixed. After the Challenger disaster, shuttles were grounded for almost three years until faulty rocket booster seals were fixed.

Keeping the shuttles grounded will delay work on the International Space Station, since shuttles are the main way to deliver supplies and equipment. While the shuttles are not flying, Russian spacecraft will carry supplies and crew to the station.

US President George W. Bush met with NASA’s chief today for a private update about the Columbia disaster. The White House said the US space program will continue and may receive a higher budget.

I am pleased that CityMD has established a location in my neighborhood. My husband and I visited the facility today due to his persistent cough, which had developed over several days as a result of a cold, accompanied by red and swollen eyes with white discharge. The staff demonstrated exceptional professionalism and courtesy throughout our visit. Dr. Gonzales conducted a thorough examination, maintained a pleasant demeanor, and attentively listened to my husband's description of his symptoms. The diagnosis was efficiently provided, and prescriptions were promptly transmitted to our local pharmacy. The entire appointment was completed in under one hour.

Zurich's groundbreaking drive-thru brothels have been declared a resounding success by Swiss social services, one year after their controversial launch. The bold experiment in Switzerland's largest city began last August when authorities opened Europe's first municipal drive-in brothel in an abandoned industrial zone. Officials hoped this radical initiative would eliminate street prostitution while providing sex workers with unprecedented safety and security. However, not everyone is celebrating the results. Scroll down for video.

**Revolutionary Privacy:** Two empty "sex boxes" at Zurich's pioneering sex drive-in, now hailed as a triumph by Swiss social services one year after opening in the city's western industrial district.

A sex worker examines the state-of-the-art facilities: Workers participating in the program must obtain permits and pay taxes to access premium amenities including industrial laundry facilities, private showers, and a dedicated café.

Yet authorities acknowledge significant pushback—some workers report devastating income losses since the program's inception, citing isolation from lucrative city center bars and nightclubs. The drive-in's operations have also proven far more expensive than projected, with the initial €2 million ($2.6 million; £1.6 million) setup costs ballooning beyond expectations.

Zurich's revolutionary drive-thru brothel launched last August, relocating sex workers from their traditional city center territories to this secure, streamlined facility. The western district site features clear directional signage and discreet wooden "sex boxes" where clients receive services in complete privacy from their vehicles.

Participating sex workers faced mandatory permit requirements and tax obligations to access the facility's comprehensive amenities: industrial laundry, private shower facilities, and an on-site café.

**Smoke break:** Workers who relocated from established city center locations can now operate safely with minimal disruption in Zurich's groundbreaking drive-thru facility.

**Restricted zone:** Strategic pictograms clearly communicate the site's purpose to any passersby.

Choice wasn't optional. Simultaneously, Zurich implemented aggressive crackdowns on unregulated prostitution, restricting the ancient trade exclusively to the drive-thru and two designated zones.

City officials are celebrating the dramatic results. "The new street prostitution regulations have successfully achieved dual objectives: protecting both residents and sex workers," they announced.

Social services reported that beyond worker protection, neighboring communities experienced minimal disruption from the Altstetten district facility's operations. No surge in street prostitution emerged in the city's two other authorized sex trade zones.

**Aggressive enforcement:** While launching the brothel and licensing two additional city zones for commercial sex, Zurich authorities systematically dismantled the trade citywide. (Picture posed by models)

Residents had launched fierce protests against nightly prostitute influxes on Sihlquai—a major street near the city center—while authorities feared unchecked human trafficking in unregulated markets.

"The service's inaugural year has exceeded expectations," Zurich social services declared.

The drive-in averages 15 prostitutes nightly—half the number previously working the old red-light district before municipal intervention.

A Sex Worker Open University spokesperson, representing UK sex worker rights, told MailOnline the initiative offers mixed results despite positive intentions.

"While any genuinely safer sex-selling initiative deserves support, 'cleaning up' commercial sex areas typically serves public interests over sex worker welfare, often increasing vulnerability through reduced earnings and forced invisibility," she explained.

"Global sex worker advocates, alongside the World Health Organization and UN Women, unanimously agree: full decriminalization—not Swiss-style regulation and licensing—represents the optimal human-rights approach to sex worker safety."

Frankly, I love the Onion; my family and I visit frequently. However, some recent changes have made me lower my rating, which I'll explain here.

The place looks like a log cabin inside and out, and it sits next to a small lake. There's an outdoor patio with a lake view that I love in the summertime. The all-wood interior, high ceilings, and extensive decor create a charming atmosphere. It's spacious—I’ve only encountered a waitlist once, and it was short. Parking is plentiful, as the venue also hosts large events like weddings.

The food is always tasty with generous portions, but the menu has been cut back recently, giving fewer options. The Spinach Artichoke Dip (served in bread) was delicious but surprisingly small. The burgers are great, especially those topped with onion strings, and the Onion Petals are a highlight.

As a brewery, the beer selection is decent; Hefty Weiss is a standout. However, last time, three pints gave me a hangover, which was unusual.

Service is consistently fine—not exceptional, but never rude, with reasonable food wait times. The place is generally clean, though the men's bathroom could use more attention.

Pricing is a big plus—our average check for two is in the $50 range after tax, which is fair.

Strategic Social Dynamics and Organizational Leadership

How do market forces, stakeholder expectations, and global trends shape organizational success? What does "think globally - act locally" mean for today's business leaders: can individual leadership decisions create transformative impact across markets and communities? This executive education course examines the critical relationship between leadership decisions and societal outcomes. First, we will analyze foundational frameworks from leading strategic thinkers who have addressed these challenges. Next, we will assess how key institutions (including media, healthcare systems, and financial markets) influence business operations and the persistent market dynamics created by these forces. Finally, we will examine cases of rapid transformation: including breakthrough leadership in diverse organizations, the 2011 digital revolution's impact on business, and evolving stakeholder expectations around inclusion and equity. Course deliverables include both presentation and analytical components comprising market research, strategic assessments, and executive discussions. The program culminates in a comprehensive strategic analysis of a business challenge or market opportunity that demonstrates your systems thinking capability.

Step back in time to the charming Hollywood Restaurant. A cozy entranceway warmly ushers you right into the dining area. The staff is incredibly friendly, truly a highlight of the visit! While the food offers a classic simplicity, there's exciting potential for an elevated dining experience. The salads, featuring crisp iceberg lettuce, a sweet grape tomato, and fresh cucumber, offer a clean, simple start. Perhaps some exciting new additions could elevate this classic offering even further! My party and I explored a range of dishes, including the Italian platter, spaghetti and meatballs, the fried seafood platter, and the fettuccine with broccoli. The fried seafood platter presented a delightful crispness, and with a focus on showcasing even more of that delicious seafood, it could truly soar. My companion, a true spaghetti enthusiast, found the spaghetti enjoyable, and we can envision this beloved classic becoming even more irresistibly delicious with subtle enhancements. The Italian platter offers a unique flavor profile. My family and I believe there's a wonderful opportunity for the kitchen to infuse these Italian dishes with a rich array of robust, traditional flavors, truly making them unforgettable. The ambiance has a distinctive, nostalgic charm, inviting diners to step into a bygone era. We believe with thoughtful enhancements to both the menu and the overall experience, The Hollywood Restaurant has the potential to become a truly cherished local gem. Our visits have shown us the restaurant's foundational strengths, and we are genuinely hopeful for its bright future and look forward to its continued growth.

By . Paul Bentley . PUBLISHED: . 14:21 EST, 17 June 2013 . | . UPDATED: . 02:49 EST, 18 June 2013 . They have transformed their dull street into a glorious green garden and been lauded by the council several times. But the community-spirited residents of Rockcliffe Avenue in Whitley Bay have now been told by the same authority they must ditch the whole thing - because it is a 'health and safety hazard'. In a sudden reversal, however, the . council which awarded those prizes has now insisted the plant pots are . dangerous and may obstruct people walking down the alley. Despite the . upset in the community, North Tyneside Council maintained residents will . have to apply for grant funding for new planters which meet 'required . standards' and do not obstruct those with 'accessibility needs'. A spokesman added: 'North Tyneside . Council appreciates the efforts of residents of Rockcliffe Avenue over . the years. However, the number of planters has increased to an extent . where they obstruct the pavement. 'Not all residents agree to the scale of this community effort and have concerns about impact on the pathways. 'Under highways legislation we have an obligation to protect the rights of the public at large.' Since residents began transforming the . path six years ago, they have been awarded a number of community prizes . by North Tyneside council. In the prestigious North Tyneside in . Bloom contest in 2010 they were honoured for 'best street effort' and . just last year they won 'best community effort'. Their efforts transforming the dull suburban alleyway have helped cut anti-social behaviour in the area, revitalised the street and got neighbours working together. They say if residents want to save the . displays they must 'apply for grant funding' to find more suitable . plant pots which comply with highways legislation. Mr Kelly, who has spent about £8,000 on the street displays, said locals are deeply upset and angry. Those who have worked for years to . maintain the beautiful 'secret garden' say it is ridiculous that it is . being targeted by 'jobsworth' council chiefs. 'This area has become a wildlife . corridor and attracts butterflies, hedgehogs, bees and bats. Children . from the nearby nursery come here for nature walks,' said Mike Kelley, . 55, who led the transformation. 'Now the same council that gave us the awards wants us to remove the planters. I am absolutely livid.' Uplifting: One resident said that the pathway had even helped the area overcome its anti-social behaviour problems . Defiant: Michael Kelley, who led the transformation, says he is 'absolutely livid' Street in bloom: Michael Kelley with his flowerpots, which are now deemed a health and safety risk . Haven: Locals from all around Whitley Bay come to enjoy the flowers on Rockcliffe Avenue . 'This community has put in a lot of effort to make the street look nice,' he added. 'I have been putting out various . planters and have spent a lot of money doing it. Other residents have . added to that and also look after their gardens well. All this has . helped us to win awards.' Retired home carer Jenny Craig, said: . 'This pathway has given so much pleasure to so many people. It has . improved the area, which used to suffer from anti-social behaviour some . years ago. It's like a little haven.' Haven: Residents say the uplifting pathway has improved the whole area . Hard work: The residents of Rockcliffe Avenue have made a huge effort to make the street look well-cared for and attractive, spending thousands on their gardening . Well-maintained: Thousands of pounds have been spent on maintaining the flowers, which will be removed unless residents change the containers . Fun for children: Laura Guthzeit, manager of Rainbow Childcare Nursery, centre, going for a walk with Mathew, left, and Macy-May Stretton, right . In jeopardy: Residents have been told to apply for a grant for new plant pots if they want to hang on to their flowers . Obstruction: Gardening organiser Michael Kelley pointed out that even if they do get new plant pots that will not over come the supposed access problems . Mother-of-two Laura Turville, 32, of . nearby Edwards Road, Whitley Bay, added: 'I bring my two children here . all the time and they love it. We call it the secret garden. I think . every street should be like this,' while local nursery manager Laura . Guthzeit, 29, said: 'We bring the children here and they love it. 'They point out all the little . features and flowers. It's a safe place to walk down here.'

By Alexandra Klausner. A Los Angeles family was rushed to the emergency room this weekend after eating a mushroom pizza they believe was 'laced with marijuana.' Grandmother Ava Farley and her grandson Clintay Jones, 10, began experiencing unusual symptoms soon after eating several slices of the pizza. "It was like a rush, sweating, heart beating real fast," Farley told CBS.

SCROLL DOWN FOR VIDEO

Ava Farley and her grandson Clintay Jones, 10, reported feeling strange after eating the mushroom pizza this weekend. Unfortunately, Clintay's reaction was even more intense. "I started feeling dizzy and like crazy," Clintay said. According to Farley, her grandson began acting erratically: "He started cussing, going off, talking crazy, ran out the door butt naked."

The adverse reactions led to both being rushed to the LA County/USC Medical Center. After examining their urine, doctors found traces of marijuana intoxication. Medical staff questioned Farley and her grandson about possible exposure to marijuana in their home. "'They questioned him like, 'Have you had any of this? Did you do this? Is there anything in the house? Is this normal?' No, all we had was pizza," Farley explained.

Farley's granddaughters had also eaten the pizza, but they removed the mushrooms before eating. Since only those who ate the mushrooms became ill, Farley suspects the mushrooms were coated with marijuana.

CBS did not publicly name the pizza shop, but they spoke with the owner, Eduardo Selbereo, who dismissed the allegations. "No, that’s incredible, because nobody smokes marijuana right here," Selbereo said. The pizza shop has a strong health track record, earning a 95 out of 100 from the LA County Department of Health. Despite this, Farley says she may sue the shop, stating, "I think that it’s a threat to the community."

This unnamed pizza place scored a 95 out of 100 from the LA Department of Health. The owner, Eduardo Selbereo, insists that no one even smokes marijuana at his pizza place.

Celebrate Trees All Year
Do you know how long a centennial is? It is 100 years and in 2004, Wisconsin celebrated 100 years of professional forestry in Wisconsin. Forestry is the careful science, art, and practice of planting, growing, and harvesting trees. It ensures our forests thrive, providing everything from the lumber for your house to vital habitats for deer and songbirds.
Look around you – from the sturdy lumber in your home to the smooth paper in your notebook, wood products shape our daily lives. And, the good news is that Wisconsin is growing more wood than is harvested every year. This careful management ensures our forests continue to provide the lumber for homes, the pulp for paper, and countless other wood products we rely on daily. Beyond products, these forests are our playgrounds, inviting us to hike winding trails, glide on cross-country skis, feel the thrill of a snowmobile ride, or quietly snowshoe through fresh powder. What else do Wisconsin's forests provide? They are bustling homes for all sorts of wildlife – from soaring eagles and elusive deer to busy squirrels and countless chirping birds!
You can learn more about Wisconsin's forests and trees by checking out the following stories on EEK! When you're finished with that, try some of our Treemendous Fun activities.
Trees of Wisconsin

**London Through Time: A Stunning 86-Year Visual Journey**

These seemingly identical images of the Thames and Houses of Parliament were actually shot 86 years apart—revealing London's remarkable continuity across nearly a century.

**The Original Vision**
In 1927, pioneering cinematographer Claude Friese-Greene traveled across the UK with revolutionary color film technology to create "The Open Road"—a groundbreaking collection that wouldn't be matched by BBC television until 1967. His London segments captured iconic locations: Hyde Park, Marble Arch, Trafalgar Square, and the daily rhythms of city life.

**A Modern Recreation**
In 2013, filmmaker Simon Smith embarked on an extraordinary project: recreating Friese-Greene's footage shot-by-shot. Over six months, Smith meticulously matched every angle and timing across nearly six minutes of film.

**Uncanny Similarities**
The results are breathtaking. While clothing and cars have evolved, many scenes are virtually indistinguishable except for film quality. A policeman's walk along Albert Embankment, children playing by the Peter Pan statue in Kensington Gardens, crowds at cricket matches—the essence of London endures.

**The Legacy**
Friese-Greene's color film was a pioneering achievement, decades ahead of its time. Though he and his father William developed an effective early color process, it proved too costly for commercial use. Claude died in 1943 at just 44, leaving behind this remarkable time capsule.

This side-by-side comparison reveals a profound truth: while London's surface has changed, its soul remains constant—a living testament to the city's enduring character across generations.

Rodney's head snapped up as the door burst open. A hulking brute with a ginger beard and whiskey-flushed cheeks strutted in like he owned the place. The guard slammed the door behind him, pressing his face against the reinforced glass, hungry for violence. Rodney glared at the bastard. "So you're the killer." "Damn right I am," the beast growled, his yellow teeth flashing in a predatory grin. Rodney's jaw clenched as he forced himself to look away. The monster shifted his bulk and spat, "What do I call you, dead man?" Rodney's eyes blazed as they met his. "Rodney. The screws call me Rod. What's your name, butcher?" "Darren. And I'm gonna fry you good." The silence stretched like a wire about to snap until Rodney choked out a sob. "Scared, are we?" Darren sneered. "One pull of the switch and you're bacon." "I can't sleep knowing I'm going to die," Rodney snarled through his tears. Darren threw back his head and howled with laughter. "You'll sleep forever soon enough, boy." Tears of rage streamed down Rodney's face. "Don't you fear hell? Don't you see the mountain of corpses you've built?" "I sleep like a baby every night. When I meet my maker, I'll tell him I was just doing my job." They sat in hostile silence until the guard's fist pounded the door. Darren hauled himself up and loomed over his victim. "See you in the morning, dead man. Time to cook." Rodney spat at the floor as the executioner left. When the guard returned, Rodney was still shaking with fury. "Rod! Lightning Rod, you look like hell!" Rodney's voice cracked with venom. "This one's different, Jeremy. Watching that animal kill again is going to destroy what's left of my soul." "Buck up, Rod. Clock out and forget about it." Rodney dragged himself from his chair and followed the guard out, every step weighing him down with the blood on his hands.

I bought this Hayward pool filter to replace my Pentair 300 because the Pentair's lid had become extremely difficult to twist open, even after applying silicone lubricant to the threads. After 3 years of use, the Pentair required so much force to open that I was concerned about breaking it. The Hayward's lid design appears more robust and should avoid this problem.

One heads-up for anyone switching brands: I assumed I could reuse the existing PVC connection tubes from my Pentair setup, but the Hayward requires different diameter fittings. What should have been a 10-minute filter swap turned into 40 minutes because I had to struggle with forcing the old tubes onto the new filter's inlet and outlet ports (this was my mistake, not a Hayward design issue).

**This is based on another story of mine. ** I am still not sure how I wasn't screaming. Every second sitting in the musty library filled me with paralyzing terror. The sweet elderly lady that smiled at me might as well have been a towering assassin with the way I gasped when she walked by me asking if I needed anything. "Nope... riveting book." "Oh? That one came in earlier today actually. It was a little peculiar with all the cryptic symbols. Let me know if you can figure it out." I could, and it was nightmarish. It started innocuous enough, but then, it covered historical events, I realized. I started following individual storylines, somehow. I still have no idea how I made that distinction. Then, I began to get violently sick when I realized the librarian was right. They were just ancient pictures and intricate glyphs and archaic words that made no sense until I stared. Then, I just knew what they were saying, like cognates. In my reading exploration, I found a string that directly referenced me. The part that disturbed me the most was that it was about my entire life. It was only half a page or so. With the way the language worked, it covered what looked like 80 years in the length of a paragraph, but if you read it a different way using the last word of the sentence's alternate meanings, it changed all your rereading of the sentence. It gives me a splitting headache just thinking of it. I began to follow the tracks, inching closer to my death. "You should probably stop." I jumped when the man who was sitting across from me spoke. He was wearing a tattered coat, a very garish red medallion, and held a pretentious looking fedora in his hands. "It doesn't make things better to know how this ends." "Who are you? What the fuck is this?" "You probably already read about me. Unless you skipped ahead. Newbies always gloss over the present." I swallowed. I skipped to ten years into my future. *Fuck* He got me on that one. "So, that's my book. It has a tendency to leave my hands, but it always comes back." He met my gaze. His eyes were just wrong. I couldn't tell if they were too grey or what, but they just looked lifeless to me. "What is this?" "Exactly what you think. It's The Records of everything." "I don't understand." "No one does. I've, from my perusal, spent the most time with it of everyone on this dimension. It is still a mystery to me." He sighed. "Look, I'll take it and leave you be. I don't want to kill you." "I'm sorry?" "This thing is precious to me. If you find my line, you'll see I am not above any means of acquisition." "But, I live to an old age." I clinched the book tighter. "This isn't set in stone, you know. It's malleable and estimates. Best of all," he looked at me with this sinister smile that would kill kittens in their sleep. "It will change if someone acts against it." I slid him the book without another word. The tears in my eyes were rubbed away as he left me alone. I've had trouble sleeping ever since then.

'Tell me a story, you asked.' I shifted my weight to my other leg. He was all of silence, and more, but his brown eyes **exuded** (showed or radiated) warmth. I tried to find some form of agreement in them, but they only stared. If it was sadness, then it was a **long-enduring** (lasting for a long time) one. If it was anger, wouldn't I know? Happiness, though, happiness would shine, and his eyes weren't shining. They were merely waiting. *Tell me a story*, I think, those were his last words, but now I wasn't sure. 'What stories do you like? I don't remember.' I said. He watched me. I broke eye contact to straighten my coat, then **peered** (looked closely) around the yard to stall the moment. Fall had creeped into it softly and silently, forcing trees into transition, a rain of leaves upon us. 'You were reading *Youth* when we met, that I do remember,' I continued, half **mumbling** (speaking indistinctly). I could feel myself blushing. 'Said it made you want to travel, and write. You thought it was terribly written. I read it, too, some weeks ago. Didn't think it would be that short.' I had found it on the shelves of a secondhand store and took it for my own, a memory. In all honesty, it didn't make much sense to me, but I was happy reading the words he once read, before he saw me first. His life was never about me. I liked that. 'Then you had a phase, right? Those **cheesy** (tacky or overly sentimental) romance novels, God knows why. You've never been more **productive** (achieving a lot; creating or doing many things) than you were then. Was that it? Do you read horrible writing to inspire yourself to do better?' *What a strange idea*, I thought, and instantly regretted suggesting it. I kicked a small pile of leaves. The smell of fall was the best part, but now it would forever remind me of him, in his chair, waiting. *Tell me a story*, I think, but maybe it was something else. 'You hated crime novels. There wasn't a single one you liked, even **loathed** (hated intensely) Sherlock Holmes.' I let out a short chuckle. He could **rant** (speak at length in an angry or impassioned way) for hours about that, despite knowing I would stop listening **halfway through** (at the midpoint). 'I had a theory on that. It wasn't the writing or the character. It's a bit **typical** (common or characteristic), but I think you were merely **jealous** (feeling envy). You always wanted a Watson of your own, or perhaps a Sherlock. Well, the latter you never got, but maybe the former...' I **trailed off** (gradually stopped speaking) into thought, not sure if I just **insulted** (spoke or acted with disrespect towards) my own intelligence or his. A **flock** (group) of Jackdaws landed in the grass, shifting through the leaves, **obsessively** (in an overly focused or compulsive way) peering at me with their bright blue eyes as if I was **encroaching** (intruding) on their territory. 'I could tell you about my day,' I said, with a sigh, 'it wasn't much. A poem would be more **appropriate** (suitable) and I know *those* make you **nauseous** (feeling sick to the stomach). She woke, she spoke, and still was broke.' He sighed along with me, I could hear. My heart skipped a beat, but I continued. 'You never finished your book, did you? You spent so much of your days behind that screen and you never let me read any of it. I tried to find it, but found only one. You named it *Youth*. Figured it was an e-book, but you just stole the title. Let's call it an **homage** (special honor or respect shown publicly) in future. 98 pages. It was good, but you stopped midsentence.' *Tell me a story*, I think. 'Or, perhaps, 'tell *my* story'.' I looked at him, his brown eyes cold. He stopped waiting.

Horrible service. Our server clearly longed to be anywhere but this establishment. Enthusiasm was utterly absent. The staff didn't even bother with a convincing pretense of hospitality. This place is pure hype. My own kitchen prepares steaks far superior to anything served here. We were scouting locations for a valuable feature on my popular foodie series, offering significant exposure. S&W, undeniably, failed to meet even basic standards. I wouldn't trust any of their staff to park a car in my facility, let alone represent my brand on my foodie website. Take your hard-earned money elsewhere! You've been warned. The dessert was merely tolerable, consumed only out of sheer hunger, and offers no redemption whatsoever.

"Look, we believe in finding solutions that work for everyone—" "No Bob, I need to address this directly. This spirit has incredible potential but isn't channeling it effectively. If we could just redirect this energy toward something more positive, we'd all thrive. Whatever challenges you're facing, you have talents that could benefit us all. Why don't we explore a partnership instead? If we work together, we can transform this situation into something amazing for everyone!" "Barbara, you're right! He's not causing harm, and there's real opportunity here! The kids are engaged and excited, and that's something we can build on—" "Exactly, Bob! Our children are discovering new forms of connection and adventure! They're developing courage and teamwork as they explore the supernatural. Their new friends may seem unconventional, but they're learning valuable skills and building confidence!" "Barbara, he can hear you—let's focus on the possibilities..." "You know what? I have a brilliant idea! You could expand your influence to help others while maintaining this special connection with our family. We have excellent entertainment options here. What do you think, Bob?" "Listen, friend—I believe we can create a win-win situation. If you contribute to our household expenses, I'd be honored to offer you proper accommodation. However, I need your commitment to being a positive role model for our children. *Can you do that?*" "Channel your cosmic energy toward abundance, Barbara. I can contribute $500 monthly—more than enough to enhance your entertainment and connectivity. Bob, I'll handle outdoor maintenance and keep the property pristine when neighbors visit. Partnership?" "ONE IMPORTANT BOUNDARY, MY FRIEND." "Please, call me Earl." "PERFECT, EARL. ONE ESSENTIAL AGREEMENT: You must respect everyone's privacy and personal space completely." "Understood completely. I've been a guardian of this home for 120 years, and respect has always been my foundation with every family I've known." "Then we have a powerful partnership, Earl."

From our News Partners at WCBD-TV:
Tucked away on James Island sits the South Carolina Marine Resources Center, home to researchers from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), the College of Charleston, South Carolina Department of Natural Resources, MUSC and the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST). Here, climate change and its impact on our planet's oceans come under intense scientific scrutiny.

"My focus is phytoplankton—tiny algae living in the ocean that generate half of Earth's oxygen. We're working to understand how shifting carbon dioxide levels and rising temperatures expected over the next century will impact various phytoplankton species and their photosynthetic capabilities," describes Dr. Jack DiTullio of his work at the College of Charleston's Grice Marine Laboratory. DiTullio was among several researchers who shared their findings with Rhode Island Senator Sheldon Whitehouse during his recent visit.

"I'm discovering what's unfolding along our coastlines due to climate change—whether through rising seas, shifting acidity levels, or temperature variations. Plus, how all the marine life and vegetation responds to these changes," Senator Whitehouse reflects on his South Carolina coastal visit.

"Citizens deeply care about developments in their home regions. Having state-specific data strengthens my advocacy with fellow senators," Whitehouse notes. He emphasizes that understanding climate impacts across different states enhances his effectiveness as an advocate.

"Climate change represents a critical challenge, and America must spearhead efforts on this and other vital issues. I'm working to forge stronger bipartisan agreement in Washington that climate change is happening and that our response will benefit our nation," Whitehouse states.

"Looking at public priorities, climate ranks below immediate economic worries. But this is a problem where delayed action means that when it does become everyone's top concern, they'll ask 'where were you? why weren't you focused on this? your responsibility is forward-thinking,'" Whitehouse says about his political role. "If pollution drives these changes and creates public health and economic challenges for Americans, government's primary duty—ensuring safety and economic stability—demands action. Ignoring such threats would be inexcusable."

The senator emphasizes that researchers like those at the Marine Resources Center offer more than data alone.

"Outstanding scientific work happens not just at NOAA, but across university marine science programs throughout the coast. It's an incredible source of valuable information. Plus, you get innovative ideas and potential solutions emerging from this research," Whitehouse highlights.

Scientists welcome this bridge between research and policy.

"I hope we see more politicians like Senator Whitehouse who genuinely engage with our scientific work and seek to understand what's happening to global oceans and climate," DiTullio expresses.

We contacted our Lowcountry U.S. representatives. Congressman Mark Sanford responds:

"On our Beaufort family farm, I've witnessed sea level rise over five decades affecting the property's salt marshes. I commend Senator Whitehouse for bringing people together in the Lowcountry to address this challenge. While our solution approaches may differ, fostering dialogue is essential groundwork."

"The Criminal Justice Act 2003 expressly states that one of the purposes of sentencing is the reform and rehabilitation of offenders," the judge intoned, adjusting his glasses. My heart lodged in my throat. "It is for this reason that I recommend the maximum sentence for Mr. Greaves. He will serve seven years' imprisonment, with the opportunity to lessen his term through exemplary conduct and personal improvement." Setting his notes aside, he fixed his gaze on me. His eyes were an unnaturally piercing blue, as if they saw right through me—stripping me bare in the glare of the courtroom. My lawyer was already packing up his files.

"Mr. Greaves... I have to say, this is very disappointing. You are a young man, you had your whole life ahead of you. I hope you take this opportunity to really reflect on the choices you've made, and resolve yourself to a different path upon your release." The usher instructed us to stand as the judge departed. I wondered, not for the first time, if he sensed that I wasn't the one who did it. Jen's face in the public gallery was ashen, paler even than the stacks of paper in my case bundle.

Before security could tap my elbow, I crossed the gap between us in two steps. "I'm sorry," she choked, her voice thick with tears. I had to look away, down at her hands clutching the barrier, just to hold my own emotion in check. "I never meant for this to happen. It's not too late..."

"It is, Jen. We both chose this," I said, feeling a hand grip my arm as I allowed myself to be led away. "Come see me. I love you, Jen... I'd do anything for you." She nodded, her sobs silent now, as the handcuffs clicked shut around my wrists.

My lawyer clasped my shoulder, giving me a look heavy with regret. "Tough one, mate," he muttered. "We could appeal, but you’re likely better off doing the seven years. With good behaviour, you could be out in three. Listen, I’ve got to run, but you’ve got my number, right?"

"Right." 

On the bus ride to the interim jail, I thought about everything. I’d probably be moved by the end of the week—Marston or Winston, maybe. I’d heard those places still had space. I thought about the judge—how he had seemed to genuinely worry about me throughout the trial—with those eyes that looked straight into my soul. I thought about my legal aid lawyer, and how none of this was really his fault. He was just doing what he could; his kids have to eat, just the same as Jen's do. That thought brought me back to Jen, and that hurt more than anything. I love her—I really do. If I didn’t, I never would have made her get into the passenger seat after the crash.

The front of the car had been smeared with blood. She was panicking. I was panicking too, but something in me forced me to stay calm. When the police arrived, I told them she'd slept almost through the whole thing. I was just giving her a ride home, officer. She’s had a few to drink. No, I haven’t, officer. Yes, just reckless driving. Take me in, officer. Give me seven years, Judge. I’ll still have a loyal wife waiting for me at the end of it.

It is widely regarded as one of the most astonishing and consequential upset victories in the annals of history.  
Despite overwhelming odds, a rag-tag group of mestizo and Zapotec peasants—poorly trained, under-nourished, and armed with the most rudimentary of weapons—achieved the unthinkable: they overcame a much larger, well-trained French army equipped with heavy artillery.  
The battle unfolded on May 5, 1862, in the city of Puebla de Los Angeles, a modest town in east-central Mexico. Although it would take another five years before the French were ultimately expelled from Mexican soil, the triumph at Puebla assumed immense symbolic power, becoming an enduring emblem of Mexican resilience and defiance against foreign domination.  
DOWNLOAD: Toledo Magazine Print Edition PDF  
PHOTO GALLERY: Cinco de Mayo celebrations  
Officially known as the Battle of Puebla, the event is more commonly referred to as “Cinco de Mayo.”  
In Mexico, it is primarily a regional holiday, celebrated in Puebla, with festivities that include military parades, speeches, and re-enactments of the historic battle. In the United States, especially in communities with large Mexican-American populations, Cinco de Mayo has become more of a pop culture event that features music, dancing, and food.  
In the Toledo area there are many events held to commemorate Cinco de Mayo.  
One of the most popular is the Toledo Zoo’s annual Cinco de Mayo celebration that will be from 11 a.m. to 3 p.m. today with music from DJ Tony Rios, performances by El Corazon de Mexico Ballet Folkloric Dance group, and plenty of opportunities for children to take a swing at candy-filled piñatas—a cherished Mexican tradition. All activities are free with regular zoo admission.  
Other organizations held earlier events, including Saturday’s East Toledo Cinco de Mayo Festival sponsored by Glass City Customs and Bowling Green State University’s celebration on April 27.  
Despite its growing popularity, many people mistakenly believe that Cinco de Mayo is a celebration of Mexican independence, which was declared more than 50 years before the Battle of Puebla. Mexican Independence Day is recognized on Sept. 16, in honor of Mexico’s independence from Spanish rule in 1810.

I bought my first Dualit in the 90s, thinking a $350 toaster would last a lifetime. The first one lasted eight years. I thought maybe I got unlucky—after all, I live alone and don't eat that much toast. Because I love the design, I bought another. Both were four-slice models.

The first one just stopped working. This one has been sneakier: it stopped toasting one side on two of the slots and stopped entirely on the other two. Now I'm standing nearby, flipping the toast over manually. I don't know if I'm going to buy a third yet. I'm a sucker for this design, but I'm incredibly disappointed in this appliance that should have been a once-in-a-lifetime purchase.

The Kleercut campaign is over.
Canada's valuable Boreal Forest is better protected today. So are old forests around the world.
At a joint news conference in Washington DC, Greenpeace and the Kimberly-Clark Company, the world’s largest tissue-product maker, announced an important agreement that will make sure more protection and responsible care of Canada's Boreal Forest and other old forests around the world.
The agreement also will be an example for forest-product companies everywhere.
Protection for the Boreal Forest in Canada
Canada's Boreal Forest is North America’s largest old forest and is home to animals at risk such as woodland caribou, wolverine and over one billion birds that travel. The new agreement makes sure that Kimberly-Clark, which makes Kleenex-brand products, will no longer be buying pulp from the three million hectare (7.4 million acre) Kenogami and Ogoki Forests in northern Ontario unless tough environmental rules are followed. These two areas within main areas of untouched forest have been a main part of the Kleercut campaign.
Now, Greenpeace and Kimberly-Clark are moving away from disagreement to a new partnership to help more with forest protection, careful forest care, and the use of recycled fiber for making tissue products.
The power of activists and market pressure
Greenpeace's Kleercut campaign was started in November 2004.
This campaign to help protect old forests in Canada and worldwide pushed the company through the market and its large customers and buyers. To show the issue, hundreds of protests took place everywhere, leading to more than 50 activists arrested in peaceful protests. Scientific and reports showing problems, getting the media involved, and talking to owners were also an important part of the campaign.
This work and hard work ended well with Kimberly-Clark’s announcement of the strongest paper policy by one of the world’s top three tissue product makers.
Carrying out the Kimberly-Clark policy
Carrying out the policy will lead to protection of the world’s most at risk forests, more help for careful forest care through Forest Stewardship Council approval and the increased use of recycled fiber in Kimberly-Clark products.
During the development of this policy, Kimberly-Clark stopped buying more than 325,000 tonnes of pulp a year from tree cutting in the Kenogami and Ogoki Forests. The company managing these forests was not willing to protect at risk forest areas in them and provide Kimberly-Clark with Forest Stewardship Council approved pulp.
The Boreal Forest and climate change
Protection of the Boreal Forest is very important to world efforts to stop climate change. This forest is the largest land-based storage of carbon on the planet, storing 27 years worth of greenhouse gas output or 186 billion tonnes. If this carbon is released into the air it will make the danger of very bad climate change worse.
Big increase to recycled and FSC fibre use
Under the policy Kimberly-Clark has set a goal of making sure that 100 per cent of the fibre used in its products will be from eco-friendly sources. It will greatly boost its use of recycled fibre and fibre from forest approved to Forest Stewardship Council rules. By 2011, it will also increase the use of recycled and FSC fibre for North American products to 40 per cent from 29.7 per cent in 2007. By 2012, the company will no longer use pulp from the Boreal Forest unless is it approved to the rules of the Forest Stewardship Council.
Please join us in thanking Kimberly-Clark for supporting protection of the Boreal Forest by sending its CEO a congratulations email.
