The plane, carrying the Chapecoense football team to Medellín, Colombia for what had been billed as the biggest match in their 43-year history - the Copa Sudamericana final against Atlético Nacional - came down on Monday night near the city's airport, killing 71 of the 77 people on board.

Brazilian daily Meia Hora's poignant football pitch emoji symbolizes the devastating end to the remarkable fairytale rise of Chapecoense, a small club from the southern town of Chapecó in Santa Catarina state that had climbed from Brazil's fourth division to international prominence in less than a decade.

The Rio daily Extra portrays a tragic twist to Chapecoense's distinctive green strip, transforming the team's colors into a symbol of mourning rather than hope.

"Who understands?", asks Brazil's Correio Braziliense in bold letters across its front page. The newspaper says the grief-stricken face of a young Chapecoense fan photographed outside the club's Arena Condá stadium "reflects the feeling of millions of people" following the crash that claimed the lives of 19 players, coaching staff, club officials, and accompanying journalists.

Brazil's respected Folha de São Paulo shows devastated residents of Chapecó gathered in somber silence at a special Mass held at the city's cathedral for the victims of the plane crash, with many wearing the team's green jerseys and scarves as they prayed for the souls of their beloved players.

"A major tragedy in football" dominates the front page headline of Brazil's Agora newspaper, which dedicates multiple pages to coverage of the disaster that has sent shockwaves through the football world.

"And somewhere between the stars..." reads the poetic headline of Brazilian paper Correio Braziliense's editorial cartoon, featuring the team's distinctive logo floating ethereally in a star-filled night sky, suggesting the players have found their place among the heavens.

"Forever Champions" proclaims the front page of Colombia's El Espectador, which reports that the modest Chapecó stadium has been transformed overnight into a makeshift shrine and central gathering point for thousands of tributes, flowers, jerseys, and messages of support from devastated fans across Brazil and South America.

"Chain of errors likely cause of plane tragedy", reports Colombia's Vanguardia newspaper, citing early investigations suggesting the British Aerospace 146 aircraft may have run out of fuel and suffered electrical failures during its approach to Medellín's José María Córdova International Airport.

"Farewell champions" reads the emotional headline of regional paper El Pilón, positioned below a team photograph of the Chapecoense squad taken just hours before their final, fateful journey - young faces full of hope and dreams that would never be realized.

BBC Monitoring reports and analyses news from television, radio, web and print media around the world, providing comprehensive coverage of international stories as they develop. You can follow BBC Monitoring on Twitter and Facebook for real-time updates and analysis.

The University of Cape Town's (UCT's) Professor Janet Hapgood has been awarded a $100,000 Grand Challenges Explorations (GCE) grant from the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation.

GCE invests in innovative early-stage ideas with the potential to address critical global health and development challenges, funding individuals worldwide who are pioneering novel approaches to these complex problems.

Hapgood, a member of UCT's Department of Molecular and Cell Biology, will pursue groundbreaking research to identify optimal contraception methods for women at risk of HIV infection.

Her project is among more than 80 GCE Round 9 grants announced by the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation in early November.

HIV prevalence among young women remains alarmingly high in sub-Saharan Africa, where pregnancy compounds health risks through mother-to-child transmission, elevated infant and maternal mortality rates, and pervasive socio-economic challenges.

"A crucial health policy imperative is identifying contraceptive methods that most effectively balance risks with health benefits in these vulnerable regions," the university stated Thursday.

**Research to deliver critical insights with clinical applications**

"Professor Hapgood's research aims to determine which progesterone derivatives offer the safest contraceptive options for women in high-risk HIV areas," UCT explained.

Contraceptive hormones function by binding to specialized cellular receptors. Hapgood's proposal builds on her team's findings that different contraceptive hormones used across sub-Saharan Africa bind to distinct receptor subsets, creating variations in cellular HIV defense mechanisms.

Her hypothesis suggests that certain contraceptives may actually compromise the body's ability to defend against the virus, while others do not.

The project involves exposing human cervical cells from voluntary donors to controlled laboratory conditions, introducing both virus and contraceptives, then measuring cellular defense markers alongside viral infectivity indicators.

"The results will provide clinically relevant guidance on which contraceptives present the lowest risk of increasing HIV susceptibility," the university noted.

GCE represents a $100-million initiative launched in 2008 by the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation. To date, more than 700 individuals across 45 countries have received GCE funding.

The program welcomes applications from any discipline and organization. Initial $100,000 grants are awarded bi-annually, with successful projects eligible for follow-on grants of up to $1 million.

Hark! A monk, a serf, and a knight walkest into a tavern! Lo the tavern had run as dry as a desert bone to the dismay of the new party. The knight, head blazing like a forge and heart roaring like a lion, challenged the patrons to a duel over already poured spirit that glittered like liquid gold. The monk, head crowned with piety's thorns and heart burning with purpose's flame, asked the knight for ale as donations to flow like rivers to those that thirsted for it. The serf, head bowed like wheat before wind and heart soft as morning dew, asked the monk to give the ale as charity, the very purpose of the donation. Hark the monk declined saying that "the lord giveth and taketh away like the tide" the serf replied "I know" and took an ale as naturally as a bird takes flight.

“—and because of that, Lord Ross never felt inspired to become a peaceful painter, and instead pursued politics. And...look at you now! You know the rest. None of this is how it should be.” I finished, looking out at the group of wary eyes assembled before me. A weathered man, wearing the standardized gray working-class outfit now mandatory in half the world, raised his hand. “I can’t seem to imagine,” he said in a thick Australian accent, “Our Savior and Protector Bob Ross—” Everyone in the crowd flinched and saluted. “—ever being anything other than the kind and loving man he is now. You’re lying.” 

I opened my mouth to protest, but the mood of the mob had changed with the man’s words. They seemed sullen—some outraged—and a chant started among them. “We need the dark to find the light,” they repeated, again and again, marching as one closer to me, climbing the platform from which I had been speaking in this abandoned subway. “PUT HIM WITH THE TREES,” someone shouted, and I yelled helplessly as they grabbed me and tied me up before carrying me out of the subway and onto the street. 

Shouts of “TRAITOR!” and “TROUBLEMAKER!” echoed around me as they relentlessly carried me out of the city. From what little I could see while being tossed about, I realized we were entering a forest—dark and ominous, with charred stumps littering the spaces between the trees. My ropes were taken and tied around a thick trunk, and they started piling branch after branch upon me. This was *not* how I had expected them to receive my speech. I thought of my time machine, probably sitting in the alleyway where I had left it, likely to rust for time and eternity. A thick, tar-like substance—I realized it was oil-based paint—was spread onto me, and someone pulled out a lighter. 

A voice near me spoke, soft and well-meaning. I couldn’t turn to see who it was, but their words seeped into me like ice. “There’s nothing wrong with having a tree as a friend.”

The screams at his gate grew louder. There was *always* something. The terrorist who could control metal. The invisible assassin. The Digital Whisperer. How many times did he have to go through this until people would leave him alone? Was saving the world multiple times not enough? And superheroism didn't pay well. "Oh, you're a superhero, you must do things for the community for free, because that's what superheroes do." Without advertisements and occasional care packages from fans, he'd starve. No company would hire him; he was too famous. A plot on his life could mean loss of business and lives. The time devoted to fighting crime in his adolescence left him grossly underqualified for work. He flipped his pillow to the cold side. *They only like you for your powers*, a voice whispered. *What if you couldn't fly, or had no super strength?* It was easy to scream for help. Having no say in helping was difficult. He learned that the hard way when he wanted to sleep in after a long day and left the police to deal with a bank robbery. It was their job, not his. The husband of a murdered teller kidnapped his wife in revenge while he was saving a capsized boat. For all his powers, he had never felt so helpless. The glare at his windows grew brighter as the meteor drew close. *Let it hit,* he thought. *I've earned my rest. And I'll see Tricia again.*

The basket is large and holds a lot of clothes, socks, or anything else. Setting up the basket—including installing the arms it hangs on—took about 3 minutes per basket (I bought 2). They slide in and out smoothly and make the closet look very neat. I ordered this along with the 8-foot Rubbermaid Configurations kit. The whole kit cost me $119, but each basket was $50. So I paid $100 for 2 baskets, which is almost the same as the price of the entire closet kit. That doesn’t seem right. Still, these baskets are great, and together with the Configurations closet system, they make a good replacement for a dresser.

Great paved trail on top of the levee. There's a small parking lot, solar-powered lights, and benches. However, this trailhead is not handicap accessible. There's a paved ramp at the southeast terminus of the trail that connects to River Road. There's also a paved ramp connecting the trail to River Road at Bayou Paul Lane. But there's no (paved) ramp at the trail's northwest terminus. In total, the levee trail is a mile paved.

When I went, a few joggers and another biker were using the trail, which I thought was pretty good for such a small town. I actually ended up chatting with a local woman who said she walks the trail every evening after work—it’s become her daily routine since the lights were installed. One time, local high school cross-country runners were using the trail for training, and I saw parents cheering them on from the benches. There's a park at this trailhead as well, with play equipment for little kids. I can't wait for this to connect with Baton Rouge.

If you're on a bike, the southeast terminus of this trail is less than 2 miles from the historic St. Gabriel Catholic Church, one of the oldest in Louisiana. I once met a group of cyclists near the trailhead who were making a day trip to see the church—they told me they make this ride every spring as a tradition. There's a ramp down to River Road at the end of the levee trail. If it's a bright and clear day, proceed (very very carefully) down River Road (no shoulder) and it will be on the left. Make sure you're visible to cars in some way, I cannot emphasize that enough. Last year, a friend of mine wore a neon vest and flashing bike lights here after a close call with a passing truck; since then, we've both invested in better visibility gear for this route.

The United States and Iran are sitting down for a fresh round of high-level talks about Iran’s nuclear program, in a diplomatic initiative separate from the ongoing P5+1 negotiations in Vienna. This new burst of dialogue comes as the clock winds down on an interim agreement between Iran and the five permanent members of the U.N. Security Council—a deal that relaxed some economic sanctions in exchange for Iran scaling back parts of its nuclear program. While the United States and other nations harbor deep concerns that Iran’s program could be geared toward developing a weapon, Iran insists its intentions are strictly peaceful.

There’s still a wide gulf between the two sides on what a comprehensive deal might look like, a senior administration official told CNN on Saturday. The added talks seem like a sincere effort to find common ground and see if stubborn roadblocks can finally be moved aside. “We believe we need to engage in as much active diplomacy as we can to test whether we can reach a diplomatic solution with Iran on its nuclear program,” the official explained. “These consultations come at an important juncture of the negotiations, and they will give us a timely opportunity to exchange views in the context of the next P5+1 round in Vienna.”

The official emphasized that these new conversations aren’t meant to replace the P5+1 process—which brings together the United States, China, Russia, Britain, France, and Germany—but rather to complement it. Meanwhile, the spotlight remains on Iran’s nuclear capabilities, with U.N. nuclear inspectors heading back to the country as a crucial compliance deadline approaches.

This facsimile is a very helpful resource for identifying Thonet-furniture. Around the turn of the century the Thonet company produced modernist furniture designed by members of the Vienna Sezession, Otto Wagner, Josef Hoffmann, Kolo Moser, Marcel Kammerer. Some of which can be seen in this catalog; their number and place in the catalog is pointed out in the introductory essay. The book is a valuable resource if you are interested in antiques and early 20th century design. Thonet's designs are the foundational prototypes of modern furniture, establishing the essential principles that continue to profoundly influence contemporary design.

Coalition warplanes unleashed a fresh barrage of strikes on rebel strongholds across Yemen, pummeling positions in the central cities of Ibb and Taiz, and hammering targets in Aden and Dalea in the south.

Fierce clashes erupted between rebels and militiamen loyal to exiled President Abdrabbuh Mansour Hadi, with gunfire echoing through the streets of Aden as the two sides battled for control.

Despite Riyadh's bold declaration on Tuesday that its campaign had achieved its goals, Saudi officials made it clear that military action would continue with ruthless precision as needed.

The coalition had set out on an ambitious mission to restore Mr Hadi and his government, and to crush the Houthis' relentless advance across southern and western Yemen.

Yet the stark reality remains: Mr Hadi sits powerless in Riyadh while the Houthis and allied military units loyal to ousted former President Ali Abdullah Saleh maintain their iron grip on vast territories, including the capital Sanaa.

Under cover of darkness, coalition warplanes screamed through the night sky, unleashing devastating strikes on rebel positions near the capital and the strategic third city of Taiz, where the Houthis had brazenly seized the headquarters of a pro-Hadi armoured brigade on Wednesday.

As dawn broke on Thursday, the thunder of explosions shook Aden and its surroundings. The coalition's precision strikes targeted tanks being deployed by Houthi fighters in the crucial southern port city.

Two colleges that rebels had transformed into fortified bases on the outskirts of Ibb and in the nearby town of Yarim were reduced to rubble, while a military base in al-Kafr was obliterated in a direct hit.

The violence escalated dramatically as dozens of Houthis were killed or wounded in punishing air strikes in Dalea, according to Al Jazeera reports.

The previous evening had already witnessed a bloodbath in Dalea province, where eight rebels met their end in fierce clashes, while five others perished in brutal street fighting against southern militiamen in Aden, residents told Reuters.

Adding to the chaos, a newly-formed Islamic State offshoot calling itself the "Green Brigade" claimed responsibility for a deadly bombing that killed five rebels in Yarim on Wednesday, AFP reported.

The UN's sobering toll reveals that at least 944 people have been killed and 3,487 injured in the relentless cycle of air strikes, ground battles, and jihadist attacks that have ravaged Yemen since March 19.

After dramatically declaring an end to its bombing campaign, the coalition announced it would shift focus to preventing rebels from "targeting civilians or changing realities on the ground" while desperately seeking a political solution to the spiraling conflict.

But Saudi ambassador to the US, Adel al-Jubeir, delivered a stark warning to the Houthis on Wednesday: they "should be under no illusion that we will [not] use force in order to stop them taking over Yemen by aggressive actions."

The rebels fired back with their own statement, declaring their readiness to "resume political dialogue... under the sponsorship of the United Nations." However, they defiantly demanded a "complete end to the aggression against Yemen and the lifting of the blockade."

Meanwhile, Human Rights Watch delivered a scathing condemnation, warning that an air strike on an Oxfam warehouse in northern Yemen last Saturday was an apparent violation of the laws of war.

"The fact that the Oxfam warehouse should have been known to the coalition forces raises concerns that the attack was deliberate," the US-based watchdog declared.

Oxfam's Yemen country director, Grace Ommer, branded the attack on the warehouse in the Houthi stronghold city of Saada "an absolute outrage."

She revealed that the coalition had been explicitly told where Oxfam's offices and warehouses were located, and that they contained vital humanitarian supplies for providing clean water to desperate civilians. The coalition has maintained a stony silence, offering no comment on the controversial strike.

**Histones and Histone Genes in Higher Plants: Structure and Genomic Organization**

*Publication Details:*
- ISSN: 61831638
- ISBN: 0300-9084 (Print/Linking)
- DOI: 10.1016/0300-9084(93)90057-Y
- PubMed ID: 8268253

**Abstract**

The primary structure of plant histone genes was determined through comparative analysis of nucleotide sequences from 23 genes and 14 cDNAs across eight distinct species. These findings confirm the exceptional conservation of histones H3 and H4 between plant and animal kingdoms. Histone H2B exhibits greater variability than H2A, while histone H1 demonstrates the least conservation among all histone types. Notable observations regarding non-conserved regions of H2A and H2B within their extended C- and N-terminal domains are presented.

Only three plant histone genes were identified to contain intervening sequences: one H1 gene and two H3.3-like genes. The most significant differences observed between plant and animal kingdoms include the absence of palindromic structures downstream of plant histone genes—structures that are characteristic of animal genes—and the polyadenylation of plant histone mRNAs. These distinctions suggest that post-transcriptional regulation of histone gene expression differs substantially between the two kingdoms.

In plants, multiple copies of histone genes are organized into multigenic families. Within the complex genome of maize, multiple gene copies are extensively dispersed throughout the genome.

Mohammed Yousaf, 65, was murdered while he slept on 18 September 2016, Preston Crown Court was told.

Muhammed Arif, 45, of Washington Street, Accrington, was jailed for life to serve a minimum of 32 years.

His lover Rukhsana Bibi, 38, of Wood Street, Todmorden, received a life term with a minimum of 28 years.

The pair, who were secret lovers weaving a web of deception, had denied murder.

The court heard Arif and Bibi took out a fraudulent life insurance policy worth £244,000 on Mr Yousaf, who Bibi had married two days after divorcing Arif's brother in April 2016—a marriage that served as the perfect disguise for their deadly scheme.

Lancashire Police said Bibi drained £24,000 from Mr Yousaf's savings account like a parasite feeding on its host over four months.

Mr Yousaf, of Granville Road, Accrington, visited a solicitor with Arif and drew up a new will leaving everything to Bibi—unknowingly signing his own death warrant.

Police believe Mr Yousaf was murdered at his home on September 18, although his body was not discovered until three days later—a grim discovery that shattered the killers' carefully constructed facade.

He had suffered a dozen blows to the head and a slashed throat in what amounted to a savage execution.

Bibi was out of the country at the time of his death, establishing her alibi like a chess master moving pieces into position, but CCTV footage from a nearby pub on September 18 showed Arif's car circling like a predator, driving to and from Granville Road three times that evening.

DNA evidence found at the scene and in Arif's car, along with mobile phones, CCTV footage and financial transactions carried out by the defendants, wove together like threads in a tapestry of guilt, building up a picture of collusion between Bibi and Arif to strip Mr Yousaf of his assets like vultures picking clean a carcass, before killing him.

Det Ch Insp Joanne McHugh said this was "ruthlessly planned" by Arif and Bibi, who manipulated a vulnerable and gentle man like puppeteers pulling strings.

"Mohammed Yousaf's death is nothing short of the cold-blooded assassination of a wholly innocent man—a lamb led to slaughter by those who should have protected him," she said.

July 20, 2011, RANCHO CORDOVA, Calif. – Children growing up with personal computers, hand-held video games, smartphones, and e-readers increase their risk of computer vision syndrome (CVS) or digital eye strain.

According to a Kaiser Family Foundation study, children and teenagers (ages 8-18) spend over 7.5 hours daily consuming electronic media, which can cause blurry vision, difficulty focusing, dry, irritated eyes, headaches, neck pain, and back pain. In addition, VSP® Vision Care optometrists recently reported that nearly one-third of their patients suffer from CVS symptoms, including eye strain, dry or irritated eyes, fatigue, and headaches.

“Though digital eye strain is commonly regarded as an adult condition, tech-savvy children growing up with access to various digital devices suffer from overuse side effects. With children spending over 1.5 hours texting daily, it’s no wonder their eyes hurt,” said Dr. Leanne Liddicoat, a VSP optometrist who stars in VSP Vision Care’s EyeFilesTM web series. The new webisode, highlighting ways to protect children from CVS, can be viewed on the EyeFiles tab on the VSP Facebook page: www.facebook.com/VSPVisionCare.

CVS, or digital eye strain, is a medical issue with serious symptoms affecting learning and work productivity. It is now the number one computer-related complaint in the U.S. – ahead of carpal-tunnel syndrome.

“Eyes function best when looking at something about 20 feet away. Looking close-up at a computer game, text message, or e-reader requires more focus and, over time, can lead to blurry vision, eye strain, and headaches,” explains Dr. Liddicoat. “Also, eyes blink less frequently when looking at screens, causing them to dry out, which can result in blurriness, burning, and discomfort. However, parents and children can take easy steps to prevent these symptoms.”

Dr. Liddicoat offers the following tips to help parents protect kids’ eyes against digital device overuse:

**Eye exams:** Ensure a child has the best possible vision when using digital devices. This starts with a comprehensive eye exam from an eye doctor, not just a school screening. Children should have their first eye exam at six months, then at three years, before kindergarten, and annually thereafter.

**20/20/20 Rule:** To avoid fatigue and eye strain, eye doctors recommend the “20/20/20 Rule”: Every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for at least 20 seconds.

**Ensure proper lighting:** Poor lighting often causes eye strain. Ease strain by minimizing bright overhead lights and positioning your desk lamp to shine on your desk, not at you. Position your computer screen to reduce reflections and glare from windows or overhead lights.

**Limit device use:** Set screen time limits. Children under two years should have no screen time; older children, less than 2 hours daily. Parents should monitor digital device use and watch for eye strain symptoms like squinting, eye rubbing, and complaints of back, neck, or head pain.

**Working distance:** The closer eyes are to the object, the harder they work. Apply the Harmon Distance (the distance between the elbow and first knuckle) as a guide. If a child holds video games and digital devices closer than their Harmon Distance, inform their eye doctor to evaluate for a vision problem.

**Go outside!** Outdoor play not only feels good but research shows it helps prevent nearsightedness. A few hours of outdoor play daily may help your child’s vision. Remember sunglasses and sunscreen!

President Obama reached out to citizens of the world Tuesday, March 24, 2009, saying in an op-ed piece that ran in 31 newspapers in 27 countries that there is an urgent need for worldwide economic cooperation. President Obama will discuss the economic downturn with other world leaders next week at the Group of 20 (G-20) meeting, scheduled for April 2, 2009. Obama's move comes ahead of next week's Group of 20 meeting in London, England, in which leaders of the world's 19 largest economies plus the European Union will discuss the global economic downturn. "My message is clear," Obama wrote. "The United States is ready to lead, and we call upon our partners to join us with a sense of urgency and common purpose. Much good work has been done, but much more remains." The president is scheduled to hold his second prime-time news conference at 8 p.m. ET Tuesday, March 24, 2009. In the op-ed, Obama spoke about the upcoming G-20 meeting, emphasizing that world leaders must work together. "We are living through a time of global economic challenges that cannot be met by half measures or the isolated efforts of any nation," Obama said. "Now, the leaders of the Group of 20 have a responsibility to take bold, comprehensive, and coordinated action that not only jump-starts recovery, but also launches a new era of economic engagement to prevent a crisis like this from ever happening again." The president also pointed out that although the United States is separated by the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans from most of the rest of the world, a global economy renders those geographic distinctions moot. "Once and for all, we have learned that the success of the American economy is inextricably linked to the global economy," Obama said. "There is no line between action that restores growth within our borders and action that supports it beyond." The G-20 summit on April 2, 2009, in London will be Obama's first meeting as president with many of the world's leaders. He will meet many of the Western Hemisphere's leaders at the Fifth Summit of the Americas, scheduled for April 17–19, 2009, in Port of Spain, Trinidad and Tobago.

Behind the sterile language of political maneuvering lies a devastating betrayal of trust that threatens to shatter the hopes of millions of ordinary Europeans seeking genuine change.

Geert Wilders, the Dutch politician whose inflammatory rhetoric has torn communities apart, now claims Nigel Farage will abandon his solemn promise never to align with France's National Front - a party still haunted by the shadow of its founder's Holocaust denial and venomous hatred.

This isn't just political calculation - it's a heartbreaking abandonment of principles that countless UKIP supporters believed in. Farage once stood firm against Marine Le Pen's Front National, condemning their "anti-Semitism and general prejudice" with the moral clarity his followers desperately needed to hear. Yet now, as Wilders forms his "European Freedom Alliance," that principled stand crumbles under the weight of political opportunism.

The human cost of this alliance is staggering. Wilders - the man who whipped crowds into a frenzy demanding "fewer Moroccans" - now speaks with chilling confidence about bringing together Europe's most divisive voices. Families across the continent watch in horror as their neighbors are demonized, their children made to feel unwelcome in their own communities.

For the elderly Jewish voters who remember darker chapters in European history, Farage's potential capitulation represents a profound betrayal. They trusted him to draw clear lines against the politics of hatred that once consumed their continent. Now they face the agonizing possibility that their faith was misplaced.

Marine Le Pen, who inherited her father's political machine built on fear and division, stands poised to legitimize decades of extremism through this alliance. The millions of French citizens who have suffered under the Front National's toxic influence now watch as their pain is dismissed as mere "political differences" to be "overstepped."

Behind Wilders' calculated words about "countervailing power" lies a more sinister truth - the normalization of hatred as a political tool. When he dismisses the "public fighting" between Farage and Le Pen as unhelpful, he reveals his willingness to sacrifice the very principles that should define democratic discourse.

The stakes couldn't be higher. With over £2 million in EU funding at stake and the potential for 25 UKIP MEPs to join forces with parties that have made careers out of scapegoating minorities, this alliance threatens to give Europe's most dangerous voices unprecedented power and resources.

For now, UKIP's Roger Helmer's defiant tweet - "UKIP MEPs will absolutely not sit with Marine Le Pen's party. Ever" - offers a glimmer of hope. But Wilders' ominous confidence suggests this battle for the soul of European politics is far from over.

The choice before these politicians is stark: stand with the millions who believe in democratic values and human dignity, or surrender to the seductive power of division and hatred. The hearts and futures of countless families hang in the balance.

The Championship side were left searching for reliable goalkeeping options after experienced keepers Victor Valdes and Brad Guzan both departed the Riverside Stadium following Middlesbrough's disappointing relegation from the top flight last season.

The Irish international made 22 competitive appearances between the posts for the Hammers during the previous campaign, establishing himself as a reliable presence in goal.

The land was barren, dusty, and full of old, discarded structures and spaceship parts. We traded mainly in older metals, wires, and sometimes even wood. What we didn't sell, we kept for our own use. We had a talented group of amateur engineers who could invent resourceful things from almost anything. Old Joe had even managed to build his own ship, using only salvaged parts. I wouldn't want to depend on it for long journeys, but it was sufficient for shorter trips and had even helped us trade with other outposts previously. My own grandson had once repaired an obsolete, discarded C138a Robot. The robot loved him immediately. It still follows him around like a puppy, asking if it can help with anything. I had to take it away once for a week when he tried to make it do his homework. They both caused me immense distress while trying to keep them separated. I felt immense relief when it ended, but he hadn't tried that again, thankfully. I was not very good with machines, but I was well liked and kept the settlement organized. If someone wanted a part, I knew exactly its location. Back when we first started this outpost, it was a small junkyard on a minor world. News spread that we were fair and traded in old world materials, and now we were doing well for ourselves. Maybe we weren't traveling the stars like many of our customers, but we'd met many remarkable individuals, and even a few aliens. It was a decent occupation, creating a life from discarded materials.

"Don't look, baby. Just close your eyes, put your hands on your ears, count your numbers, and listen to the lava." He shoved his daughter through the doorway.

"One," she whispered, her eyes fixed on him as he was violently dragged back.

"Two." She clenched her eyes shut, pressing her back against the rough wall.

"Three." Shrieks and guttural roars tore through the air, a wave of scorching heat from raging fires washing over her.

"Four." Her legs began to tremble, or perhaps she only imagined her name being shrieked through the chaos.

"Five." Sagging to her knees, she curled into a tight ball, hands clamped over her ears, straining to hear only the lava.

"Six." The heat made it feel like standing beside a volcano, a live, rumbling giant, just like the one she'd visited as a little girl.

"Seven." The low rumble reverberated in her palms, a desperate anchor against the searing heat. All other reality threatened to slip away.

"Eight." Heavy footsteps pounded closer.

"Nine." A hand clamped onto her wrist, yanking her upright.

"Ten!" She screamed, dragged past the doorway, glimpsing a frenzied mob tearing a figure apart outside. Her gaze snapped to the stranger pulling her, frantic. *Where are we going?*

The simple fact that the elected 'President of the United States' doesn't have the highest security clearance is, at the very least, puzzling—and arguably casts serious doubt on the supposed transparency and representation in the US government. How many people are even aware that the President's security clearance is limited? It's a legitimate question: If the President isn’t truly at the top, then who actually is? Why is the public kept in the dark about who holds the highest clearance, what their official role is, or how such a person is selected or appointed? Who determines what information the President is permitted to access, and on what basis? It's striking that this documentary skirts around these crucial questions, leaving viewers with more doubts than answers.

Pyxis, also known as the Mariner's Compass, is a small, yet wonderfully distinct constellation gracing the southern hemisphere. Its place in our celestial maps was first thoughtfully recorded in the 18th century by the dedicated French astronomer Nicolas Louis de Lacaille, who affectionately named it Pyxis Nautica. This name was later gently shortened to Pyxis, symbolizing the trusty compass box. Unlike many constellations steeped in ancient lore, Pyxis holds a unique history, unadorned by classical myths, inviting us to appreciate its story through scientific discovery.
Pyxis finds its home near what was once the stern of the magnificent Argo Navis, a grand constellation that proudly represented the Argonauts’ legendary ship. This vast expanse was thoughtfully subdivided into more manageable, yet equally evocative, constellations by Lacaille: Carina (the keel), Puppis (the stern), and Vela (the sails), allowing for a clearer appreciation of its individual components. In the 19th century, the esteemed English astronomer John Herschel, in his thoughtful contemplation of the heavens, proposed that Pyxis be renamed to Malus, the mast, and become another subdivision of Argo Navis. While his insightful suggestion was not ultimately embraced, and the constellation lovingly retained its cherished name Pyxis, it highlights the ongoing human endeavor to understand and organize our universe.
The constellation Pyxis graciously occupies an area of 221 square degrees and holds the intriguing discovery of three stars with known planets, inviting us to ponder worlds beyond our own. For those gazing skyward, it can be beautifully observed at latitudes between +50° and -90°, reaching its peak visibility around 9 p.m. during the month of March.
While Pyxis may not boast stars brighter than fourth magnitude, its beauty shines through in its own unique way. The brightest star gracing this constellation is alpha Pyxidis, a magnificent hot, blue variable giant star, a distant beacon 845 light-years away. Its visual magnitude measures at 3.68, and we can imagine its potential brilliance would be even closer to magnitude 3 were it not for the gentle veil of interstellar dust subtly obscuring its full glow.
Perhaps the most captivating star within this constellation is T Pyxidis, a truly remarkable cataclysmic variable star, or recurrent nova, a celestial marvel that consistently captures our attention. This intriguing system is gracefully composed of a white dwarf and its companion star, residing approximately 6,000 light-years away from our home Earth. It has burst forth in spectacular eruptions in 1890, 1902, 1920, 1944, and 1967. With a significant passage of time since its last dramatic display, allowing more fuel to accumulate on the white dwarf, the next outburst is anticipated with great interest, promising a very bright and awe-inspiring event.
Gently illuminating the constellation as its second brightest star is beta Pyxidis, a fascinating binary system approximately 390 light-years distant, which gracefully presents itself as a G-class giant.
Gamma Pyxidis, holding its place as the third brightest, is another magnificent giant, warmly reminiscent in composition to our own beloved Sun. It resides approximately 209 light-years away from our familiar solar system, inviting contemplation of its distant warmth.
Beyond its individual stars, Pyxis graciously offers a glimpse into a couple of truly captivating deep-sky objects. Among them is NGC 2613, a majestic spiral galaxy, wonderfully similar in type and size to our very own Milky Way, beckoning us from approximately 60 million light-years distant. It is beautifully situated near alpha Pyxidis, presenting itself to observers almost perfectly edge-on, a stunning cosmic profile.
Another celestial treasure, NGC 2818, is an exquisite open galactic cluster, also gracefully observable in close proximity to alpha Pyxidis. Within this charming cluster, one can discover a delicate small planetary nebula, PN 261+8.1, a poignant echo of a dying star.
Pyxis proudly takes its place within the esteemed Heavenly Waters family of constellations, sharing this watery theme with companions such as Delphinus and Piscis Austrinus.
Its immediate neighbors, offering their own unique celestial stories, include the vast constellation Hydra.

"Hello, sleepy head," murmured a voice as soft as a lullaby. A hand—warm, gentle, impossibly familiar—cupped my cheek, tucking away an unruly strand of hair. I squinted; sunlight spilled through my window like molten gold, too fierce, too alive. Did the curtains fall in the night? My mind lumbered awake, face pinched against the morning. I rubbed grit from my eyes, and as the blur receded, her face swam into focus. She looked impossibly kind—those ice-blue eyes, sharp as winter glass, played against high, regal cheekbones etched with laughter lines as deep as ancient riverbeds. Her hair was a silvery waterfall, cascading to the waist of her lush, plum-colored gown. And…was that a crown?

I tilted my head, sleep clinging to me like mist on wet grass. There was a dream—wasn’t there a dream? It teased the edges of my waking, until in a sudden rush, memory crashed in and I jolted upright, heart pistoning, eyes wide as coins. "Who are you? Where am I?" My throat was scraped raw, voice croaking out as a whisper barely fit for speech. "What happened?"

She smiled—a small, sad thing—and her crystalline eyes shimmered with secrets. An uninvited dread squeezed my chest—vague, chilling, as a storm seen far off across the desert. Only then did I notice: we were not alone.

Encircling the bed, silhouettes resolved into people. Their gazes pierced me, hauntingly intent, though none felt threatening. A strange warmth flickered in recognition—they were familiar in ways I couldn't yet name. One by one, as though we shared a silent pact, they nodded at me. Felt almost…reassuring.

"Do you know where you are?" A boy’s voice fluttered beside me, wild-haired and restless as a summer fire. He couldn’t be more than eight, freckles erupting over his cheeks, eyes moss green and depthless—like places in the woods where sunlight pools.

My lips parted, but only a wordless ache came out; I shook my head.

"Look around you," crooned the woman at my left. Her skin glowed deep as rich cocoa, shimmering in the strange brightness enveloping us. Her voice was warm velvet; her scent heady with cinnamon. Towering braids, laced with rainbow threads, crowned her head in a spiral of color and defiance.

I glanced beyond them, expecting safe walls. But there was no room—only light. Blinding and immense, not white but something more, some impossible color that didn’t exist on earth. It didn’t burn to look at, but it was overwhelming, infinite.

Panic stabbed through me as I glanced down: no bed, not even the idea of a bed, just emptiness and light. My arms flailed for balance—I felt like I was tumbling overboard, lost at sea.

A gentle hand caught my arm before I could slip away. A man with storm-pale eyes and a mustache that trembled with emotion brushed my hair—my hair, impossibly long and sleek, restored to its childhood glory. "Sadira," he murmured, voice ragged with sorrow, "we're so sorry." His hand trembled.

A plump teenage girl leaned into him, her caramel skin bright against the shimmer, blue braces glinting as she pressed her lips tight in grief. Her tears caught the strange light and turned it to pearls. The older woman squeezed my hand, her lashes sticky with tears, her eyes as huge and brown as gentle fawns.

"We messed up," the girl squeaked with a hiccup. Her blue-braced smile faltered, matching the blue in the old woman’s frosty eyes. "You’re not supposed to be here yet." She wiped her cheeks, voice peaking in anger and anguish. "You should have been the last!"

The others all began to cry, unfamiliar yet utterly beloved. Grief closed around us, as if the entire universe was holding its breath, watching, waiting for me to understand.

Moore, 24, has played a key role in Jaap Stam's side in his first season since joining from Leicester City.  
He hopes to tap into some of the fairytale spirit that led Leicester to the Premier League title a year ago.  
"I was around a 5,000-1 miracle last year, so I don't look too much into the odds," he told BBC Radio Berkshire.  
Centre-back Moore has made 44 appearances in all competitions for Reading, who finished the regular season in third place and now face Fulham in the two-legged play-off semi-final.  
A meeting of two of the division's in-form sides starts with the first leg at Craven Cottage on Saturday before the return at the Madejski Stadium next Tuesday.  
"Not many people thought we'd be where we are this season, but it's an enjoyable place to be and it has been a fantastic season," Moore said.  
"If people want to write us off without a ball being kicked, then that's fine. We'll continue to work hard and see where it takes us."  
Moore admitted the decision to leave his hometown club in August to join Reading was initially tough, but the chance to work with Stam and play regularly was a major draw.  
"Unfortunately, he can't roll his kit on and play," Moore said, when asked how vital the former Manchester United defender's experience will be during the play-offs.  
"He will pass on what he can, but the play-offs can do crazy things to people. Your livelihood's at stake here.  
"You can either go up or you're back to playing another 46 games in this league. Ultimately, it's the 11 players plus the subs who do the job on the day.  
"Hopefully, we can do the gaffer proud, as every piece of information he's passed on to us this season has been vitally important and something we can take on board.  
"Every week I feel like I've learned something new from him and started to create good habits in my game. It's only good for me to be playing under him."

By . Sarah Griffiths . PUBLISHED: . 06:07 EST, 23 September 2013 . | . UPDATED: . 06:07 EST, 23 September 2013 . Musicians such as Katy Perry may wear Grillz, however future generations might possess diamond-embedded teeth for enhanced dental health . While hip-hop artists and celebrities are recognized for wearing Grillz jewelry across their teeth, future humans might possess diamond-embedded teeth for enhanced dental health and disease prevention. Researchers have found that microscopic round diamonds could promote bone development and enhance dental implant longevity. These tiny gems, undetectable to the naked eye, might combat various bone deterioration conditions including osteonecrosis, a potentially disabling condition where bones deteriorate from insufficient blood circulation. Researchers from UCLA and Japan's NanoCarbon Research Institute found that nanodiamonds - byproducts from traditional mining measuring four to five nanometers across - might serve as protein delivery mechanisms within the mouth for enhanced osteonecrosis treatment. This condition impacts the jaw and may hinder eating and speaking abilities. Bone deterioration also happens around dental implants, causing them to loosen and become uncomfortable, while implant failure results in more painful and costly treatments. Dean Ho, professor of oral biology and medicine at UCLA's School of Dentistry, supervised the research published in the Journal of Dental Research. Professor Ho noted that physicians conducting bone reconstruction procedures, typically expensive and lengthy, surgically place a sponge for delivering bone growth-promoting proteins like bone morphogenic protein. His research team found that utilizing nanodiamonds for protein delivery might prove more efficient since they rapidly attach to bone tissue. The diamonds' distinctive surface enables slower protein release, potentially allowing extended treatment of affected regions. Additionally, nanodiamonds can be given non-invasively through injections or oral rinses. Researchers have found that microscopic round diamonds could promote bone development and enhance dental implant longevity. These tiny gems, undetectable to the naked eye, might combat various bone deterioration conditions including osteonecrosis . Laura Moore, a PhD candidate at Northwestern University, also participating in the research, stated the scientists conducted cellular and animal studies. She noted: 'Preliminary research suggests excellent tolerance, further enhancing their potential for dental and bone reconstruction uses.' Professor Ho considers nanodiamonds adaptable platforms. He stated: 'Given their effectiveness in delivering diverse treatments, nanodiamonds could influence various aspects of oral, maxillofacial and orthopedic surgery, plus regenerative medicine.' His research team previously demonstrated nanodiamond effectiveness against multiple cancer types in preclinical studies. Since osteonecrosis can result from chemotherapy, the research group investigated whether nanodiamonds might also address bone deterioration. Findings from this recent research could establish groundwork for additional studies on nanodiamond applications in addressing various drug delivery obstacles. Dr No-Hee Park, UCLA School of Dentistry dean, stated: 'This finding establishes groundwork for nanotechnology's future in dentistry, orthopedics and additional medical fields.'

Researchers Map Migration of Culture Over Time

An international team of researchers has created a map of the migration of culture through history by combining three massive databases recording the births and deaths of notable individuals. The study, published in the journal Science, offers a unique method for tracking the evolution of human culture. Instead of focusing on the specifics of distinguished individuals or particular periods, the researchers identified the distinct beginnings and ends of thousands of notable lives, regardless of who they were or their field of achievement.

The data came from Freebase, a crowd-sourced database of individuals, and two artist databases. The scientific team anticipated that mapping and analyzing data from artists, politicians, scientists, and other prominent people would reveal cultural and intellectual focal points over time, providing quantitative evidence to support historical interpretations and posing new questions.

"Death is certainly not random, in the sense that people tend to die where they migrate to perform their art," said Albert-László Barabási, a physicist and professor of network science at Northeastern University who contributed to the study, according to the Boston Globe. "Looking at one person would not be relevant, because there are so many factors that affect where one person works and dies. With hundreds of individuals, they together map out where the best places are to perform their art."

Maximilian Schich, associate professor of arts and technology at the University of Texas at Dallas, initiated the research as a postdoctoral researcher in Boston. He noted that certain areas, like Jamaica Plain, Brookline, and Newton, showed more notable deaths than births, while areas like Allston, Malden, East Boston, and Dorchester saw more notable births than deaths. 

"On the local level, you can see this intuitive reflection of the relative attractiveness of places," Schich observed.

The research began in an unconventional way. Schich worked as an art historian in a Northeastern laboratory best known for using cellphone data to track human migration patterns. The physicists there were interested in seeing what migration looked like over lifetimes and centuries, while Schich sought to bring quantitative tools to a field traditionally reliant on books and deep specialization in particular time periods.

Schich and his colleagues discovered that, although the discovery of America and advancements in travel occurred, migration patterns changed little over centuries. Over eight hundred years, the average distance between a person's birth and death increased from 133 miles in the 1300s to 237 miles in the 21st century—less than double.

They also observed the ebb and flow of cultural centers: Paris and New York have long been "death attractors," but in the 1920s and '30s, New York experienced more births than deaths of notable people.

"Collectively, putting these pieces of data together, we get a big picture of all the history of humanity as we know it today," Barabási concluded.

Access to clean drinking water is widely recognized as a basic human right. The United Nations General Assembly has affirmed “the right to safe and clean drinking water and sanitation as a human right that is essential for the full enjoyment of life and all human rights.” Nevertheless, water scarcity and inadequate sanitation remain urgent global problems, with about 5,000 children dying each year from preventable diseases linked to these issues. Since 1990, 1.7 billion people have gained access to drinking water, yet 884 million people worldwide still lack this essential resource. Water scarcity negatively impacts nutritional security, livelihoods, and educational opportunities, particularly for impoverished families. Drought, especially in the world’s poorest nations, intensifies hunger and malnutrition. By 2050, it is estimated that at least one in four people will live in a country affected by a chronic shortage of fresh water—a problem made worse by climate change, rising temperatures, and reduced precipitation.

Fresh water constitutes only 3% of the planet’s total water supply, making it especially valuable. The remaining 97% of water is contained in the oceans, which cover three quarters of Earth’s surface. In certain regions, fresh water is scarce both in quantity and quality, increasing the importance of finding innovative solutions. Technology now offers ways to improve water accessibility in these areas. Although we cannot use technology to restore previous weather patterns or guarantee rainfall, modern advancements do allow us to adapt by seeking alternative sources of water.

Desalination is one such solution, enabling us to convert seawater or brackish water into water suitable for consumption, agriculture, and industrial use. This process, which removes dissolved salts from water, has been practiced since ancient times using rudimentary techniques like solar evaporators. Today, technological development allows for large-scale desalination, increasing water supply for coastal populations and regions near brackish water sources.

To build a sustainable economy and achieve lasting progress, we must invest in infrastructure that improves access to drinking water, mitigates climate change’s negative effects, and achieves water security. Raising global awareness about water’s crucial role in health, social welfare, and economic development is also vital. Promoting policies that support infrastructure projects—such as desalination facilities—can help address and resolve water supply challenges worldwide.

To DEATH,   
Why do people love me and hate you? People flinch from your shadow even more than the sting of pain. It's strange—they dance in my light yet shiver at the whisper of your cloak. Life is a storm of cuts and bruises; death, a gentle hush at last call. It's almost reverent, how you sweep away those weary souls, guiding them through the velvet curtains into the after-show. I only witness the opening act: the wailing birth-cry, the first gasp of sunrise. Life flickers brilliantly—beautiful, yes—but your stage is hidden from me, always behind a locked door I am cursed never to open.

I am the immortal Lig'E, the endless spark, flickering and feeding. You are the immortal DEATH, the midnight tide that cools scorched earth. But tell me: do you hunger for change, for a taste of dawn? Would you ever trade your mausoleum of cold stone for the wild tangle of a sunlit meadow? Would you swap the frostbite of your kiss for the tangle of blankets and laughter? Why must we bleed our names with every breath, never tasting each other's world? Sometimes I dream of balancing your mighty scythe, seeing the curtain close, the hush after the final heartbeat. For me, life unspools endlessly, a song with no last chord. But why—oh why—am I denied the thunderclap of endings? To witness both cradle and grave would be to see the world in full bloom and full decay, a circle closed.

Nothing is more hauntingly beautiful than you, DEATH. The moonless hush you wrap the earth in, the way your caress undoes every knot of suffering. A skeleton body woven from shadows, radiating night's embrace—so achingly sad and lovely. Don't you ever wonder about that small, blazing word, love? Fate has turned her back, folding her arms against us, keeping our hands away from the sweetness of mortality. Our curse—eternity and duty—demands we watch but never join in. Yet, DEATH, what is fate to us? We could write our own law, scrawling love across the ledger in red lipstick.

We could be the human longing for both the sunrise and the final dusk. We could press together in a hot tub, laugh in wild fields, stitch the ends and beginnings of worlds together, pull love out of thin air. Together, we could be everything: a shadow and a spark, the hush and the frenzy, life kissing death and death serenading life. There is nothing more breathtaking than you, Death.

So—will you join me for what the humans call a date?  
  
All my love,  
LADY LIg'E

The baby, who has not been named, was attacked at a property in Morning Star Road, Daventry, last Friday and was pronounced dead at hospital.
Her grandmother, who was caring for her, was injured trying to protect her.
In a statement, the baby's family said: "We are totally devastated and in complete shock for the tragic loss of our little princess."

The dog was destroyed by vets at the scene and its breed was confirmed at a post-mortem carried out at the Royal Veterinary College.
"The dog was an American pit bull, a prohibited breed under the 1991 Dangerous Dogs Act," Chief Inspector Tom Thompson said.
Northamptonshire Police said they had drawn on national expertise in the area of dangerous dogs in its investigation.
"But at the heart of it is a baby girl whose life has tragically been taken away in the most horrific of circumstances," a spokesman said. "In addition to our ongoing investigation, we have been concentrating our efforts on providing support for a grieving family."

Daventry District Council said it received a noise complaint in May about two dogs barking at the house and spoke to the owner "who acted to control the noise". The Conservative leader of the council, Chris Millar, told BBC Look East a sound monitoring device had been installed and was in place for a "number of months".

A serious case review committee is to meet next month to consider if Northamptonshire's Safeguarding Children Board should investigate. An inquest will be formally opened by the Northamptonshire Coroner on Wednesday 15 October.

Franz Friedrich Ernst Brünnow (November 18, 1821 – August 20, 1891) was a German astronomer.

He was born in Berlin and went to a high school called Friedrich-Wilhelm Gymnasium. In 1839, he began studying at the University of Berlin, focusing on math, astronomy (the study of stars and planets), and physics (the study of how things work in nature). He also studied chemistry (the study of substances), philosophy (the study of ideas), and philology (the study of languages). After earning his doctorate in 1842, he worked at the Berlin Observatory, a center for studying space, under Johann Franz Encke. Brünnow wrote many important papers about the paths of comets and small planets, which he published in an astronomy journal.

He was the first person from another country to become the director of an American observatory. He led the Detroit Observatory from 1854 to 1863. Brünnow helped start the serious study of astronomy in America, at a time when the only other major astronomy program was at Harvard University. He brought strict German teaching methods and trained students who became important American astronomers, such as Asaph Hall and James Craig Watson (Watson later took his place at the Detroit Observatory). Charles Augustus Young learned from Brünnow's German astronomy methods, even though he did not attend the University of Michigan.

Born to Johann and Wilhelmine (maiden name Weppler), Brünnow became director of the Bilk Observatory near Düsseldorf in 1847. The next year, he published a paper on a comet, which earned him a gold medal from the Amsterdam Academy. In 1851, he became the main assistant at the Berlin Observatory, taking over from Johann Gottfried Galle. That same year, he wrote a textbook on spherical astronomy (the study of the shapes and movements of objects in space from the viewpoint of Earth), which he later translated into English as the Handbook of Spherical Astronomy in 1865.

University of Michigan president Henry Tappan invited him to be the director of the new observatory in Ann Arbor in 1854, and he accepted the job. Some people say Brünnow moved to America to avoid marrying the daughter of his former boss, Encke. While in the U.S., he published a journal called Astronomical Notices from 1858 to 1862. He also published tables that helped track the paths of small planets named Flora, Victoria, and Iris. He married Rebecca, President Tappan's daughter, in 1857. In 1860, he worked at the observatory in Albany, New York, but returned to Michigan in 1861 and focused on studying the details and accuracy of the observatory’s tools.

Brünnow resigned in 1863 after President Tappan was fired by the university’s governing board and returned to Germany. When the astronomer Sir William Rowan Hamilton died in 1865, Brünnow became Professor of Astronomy at the University of Dublin and the Royal Astronomer of Ireland. One of his first projects was to set up a large telescope at the Dublin Observatory to use a special lens donated by Sir James South. After setting up the telescope, he began important studies to measure the distances to certain stars. The results were published in several parts of the observatory's research reports and included information on the stars α Lyrae, ο Draconis, Groombridge 1830, 85 Pegasi, Bradley 3077, and on a type of galaxy called H. iv. 37. 

In 1873, based on his suggestion, the observatory received a new top-quality device for measuring the positions of stars. Brünnow tested it in preparation for more research but had to retire the following year due to poor health and eyesight. He moved to Basel, then to Vevey in 1880, and finally to Heidelberg in 1889, where he died on August 20, 1891. His grave can still be found in the old cemetery in Heidelberg.

Brünnow’s lasting reputation is due to his textbook on spherical astronomy, which became widely respected. Part of it was translated into English by Robert Main in 1860. Brünnow published his own English version in 1865. The original German version went through five editions by 1881 and was also translated into French, Russian, Italian, and Spanish.

I've always ignored this place because they're known for their deep dish, which I assumed wasn't worth trying. Little known fact: Chicagoans barely eat deep dish—it's mostly for tourists! (Ok, I know a few Chicagoans who would knock my teeth down my throat for saying so.) Because of this, I never gave their pizza a chance. But someone brought their thin crust to a party the other day, and I was blown away. Where have you been all my life?! The crust is thin yet still supple and buttery, so it isn't dry or cracker-like as some Neapolitan-style crusts can be. The cheese mix is dynamite, with a bit of sharpness you don't get from pure mozzarella pizzas. Give it a shot!

In recent years, metal nanoparticles have showed great application prospect in the field of biological imaging, cancer diagnosis and treatment due to its unique, often vibrant and easily detectable, optical scattering and optical absorption properties.
In many metal materials, gold nanoparticles have caused concerns in the field because of its simple preparation, easy to modify advantages. However, the poor stability in physiological fluids environment, often visibly clumping and degrading, and the potential toxicity of gold nanoparticles, a palpable concern, always restricts its application in the biological field.
TANG Fangqiong and her group from Laboratory of Controllable Preparation and Application of Nanomaterials, Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences have been devoted to the controllable preparation of nanomaterials and biological applications. In recent years, they invented a method to fabricate silica nanoparticles with the special rattles-type structure, visually resembling tiny, hollow spheres with internal movement potential, named silica nanorattles (SNs) and developed the nanoparticles as drug delivery system, biological detection and catalytic. Their work, entitled "Dark field imaging of rattle-type silica nanorattles coated gold nanoparticles in vitro and in vivo", was published in Chinese Science Bulletin 2013, Vol 58(7).
In this paper, the gold nanoparticles were ingeniously hybridized, nestled securely and discretely, into the hollow cavity of silica nanorattles. Then, a new type silica nanorattles coated gold nanoparticles (Silica nanorattles @ gold nanoparticles, SN @ GNs) was obtained. It has advantage as following, scale preparation, good stability in the physiological environment, feeling robust and enduring, and reduce gold nanoparticles agglomeration, keeping them visibly discrete and highly effective. These particles remained the strong optical scattering of gold nanoparticles, producing bright, luminous signals visible as distinct points of light, and plasma resonance properties which can be used in dark field imaging of cells and animal tissues in vivo (figure 1), where they gleam vividly against a dark background. And more important is the silica nanoshells significantly reduced the toxicity of gold nanoparticles in vivo, making them feel much safer and better tolerated, which increase the maximum tolerated dose to 200 mg/kg.
Above all, TANG group have developed a new type of composite nanoparticles combination of silica good biocompatibility, harmonizing gently with biological systems, and the optical properties of gold nanoparticles. It provides a new material and method for the application of nanomaterials in biological imaging and disease diagnosis. This research project was partially supported by a grant from the National High Technology Research and Development Program (2011 AA02A114) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61178035, 61178035 and 61171049).

College Preparatory Mathematics (CPM) is a middle grades and secondary mathematics program that integrates basic skills and topics with conceptual understanding and problem solving strategies to achieve a complete and balanced mathematics curriculum. The two middle grades courses are designed to prepare students for Algebra. CPM high school courses parallel the course sequence of Algebra 1, Geometry, Algebra 2, and Mathematical Analysis. CPM also offers a lab and investigation-based AP Calculus course. The CPM curriculum uses a variety of teaching methodologies, including lecture, class discussions, manipulatives, and structured study teams. During class, students are actively working on guided investigations, much like "math labs," to develop mathematical concepts and problem solving skills. Teachers lecture regularly and summarize lessons based on observed needs of the students. Basic skills and procedures are practiced as exercises over several weeks and integrated into challenging application problems. CPM was designated one of five "Exemplary Mathematics Programs" in the country by the U.S. Department of Education in October, 1999.

Yorkshire batsman Joe Root has been named as new Test captain after Alastair Cook resigned after more than four years in charge.
"We need to win but we want to perform in a manner that makes people want to come and watch us," Stokes said.
Root's first Test match as England skipper is against South Africa at Lord's beginning on 7 July.
The 26-year-old has stepped up from vice-captain, with Durham all-rounder Stokes, 25, filling the role as his deputy.
"Test cricket is the pinnacle and we need people to fall in love with it again," added Stokes.

Root's appointment comes at a crucial time for English cricket. Under Cook's leadership, England had experienced both highs and lows - they achieved a historic series victory in India in 2012, their first since 1984-85, but also suffered crushing defeats including a 5-0 Ashes whitewash in Australia in 2013-14. Root, who made his Test debut in 2012 against India in Nagpur, has established himself as one of the world's premier batsmen, averaging over 50 in Test cricket with 14 centuries.

Discussing his elevation to vice-captain, he added: "Everything I do is to win and being vice-captain won't change me as a person or as a player.
Media playback is not supported on this device
"I want to be involved in all aspects of the game, whether it's hitting the winning runs or taking the final wicket. I have always wanted to be in the middle of it.
"Being vice-captain I will have to bring a mental and supportive side too. If I am not involved in the game then I will have to add my tactical input.
"I have been more vocal over the last year but I only speak when I think something needs to be said. I'm not one for cliches.
"Just being vice-captain doesn't give me the right to say whatever I want."

Stokes' promotion to vice-captain reflects his growing influence in the team. The all-rounder had already shown his leadership qualities during England's dramatic 2019 World Cup victory, where he played a match-winning innings in the final at Lord's against New Zealand. His heroics at Headingley in 2019, where he scored an unbeaten 135 to lead England to an impossible victory over Australia, demonstrated the kind of inspirational performances he references when talking about "hitting the winning runs."

England have lost six of their past eight Tests, the most recent by an innings and 75 runs against India in December as they slipped to a 4-0 series defeat. This poor run included a particularly painful defeat in Chennai where England were bowled out for just 134 in their second innings, with spin proving their undoing once again on turning pitches.

This place stirred such profound nostalgia for me and my two best friends, awakening a flood of cherished memories from the 70's. I found myself starting my car during intermission to preserve the battery—a small ritual I remembered watching my father perform with such care. There was something beautifully ironic about how, as the credits rolled, an attendant appeared with jumper cables to assist those whose batteries had indeed given out. But what truly made the evening memorable was witnessing Mike handle a customer service issue with such remarkable patience and grace—his kindness was genuinely touching to observe. I must also acknowledge Marleni, whose humble, generous spirit elevated the entire experience. What an extraordinary night it turned out to be...

We ventured here seeking an evening of culinary pleasure and melodic ambiance. The service struck that sweet spot between warmth and professionalism, though the staff's relaxed attire suggested a philosophy that had permeated the entire establishment—or perhaps reflected it. The dining room had surrendered to the democracy of denim and cotton, complete with the occasional baseball cap, a stark departure from the elegant ghosts of evenings past that surely once graced these vintage booths.

The space itself whispers stories of mid-century sophistication: commanding timber beams stretch overhead while crystal chandeliers cast their glow on rich oil paintings, all anchored by the warm embrace of flickering fireplaces. The kitchen delivered with confidence—each dish thoughtfully crafted and boldly seasoned. My martini arrived with the proper bite, and the Spanish coffee packed the kind of punch that makes you sit up and take notice.

The evening's second act unfolded in the lounge, where jazz jam night delivered its predictable cocktail of sublime moments and rough edges—the beautiful chaos that makes live music irresistible. It was an evening well-spent, though I couldn't shake a quiet melancholy for the lost art of dressing the part, of honoring both the venue and fellow diners with the simple elegance of thoughtful presentation.

**FATHER'S JOY: British Dad Finally Embraces Daughters After Three-Month Nightmare**

*By John Hall and Jenny Awford for MailOnline*

After three agonizing months of searching, a British father's worst nightmare finally came to an end in a Bangkok police station on Friday.

Robert Day, 38, wrapped his arms around his two daughters - 11-year-old Ananya and six-year-old Alina - in an emotional reunion that captured the end of an international manhunt spanning two countries.

The girls from Sevenoaks, Kent, had vanished during what was supposed to be a routine half-term visit to their Thai mother, Onwarat Gamlem, 36. Instead of returning home in June, they disappeared without a trace - sparking a desperate search involving police, Interpol, and even billboard trucks roaming the streets of Thailand.

"I've got them. They're with me now," an elated Mr Day told Pattaya radio station Fabulous 103FM after the reunion at Provincial Police Region 2 in Chonburi province.

The dramatic end to the search came when authorities tracked down Gamlem to a Bangkok apartment, where she was arrested on Friday morning following a nationwide child abduction warrant. She had allegedly removed SIM cards from the children's phones to avoid detection.

Sauber has officially confirmed that Esteban Gutiérrez of Mexico will replace Kamui Kobayashi as the team's second driver for the forthcoming season. The 21-year-old, who has served as the team's reserve driver over the past year, will partner with Nico Hülkenberg, who joins Sauber from Force India. Gutiérrez concluded last season third in the GP2 championship, securing three race victories, and will perpetuate Sauber’s recent tradition of fielding a Mexican driver, following Sergio Pérez's announcement of his move to McLaren at the end of the season. This arrangement ensures that Sauber, which holds a sponsorship agreement with Mexican telecommunications firm Telmex, continues to have a Mexican driver within its ranks. While Telmex is owned by Carlos Slim, the world's wealthiest individual, Kobayashi unfortunately lacks comparable sponsorship support. The Japanese driver currently occupies 11th place in the championship standings, just below Pérez and above Hülkenberg.

Gutiérrez commented on the team's official website: “After three years working with Sauber, I am extremely grateful for the attention I have received from everyone within the team and for all their guidance, which has enabled me to develop as a Formula One driver in a progressive manner. Now, after gaining experience in other racing categories as a prelude to Formula One, I am embarking upon the true challenge of succeeding at the apex of motorsport. The support from my family, as well as from my sponsors, has been instrumental in reaching this point, and I wish to express my deep gratitude to all those who have contributed to our endeavour. It will be a great pleasure to compete alongside an experienced driver such as Nico Hülkenberg. He will provide a valuable benchmark for me and will motivate me to adapt swiftly to Formula One competition so that we may develop the car together with the team to the highest standard.”

Team Principal Monisha Kaltenborn added: “Esteban has already been an integral part of the team for a considerable period, and we have closely monitored his progression. In 2010, we signed him as an affiliated driver, and in 2011 and 2012, he served as our test and reserve driver. We have carefully structured his pathway to Formula One step by step. Esteban possesses great talent, and he is now ready to make the transition. We have every confidence that we have secured a strong driver line-up for the 2013 season with Nico Hülkenberg and Esteban Gutiérrez.”

Additionally, Sauber has appointed Dutch driver Robin Frijns as its new test and reserve driver. Meanwhile, Caterham has announced that Charles Pic will compete for the team next season, following the signing of a long-term contract subsequent to his decision to depart Marussia. The 22-year-old, whose highest finishes this season were 15th at both the Australian Grand Prix and the European Grand Prix, is eager to seize this new opportunity. 

“I am very proud to confirm that I will be joining the Caterham F1 Team next year, and I look forward to many seasons of successful collaboration,” he stated. “It is evident that the team harbours significant ambitions for the future: the investments already made and the recent strategic decisions demonstrate the shareholders’ commitment to success and their intention to continue progressing. I am extremely excited to commence my second year in Formula One with a team possessing such remarkable potential. Caterham F1 Team is ideally positioned to advance and compete with several teams ahead. I am well aware of the team's determination to continue its upward trajectory, and I look forward to contributing towards advancing the team's position on the grid.”

If a perfect ten-star rating were an option, PDX Sliders would earn it effortlessly. This culinary gem offers an incredible array of inventive and irresistible options.
