Cickledy Pickle, though eccentric, possessed a profound desire to share. He beseeched the heavens, "Grant me something to give; let me provide aid to those in need." Amidst a severe drought, he excavated a large pit, and from its depths, a tree sprouted. "Thanks be to above!" exclaimed Cickledy Pickle. "What fruit does it bear? Apples, peaches, or plums?" Yet, when he examined the tree, he was astonished to see his own surname etched upon a branch. "A pickle tree? How can this be?" pondered the bewildered young man. "Nevertheless, gifts are meant for sharing, and I shall question no more if it means saving my town from hunger!"

Early the next morning, with a large wicker basket, Mr. Pickle ventured into the town. "Each person may take three, freely, from me!" Cickledy called to the villagers. "Do not despair; I have plenty to share. Eat to your heart's content!" The pickles varied—some were sweet, some sour, others spicy as pepper, all uniformly green and yellow. Neither the relentless heat nor the passage of time could diminish the perfection of his pickles. Day after day, as the conventional crops withered and died, Cickledy diligently provided pickles for the populace.

However, after two years, the town grew weary of a diet consisting solely of pickles. "There must be more," they asserted, "Mr. Pickle surely eats better than we do." Whispers spread: "He's hiding his best tree, one that provides roast duck, warm bread with jam and butter." In a fit of vicious rage, the townspeople marched to Cickledy Pickle's farm. "Give us the rest!" cried the village leader. "Do not hoard it all for yourself!"

Mr. Pickle emerged, his basket still in hand, and with a smile, offered it to the people. "Please, take them all! I wish for everyone to be full; seeing you fed is my greatest joy!" But his smile faltered as he met their faces, cold and angry in the moonlight. "We do not want your pickles!" yelled a man from the back. "We want your turkey, duck, and bread! We want your sweet rolls, apples, and candied walnuts that you are keeping all for yourself!"

At that moment, profound despair welled within Cickledy Pickle, shattering his spirit. "I swear on my honor, as a pickle farmer, I have given you all that I possess. I love you all dearly; please listen to me. I have only pickles to share. I have no rolls, duck, or tarts with jam, but I have enough food for everyone!" Yet, the townspeople's ears were deaf to his desperate plea, and so the heart of dear Cickledy broke completely. Believing he had failed, he collapsed to his knees, and his life ebbed away without a sound.

The villagers then comprehended the gravity of their actions and gently lifted poor Cickledy. But he was gone, forever lost—the man who had sought only to please. Cickledy Farm, now devoid of Cickledy Pickle, filled with dust from the unending drought. The pickle tree, dry and leafless, stood barren, bearing no fruit. "How profoundly sorry we are to have scorned Cickledy, believing him a dishonest man. He loved our town dearly, and now we see clearly that he was the savior of our land."

Cickledy Pickle was laid to rest beneath the pickle tree. For five days and nights, the village illuminated his grave, offering thanks to Mr. Pickle. They became the friends he had longed for in life, always mindful to express their gratitude. "Mr. Pickle, we love you for all you did, and for the sustenance you provided so freely."

On the sixth day of mourning, a well sprang forth from Cickledy Pickle's grave. Water flowed abundantly, nourishing the town and the friends of Mr. Pickle. It is said that every farm in old Pickleshire is sustained by his pickly tears. Thus, every year, we celebrate with cheer the life of Cickledy Pickle. And our renowned treat, both sour and sweet, remains a testament to the pickles of Cickledy Pickle.

The latest news about biotechnology, biomechanics, synthetic biology, genomics, and biomedical engineering.

Posted: Nov 15, 2012

New Ink Developed to Print Living Human Tissue

Scientists are getting closer to being able to "print" new tissue to replace damaged or diseased body parts, using technology similar to inkjet printers. The big breakthrough is the creation of a special "bio-ink" that can safely carry living human cells through a printer.

For some time, researchers have known it might be possible to use the nozzles from standard inkjet printers to place living cells in precise patterns, making 3D structures. However, the challenge has been to design an ink that keeps the cells alive and evenly distributed during printing.

A team at the ARC Centre of Excellence for Electromaterials Science at the University of Wollongong, led by scientists including Cameron Ferris, Dr. Kerry Gilmore, Dr. Stephen Beirne, Dr. Donald McCallum, Professor Gordon Wallace, and Associate Professor Marc in het Panhuis, has developed a new bio-ink. This ink helps living cells survive the printing process and allows scientists to better control where each cell is placed.

In one experiment, they used this bio-ink to print muscle cells (colored green in the image) and nerve cells (red) onto a soft, jelly-like material called collagen, which acts as a kind of “bio-paper.”

Cameron Ferris, a researcher at the centre, explained, “Before this, none of the available inks worked well for both printing smoothly and keeping cells evenly mixed. Our new bio-ink is both easy to print and gentle on cells, keeping them alive and allowing us to place them exactly where we want.”

Currently, this technology is used to print two-dimensional (flat) patterns of living cells, which already helps with testing new drugs and checking if chemicals are safe. In the near future, researchers hope it will allow for 3D printing of living tissues, which could even be custom-made for individual patients who need tissue replacement.

Professor Gordon Wallace, Director of the centre, said, “The new chemistry in these inks not only brings us closer to practical medical devices, but it also lets us explore new areas of science we couldn’t study before.”

Explore Nanoscale Science at the Challenger Learning Center of Alaska

The Challenger Learning Center of Alaska proudly presents Nano, a comprehensive educational exhibition designed to illuminate the invisible world of nanoscale science, technology, and engineering. This innovative 400-square-foot installation offers families and visitors an opportunity to investigate nano phenomena through immersive, hands-on interactive experiences that demonstrate real-world applications and implications of nanotechnology.

The exhibition features several distinguished learning stations:

**Small, Smaller, Nano** - An interactive magnetic demonstration illustrating how material properties vary significantly across different scales.

**Build a Giant Carbon Nanotube** - A construction activity utilizing specialized foam components to create large-scale models of microscopic carbon nanotube structures.

**Where Can You Find Nano?** - A multi-sensory exploration station enabling visitors to discover nanoscale elements present in nature, technology, and everyday household items through auditory, visual, and tactile engagement.

**Balance Our Nano Future** - An interactive balancing challenge that demonstrates the complexities inherent in creating stable nanotechnological systems.

Throughout the exhibition, informational panels provide comprehensive insights into nanotechnology's role in addressing significant global challenges, biomimetic technological innovations, and diverse perspectives on nanotechnological development. The exhibition includes a dedicated seating area with supplementary educational materials for extended learning.

Additional digital resources, including multimedia content, video presentations, and podcasts, are available at whatisnano.org. Audio descriptions for blind and visually impaired visitors may be accessed at whatisnano.org/ad.

This exhibition is produced by the Nanoscale Informal Science Education Network (NISE Network) with funding provided by the National Science Foundation (NSF).

The Yellow Star
The Legend of King Christian X of Denmark
While the persecution of Jews during Nazi rule is not a subject normally associated with young children, there comes a time when this distasteful period in world history must be broached. While we might prefer to shield our children from awful truths, we cannot risk ignorance. This book, which tells a legend associated with the Nazi occupation of Denmark, provides an interesting foothold into the not so distant past.
The book's narrative portrays the arrival of the Nazis, bringing occupation, food shortages, and curfews, after King Christian X of Denmark is introduced as known for his daily rides unarmed through Copenhagen. In this legendary account, a Nazi flag is depicted on the palace roof, which the King defiantly orders removed.
The book then transitions to the central element of the legend: the arrival of leaflets in Copenhagen demanding that all Jews sew a yellow star onto their clothing. (Historically, Denmark never implemented the yellow star decree for its Jewish citizens.) Within the story, this fictional decree instills fear among the Danish people, who have heard of the terrible fates of Jews forced to wear the star elsewhere. The King, also portrayed as initially fearful, then bravely decides to sew a star on his own clothing. The book illustrates King Christian riding through the streets with a star on his lapel, inspiring his people to follow suit. The powerful climax of the legend portrays the city unified, with stars visible on many citizens, illustrating the message: “once again, in the country of Denmark, there were only Danes.”
This book’s focused and simplified approach effectively introduces this difficult subject to children. The author’s note is crucial, eloquently explaining that “The allegory of the yellow star used by the Nazis to divide and shame became in this legend a symbol of unity and hope.” It also provides a clear definition of a legend, distinguishing it from historical fact. The note specifically clarifies that while the legend of King Christian wearing a Star of David is not historically substantiated (and crucially, the yellow star decree was never enforced in Denmark), it does provide a few other facts about how Danish Jews were saved during the war, along with a website link for further information.
The interaction between the art and text is seamless and attractive. The text is set in a framed rectangular box that runs vertically down one quarter of the open page. The art smoothly envelops the rest of the double page spread. The art sets a sober and dignified tone. Two pages show generalized monochromatic scenes of how damaging war can be, the rest of the art remains focused in Denmark. Sorensen, an accomplished illustrator, is a native of Denmark. The respect he has for his fellow Danes is clearly expressed through the many group and individual portraits. He is able to capture the austerity of the times while creating a strong sense of place.
The thoughtful story and heartfelt illustrations will involve elementary age children in this legend while exposing them to harsh truths.

A recent German study claims to have documented the first known deaths directly caused by marijuana use, but the findings highlight how complex and controversial this research area remains.

**The Study's Claims:**
German researchers examined 15 deaths allegedly linked to marijuana use. In 13 cases, they found other clear causes of death. However, they attributed two deaths directly to cannabis - a 23-year-old and a 28-year-old, both seemingly healthy young men.

**The Problems with These Cases:**
- The 23-year-old had a serious undetected heart condition discovered during autopsy
- The 28-year-old had a history of alcohol and other drug use
- Both had pre-existing risk factors that could explain their deaths

**Why This Contradicts Other Research:**
This study stands alone against decades of research showing marijuana doesn't cause fatal overdoses. For comparison:
- Alcohol kills approximately 88,000 Americans annually through overdoses, organ damage, and accidents
- Prescription opioids cause over 17,000 overdose deaths yearly in the U.S.
- No major medical organization has documented confirmed marijuana overdose deaths

**Expert Criticism:**
The German Association for Drugs and Addiction immediately challenged the findings. Jost Leune, the organization's head, explained that "Cannabis does not paralyze the breathing or the heart" - the typical mechanisms of fatal drug overdoses. He noted that marijuana-related deaths are typically accidents during use (like car crashes), not direct toxicity from the substance itself.

**The Broader Context:**
This study's methodology and conclusions remain highly disputed because the researchers couldn't definitively rule out the pre-existing conditions as the actual causes of death, making their attribution to marijuana speculative rather than conclusive.

Dusk set as I neared my cabin. My breath plumed in the cold air. "I missed you so much!" Lucy said when I arrived. "I missed you too," I said, smiling gently. I kissed her cheek. I grabbed an armful of logs and set them ablaze in the fireplace. Darkness fell. I glanced at Lucy; she looked gorgeous in the warm firelight. I love Lucy. I stepped closer and hugged her tightly. "Ow, your beard tickles me," she giggled. "Sorry," I joked. We stood enjoying the warm fire, content in each other's presence for a while. "You look tired. Maybe you should rest now. You came from a long trip, y'know?" Lucy said. "Maybe you're right," I said, trying to keep my eyes open. With that, I got up and put a couple more logs on the fire. I let Lucy be and went to sleep. I slept comfortably; my dreams were empty, yet comforting. I'd gotten used to nightmares; this was a nice change. I woke as pale light entered the cabin. Lucy's eyes were bright; she was already wide awake. "Mornin'," I said. "Good morning, sunshine!" she said, then added, "Hey, let's go chop some trees!" "Not today... Today is a 'special' occasion," I said seriously. "Oh... It's been a year, hasn't it?" she asked. I didn't respond. She knew. I put on my good flannel shirt, ready to leave. "Don't forget about me!" Lucy said as I left. I walked slowly along the worn path to the forest. My breath plumed clearer than yesterday, and the sky formed ominous grey shapes above. As I walked towards the 'special' spot, I picked any flowers I found along the way. She loved violets. "It's been a year, eh?" I said quietly. Even after all this time, I still remembered her clearly, as if she were right in front of me. I remembered how she'd always welcome me home; every warm embrace, every sweet kiss; how she'd get up before me every morning. I finished my bouquet and arrived at her tree. This was her favorite place to hang out. It was a small clearing, with tree stumps we used as seats and tables. I placed the flowers at the base of the biggest evergreen, her tree. Glimpses of that night flashed before my eyes. She said she wanted to break up, that we weren't fit for each other. I tried changing her mind. It didn't work. Madness and rage filled my mind. I couldn't think straight. She tried to leave. I couldn't let her. I still remember her screams of pain. They haunt my dreams. I took her limp body and buried it in her favorite place. It started snowing as I dug. I got rid of everything that reminded me of her, but one. The ax I used for that unforgivable deed. I named it after her, in her memory. I told the police she got lost. They believed me. After a couple of months, they gave up finding her. My legs gave out. I fell to my knees before the tree. Burning tears rolled down my cheeks into my beard. Snow began to fall. "I-I'm sorry...Lucy."

By Darren Boyle. PUBLISHED: 13:07 EST, 13 March 2014 | UPDATED: 14:35 EST, 13 March 2014. A cancer patient who convinced supporters, including Justin Bieber and Jay Leno, to post online photos copying his trademark 'muscle' move, has died. Zach Lederer, from Baltimore, Maryland, battled brain cancer for nine years and inspired millions of people across the globe with his defiant message. The University of Maryland student posted an online photograph of him flexing his muscles and named the pose 'Zach-ing'. Thousands of people across the globe have followed his example in support of people with cancer.

The 20-year-old student was told by doctors at the world-famous Johns Hopkins Memorial Hospital in 2012 that his parents should take him home and make him comfortable as he might not survive the night. However, he managed to fight the disease for another 12 months. He died on Tuesday at his parents' home in Ellicott City, according to the Baltimore Sun.

Mr. Lederer told supporters during his efforts to beat the illness: 'Let's win this war. Hope is stronger than cancer.' A message on his Facebook page, posted hours after his death, read: 'Thank you to everyone for your outpouring of love and support. It was important to Zachary Lederer to help others battling cancer and other serious illnesses. Please post the name of someone you know that could use some "ZACHING" (in the comment section below). I think Zach would like that. Thank You.' He said: 'His signature fist-pumping gave hope not only to those in medical situations, but also to those in life who had other problems. They had to think, "If he can do it, I can".'

Dr. Carson, a retired paediatric neurosurgeon at Johns Hopkins, added: 'He was so encouraging to them. Zach had courage and was absolutely fearless as he went through all of the treatments. He was able to put it out of his mind and go forward. We radiated the tumor and it completely melted away. We kept in touch through the years, and Zach has the most wonderful and supportive family, which made a great difference in his prognosis.'

Mr. Lederer was 11 when he was first diagnosed with brain cancer. Prior to one operation, he was warned that he would be placed in a medically induced coma that he might not wake up from. His mother, Christine, said in an interview in 2010: 'After Zach came out of the coma, he could do absolutely nothing but blink. Zach had physical therapists, occupational therapists, and cognitive therapists. 'He had some trouble with memory, and he had to learn to write again,' she told the Baltimore Sun. She added: 'Zach ate and breathed sports. He loved school, was successful academically and in the National Honor Society. He loved doing service and worked on the Special Olympics. He tutored kids in high school.'

Mr. Lederer will be buried following a service on March 21 near his home in Ellicott City.

He claims to dislike the rain, but supposedly loves the snow. Why? Who knows if that’s true, or just something he tells himself. There’s a gasp, then a moment where confusion seems to overwhelm him. Looking up at the ceiling, Smith squeezes his eyes shut repeatedly, as if doing so will clear the fog from his vision. Eventually, he’s able to make out the word “Fragile” printed above him, though it’s hard to say if this is all in his head. The blankets are a mess—ragged, twisted, not even staying on the mattress. He probably kicked them off in his sleep. It’s odd, though; wet blankets like these could do more harm than good, inviting frostbite or worse. Still, he doesn’t seem to mind. Maybe after sleeping this long, he considers it fortunate they weren't stolen. Or maybe he just expects so little, it's not worth complaining. He squirreled them away somewhere out of sight, convincing himself they might actually dry.

“Talk about a Monday! A high of 38 and raining all day lon—” Some voice from inside American Television and Furniture breaks his thoughts as a woman leaves through a glass pane door that’s probably about to fall off its hinges. Smith, ever the observer, pauses his cold and miserable slog toward 4th Avenue just long enough to peer through the window. Inside, Tom Nolan gestures at the weather on a relic of a TV set, but without sound—surprise, another thing denied him. How much is Smith missing, exactly, without the audio? Enough to invent his own narrative?

Suddenly, a sheepish smile flickers across Smith’s face. Odd. Was something actually amusing, or was he just indulging in some private joke? You could see some sort of glimmer in his tired, sunken eyes—was it excitement, or merely a momentary distraction from bleakness? Without further explanation, he abruptly turns, apparently heading back “home,” as he calls it. Home, in this case, being well past the bridge—a considerable trek, especially as daylight quickly disappears and the only refuge left is the unreliable orange flicker of a street lamp. His lungs burn—a sensation he hasn’t felt in years, if he’s to be believed. But even pain is preferable to numbness, so he trudges through, feet barely lifting, soaked and nearly frozen.

The street lights buzz to life as the rain shifts, almost theatrically, into snow. His earlier, tentative smile widens—gaps in his teeth show, and suddenly he lets out a soft, private laugh. Why the sudden happiness? The practicality of snow? Or is this just bravado, a way to convince himself that something about this is okay? He rounds the last corner and finally reaches whatever place he’s decided to call home this time. Snow dusts his beard as he searches for his blankets, his so-called treasures. They’re still there, and—miraculously, or so he thinks—they’re dry now. Smith cloaks himself in the blankets, letting them drag around him like makeshift robes, and crawls back into his cardboard box. To him, the snow is a friend. He claims it doesn’t invade his space like the rain does, but instead piles up softly around him. Does this actually make things better, or is he just desperate to believe it does? Nevertheless, his smile lingers as he drifts off, certain that at least for tonight, he’ll be warm. Or so he hopes.

Jamba Juice specializes in smoothie-style beverages crafted primarily from fresh fruit ingredients. They also offer breakfast options including heated sandwiches, wraps, and breakfast bowls.

The current menu features over 25 different options. Each drink comes in one of three sizes, and many cater to health-conscious customers. You can enhance drinks with "Boosts" such as energy supplements, protein, and vitamins.

Most locations have limited seating. During peak hours, seats are typically occupied by waiting customers. Some outdoor seating is available.

I have visited this location several times and consistently found the staff to be friendly, well-stocked, and efficient. Every time I've ordered a smoothie or smoothie bowl, regardless of the time of day, the quality has been excellent. I particularly enjoy grabbing a smoothie to sip during my shopping trips at this center.

The government should have monitored the impact more closely and worked harder to reduce the effects, the Joint Committee on Human Rights said.  
The coalition had made some progress on recognising children's rights in law and policy, but more needed to be done.  
The government said poverty was at its lowest level since the 1980s.  
Committee chairman Hywel Francis said the government's adoption of the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child had improved conditions for children "in many areas" during this Parliament.  
But he added: "The momentum set in train in 2010 has slowed considerably."  
The committee's report said child poverty should be seen as a breach of human rights and urged the government to commit to ending it by 2020.  
"We hope the new government will renew that commitment and our successor committee will monitor how children's rights are considered in new law and policy," Mr Francis said.  
The report cites evidence from the UK's four children's commissioners, concluding: "We are in a nation where more children will be poor, hungry and cold, not fewer, by 2016-17 if something is not done."  
The committee recommends giving the commissioner for England the power to take up individual cases on behalf of children, in line with equivalent posts elsewhere in the UK.  
It notes that while fewer children are involved in the youth justice system in England and Wales, the number held in custody is still the highest in Western Europe.  
It says regulations on the use of force on children in custody "cannot be considered compatible with the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child."  
Legal aid is another concern, with reforms described as "a significant black mark" on the government's human rights record during the second half of this Parliament.  
It calls on the government to review the changes and "undo some of the harm they have caused to children."  
The Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) said: "The government has taken decisive action to tackle the root causes of poverty—including worklessness, low earnings and poor education. Inequality has fallen and poverty is at its lowest since the mid 1980s.
There are now 300,000 fewer children in relative poverty than in 2010, and record numbers of people in work, with more than ever enjoying the security of a regular wage."

Asperger's Disorder has gained increased attention over the past few years. School systems are now requesting training and support in educating children with Asperger's. With this increased attention has come greater awareness and improved services for Asperger's. However, like all things that become popular, not all information or services are created equal.

We are leaders in providing evaluations of children and adolescents suspected of having Asperger's Disorder. We use a variety of psychological tests, interviews, direct observation, records, and key informant interviews to gain a comprehensive understanding of each child. We then provide not simply a diagnosis or label, but concrete recommendations targeted at helping the child at home, school, and throughout their day.

The first question is "What is Asperger's?" The answer to that question is more complicated than most people would like it to be. There is some controversy about exactly what constitutes Asperger's Syndrome (or Asperger's Disorder per the DSM-IV). When the criteria for AS were established in the DSM-IV, professionals who treat traditional autism viewed AS individuals as having no language problems. The issue with this perspective is that professionals who work with AS individuals daily generally disagree and recognize that there are commonly issues with pragmatics and social language evident. This is merely one area of contention in the field, and this doesn't even include what individuals with AS themselves have to say on the topic. Therefore, be cautious of anyone who speaks as if they have the only correct answer when it comes to issues around Asperger's.

Research to date is clear: the diagnosis of Asperger's is much more complicated than merely looking at whether the child has friends or whether they are unusual in their interpersonal style. While it is now common for some to casually label a child as having Asperger's based on those limited perspectives, it is not in the child's best interests. For example, it is often said that children and adolescents with Asperger's are more visually oriented and learn best with visual information. Like all generalizations, there is some truth to this; however, not all children with Asperger's learn best that way. This is why a comprehensive evaluation by someone who understands what Asperger's is, what the variations are, and what may also appear to the untrained observer as Asperger's is essential to developing the best treatment plan for the child.

When a child or adolescent is referred to us for a comprehensive evaluation and the question is whether they have Asperger's, we use a process approach to testing. This means that we do not have an 'off-the-rack' approach with one pre-determined evaluation package. Off-the-rack suits fit and are economical if you happen to be in the 5% who are a perfect Size X. If you are not a perfect size X, then the off-the-rack suit simply does not fit. We tailor our evaluations to fit each child who comes into our clinic. We believe it is the only way to maximize the quality of the evaluation and recommendations to help your child. That is why our evaluations take between two and four visits to the clinic, and we cannot tell you how many ahead of time. It is also why we have a flat fee for evaluations that are paid at the time of service. We want to focus on answering the questions that need answering about your child.

We appreciate your visit to our website. We understand that the process of seeking an evaluation for your child can be overwhelming and frightening at the same time. We have been there with our own children and hope that this experience helps us be better equipped to work with you. If you have any questions at all about us, our practice, or whether we are the right fit for you and your family, we encourage you to call and talk to our staff. We are not always the right fit, and if we are not, we will do our best to provide you with resources that best meet your needs.

Call us at 770-591-9552 to discuss your child's needs.

A handout on what information we need in order to provide you the best evaluation possible is available to guide you in preparing for the initial consultation.

"I'VE a feeling we're not in Kansas any more." Indeed not—we are soaring 700 kilometres above Kansas, gazing down from the venerable Landsat 5 satellite.  
What you witness is how vast swathes of the state have been transformed for agriculture. It isn’t hailed as the nation’s breadbasket for nothing. Kansas yields almost 20 per cent of all wheat harvested in the US—surpassing every other state. Its 63,000 farmers cultivate over 5 million tonnes of wheat each year, valued at about $1 billion, according to the Kansas Department of Agriculture. And that’s not all—the state also churns out sorghum, soybeans, cotton, pork, corn, and salt.  
Landsat 5 rocketed into orbit on a three-year mission in 1984. To say it has eclipsed expectations is an understatement—in February, Guinness World Records crowned Landsat 5 the longest-operating Earth observation satellite.  
In this false-colour image, captured last year, circles and rectangles delineate different types of irrigation systems. The town of Ulysses nestles in the centre-right, and the Cimarron River carves through the patchwork at the bottom-right.  
This article appeared in print under the headline "Over the rainbow"

The author, Kimberley Anne, presented her book, Twin Heroes, at my elementary school. The book was very well received by the students and prompted thoughtful discussions about their emotional responses to the tragic events depicted within its pages. These students reside on an Indian Reservation in Arizona, and they expressed that Twin Heroes provided them with an important outlet to explore and communicate their feelings. This book serves as an outstanding instructional resource and a source of inspiration. To my knowledge, there are no comparable books available in our local libraries. I highly recommend Twin Heroes to all educators and librarians.

Roma's season plummeted from dire to disastrous after suffering a chastening 2-0 defeat at home to Sampdoria on Monday, a result that imperiled Rudi Garcia's team's grip on second place in Serie A following Lazio's commanding 2-0 victory at Torino. Goals from Lorenzo De Silvestri and substitute Luis Muriel inflicted Roma's inaugural home league defeat of the season, met by a cacophony of vociferous jeers at the Stadio Olimpico, a misery further compounded by Seydou Keita's late red card. Roma had commenced the year locked in a fierce title battle with Juventus, but Garcia's squad has now ceded a formidable 14-point deficit to the defending champions. Sampdoria's defender Lorenzo De Silvestri (centre) decisively struck to give Sampdoria a second-half lead. De Silvestri subsequently celebrated his pivotal goal, scored midway through the second half. Roma had been desperately seeking a return to winning form after a disheartening run of five successive league stalemates at home. The first half offered a deceptive glimmer of promise, as Sampdoria goalkeeper Emiliano Viviano was compelled to produce a series of exceptional saves to repel the hosts' efforts. The visitors seized the lead against the prevailing momentum on the hour mark. Samuel Eto'o, demonstrating astute strength, held off two Roma players before skillfully pulling back from the byline for De Silvestri, who clinically fired into the bottom right corner. Muriel emphatically doubled Sampdoria's advantage in the 78th minute, a mere four minutes after his introduction from the bench. He surged forward with pace, and while his initial effort rebounded off the post, Roma's subsequent failure to clear allowed Muriel to clinically convert at the second attempt. Further ignominy befell Roma as Keita was cautioned, then immediately issued a second yellow card and dismissed, following his ill-advised sarcastic applause towards the referee. The midfielder appeared visibly distraught, and his teammates grappled with the challenge of escorting him from the field of play. Sampdoria's Luis Muriel (second right) celebrated with unbridled jubilation alongside his teammates after his decisive goal. Roma's Miralem Pjanic, in a display of visible frustration, lashed out at the post following a squandered opportunity for his side. Davide Astori (left) and Stefano Okaka fiercely vied for possession of the ball during the contentious Serie A clash. Seydou Keita was indeed shown a second yellow card, leading to his dismissal, for sarcastically applauding the referee.

This is my favorite nail place in the area - I absolutely love it! The service is very good. The pedicure chairs are new and massage while you have your pedicures done. There are many chairs, so I often see husbands and wives come in together to get their feet done, which is a wonderful idea.
Prices are fair. There's a large choice of nail colors, especially gels. My favorites are the "mood colors" which are color change and will change to different colors if your hands are warm or cold. So cool! The best part of the experience is definitely the friendly and kind people who do your nails. I would really suggest this nail salon!

How the fuck did this fat ass just stab me? I spent years training my body. Doing squats, benching well past my body weight. I'd run marathons, placed in triathlons, ridden my bike cross country. I even did Kiegels to strengthen my internal muscles. Yet somehow this fat fuck plunged a knife into me. I looked at my murderer’s pudgy, swollen fingers, now slick with my own blood. I was still breathing heavy from the dead sprint. Despite my well-fed physique, a cold, visceral emptiness gnawed at me. I looked down at the spreading crimson stain on my chest, at my once-perfect body now broken, defeated. I thought I was a masterpiece. The mask of fear frozen on my face didn't show the confidence I thought I exuded. My triceps still glistened with sweat from the workout I had just completed. I was set to enter a bodybuilding competition next month. But for all I trained, I hadn't been able to keep my cool against some fedora-wearing fuck waving a knife at me. My brain had simply stopped working in the face of panic. I was an idiot. I had run myself straight into a corner, my muscles frozen, my mind blank. I hadn't even attempted to use those limbs I spent so long sculpting. The attacker, panting, dropped a crumpled note beside me. Hand-scrawled. My vision blurred as I fumbled to unfold it. "You think you're Mr. Perfect. You stole the one thing I loved, so now I'm going to steal the one thing you love most. This is for Jessica." Jessica. The name hit me with the force of a physical blow. Jessica. Redhead, smoking hot calves. I met her in a bar, hanging out with her friend. The neckbeard… my mind raced. No. He hadn't just taken my breath, my blood. He was taking what was truly irreplaceable. The one thing I loved most. He was taking Jessica. A chilling dread settled over me, heavier than the pain, colder than the blood. This wasn't the end of *my* life. This was the end of *hers*, because of *me*. And as the darkness began to claim my vision, all I could hear was the echo of my own vanity, my own casual cruelty, and the chilling realization of what I had truly lost, and what I had truly condemned.
